[13] I'm writing this to you who believe in the name of the Son of God so you'll know — actually know — that you have eternal life.

[14] And here's the confidence we have with Him: when we ask anything in line with His will, He hears us. [15] And if we know He hears us — whatever we ask — then we know we already have what we asked Him for.

[16] If you see your brother committing a sin that doesn't lead to death, ask, and God will give him life — for those whose sin doesn't lead to death. There is a sin that leads to death. I'm not saying to pray about that one. [17] Every wrongdoing is sin, but not all sin leads to death.

[18] We know this: anyone born from God doesn't keep on sinning. The One born from God guards them, and the evil one can't lay a hand on them. [19] We know we belong to God, and the whole world lies in the grip of the evil one. [20] And we know the Son of God has come and has given us understanding so we can know the One who is real. And we are in the One who is real — in His Son, Jesus the Anointed. This is the true God. This is eternal life.

[21] Little children, guard yourselves from idols.

[1] King David was old now, weighed down with years. They piled blankets on him, but the cold never left his bones.

[2] His servants said, "Let us find a young virgin for our lord the king — someone to wait on him, attend him, lie against his chest. She will warm him."

[3] So they swept through every corner of Israel searching for the most beautiful young woman they could find, and they brought back Abishag of Shunem.

[4] She was stunning. She nursed the king, served him — but he never knew her.

[5] Meanwhile Adonijah, Haggith's son, was puffing himself up. "I will be king," he announced. He set up chariots, cavalry, fifty men to run ahead of him.

[6] His father had never once crossed him, never asked, "Why are you doing this?" Adonijah was striking to look at, born right after Absalom.

[7] He cut deals with Joab son of Zeruiah and with Abiathar the priest — both threw in with him.

[8] But Zadok the priest, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, Nathan the prophet, Shimei, Rei, and David's elite warriors — none of them sided with Adonijah.

[9] Adonijah staged a feast at the Stone of Zoheleth, near En Rogel — sheep, oxen, fatted calves slaughtered for the occasion. He invited every one of his royal brothers and all the king's officials from Judah.

[10] But Nathan the prophet, Benaiah, the elite warriors, his brother Solomon — them, he did not invite.

[11] Nathan went to Bathsheba, Solomon's mother. "Have you heard? Adonijah son of Haggith has made himself king — and our lord David doesn't even know.

[12] Listen to me. I am about to save your life and your son Solomon's life.

[13] Go to King David. Now. Say this: 'My lord the king, didn't you swear to me, your servant, that Solomon my son would reign after you, that he would sit on your throne? So why is Adonijah king?'

[14] While you're still speaking, I will come in behind you and confirm every word."

[15] Bathsheba went into the king's chamber. He was very old. Abishag the Shunammite was tending him.

[16] Bathsheba bowed low, dropped to her knees. "What is it?" the king asked.

[17] "My lord," she said, "you yourself swore by the Lord your God to your servant: 'Solomon your son will reign after me, he will sit on my throne.'

[18] And now — look — Adonijah has crowned himself king, and you, my lord, you have no idea.

[19] He has slaughtered oxen and fatted calves and sheep — a massive feast — and invited all your sons, Abiathar the priest, Joab the commander. But Solomon, your servant? Not invited.

[20] My lord the king, all Israel is watching, waiting for you to say who will sit on your throne after you.

[21] If you don't speak, the moment my lord lies down with his ancestors, Solomon and I will be branded traitors."

[22] She was still speaking when Nathan the prophet arrived.

[23] They told the king, "Nathan the prophet is here." He entered, bowed face to the floor.

[24] Nathan said, "My lord the king — did you decree that Adonijah would reign after you, that he would sit on your throne?

[25] Because today he has gone down and slaughtered oxen and fatted calves and sheep without count, and he has invited every one of the king's sons, the army commanders, Abiathar the priest. Right now they are eating and drinking with him, shouting, 'Long live King Adonijah!'

[26] But me — your servant — and Zadok the priest, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and your servant Solomon — he did not invite us.

[27] Did this come from my lord the king? Did you really not tell your servants who would sit on your throne after you?"

[28] King David said, "Bring Bathsheba back to me." She came in and stood before him.

[29] He swore: "As surely as the Lord lives — the One who has rescued me from every trouble —

[30] just as I swore to you by the Lord, the God of Israel, that Solomon your son would reign after me, that he would sit on my throne in my place — I will do exactly that. Today."

[31] Bathsheba bowed face to the ground, knelt before the king, and said, "May my lord King David live forever."

[32] Then David said, "Call in Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada." They came before the king.

[33] "Take my own servants with you," he ordered. "Put Solomon my son on my own mule. Bring him down to Gihon.

[34] There, Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet are to anoint him king over Israel. Sound the ram's horn and shout, 'Long live King Solomon!'

[35] Then escort him back up. He will come and sit on my throne and reign in my place. I have appointed him ruler over Israel and Judah."

[36] Benaiah son of Jehoiada answered the king: "Amen. May the Lord, the God of my lord the king, declare it so.

[37] As the Lord was with my lord the king, so may he be with Solomon — and may he make his throne even greater than the throne of my lord King David."

[38] So Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, the Kerethites and the Pelethites went down. They put Solomon on King David's mule and escorted him to Gihon.

[39] Zadok the priest took the horn of oil from the sacred tent and anointed Solomon. They blew the ram's horn, and the people roared: "Long live King Solomon!"

[40] The whole crowd went up after him, playing flutes, rejoicing with such force the earth itself split open with the sound.

[41] Adonijah and all his guests heard it just as they were finishing the feast. When Joab caught the sound of the ram's horn, he said, "Why is the city in an uproar?"

[42] He was still speaking when Jonathan son of Abiathar the priest arrived. Adonijah said, "Come in. You're a man of standing — you must bring good news."

[43] "No," Jonathan answered. "Our lord King David has made Solomon king.

[44] The king sent Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, the Kerethites and the Pelethites with him. They put him on the king's own mule.

[45] Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet anointed him king at Gihon, and they came up from there cheering — the city is shaking with it. That's the sound you hear.

[46] Solomon is on the royal throne now.

[47] The king's officials have already gone in to congratulate our lord King David, saying, 'May your God make Solomon's name greater than yours and his throne greater than your throne.' And the king bowed in worship on his bed,

[48] saying, 'Blessed is the Lord, the God of Israel, who has set someone on my throne today while my own eyes can see it.'"

[49] At that, every guest of Adonijah leapt up in panic and scattered.

[50] Adonijah was terrified of Solomon. He bolted for the sanctuary and seized the horns of the altar.

[51] Word came to Solomon: "Adonijah is afraid of you. Look — he's gripping the horns of the altar, saying, 'Let King Solomon swear to me today that he will not put his servant to the sword.'"

[52] Solomon said, "If he proves himself a worthy man, not a hair of his head will fall to the ground. But if evil is found in him, he dies."

[53] So King Solomon sent for him. They brought him down from the altar. He came and bowed before King Solomon, and Solomon said, "Go home."

[1] David's days were running out. He called his son Solomon close and gave him his final charge:

[2] "I'm going where everyone goes. Stand strong. Be a man.

[3] Keep watch over what the LORD your God requires of you. Walk in his ways. Hold his statutes, his commands, his judgments, his testimonies — every one of them written in the Law of Moses. Do this, and you'll succeed in everything you touch, everywhere you turn.

[4] Do this, and the LORD will make good on what he swore to me: 'If your sons guard their path and walk faithfully before me with all their heart and all their soul, you will never lack a man on Israel's throne.'

[5] Now — you know what Joab son of Zeruiah did to me. What he did to the two commanders of Israel's armies, Abner son of Ner and Amasa son of Jether. He killed them. Spilled the blood of war in a time of peace. Put war's blood on the belt around his waist and on the sandals on his feet.

[6] Use your wisdom. Don't let his gray head go down to the grave in peace.

[7] But show kindness to the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite. Let them eat at your table. They were there for me when I ran from your brother Absalom.

[8] And there's Shimei son of Gera, the Benjamite from Bahurim. He cursed me — a vicious curse — the day I went to Mahanaim. Then he came down to meet me at the Jordan, and I swore to him by the LORD I wouldn't kill him by the sword.

[9] But don't leave him unpunished. You're a wise man. You know what to do. Bring his gray head down to the grave with blood on it."

[10] David slept with his fathers and was buried in the City of David.

[11] He reigned over Israel forty years — seven in Hebron, thirty-three in Jerusalem.

[12] Solomon took his father David's throne, and his kingdom was rooted deep.

[13] Then Adonijah son of Haggith came to Bathsheba, Solomon's mother. "Do you come in peace?" she asked. "In peace," he said.

[14] Then: "I have something to say to you." "Speak."

[15] "You know the kingdom was mine. All Israel was looking to me to reign. But it turned. The kingdom went to my brother — because it came to him from the LORD.

[16] One request. Just one. Don't turn me away." "Speak," she said.

[17] "Please — ask King Solomon. He won't refuse you. Ask him to give me Abishag the Shunammite as a wife."

[18] "All right," Bathsheba said. "I'll speak to the king for you."

[19] So Bathsheba went to King Solomon to speak on Adonijah's behalf. The king rose to meet her, bowed to her, sat back down on his throne, and had a throne brought for the king's mother. She sat at his right hand.

[20] "One small request," she said. "Don't refuse me." "Ask, mother. I won't refuse you."

[21] "Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to your brother Adonijah as a wife."

[22] Solomon answered his mother: "Why are you asking for Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? Why not ask for the whole kingdom while you're at it — he's my older brother, after all — for him and Abiathar the priest and Joab son of Zeruiah!"

[23] Then King Solomon swore by the LORD: "May God do worse to me than I can imagine if Adonijah doesn't pay for this request with his life.

[24] As surely as the LORD lives — the one who established me, set me on the throne of my father David, who built me a house just as he promised — Adonijah dies today."

[25] Solomon sent Benaiah son of Jehoiada. He struck him down. Adonijah died.

[26] To Abiathar the priest, Solomon said: "Go home. Go to your fields in Anathoth. You deserve to die, but I won't kill you today, because you carried the Ark of the Lord GOD before my father David, and you suffered through everything he suffered."

[27] So Solomon banished Abiathar from being priest to the LORD — fulfilling the word the LORD had spoken against the house of Eli at Shiloh.

[28] News reached Joab. Joab had thrown in with Adonijah, though not with Absalom. He ran to the tent of the LORD and grabbed the horns of the altar.

[29] Word came to Solomon: "Joab has fled to the tent of the LORD. He's beside the altar." Solomon sent Benaiah son of Jehoiada: "Go. Strike him down."

[30] Benaiah came to the tent of the LORD. "The king says: Come out." "No," Joab answered. "I'll die here." Benaiah took the answer back: this is what Joab said, this is how he answered me.

[31] The king said: "Do what he said. Strike him down. Bury him. Clear from me and from my father's house the innocent blood Joab spilled.

[32] The LORD will turn his blood back on his own head — because he struck down two men more righteous and better than he was, killed them with the sword while my father David knew nothing of it: Abner son of Ner, commander of Israel's army, and Amasa son of Jether, commander of Judah's army.

[33] Their blood comes back on Joab's head and on the heads of his descendants forever. But for David and his descendants, his house and his throne — peace from the LORD, forever."

[34] Benaiah son of Jehoiada went up. He struck Joab down. Killed him. They buried him at his own home in the wilderness.

[35] The king put Benaiah son of Jehoiada over the army in Joab's place, and put Zadok the priest in Abiathar's place.

[36] Then the king sent for Shimei. "Build yourself a house in Jerusalem. Live there. Don't leave — not for anywhere.

[37] The day you cross the Kidron Valley, know this for certain: you will die. Your blood will be on your own head."

[38] "The terms are fair," Shimei said to the king. "Your servant will do as my lord the king says." Shimei lived in Jerusalem for a long time.

[39] Three years passed. Two of Shimei's slaves ran off to Achish son of Maacah, king of Gath. Word came to Shimei: "Your slaves are in Gath."

[40] Shimei saddled his donkey and rode to Gath, to Achish, to get his slaves back. He went. He brought them home from Gath.

[41] Solomon was told: Shimei went from Jerusalem to Gath and came back.

[42] The king summoned him. "Didn't I make you swear by the LORD? Didn't I warn you: the day you leave — anywhere — know for certain you will die? And you said: 'The terms are fair. I accept.'

[43] So why didn't you keep your oath to the LORD and the command I gave you?"

[44] The king went on: "You know — in your heart you know — all the evil you did to my father David. The LORD turns your evil back on your own head.

[45] But King Solomon will be blessed. David's throne will stand secure before the LORD forever."

[46] The king gave the order to Benaiah son of Jehoiada. He went out. He struck Shimei down. He died.

The kingdom was firm in Solomon's hand.

[1] Solomon sealed an alliance with Pharaoh, king of Egypt, by marrying his daughter. He brought her into the City of David and kept her there until he finished building his own house, the house of the LORD, and the wall encircling Jerusalem.

[2] The people, meanwhile, were still sacrificing at the high places, because no house had yet been built for the name of the LORD.

[3] Solomon loved the LORD and walked in the ways his father David had laid down — except he too kept sacrificing and burning incense at the high places.

[4] The king went to Gibeon to offer sacrifice there, since it was the great high place. On that altar Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings.

[5] That night in Gibeon, the LORD appeared to Solomon in a dream. God said, "Ask. Whatever you want — ask."

[6] Solomon answered: "You showed staggering loyalty to your servant David, my father, because he walked before you in truth, in righteousness, with a heart set straight toward you. And you have kept that loyalty alive — you gave him a son to sit on his throne, exactly as this day proves.

[7] And now, LORD my God, you have made your servant king in place of David my father. But I am a boy. I do not know how to step out the door or back in.

[8] Your servant stands in the middle of your chosen people — a nation so vast it cannot be counted, cannot be numbered.

[9] So give your servant a listening heart, to govern your people, to discern between good and evil. Because who could possibly judge this weighty people of yours?"

[10] The Lord was pleased that Solomon had asked for this.

[11] God said to him: "Because you asked for this — not long life, not wealth for yourself, not the death of your enemies, but discernment to hear and judge rightly —

[12] watch what I do. I am giving you a wise and discerning heart. There has never been anyone like you before, and there will never be anyone like you after.

[13] And the things you did not ask for — those too: wealth, honor — more than any king in your time will ever see.

[14] And if you walk in my ways, keeping my statutes and commandments as your father David did, I will stretch your days out long."

[15] Solomon woke up. It had been a dream. He went back to Jerusalem, stood before the ark of the covenant of the Lord, offered burnt offerings and peace offerings, and threw a feast for all his servants.

[16] Then two women came to the king. Prostitutes. They stood before him.

[17] One of them said: "Please, my lord — this woman and I live in the same house. I gave birth while she was there with me.

[18] Three days after I delivered, she gave birth too. It was just the two of us. No one else in the house. Nobody.

[19] Her son died in the night. She had rolled over on him.

[20] So she got up in the middle of the night, took my son from beside me while your servant was asleep, laid him at her breast, and laid her dead son at mine.

[21] I woke up in the morning to nurse my son — and he was dead. But when I looked closely in the light, I saw — that was not the son I had borne."

[22] The other woman cut in: "No — the living one is mine. The dead one is hers." And the first shot back: "No — the dead one is yours. The living one is mine." On and on, in front of the king.

[23] The king said: "One says, 'This living child is mine, and the dead one is yours.' The other says, 'No — yours is dead, mine is alive.'

[24] Bring me a sword." They brought a sword to the king.

[25] "Cut the living child in two," he said. "Half to one, half to the other."

[26] The woman whose son was alive — her insides twisted for her boy — said to the king, "Please, my lord, give her the living child. Just don't kill him." But the other woman said, "Cut. Neither of us gets him."

[27] The king delivered his verdict: "Give the living child to the first woman. Do not kill him. She is his mother."

[28] All Israel heard the ruling the king had handed down, and they stood in awe of him — because they saw it plainly: the wisdom of God was in him, to do justice.

[1] When Hiram king of Tyre heard that Solomon had been anointed king to succeed his father, he sent envoys to him — Hiram had always been David's friend.

[2] Solomon sent this word back to Hiram:

[3] "You know how it was with my father David. He couldn't build a house for the name of the LORD his God because of the wars surrounding him on every side, until the LORD finally put his enemies under his feet.

[4] But now — the LORD my God has given me rest on every side. No adversary. No disaster.

[5] So I intend to build a house for the name of the LORD my God, exactly as the LORD told my father David: 'Your son, the one I'll set on your throne in your place — he is the one who will build the house for my name.'

[6] So give the order: have my crews cut cedars of Lebanon for me. My men will work alongside yours, and I'll pay your men whatever wage you set. You know it well — no one among us can shape timber like the Sidonians."

[7] When Hiram heard Solomon's message, he was overjoyed. He said, "Blessed be the LORD today, who has given David a wise son to rule this great nation."

[8] Then Hiram sent back to Solomon: "I've received your request. I'll handle everything — the cedar and the cypress.

[9] My men will haul the timber down from Lebanon to the sea. I'll float it in rafts along the coast to wherever you direct me, break the rafts apart there, and you can take it from there. In return, you'll supply provisions for my household."

[10] So Hiram kept Solomon in cedar and cypress — all he wanted —

[11] and Solomon gave Hiram twenty thousand kors of wheat as food for his household and twenty kors of pressed oil. Year after year, Solomon paid this to Hiram.

[12] The LORD gave Solomon wisdom, just as he had promised. Peace held between Hiram and Solomon, and the two of them cut a treaty.

[13] King Solomon conscripted forced labor from across Israel — thirty thousand men.

[14] He rotated them in shifts to Lebanon: ten thousand a month. One month in Lebanon, two months home. Adoniram was over the labor force.

[15] Solomon also had seventy thousand men hauling loads and eighty thousand quarrying in the hill country,

[16] besides the thirty-three hundred foremen who supervised the workers and ran the project.

[17] At the king's command, they quarried massive stones — costly, dressed stone — to lay the foundation of the house.

[18] Solomon's builders and Hiram's builders, together with the men from Byblos, cut and prepared the timber and stone to build the house.

[1] Four hundred and eighty years after Israel walked out of Egypt — in the fourth year of Solomon's reign, in the month of Ziv, the second month — Solomon broke ground on the house for the LORD.

[2] The house Solomon built for the LORD measured ninety feet long, thirty wide, forty-five high.

[3] The portico across the front stretched thirty feet wide — matching the width of the house itself — and extended fifteen feet out from the face of the building.

[4] He set narrow recessed windows into the walls.

[5] Against the outside walls — wrapping the sanctuary and the inner chamber — he built a tiered structure of side rooms, ringing the house on every side.

[6] The lowest tier was seven and a half feet wide, the middle nine, the upper ten and a half. He stepped the walls of the house inward as they rose, so the floor beams of the side rooms could rest on ledges without piercing the sanctuary walls.

[7] When the house went up, it rose from stones already finished at the quarry — no hammer, no chisel, no iron tool of any kind made a sound on the site.

[8] The entrance to the lowest side rooms sat on the south face; a spiral stair climbed from there to the middle tier, and from the middle to the upper.

[9] So he built the house and finished it, roofing it with beams and planks of cedar.

[10] The side structure he built against the whole house rose seven and a half feet per tier, fastened to the house with cedar timbers.

[11] Then the word of the LORD came to Solomon:

[12] "This house you are building — if you walk in my statutes, carry out my judgments, keep all my commandments and live by them, I will make good on the promise I gave your father David through you.

[13] I will live among the people of Israel. I will not abandon them."

[14] So Solomon built the house and brought it to completion.

[15] He paneled the interior walls with cedar — from floor to ceiling — and laid the floor with cypress planks.

[16] At the back of the house he partitioned off thirty feet with cedar boards running floor to ceiling, creating the inner sanctuary — the Most Holy Place.

[17] The main hall — the sanctuary in front of it — was sixty feet long.

[18] The cedar inside the house was carved with gourds and open flowers. Cedar everywhere. Not one stone visible.

[19] He prepared the inner sanctuary deep inside the house to house the ark of the covenant of the LORD.

[20] The inner sanctuary was thirty feet long, thirty wide, thirty high, and he overlaid it with pure gold. He overlaid the cedar altar as well.

[21] Solomon coated the interior of the house with pure gold and stretched gold chains across the front of the inner sanctuary, which he also overlaid in gold.

[22] He gilded the entire house — every inch of it — until the whole structure was finished. And he overlaid in gold the altar that belonged to the inner sanctuary.

[23] Inside the sanctuary he set two cherubim of olive wood, each fifteen feet tall.

[24] Each wing of the cherub was seven and a half feet long — fifteen feet from wingtip to wingtip.

[25] The second cherub matched the first — same measurement, same form.

[26] Both stood fifteen feet high.

[27] He placed them inside the innermost room with their wings extended. One wing of the first touched one wall; one wing of the second touched the other wall; and their inner wings met, tip to tip, at the center of the room.

[28] He overlaid the cherubim with gold.

[29] On every wall of the house — inner room and outer — he carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers.

[30] He overlaid the floor with gold, inside and out.

[31] For the entrance to the inner sanctuary he made doors of olive wood, with a five-sided frame.

[32] On those two olive-wood doors he carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers, then overlaid the whole thing with gold, hammering the gold down into the carvings of the cherubim and the palms.

[33] For the entrance to the main sanctuary he made doorposts of olive wood, four-sided,

[34] and two doors of cypress — each door folding in two leaves that turned on their pivots.

[35] He carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers on them, and overlaid them evenly with gold, pressed into every carving.

[36] He built the inner courtyard with three courses of cut stone and one course of cedar beams.

[37] The foundation of the house of the LORD was laid in the fourth year, in the month of Ziv.

[38] In the eleventh year, in the month of Bul — the eighth month — the house was finished, every detail according to plan. Seven years he spent building it.

[1] Solomon built his own palace too — and it took thirteen years to complete the whole thing.

[2] He built the House of the Forest of Lebanon: a hundred and fifty feet long, seventy-five wide, forty-five high, resting on four rows of cedar pillars with cedar beams laid across them.

[3] The ceiling was paneled in cedar above the forty-five beams — fifteen to a row — that rested on the pillars.

[4] Three tiers of windows faced each other, light answering light across the hall.

[5] Every doorway and frame was squared off, and the windows lined up in matching rows, three against three.

[6] Then he built the Hall of Pillars — seventy-five feet long, forty-five wide — with a portico in front of it: pillars and a canopy facing outward.

[7] He built the Throne Hall — the Hall of Judgment — where he would render verdicts. It was paneled in cedar from floor to ceiling.

[8] His own living quarters, set in a courtyard behind the hall, were built the same way. And he built a similar palace for Pharaoh's daughter, the woman he had married.

[9] All of it — from foundation to eaves, from the outside in to the great courtyard — was costly stone, cut to measure, sawn smooth on every face.

[10] The foundations were massive — fifteen-foot stones, twelve-foot stones, the finest cut.

[11] Above them, more costly stone shaped to size, and cedar on top of that.

[12] The great courtyard was ringed with three courses of dressed stone and one course of cedar beams — the same pattern as the inner court of the LORD's house and the portico of the palace.

[13] King Solomon sent for a man from Tyre named Hiram.

[14] His mother was a widow from the tribe of Naphtali. His father, a Tyrian, had been a bronze worker. Hiram had inherited the craft — and more: he was filled with wisdom, with understanding, with skill for every kind of bronze work. He came to Solomon and went to work.

[15] He cast two bronze pillars. Each one stood twenty-seven feet tall, and a cord eighteen feet long could wrap around it.

[16] He made two capitals to crown them, cast in bronze — each one seven and a half feet high.

[17] Lattices of woven chainwork draped the capitals — seven for one, seven for the other.

[18] He made pomegranates — two rows of them circling each lattice — to dress the capitals on top of the pillars. Same for both.

[19] The capitals at the top of the portico pillars were shaped like lilies — six feet high.

[20] Above the rounded swell of each capital, just past the lattice, hung two hundred pomegranates in rows ringing both capitals.

[21] He set the pillars at the entrance of the temple. The one on the south he named Jachin — *He establishes.* The one on the north he named Boaz — *In him is strength.*

[22] Lilies crowned the tops. The pillars were finished.

[23] Then he cast the Sea — a massive bronze basin, perfectly round, fifteen feet across, seven and a half feet deep, forty-five feet around.

[24] Below the rim, two rows of gourds circled it — ten to every foot and a half — cast as one piece with the basin itself.

[25] It rested on twelve oxen: three facing north, three west, three south, three east. Their hindquarters all turned inward. The Sea sat on their backs.

[26] The bronze was a handsbreadth thick. The rim flared like the lip of a cup, like the bloom of a lily. It held twelve thousand gallons.

[27] He made ten bronze stands — six feet long, six wide, four and a half high.

[28] Each stand was built like this: paneled frames set into crossbars,

[29] and on the panels — lions, oxen, cherubim — and around the frames the same. Below the lions and oxen, wreaths hammered into the bronze.

[30] Each stand rolled on four bronze wheels with bronze axles. At its four corners were supports cast beneath the basin, with wreaths flowing down each side.

[31] The mouth of the stand rose into a collar a foot and a half high, round, eighteen inches deep, engraved — but its panels were square, not round.

[32] The four wheels turned under the panels. Their axles were anchored in the stand itself. Each wheel was two and a quarter feet high.

[33] The wheels were shaped like chariot wheels — axles, rims, spokes, hubs — all bronze, all cast.

[34] At the four corners of each stand stood four supports, of one piece with the stand.

[35] At the top of the stand was a round band nine inches high, joined seamlessly to the stays and panels below.

[36] On every surface that would take engraving — the stays, the panels — he carved cherubim, lions, palm trees, packed in tight, wreaths all around.

[37] That was how he made the ten stands. One mold. One measure. One shape. All identical.

[38] Then he made ten bronze basins. Each one held two hundred and forty gallons and measured six feet across. One basin to each of the ten stands.

[39] He set five stands on the south side of the temple, five on the north. The Sea he placed on the southeast corner.

[40] Hiram made the pots, the shovels, the bowls. And with that, Hiram finished every job King Solomon had given him for the house of the LORD:

[41] the two pillars; the two bowl-shaped capitals at their tops; the two latticeworks covering them;

[42] the four hundred pomegranates for the lattices — two rows for each, ringing both capitals;

[43] the ten stands and the ten basins set on them;

[44] the one Sea and the twelve oxen beneath it;

[45] the pots, the shovels, the bowls — every vessel Hiram crafted for King Solomon for the LORD's house was burnished bronze.

[46] The king cast them all in clay molds in the Jordan plain, between Succoth and Zarethan.

[47] Solomon left all the vessels unweighed — there were too many. The weight of the bronze was beyond reckoning.

[48] Solomon also made every furnishing for the house of the LORD: the gold altar; the gold table where the bread of the Presence was set;

[49] the lampstands of pure gold — five on the right, five on the left — in front of the inner sanctuary, with their gold flowers, lamps, and tongs;

[50] the gold basins, snuffers, bowls, dishes, and firepans; and the gold hinges for the doors of the inner room, the Holy of Holies, and for the doors of the main hall.

[51] When the work King Solomon had done for the house of the LORD was finally complete, he brought in everything his father David had set apart — the silver, the gold, the vessels — and placed them in the treasuries of the LORD's house.

[1] Then Solomon summoned Israel's elders, every tribal chief, every patriarch of every house — they all came before him in Jerusalem to bring up the Ark of the LORD's covenant from the City of David, from Zion.

[2] Every man of Israel gathered to King Solomon at the festival in the month of Ethanim — the seventh month.

[3] When all the elders of Israel had arrived, the priests lifted the Ark.

[4] They carried it up — the Ark of the LORD, the Tent of Meeting, and every sacred vessel inside the Tent. The priests and Levites carried them together.

[5] King Solomon and the entire assembly of Israel gathered around him before the Ark, sacrificing sheep and oxen beyond counting, beyond record — a slaughter too vast to tally.

[6] The priests brought the Ark of the LORD's covenant into its place — into the inner sanctuary of the house, the Most Holy Place, beneath the wings of the cherubim.

[7] The cherubim stretched their wings over the place of the Ark, sheltering the Ark and its carrying poles from above.

[8] The poles were so long that their tips could be seen from the Holy Place in front of the inner sanctuary — but not from outside. They remain there to this day.

[9] Nothing was in the Ark except the two stone tablets Moses had placed there at Horeb, where the LORD cut covenant with Israel after they came out of Egypt.

[10] When the priests came out of the Holy Place, a cloud filled the LORD's house.

[11] The priests could not stand to minister because of the cloud — the glory of the LORD had filled the house of the LORD.

[12] Then Solomon spoke: "The LORD said He would dwell in thick darkness.

[13] I have built You a house of grandeur — a settled place for You to dwell forever."

[14] Then the king turned and blessed the whole assembly of Israel while every Israelite stood.

[15] He said: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who spoke with His mouth to my father David and with His hand has made it real. He said:

[16] 'From the day I brought My people Israel out of Egypt, I chose no city from any tribe of Israel to build a house where My name would live. But I chose David to lead My people Israel.'

[17] My father David had it in his heart to build a house for the name of the LORD, the God of Israel.

[18] But the LORD told my father David: 'Because you had it in your heart to build a house for My name — you did well to have that in your heart.

[19] But you will not build the house. Your son — the one who comes from your own body — he will build the house for My name.'

[20] The LORD has kept the word He spoke. I have risen in my father David's place, I sit on the throne of Israel as the LORD promised, and I have built the house for the name of the LORD, the God of Israel.

[21] I have set a place there for the Ark, which holds the covenant the LORD made with our ancestors when He brought them out of Egypt."

[22] Then Solomon stood before the LORD's altar in front of the whole assembly of Israel, spread out his hands toward heaven,

[23] and said: "LORD, God of Israel — no god above in heaven or below on earth is like You. You keep covenant and faithful love with Your servants who walk before You with their whole heart.

[24] You have kept what You promised Your servant David, my father. You spoke with Your mouth, and today with Your hand You have made it real.

[25] So now, LORD, God of Israel — keep what You promised Your servant David, my father, when You said: 'A man of yours will never lack a place before Me on the throne of Israel — only if your sons watch their way, walking before Me as you have walked before Me.'

[26] So now, God of Israel — let the word You spoke to Your servant David, my father, prove true.

[27] But will God really dwell on earth? Heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain You — how much less this house I have built!

[28] Yet turn toward Your servant's prayer and his plea, LORD my God — hear the cry, hear the prayer Your servant prays before You today.

[29] Let Your eyes stay open toward this house night and day — toward the place You said, 'My name will be there' — so that You hear the prayer Your servant prays toward this place.

[30] Hear Your servant's plea — and Your people Israel's — whenever they pray toward this place. Hear from heaven, where You dwell. Hear and forgive.

[31] When a man sins against his neighbor and is required to swear an oath, and he comes to swear it before Your altar in this house —

[32] hear from heaven. Act. Judge between Your servants. Condemn the guilty, bringing his ways down on his own head. Vindicate the innocent, paying him back according to his righteousness.

[33] When Your people Israel are struck down before an enemy because they sinned against You — and they turn back to You, confess Your name, pray and plead with You in this house —

[34] hear from heaven. Forgive the sin of Your people Israel. Bring them back to the land You gave their ancestors.

[35] When the sky shuts up and there is no rain because they sinned against You — and they pray toward this place, confess Your name, and turn from their sin because You have humbled them —

[36] hear from heaven. Forgive the sin of Your servants, Your people Israel. Teach them the good way they should walk. Send rain on Your land — the land You gave Your people as their inheritance.

[37] If famine comes to the land — if plague, blight, mildew, locust, or grasshopper come — if an enemy lays siege to one of their cities — any disaster, any disease —

[38] whatever prayer, whatever plea any person or all Your people Israel might pray, each one aware of the affliction in his own heart, spreading out his hands toward this house —

[39] hear from heaven, where You dwell. Forgive. Act. Deal with each person according to his ways, since You know his heart — for You alone know every human heart —

[40] so that they will revere You all the days they live on the land You gave our ancestors.

[41] And the foreigner — the one who is not from Your people Israel but comes from a far country for the sake of Your name —

[42] for they will hear of Your great name, Your strong hand, Your outstretched arm — when he comes and prays toward this house,

[43] hear from heaven, where You dwell. Do everything the foreigner asks of You. Then every people on earth will know Your name and revere You as Your people Israel do, and they will know that this house I built bears Your name.

[44] When Your people go out to battle against their enemy on whatever path You send them — and they pray to the LORD toward the city You chose and the house I built for Your name —

[45] hear their prayer and plea from heaven. Uphold their cause.

[46] When they sin against You — and there is no one who does not sin — and You become angry with them and hand them over to an enemy who carries them captive to the enemy's land, far or near —

[47] and they take it to heart in the land where they were carried, and they turn back, pleading with You in the land of their captors, saying, 'We have sinned, we have done wrong, we have acted wickedly' —

[48] and they return to You with all their heart and all their soul in the land of the enemies who took them captive, and they pray to You toward the land You gave their ancestors, the city You chose, and the house I built for Your name —

[49] hear their prayer and their plea from heaven, where You dwell. Uphold their cause.

[50] Forgive Your people who sinned against You — every transgression they committed against You. Stir compassion in their captors so they show them compassion.

[51] For they are Your people, Your inheritance — the ones You brought out of Egypt, out of the iron furnace.

[52] Let Your eyes stay open to Your servant's plea and to the plea of Your people Israel. Listen to them whenever they cry out to You.

[53] For You set them apart from every people on earth as Your inheritance — just as You said through Moses Your servant when You brought our ancestors out of Egypt, O Sovereign LORD."

[54] When Solomon finished praying all this prayer and plea to the LORD, he rose from before the LORD's altar — where he had been kneeling with hands spread out toward heaven.

[55] He stood and blessed the whole assembly of Israel with a loud voice:

[56] "Blessed be the LORD, who has given rest to His people Israel exactly as He promised. Not one word has failed of all the good He promised through Moses His servant.

[57] May the LORD our God be with us as He was with our ancestors. May He never leave us, never forsake us.

[58] May He turn our hearts to Him — so we walk in all His ways, keeping the commands, statutes, and judgments He gave our ancestors.

[59] And may these words of mine — words I have pleaded before the LORD — stay close to the LORD our God day and night, so He upholds the cause of His servant and the cause of His people Israel as each day requires —

[60] so every people on earth will know that the LORD is God. There is no other.

[61] So let your heart be wholly His — the LORD our God's — walking in His statutes, keeping His commands, like today."

[62] Then the king and all Israel with him offered sacrifices before the LORD.

[63] Solomon offered as fellowship offerings to the LORD: twenty-two thousand oxen, a hundred and twenty thousand sheep. With this, the king and all Israel dedicated the house of the LORD.

[64] That day the king consecrated the middle of the courtyard in front of the LORD's house — he offered burnt offerings, grain offerings, and the fat of fellowship offerings there. The bronze altar before the LORD was too small to hold them all.

[65] So Solomon kept the festival, and all Israel with him — a vast assembly stretching from Lebo-hamath to the Wadi of Egypt — before the LORD our God, seven days and seven days, fourteen days.

[66] On the eighth day he sent the people away. They blessed the king and went home to their tents — joyful, light-hearted over all the goodness the LORD had shown David His servant and Israel His people.

[1] When Solomon finished building the temple of the LORD, the royal palace, and every other project his heart had set out to accomplish,

[2] the LORD appeared to him a second time, just as he had appeared at Gibeon.

[3] The LORD said: "I have heard the prayer you prayed before me. I have set apart this house you built — my Name will live here forever. My eyes and my heart will never leave this place.

[4] As for you — if you walk before me the way David your father walked, with integrity and a whole heart, doing everything I commanded, keeping my statutes and my judgments,

[5] then I will lock down your royal throne over Israel forever, just as I promised David your father when I said, 'A descendant of yours will never be cut off from the throne of Israel.'

[6] But if you turn away — you or your children — and abandon me, refusing to keep the commands and statutes I set before you, and you walk off to serve other gods and bow down to them,

[7] then I will rip Israel out of the land I gave them. This house I set apart for my Name — I will hurl it from my sight. Israel will become a punchline, a cautionary tale among the nations.

[8] And this temple, once exalted — every passerby will stare at it in horror and let out a low whistle. They'll ask, 'Why did the LORD do this to this land and this house?'

[9] And the answer will come: 'Because they abandoned the LORD their God, who brought their ancestors out of Egypt, and they latched onto other gods, bowed down to them, and served them. That's why the LORD brought all this disaster on them.'"

[10] Twenty years passed while Solomon built those two structures — the temple of the LORD and the royal palace.

[11] During that stretch, Hiram king of Tyre had kept Solomon supplied with cedar, cypress, and all the gold he wanted. In return, King Solomon handed Hiram twenty towns in the region of Galilee.

[12] Hiram came up from Tyre to inspect the towns Solomon had given him — and he was not impressed.

[13] "What kind of towns are these, my brother?" he said. And he named the region Cabul — "Worthless" — a name it still carries.

[14] (Hiram had, by the way, sent the king one hundred twenty talents of gold.)

[15] Here's the story behind the forced labor King Solomon conscripted: it was for building the temple of the LORD, his own palace, the supporting terraces, the wall of Jerusalem, and the fortified cities of Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer.

[16] (Pharaoh king of Egypt had marched up, captured Gezer, torched it, slaughtered the Canaanites living there, and handed it over as a wedding gift to his daughter, Solomon's wife.

[17] So Solomon rebuilt Gezer.) He also built Lower Beth Horon,

[18] Baalath, and Tadmor out in the wilderness, in his own land,

[19] along with all the storage cities he owned, the cities for his chariots and his cavalry — everything Solomon wanted to build, in Jerusalem, in Lebanon, throughout his entire domain.

[20] As for the people still left from the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites — the ones who were not Israelites,

[21] the descendants left in the land whom Israel had been unable to wipe out — Solomon drafted them into slave labor, and that's how it remains.

[22] But Solomon did not enslave any Israelite. They were his soldiers, his officials, his commanders, his captains, the officers of his chariots and cavalry.

[23] The chief officers in charge of Solomon's projects numbered five hundred fifty — the men who managed the workforce.

[24] As soon as Pharaoh's daughter moved up from the City of David to the palace Solomon had built for her, he built up the supporting terraces.

[25] Three times a year Solomon would offer burnt offerings and fellowship offerings on the altar he had built for the LORD, burning incense before the LORD with them. So he completed the temple.

[26] King Solomon also built a fleet at Ezion Geber, near Elath on the shore of the Red Sea, in the land of Edom.

[27] Hiram sent his own men — sailors who knew the sea — to serve on the fleet alongside Solomon's men.

[28] They sailed to Ophir, brought back four hundred twenty talents of gold, and delivered it to King Solomon.

[1] Word of Solomon's fame reached the queen of Sheba — fame tied to the name of the LORD — and she came to test him with questions designed to break lesser minds.

[2] She arrived in Jerusalem with an immense entourage: camels loaded with spices, gold by the ton, precious stones. She walked into Solomon's presence and unloaded every question she had ever wondered about.

[3] Solomon answered all of it. Nothing was too obscure for the king to unpack for her.

[4] When the queen of Sheba had taken the full measure of Solomon's wisdom, seen the palace he built,

[5] the food on his table, the seating of his officials, the choreography of his servants and their uniforms, his cupbearers, and the burnt offerings he brought up to the house of the LORD — the breath went out of her.

[6] She said to the king: "Everything I heard back home about your accomplishments and your wisdom — it was all true.

[7] I didn't believe the reports until I came and saw it with my own eyes. And honestly? They didn't tell me half of it. Your wisdom and prosperity blow past every rumor I heard.

[8] Lucky are your people. Lucky are these servants who stand in your presence day after day drinking in your wisdom.

[9] Blessed be the LORD your God, who delighted in you and set you on Israel's throne. Because the LORD has loved Israel forever, he made you king — to do justice, to do right."

[10] Then she gave the king a hundred and twenty talents of gold, a staggering quantity of spices, and precious stones. No shipment of spices like that ever came into Jerusalem again.

[11] (Hiram's fleet, the one that hauled gold from Ophir, also brought in massive loads of almug wood and precious stones from there.

[12] The king used the almug wood for supports in the house of the LORD and the royal palace, and for lyres and harps for the singers. Wood like that has never come in or been seen since.)

[13] King Solomon gave the queen of Sheba everything she desired, whatever she asked for — on top of what he gave her from his own royal bounty. Then she turned and went home, she and her servants.

[14] The gold coming into Solomon in a single year weighed in at six hundred sixty-six talents,

[15] not counting the revenue from traders and merchants and from all the Arabian kings and the governors of the land.

[16] King Solomon hammered out two hundred large shields of beaten gold — six hundred shekels of gold went into each one.

[17] And three hundred smaller shields of beaten gold — three minas of gold each. The king housed them in the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon.

[18] The king built a massive ivory throne and overlaid it with refined gold.

[19] Six steps led up to the throne. The back was rounded at the top. Armrests flanked the seat, and two lions stood beside the armrests.

[20] Twelve lions stood on the six steps, one on each end of every step. Nothing like it was ever made for any kingdom.

[21] All of King Solomon's drinking vessels were gold. Every utensil in the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon — pure gold. Silver didn't count for anything in Solomon's day. It was treated like nothing.

[22] The king had a fleet of Tarshish ships at sea alongside Hiram's fleet. Once every three years the Tarshish fleet would dock, hauling gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks.

[23] So King Solomon outstripped every king on earth in wealth and wisdom.

[24] The whole world wanted an audience with Solomon, to hear the wisdom God had planted in his heart.

[25] And every one of them brought tribute: vessels of silver and gold, robes, weapons, spices, horses, mules — year after year after year.

[26] Solomon stockpiled chariots and cavalry. He had fourteen hundred chariots and twelve thousand horsemen, stationed in the chariot cities and with the king in Jerusalem.

[27] The king made silver as common in Jerusalem as stones in the street, and cedar as plentiful as the sycamores in the lowlands.

[28] Solomon's horses were imported from Egypt and Kue — the king's traders bought them from Kue at market price.

[29] A chariot imported from Egypt ran six hundred shekels of silver; a horse, a hundred fifty. And through these traders the kings of the Hittites and the kings of Aram got their horses too.

[1] But Solomon loved foreign women — many of them. Pharaoh's daughter, yes, but also Moabites, Ammonites, Edomites, Sidonians, Hittites.

[2] These were the nations the LORD had warned Israel about: "Do not marry into them, and do not let them marry into you, because they will bend your heart toward their gods." Solomon clung to them anyway. He loved them.

[3] Seven hundred wives of royal rank. Three hundred concubines. And yes — they bent his heart.

[4] As Solomon aged, his wives pulled his heart after other gods. His devotion to the LORD his God was no longer whole, no longer like his father David's.

[5] He chased Ashtoreth, goddess of the Sidonians. He chased Milcom, the obscenity of the Ammonites.

[6] Solomon did evil in the LORD's eyes. He did not follow the LORD fully, the way David had.

[7] On the hill east of Jerusalem, Solomon built a high place for Chemosh — Moab's filth — and another for Molech, that abomination of the Ammonites.

[8] He did this for every foreign wife — built them shrines where they burned incense and offered sacrifices to their gods.

[9] So the LORD's anger burned against Solomon. His heart had turned away from the God of Israel — the God who had appeared to him twice,

[10] who had told him, point-blank, not to chase after other gods. Solomon ignored the command.

[11] So the LORD told him: "Since this is who you are now, since you have broken my covenant and ignored my statutes, I am tearing the kingdom out of your hands and giving it to one of your servants.

[12] But not in your lifetime — for the sake of your father David. I will tear it from your son's hand instead.

[13] And I will not tear away all of it. I will leave one tribe to your son — for David's sake, and for the sake of Jerusalem, the city I chose."

[14] Then the LORD raised up an adversary against Solomon: Hadad, an Edomite from the royal bloodline.

[15] Years before, when David had been at war with Edom, Joab the commander had gone up to bury the dead and had struck down every male in Edom.

[16] Six months Joab stayed there with the army of Israel — until every male in Edom was wiped out.

[17] But Hadad escaped, just a boy then, fleeing to Egypt with some of his father's Edomite servants.

[18] They left Midian, came through Paran, picked up more men, and arrived in Egypt — where Pharaoh gave Hadad a house, food, even land.

[19] Pharaoh took a liking to him and gave him his own sister-in-law in marriage — the sister of Queen Tahpenes.

[20] She bore him a son, Genubath, whom Tahpenes weaned in Pharaoh's palace. Genubath grew up among Pharaoh's own children.

[21] In Egypt, Hadad heard that David had died and Joab was dead too. He went to Pharaoh: "Let me go. Let me return to my country."

[22] Pharaoh said, "What's missing here? Why this urgency to leave?" Hadad answered, "Nothing. Just let me go."

[23] God also raised up another adversary: Rezon son of Eliada, who had fled his master Hadadezer king of Zobah.

[24] He gathered a band of men around him, became their leader after David's slaughter of Zobah, and they went to Damascus, settled there, and ruled.

[25] Rezon was Israel's enemy all of Solomon's days — adding to the trouble Hadad caused. He despised Israel. He reigned over Aram.

[26] Then there was Jeroboam son of Nebat — an Ephraimite from Zeredah, his mother a widow named Zeruah, a servant of Solomon's. He too raised his hand against the king.

[27] Here is how it happened. Solomon was building the Millo, repairing a breach in his father David's city.

[28] Jeroboam was a capable man — Solomon noticed how he handled work — so Solomon put him in charge of all the forced labor of the house of Joseph.

[29] Around that time, Jeroboam left Jerusalem, and the prophet Ahijah the Shilonite met him on the road. Ahijah was wearing a new cloak. The two were alone in open country.

[30] Ahijah grabbed the new cloak off his shoulders and tore it. Twelve pieces.

[31] "Take ten of them," he told Jeroboam. "Here is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'I am tearing the kingdom out of Solomon's hand and giving you ten tribes.

[32] One tribe will be left for him — for David my servant's sake, and for Jerusalem, the city I chose out of all Israel.

[33] This is because they have abandoned me. They have bowed to Ashtoreth of Sidon, to Chemosh of Moab, to Milcom of Ammon. They have not walked in my ways, have not done what is right in my sight, have not kept my statutes and judgments — not like his father David.

[34] But I will not pull the whole kingdom from Solomon's hand. I am letting him remain ruler all his days, for the sake of David my servant, whom I chose, who kept my commandments and statutes.

[35] I will take the kingdom from his son's hand and give it to you — ten tribes.

[36] One tribe I will give his son, so David my servant will always have a lamp burning before me in Jerusalem, the city I chose to place my name in.

[37] But you — I will take you, and you will reign over everything your heart desires. You will be king over Israel.

[38] If you listen to everything I tell you, if you walk in my ways, do what is right in my eyes, keep my statutes and commandments the way David my servant did — then I will be with you. I will build you a lasting dynasty, the way I built one for David, and I will give Israel to you.

[39] I will humble David's line because of this — but not forever.'"

[40] Solomon tried to kill Jeroboam, but Jeroboam fled — to Egypt, to King Shishak — and stayed there until Solomon died.

[41] The rest of Solomon's story — all he did, all his wisdom — is written in the book of the acts of Solomon.

[42] Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel for forty years.

[43] Then he slept with his ancestors and was buried in the city of David, his father. And his son Rehoboam took the throne in his place.

[1] Rehoboam headed to Shechem — the whole of Israel had gone there to crown him king.

[2] Word reached Jeroboam son of Nebat, still in Egypt where he'd fled from King Solomon. He was living there.

[3] They sent for him. So Jeroboam and the entire assembly of Israel came and laid it out to Rehoboam:

[4] "Your father broke our backs. Lighten the brutal labor and the heavy yoke he laid on us, and we'll serve you."

[5] Rehoboam answered, "Give me three days. Then come back." And the people left.

[6] King Rehoboam consulted the elders who had stood before his father Solomon while he was alive. "How do you advise me to answer these people?"

[7] They told him, "If today you become their servant — if you serve them, give them a real answer, speak kindly — they'll be your servants forever."

[8] But he tossed aside the counsel of the elders and turned to the young men he'd grown up with, the ones who stood at his side.

[9] He asked them, "What's your advice? How should I answer these people who are demanding I lighten the yoke my father put on them?"

[10] The young men he'd grown up with said, "Tell those people who came complaining about your father's heavy yoke — tell them this: My little finger is thicker than my father's waist.

[11] He loaded you down with a heavy yoke? I'll pile more on. He disciplined you with whips? I'll discipline you with scorpions."

[12] Three days later, Jeroboam and all the people came back to Rehoboam, just as the king had said: "Return to me on the third day."

[13] The king answered them harshly. He ignored the counsel of the elders

[14] and spoke the words of the young men: "My father made your yoke heavy? I'll make it heavier. He whipped you? I'll use scorpions."

[15] The king refused to listen to the people — because this was a turn orchestrated by the LORD, to make good on the word he'd spoken through Ahijah of Shiloh to Jeroboam son of Nebat.

[16] When all Israel saw the king wasn't going to listen, they answered him back:

"What share do we have in David?
What inheritance in Jesse's son?
To your tents, Israel!
Look to your own house now, David!"

And Israel went home.

[17] But the Israelites living in the towns of Judah — Rehoboam still ruled over them.

[18] King Rehoboam sent Adoram, the man in charge of forced labor — and all Israel stoned him to death. Rehoboam scrambled into his chariot and fled to Jerusalem.

[19] So Israel has been in revolt against the house of David from that day to this.

[20] When all Israel heard Jeroboam had returned, they sent for him, called him to the assembly, and made him king over all Israel. Only the tribe of Judah stayed with the house of David.

[21] When Rehoboam reached Jerusalem, he mustered the whole house of Judah and the tribe of Benjamin — one hundred eighty thousand handpicked fighters — to make war on the house of Israel and take the kingdom back for Rehoboam son of Solomon.

[22] But the word of God came to Shemaiah, the man of God:

[23] "Tell Rehoboam son of Solomon, king of Judah, and all the house of Judah and Benjamin, and the rest of the people:

[24] This is what the LORD says: Do not march out. Do not fight your brothers, the Israelites. Every man — go home. This thing has come from me."

They obeyed the word of the LORD and turned back, just as he said.

[25] Jeroboam fortified Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim and made it his seat. Then he moved out from there and fortified Penuel.

[26] But Jeroboam said to himself: "The kingdom could slip right back to the house of David.

[27] If these people keep going up to offer sacrifices at the house of the LORD in Jerusalem, their hearts will swing back to their master, Rehoboam king of Judah. They'll kill me and return to Rehoboam."

[28] So the king took counsel and made two golden calves. He told the people, "You've gone up to Jerusalem long enough. Here are your gods, Israel — the ones who brought you up out of Egypt."

[29] He set one in Bethel and the other in Dan.

[30] And this became sin. The people walked all the way to Dan to stand before the one there.

[31] He built shrines on the high places and appointed priests from every corner of the population — men who weren't Levites.

[32] Jeroboam invented a festival on the fifteenth day of the eighth month, like the festival in Judah, and he offered sacrifices on the altar. He did this in Bethel, sacrificing to the calves he'd made. And in Bethel he installed the priests for the high places he'd built.

[33] On the fifteenth day of the eighth month — a month he'd cooked up in his own mind — he went up to the altar he'd built at Bethel. He instituted a festival for the Israelites and went up to the altar to burn incense.

[1] A man of God came up from Judah to Bethel by the word of the LORD, arriving just as Jeroboam stood at the altar to burn incense.

[2] He cried out against the altar by the word of the LORD: "Altar! Altar! This is what the LORD says: A son will be born to the house of David — Josiah is his name. On you he will sacrifice the priests of the high places who burn incense on you. Human bones will burn on your stones."

[3] He gave a sign that day: "Here is the proof the LORD has spoken — this altar will split open and the ashes on it will spill to the ground."

[4] When King Jeroboam heard what the man of God had shouted against the altar at Bethel, he thrust out his hand from the altar and ordered, "Seize him!" The hand he extended withered. He could not pull it back.

[5] The altar split. The ashes poured out — exactly the sign the man of God had given by the word of the LORD.

[6] The king pleaded with the man of God: "Please — entreat the LORD your God. Pray for me. Restore my hand." The man of God prayed, and the king's hand was restored, just as it had been.

[7] The king said, "Come home with me. Refresh yourself. I'll reward you."

[8] But the man of God answered, "If you gave me half your house, I would not go with you. I will not eat bread or drink water in this place.

[9] The LORD's command to me was explicit: Eat no bread, drink no water, and do not return by the road you came."

[10] So he left by a different road. He did not retrace his steps to Bethel.

[11] An old prophet lived in Bethel. His sons came home and told him everything the man of God had done that day, and the words he had spoken to the king.

[12] Their father asked, "Which road did he take?" His sons had seen the road the man of God from Judah had taken.

[13] He said, "Saddle the donkey for me." They saddled it, and he mounted

[14] and rode after the man of God. He found him sitting under an oak. He asked, "Are you the man of God who came from Judah?" "I am," he answered.

[15] "Come home with me," he said. "Eat something."

[16] "I cannot turn back with you," he replied. "I cannot go in with you. I will not eat bread or drink water with you in this place.

[17] The word of the LORD came to me: Eat no bread, drink no water there. Do not go back by the road you came."

[18] The old prophet said, "I am a prophet too — just like you. An angel spoke to me by the word of the LORD and said: Bring him back with you to your house. Let him eat bread and drink water." He was lying.

[19] So the man of God went back with him. He ate bread in his house and drank water.

[20] While they sat at the table, the word of the LORD came to the prophet who had brought him back.

[21] He cried out to the man of God from Judah: "This is what the LORD says: Because you defied the mouth of the LORD and did not keep the command the LORD your God gave you —

[22] because you came back and ate bread and drank water in the place where he forbade it — your body will not reach the tomb of your fathers."

[23] After the man had eaten and drunk, the old prophet saddled the donkey for him — the prophet he had brought back.

[24] He rode off, and a lion met him on the road and killed him. His body was thrown down on the road, the donkey standing beside it, the lion standing beside the body.

[25] Men passing by saw the body sprawled on the road and the lion standing next to it. They went and reported it in the town where the old prophet lived.

[26] When the prophet who had turned him back heard, he said, "It is the man of God who defied the mouth of the LORD. The LORD handed him over to the lion. The lion mauled him and killed him — just as the word of the LORD had told him."

[27] He told his sons, "Saddle the donkey." They saddled it,

[28] and he went out and found the body on the road, the donkey and the lion still standing beside it. The lion had not eaten the body. It had not torn the donkey.

[29] The prophet lifted the body of the man of God, laid it on the donkey, and brought it back. He came to his own city to mourn and to bury him.

[30] He laid the body in his own grave. They mourned over him: "Oh, my brother."

[31] After the burial, he said to his sons, "When I die, bury me in the grave where the man of God is buried. Lay my bones beside his bones.

[32] The word he cried out by the word of the LORD against the altar at Bethel and against all the shrines of the high places in the towns of Samaria — it will surely come to pass."

[33] Even after this, Jeroboam did not turn from his evil way. He went right on appointing priests for the high places from every kind of person. Anyone who wanted the office, he consecrated — and they became priests of the high places.

[34] This became the sin of the house of Jeroboam — the sin that cut it off, that wiped it from the face of the earth.

[1] Around that time, Abijah, Jeroboam's son, fell sick.

[2] Jeroboam told his wife, "Get up. Disguise yourself so no one recognizes you as the king's wife, then go to Shiloh. The prophet Ahijah lives there — the one who told me I'd be king over this people.

[3] Take ten loaves, some cakes, a jar of honey. Go to him. He'll tell you what happens to the boy."

[4] So Jeroboam's wife did it. She got up, went to Shiloh, arrived at Ahijah's house. Ahijah couldn't see — his eyes had gone milky with age.

[5] But the LORD had already spoken to Ahijah: "Jeroboam's wife is on her way to ask about her son. He's sick. Tell her this and this. When she comes in, she'll be pretending to be someone else."

[6] So the moment Ahijah heard her footsteps at the door, he called out, "Come in, wife of Jeroboam. Why the disguise? I've been sent to you with hard news.

[7] Go. Tell Jeroboam: This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says. I lifted you up out of the crowd. I made you ruler over my people Israel.

[8] I tore the kingdom away from David's house and gave it to you. But you haven't been like my servant David, who kept my commands and followed me with his whole heart, doing only what I called right.

[9] You've outdone every king before you in evil. You went off and made yourself other gods — metal idols — provoking me to fury. You threw me behind your back.

[10] So watch. I am bringing disaster on the house of Jeroboam. I will cut off every male in Jeroboam's line — slave or free — in Israel. I will burn up the house of Jeroboam the way a man burns dung until nothing is left.

[11] Anyone of Jeroboam's who dies in the city, dogs will eat. Anyone who dies in the open country, birds will eat. The LORD has spoken.

[12] As for you — get up, go home. The instant your feet cross into the city, the boy will die.

[13] All Israel will mourn him and bury him. He alone of Jeroboam's house will reach a grave, because in him alone the LORD, the God of Israel, found something good.

[14] The LORD will raise up for himself a king over Israel who will hack down the house of Jeroboam — starting today. Even now. Even this moment.

[15] The LORD will strike Israel until they sway like a reed in water. He'll rip Israel out of this good land he gave their ancestors and scatter them past the River — because they made their Asherah poles and provoked him to anger.

[16] He will hand Israel over because of the sins Jeroboam committed and the sins he dragged Israel into."

[17] Jeroboam's wife got up and left. She came to Tirzah. As she crossed the threshold of the house, the boy died.

[18] They buried him, and all Israel mourned him — exactly as the LORD had said through his servant the prophet Ahijah.

[19] The rest of Jeroboam's story — his wars, how he reigned — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[20] Jeroboam reigned twenty-two years. Then he lay down with his ancestors, and his son Nadab took the throne.

[21] Meanwhile in Judah: Rehoboam, Solomon's son, was king. He was forty-one when he took the throne and he reigned seventeen years in Jerusalem — the city the LORD had chosen out of every tribe in Israel to plant his name. His mother was Naamah, an Ammonite.

[22] Judah did evil in the LORD's sight. Their sins stirred up jealousy in him as no generation of their fathers ever had.

[23] They built themselves high places, sacred pillars, Asherah poles — on every high hill, under every spreading tree.

[24] Even male cult prostitutes worked the land. They mimicked every disgusting practice of the nations the LORD had driven out before Israel.

[25] In Rehoboam's fifth year, Shishak king of Egypt marched up against Jerusalem.

[26] He stripped the treasures of the LORD's house and the treasures of the palace — took everything. He took even the gold shields Solomon had made.

[27] So King Rehoboam made bronze shields to replace them and handed them off to the captains of the guard who stood watch at the palace gate.

[28] Whenever the king went into the LORD's house, the guards carried the shields. Then they returned them to the guardroom.

[29] The rest of Rehoboam's story — everything he did — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah.

[30] War between Rehoboam and Jeroboam never stopped, all their days.

[31] Rehoboam lay down with his ancestors and was buried with them in the City of David. His mother was Naamah the Ammonite. His son Abijam took the throne.

[1] In the eighteenth year of Jeroboam son of Nebat, Abijam took the throne of Judah. [2] He reigned three years in Jerusalem. His mother was Maacah, daughter of Abishalom.

[3] He walked in every sin his father had committed before him. His heart was not whole with the LORD his God — not like the heart of David, his ancestor. [4] Yet for David's sake the LORD his God kept a lamp burning in Jerusalem, raising up a son after him and holding the city steady. [5] Because David had done what was right in the LORD's eyes and never strayed from anything he commanded, all his days — except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite.

[6] War smoldered between Rehoboam and Jeroboam through all of Abijam's days. [7] The rest of Abijam's story — everything he did — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah. War continued between Abijam and Jeroboam. [8] Abijam joined his ancestors and they buried him in the City of David, and his son Asa took the throne.

[9] In the twentieth year of Jeroboam king of Israel, Asa became king of Judah. [10] He reigned forty-one years in Jerusalem. His grandmother was Maacah, daughter of Abishalom.

[11] Asa did what was right in the LORD's eyes, like his ancestor David. [12] He purged the male shrine prostitutes from the land and tore out every idol his fathers had made. [13] He even removed his grandmother Maacah from her position as queen mother because she had built an obscene image of Asherah. Asa chopped down that image and burned it in the Kidron Valley. [14] The high places never came down — but Asa's heart stayed whole with the LORD all his days. [15] He brought into the house of the LORD the things his father had consecrated, and what he himself had dedicated: silver, gold, sacred vessels.

[16] War ran between Asa and Baasha king of Israel the whole time they reigned. [17] Baasha came up against Judah and fortified Ramah, sealing the border so no one could come or go to Asa king of Judah.

[18] So Asa took every scrap of silver and gold left in the treasuries of the LORD's house and the royal palace and put it in the hands of his officials. He sent them to Ben-Hadad son of Tabrimmon son of Hezion, king of Aram, who ruled in Damascus, with this message: [19] "Let there be a treaty between you and me, as there was between my father and your father. Look — I am sending you a gift of silver and gold. Break your treaty with Baasha king of Israel so he will pull back from me."

[20] Ben-Hadad agreed. He sent the commanders of his armies against the cities of Israel and struck Ijon, Dan, Abel-beth-maacah, and all Chinneroth, along with the whole territory of Naphtali. [21] When Baasha heard, he abandoned the fortification of Ramah and withdrew to Tirzah. [22] Then King Asa summoned every man in Judah — no exceptions — and they hauled away the stones and timber Baasha had been using at Ramah. With them Asa fortified Geba of Benjamin and Mizpah.

[23] The rest of Asa's story — all his might, everything he accomplished, the cities he built — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah. In his old age, though, his feet became diseased. [24] Asa joined his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David his father. His son Jehoshaphat took the throne after him.

[25] Nadab son of Jeroboam became king over Israel in the second year of Asa king of Judah, and he reigned over Israel two years. [26] He did evil in the LORD's eyes, walking the same road as his father, carrying on the sin his father had dragged Israel into.

[27] Then Baasha son of Ahijah, of the house of Issachar, conspired against him. Baasha struck him down at Gibbethon, a Philistine town, while Nadab and all Israel were besieging it. [28] Baasha killed Nadab in the third year of Asa king of Judah and took the throne himself.

[29] The moment he was king, he butchered the entire house of Jeroboam. He left nothing breathing — wiped them out, every last one — exactly as the LORD had said through his servant Ahijah the Shilonite. [30] All because of the sins Jeroboam had committed and pulled Israel into, provoking the LORD, the God of Israel, to fury.

[31] The rest of Nadab's story — everything he did — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. [32] War ran between Asa and Baasha king of Israel through all their days.

[33] In the third year of Asa king of Judah, Baasha son of Ahijah became king over all Israel at Tirzah, and he reigned twenty-four years. [34] He did evil in the LORD's eyes, walking the road of Jeroboam and the sin he had dragged Israel into.

[1] Then the word of the LORD came to Jehu son of Hanani, aimed straight at Baasha:

[2] "I lifted you from the dust and made you ruler over my people Israel — but you walked the road of Jeroboam, you dragged my people into sin, you provoked me with their wickedness.

[3] So I am sweeping Baasha and his house away. I will make your dynasty like the dynasty of Jeroboam son of Nebat.

[4] Whoever of Baasha's line dies in the city — dogs will eat. Whoever dies in the open country — birds will pick the bones."

[5] The rest of Baasha's deeds — his exploits, his power — they are recorded in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[6] Baasha joined his ancestors and was buried in Tirzah. His son Elah took the throne.

[7] The word of the LORD through Jehu son of Hanani hit Baasha and his house for two reasons: every evil he did in the LORD's sight, provoking him with the work of his hands and copying the house of Jeroboam — and because he struck that house down.

[8] In the twenty-sixth year of Asa king of Judah, Elah son of Baasha became king of Israel in Tirzah. He lasted two years.

[9] His servant Zimri, commander of half the chariot force, plotted against him. Elah was in Tirzah, drinking himself stupid in the house of Arza, the palace steward.

[10] Zimri walked in, struck him down, killed him — twenty-seventh year of Asa king of Judah — and took the throne for himself.

[11] The moment he sat on that throne, he butchered the entire house of Baasha. Not a single male — not a relative, not a friend — was left alive.

[12] Zimri wiped out the house of Baasha down to the last man, exactly as the LORD had spoken through Jehu the prophet —

[13] payment for every sin of Baasha and his son Elah, for what they did and what they pulled Israel into, provoking the LORD, the God of Israel, with their worthless idols.

[14] The rest of Elah's deeds — everything he did — are recorded in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[15] In the twenty-seventh year of Asa king of Judah, Zimri reigned seven days in Tirzah. The army was camped against Gibbethon of the Philistines.

[16] Word reached the camp: "Zimri has conspired and killed the king." That same day, in that same camp, all Israel made Omri, the army commander, king over Israel.

[17] Omri marched up from Gibbethon, and all Israel with him. They laid siege to Tirzah.

[18] When Zimri saw the city had fallen, he retreated into the citadel of the royal palace, set the palace on fire over his own head, and died —

[19] for the sins he committed, doing evil in the LORD's sight, walking the road of Jeroboam, repeating the sin Jeroboam had pulled Israel into.

[20] The rest of Zimri's story, and his conspiracy — they are written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[21] Then the nation split. Half threw their weight behind Tibni son of Ginath to make him king. The other half stood with Omri.

[22] Omri's faction overpowered Tibni's. Tibni died. Omri took the throne.

[23] In the thirty-first year of Asa king of Judah, Omri became king of Israel and reigned twelve years — six of them in Tirzah.

[24] He bought the hill of Samaria from Shemer for two talents of silver, fortified it, and named the city he built Samaria, after Shemer, the former owner of the hill.

[25] Omri did evil in the LORD's sight — worse than anyone before him.

[26] He walked every step of Jeroboam son of Nebat's road, dragging Israel into the same sin, provoking the LORD, the God of Israel, with their worthless idols.

[27] The rest of Omri's deeds, his power, his accomplishments — they are recorded in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[28] Omri joined his ancestors, buried in Samaria. His son Ahab took the throne.

[29] In the thirty-eighth year of Asa king of Judah, Ahab son of Omri became king of Israel. He ruled from Samaria for twenty-two years.

[30] Ahab son of Omri did evil in the LORD's sight — more than anyone before him.

[31] As if walking in Jeroboam's sins were a small thing, he married Jezebel daughter of Ethbaal, king of the Sidonians, then went and served Baal and bowed down to him.

[32] He built an altar for Baal in the temple of Baal, which he constructed in Samaria.

[33] Ahab also made an Asherah pole. Ahab did more to provoke the LORD, the God of Israel, than every king of Israel before him put together.

[34] In his days, Hiel of Bethel rebuilt Jericho. He laid the foundation at the cost of Abiram, his firstborn, and set up its gates at the cost of Segub, his youngest — fulfilling the word the LORD had spoken through Joshua son of Nun.

[1] Then Elijah the Tishbite, from the settlers of Gilead, confronted Ahab: "As the LORD lives, the God of Israel I stand before — there will be no dew, no rain in these years, except by my word."

[2] The word of the LORD came to him:

[3] "Get out of here. Turn east. Hide yourself at the Cherith ravine, just past the Jordan.
