[11] You know we treated each one of you the way a father treats his own children — [12] encouraging you, urging you, pleading with you to walk in a manner worthy of the God who is calling you into his own kingdom and glory.

[13] And this is why we never stop thanking God: when you received the word of God from us, you didn't take it as the word of men. You received it for what it actually is — the word of God, alive and at work in you who believe. [14] You became imitators, brothers, of God's churches in Judea, the ones in Christ Jesus. You suffered the same things at the hands of your own countrymen that they suffered at the hands of the Jews — [15] the ones who killed the Lord Jesus and the prophets, who drove us out, who displease God and oppose every human being, [16] trying to silence us when we speak to the Gentiles about salvation. They keep filling up the measure of their sins. But wrath has caught up with them at last.

[17] Brothers, when we were ripped away from you for a little while — out of sight, never out of heart — we ached all the more to see you face to face. [18] We tried to come, I, Paul, again and again. Satan blocked the way. [19] Because what is our hope, our joy, the crown we'll wear with pride before our Lord Jesus when he comes? Isn't it you? [20] Yes — you are our glory and our joy.

[1] Finally, when we couldn't stand it any longer, we decided it was better to stay behind in Athens alone [2] and send Timothy — our brother, God's coworker in spreading the good news about the Messiah — to steady you and strengthen your faith, [3] so that none of you would be shaken by these afflictions. You know perfectly well this is what we're built for. [4] Even when we were with you, we kept warning you: suffering is coming. And it came, just as we said. [5] That's why, when I couldn't take the silence anymore, I sent to find out about your faith — terrified that the tempter had gotten to you and our work among you had collapsed into nothing.

[6] But Timothy has just returned from you, and the news is good — your faith, your love, the warm memory you carry of us always, your longing to see us as much as we long to see you. [7] In all our distress and pressure, brothers and sisters, this is what carried us: your faith. [8] We come alive again knowing you're standing firm in the Lord. [9] How can we possibly thank God enough for you — for all the joy you give us in his presence? [10] Night and day we beg him, beyond words, to let us see your faces again and finish what's still unfinished in your faith.

[11] May our God and Father himself, and our Lord Jesus, clear the road and bring us to you. [12] And may the Lord flood you with love — for each other, for everyone — overflowing the way ours overflows for you. [13] May he anchor your hearts: blameless, holy, ready to stand before our God and Father on the day our Lord Jesus arrives with all who belong to him.

[1] One last thing, brothers and sisters — and we're asking you, urging you in the Lord Jesus: you learned from us how to walk in a way that pleases God, and you're already doing it. Now do it more. Go further.

[2] You remember the instructions we gave you, delivered with the authority of the Lord Jesus.

[3] Here is what God wants for you: that you be made holy. That means cutting sexual immorality out of your life completely.

[4] Each of you needs to learn how to handle your own body with holiness and honor —

[5] not driven by raw cravings like people who have no knowledge of God.

[6] No one should violate or exploit a brother or sister in these matters. The Lord will settle every account on this — we warned you before, and we're warning you again.

[7] God did not call us to filth. He called us to holiness.

[8] So if anyone shrugs this off, they're not shrugging off a man — they're shrugging off the God who gives you his Holy Spirit.

[9] You don't need anyone to write you a lesson on loving one another. God himself has taught you that.

[10] And you're already living it out toward every believer across Macedonia. We're just asking you — keep pressing in. Love more.

[11] Make it your ambition to live quietly, to mind your own business, to work with your hands, just as we instructed —

[12] so that outsiders see your life and respect it, and you depend on no one.

[13] Now, brothers and sisters, we don't want you in the dark about those who have fallen asleep — we don't want you grieving the way people grieve who have no hope.

[14] If we believe Jesus died and rose — and we do — then we also believe this: God will bring with Jesus those who have fallen asleep in him.

[15] Here is what we tell you on the Lord's own word: those of us still alive when the Lord comes will not get there ahead of those who have died.

[16] Because the Lord himself will descend from heaven — a commanding shout, the voice of the archangel, the trumpet blast of God — and the dead in Christ will rise first.

[17] Then we who are alive, who are still here, will be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air. And from that moment on, we will always be with the Lord.

[18] So speak these words to one another. Let them carry you.

[1] About the timing — the dates, the schedule — you don't need anyone to write you about that. [2] You already know exactly: the Day of the Lord arrives like a thief in the dark. [3] The moment people are saying "peace and security" — that's when ruin crashes down on them, sudden as labor pains hitting a pregnant woman. No escape.

[4] But you — you're not in the dark. That Day won't ambush you like a thief. [5] You belong to the light. You belong to the day. We're not creatures of night, not creatures of shadow. [6] So let's not sleep through this like everyone else. Stay awake. Stay sharp. [7] Sleepers sleep at night. Drunks get drunk at night. [8] But we live in the daylight — so let's stay clear-headed, strapped in: faith and love as the breastplate, the hope of salvation as the helmet. [9] God didn't appoint us for wrath. He appointed us for salvation through our Lord Jesus the Anointed, [10] who died for us so that whether we're awake or asleep when he comes, we live together with him. [11] So encourage each other. Build each other up. Which is exactly what you're already doing.

[12] One request, friends: recognize the people working hard among you — the ones leading you in the Lord, the ones who tell you the truth even when it stings. [13] Hold them in the highest regard, with love, because of what they do. Live at peace with one another.

[14] And we're urging you, brothers and sisters: confront the lazy, steady the anxious, stay close to the weak, be patient with everyone. [15] Make sure no one returns evil for evil. Chase after good — for each other, for everyone.

[16] Rejoice always.
[17] Pray nonstop.
[18] Give thanks in every circumstance — this is what God wants from you in the Anointed Jesus.

[19] Don't smother the Spirit's fire.
[20] Don't sneer at prophecy.
[21] Test everything. Hold onto what's good.
[22] Walk away from every shade of evil.

[23] May the God of peace himself make you completely his own — every part of you, spirit and soul and body, kept whole and blameless for the arrival of our Lord Jesus the Anointed. [24] The one who calls you is faithful. He'll do it.

[25] Pray for us too, family.

[26] Greet everyone there with a holy kiss.

[27] I'm putting you under oath before the Lord: read this letter to all of them.

[28] The grace of our Lord Jesus the Anointed be with you.

[1] From Paul, sent as an envoy of Christ Jesus by direct command of God our Savior and Christ Jesus our hope—

[2] To Timothy, my true son in the faith: grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.

[3] When I left for Macedonia, I urged you to stay put in Ephesus for one reason: certain people there need to be told, in no uncertain terms, to stop peddling a different doctrine.

[4] They need to drop the obsession with myths and endless genealogies. That stuff breeds speculation, not the work of God that comes by faith.

[5] The whole point of what we're charging them with is love—love flowing from a clean heart, a clear conscience, and faith that isn't faking it.

[6] Some have wandered off from all of that and ended up in empty talk.

[7] They want to be teachers of the Law, but they have no idea what they're saying or what they're so confidently asserting.

[8] Now, the Law is good—provided you use it the way it was meant to be used.

[9] Here's the thing: the Law isn't aimed at the righteous. It's aimed at the lawless and rebellious, the godless and sinful, the unholy and profane, those who would kill their own father or mother, murderers,

[10] the sexually immoral, men who use other men, slave traders, liars, perjurers—anything else that stands against the healthy teaching

[11] of the glorious gospel of the blessed God, which has been entrusted to me.

[12] I'm grateful to Christ Jesus our Lord, who gave me strength, because he counted me trustworthy and put me into his service—

[13] me, of all people. Once a blasphemer, a persecutor, a violent man. But I was shown mercy because I acted in ignorance, in unbelief.

[14] The grace of our Lord crashed over me like a flood, bringing with it the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus.

[15] Here's a statement you can stake your life on: Christ Jesus came into the world to rescue sinners. And I'm exhibit A.

[16] But that's exactly why I was shown mercy—so that in me, the worst-case scenario, Christ Jesus could put his unlimited patience on full display, as a template for everyone who would later believe in him and find eternal life.

[17] To the King of the ages—immortal, invisible, the only God—honor and glory forever and ever. Amen.

[18] Timothy, my son, this charge I'm giving you ties back to the prophecies once spoken over you. Use them. Fight the good fight.

[19] Hold onto faith and a clear conscience. Some have thrown the conscience overboard, and their faith has shipwrecked because of it.

[20] Hymenaeus and Alexander are among them. I've handed them over to Satan, so they can learn the hard way not to blaspheme.

[1] First things first: I'm urging that prayers of every kind — requests, intercessions, thanksgivings — be lifted up for every human being. [2] For kings. For everyone in positions of power. So that we can live quiet, settled lives, marked by reverence and dignity. [3] This is good. This is what pleases God our Savior — [4] the God who wants every person rescued, every person brought into the knowledge of what's true.

[5] Because there is one God. And one mediator standing between God and humanity — the man Christ Jesus — [6] who gave himself as the ransom for everyone. The testimony delivered right on time. [7] This is what I was appointed to herald and proclaim — I'm telling you the truth, I'm not lying — a teacher of the nations in faith and truth.

[8] So here's what I want: in every place, let the men pray, lifting hands that are clean — no rage, no arguments simmering underneath. [9] And the women — let them dress themselves with modesty and good sense, adorned not with elaborate hairstyles and gold and pearls and expensive clothing, [10] but with what actually fits a woman who claims to revere God: good works. [11] Let a woman learn — quietly, with full submission to the teaching. [12] I'm not authorizing a woman to teach or exercise authority over a man; she's to remain settled. [13] Because Adam was formed first, then Eve. [14] And Adam wasn't the one deceived — the woman was deceived, and stepped into the transgression. [15] Still, she will be brought safely through childbearing — provided they continue in faith and love and holiness, with good sense.

[1] Here's a saying worth trusting: anyone who reaches for the role of overseer is reaching for something worth doing.

[2] But the overseer has to be the kind of person no one can pin anything on — faithful to one wife, level-headed, disciplined, dignified, the kind who opens his home, who can actually teach.

[3] Not a drinker, not a brawler, but gentle. Not a fighter. Not in love with money.

[4] He has to run his own household well, with kids who respect him and take him seriously —

[5] because if a man can't manage his own family, how is he going to take care of God's people?

[6] Don't put a new convert in the role. The position will go to his head, and he'll fall the same way the devil did.

[7] He also needs a good reputation with outsiders, so he doesn't get publicly disgraced and caught in the devil's trap.

[8] Same standards for deacons: people of substance, not double-tongued, not heavy drinkers, not in it for the money.

[9] They have to hold onto the mystery of the faith with a clean conscience.

[10] Test them first. If they come through clean, then let them serve.

[11] The women, likewise — dignified, no gossip, sober-minded, trustworthy across the board.

[12] A deacon should be faithful to one wife and manage his children and household well.

[13] Because those who serve well earn solid standing and serious confidence in the faith of Christ Jesus.

[14] I'm writing all this hoping to come see you soon —

[15] but in case I'm delayed, you need to know how to conduct yourself in God's household, which is the church of the living God, the pillar and foundation of truth.

[16] And no one can deny it — the mystery at the heart of our faith is staggering:

He appeared in a body,
was vindicated by the Spirit,
was seen by angels,
was preached among the nations,
was believed in across the world,
was taken up in glory.

[1] The Spirit says it plainly: in the last days, some will walk away from the faith. They'll latch onto deceiving spirits and the teachings of demons,

[2] swallowing lies dressed up as truth from hypocrites whose consciences have been seared shut — cauterized, dead to feeling.

[3] These people will forbid marriage. They'll demand abstinence from foods God created to be received with thanks by those who trust him and know the truth.

[4] Everything God made is good. Nothing is to be rejected if it's received with gratitude,

[5] because it's made holy by God's word and by prayer.

[6] Lay this out for the brothers and sisters, and you'll be the kind of servant Jesus the Messiah deserves — nourished on the words of the faith and the good teaching you've followed all along.

[7] Have nothing to do with godless myths and old wives' tales. Train yourself for godliness instead.

[8] Physical training has its uses, but godliness is useful across the board — it holds promise for this life and the one to come.

[9] That statement is rock-solid. Worth staking everything on.

[10] This is why we sweat and strain: we've set our hope on the living God, who is the Savior of all people — especially those who believe.

[11] Command these things. Teach them.

[12] Don't let anyone look down on you because you're young. Be the example — in how you speak, how you live, how you love, in your faith, in your purity.

[13] Until I get there, give yourself fully to the public reading of Scripture, to preaching, to teaching.

[14] Don't neglect the gift inside you — the one given through prophecy when the elders laid their hands on you.

[15] Pour yourself into this. Live in it. Let everyone see how far you've come.

[16] Watch yourself. Watch the teaching. Stay with it. Do this, and you'll save both yourself and those who listen to you.

[1] Don't tear into an older man. Appeal to him like he's your father. Treat younger men as brothers, [2] older women as mothers, younger women as sisters — with absolute purity.

[3] Honor widows who are truly alone. [4] But if a widow has children or grandchildren, let them learn first what it means to live out their faith at home — to repay what they owe to their own family. This is what pleases God. [5] The widow who is genuinely alone, stripped of everything, fixes her hope on God and stays at prayer night and day. [6] The widow chasing pleasure is already dead while she's still breathing. [7] Drive this home, so no one can find fault with them. [8] Anyone who refuses to provide for their own relatives — especially their own household — has denied the faith and is worse than someone who never believed.

[9] Don't enroll a widow on the list unless she's at least sixty, has been faithful to one husband, [10] and has a track record of good — raising children, opening her home to strangers, washing the feet of God's people, helping those in trouble, giving herself to every good work. [11] Refuse the younger widows. When their desires pull them away from Christ, they want to marry again, [12] and they end up condemned for breaking their first commitment. [13] On top of that, they get lazy, wandering from house to house — not just idle, but gossiping, meddling, talking about things they have no business talking about. [14] So my advice: let the younger widows marry, have children, run their households, and give the enemy no ammunition. [15] Some have already turned aside and followed Satan. [16] If any believing woman has widows in her family, let her take care of them. Don't dump the load on the church, so the church can support those who really have no one.

[17] Elders who lead well deserve double honor — especially those who labor in preaching and teaching. [18] Scripture says: *Don't muzzle the ox while it's threshing,* and *The worker deserves his wages.*

[19] Don't even entertain an accusation against an elder unless two or three witnesses back it. [20] But those who keep sinning — call them out publicly, so the rest will feel the warning.

[21] I charge you, before God and Christ Jesus and the chosen angels: keep these instructions without prejudice. Do nothing out of favoritism.

[22] Don't be quick to lay hands on anyone. Don't get tangled up in someone else's sins. Keep yourself clean.

[23] And stop drinking only water. Use a little wine — for your stomach and your frequent illnesses.

[24] Some people's sins are obvious, marching ahead of them straight to judgment. Others' sins trail behind, catching up later. [25] Same with good works — the obvious ones are clear, and even the hidden ones can't stay hidden forever.

[1] Anyone laboring under the yoke of slavery should treat their master as worthy of full respect, so that God's name and our teaching don't get dragged through the mud. [2] And if the master is a fellow believer, that's no excuse for slacking off — quite the opposite. Serve them even better, because the one benefiting from your work is a brother, someone loved. Teach this. Press it home.

[3] If anyone peddles a different doctrine and won't align with the sound words of our Lord Jesus the Anointed — words that lead to godly living — [4] they're puffed up on nothing, knowing nothing, sick with a craving for arguments and word-fights. Out of that comes envy, strife, slander, ugly suspicions, [5] and constant friction among people whose minds have rotted and who've lost any grip on the truth. They imagine godliness is a way to make money.

[6] But here's the real profit: godliness paired with contentment is a fortune. [7] We came into this world with nothing, and we'll carry nothing out. [8] So if we have food and clothes, that's enough.

[9] Those who are dead-set on getting rich fall into a trap — snared by a thousand foolish and damaging cravings that drag people down into ruin and destruction. [10] The love of money is a root that grows every kind of evil. Chasing it, some have wandered clean off the path of faith and impaled themselves on grief after grief.

[11] But you — man of God — run from all this. Chase righteousness, godliness, faith, love, endurance, gentleness. [12] Fight the beautiful fight of faith. Lay hold of the eternal life you were called into when you made your great confession in front of all those witnesses.

[13] Before God, who breathes life into everything, and before the Anointed Jesus, who made his own great confession standing before Pontius Pilate, I am charging you: [14] keep this command spotless, beyond reproach, until our Lord Jesus the Anointed appears — [15] an appearing God will stage at the proper time, the blessed and only Sovereign, King of those who reign, Lord of those who rule, [16] the only one who cannot die, who lives in light no one can approach, whom no human eye has ever seen or could see. To him, honor and unending power. Amen.

[17] Tell the rich in this present age not to swell up with pride or pin their hopes on wealth — which is the most unreliable thing there is. Pin them on God, who lavishes everything on us to enjoy. [18] Tell them to do good, to get rich in good works, to be generous, ready to share — [19] storing up for themselves a solid foundation for what's coming, so they can take hold of life that is actually life.

[20] Timothy — guard what's been entrusted to you. Turn away from the godless babble and the so-called "knowledge" that contradicts itself. [21] Some have professed it and missed the mark of the faith entirely.

Grace be with you all.

[1] Solomon, son of David, took firm hold of his kingdom. The LORD his God was with him, and made him tower over everything.

[2] Solomon summoned all Israel — the commanders of thousands and hundreds, the judges, every leader across the land, the heads of every family —

[3] and the whole assembly went with him up to the high place at Gibeon, because that was where God's Tent of Meeting stood, the one Moses, the LORD's servant, had built out in the wilderness.

[4] (The ark of God itself, David had already brought up from Kiriath-jearim to the place he had prepared for it, pitching a tent for it in Jerusalem.)

[5] But the bronze altar that Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, had crafted — that was still at Gibeon, in front of the LORD's tabernacle. So Solomon and the assembly went there to seek him.

[6] Solomon climbed up to the bronze altar in the LORD's presence at the Tent of Meeting, and offered a thousand burnt offerings on it.

[7] That night, God appeared to Solomon and said: "Ask. Whatever you want — ask, and I'll give it."

[8] Solomon answered God: "You showed extraordinary love to my father David, and you've set me on his throne in his place.

[9] So now, LORD God — let your promise to my father David stand firm. You've made me king over a people as countless as the dust of the earth.

[10] Give me wisdom. Give me knowledge — enough to lead this people in and out. Because who could possibly govern this vast people of yours otherwise?"

[11] God said to Solomon: "Because this is what was in your heart — because you didn't ask for wealth or possessions or honor, didn't ask for the lives of your enemies, didn't even ask for a long life, but asked for wisdom and knowledge to govern my people, the people I made you king over —

[12] wisdom and knowledge are yours. And I'll also give you wealth, possessions, and honor on a scale no king before you ever had and no king after you ever will."

[13] Then Solomon left the high place at Gibeon, left the Tent of Meeting, and returned to Jerusalem. And he reigned over Israel.

[14] Solomon stockpiled chariots and horsemen — fourteen hundred chariots, twelve thousand horsemen — stationing them in the chariot cities and keeping some with him in Jerusalem.

[15] Under his reign, silver and gold became as common in Jerusalem as stones in the street, and cedar as plentiful as the sycamore-fig trees in the lowlands.

[16] His horses were imported from Egypt and from Kue — the royal merchants buying them in Kue at the going rate.

[17] A chariot imported from Egypt cost six hundred shekels of silver; a horse, a hundred and fifty. And through these traders, every king of the Hittites and the Arameans got their horses too.

[1] Solomon gave the order: build a house for the name of the LORD, and a royal palace for himself.

[2] He conscripted seventy thousand men to haul, eighty thousand to quarry stone in the hills, and three thousand six hundred to oversee them.

[3] Then Solomon sent word to Hiram, king of Tyre: "Do for me what you did for my father David when you sent him cedar to build his house.

[4] I am building a house for the name of the LORD my God — to consecrate it to him, to burn fragrant incense before him, to set out the bread of the Presence always, to offer burnt offerings morning and evening, on Sabbaths, new moons, and the appointed feasts of the LORD our God. This is Israel's standing obligation.

[5] The house I am building will be massive — because our God is greater than all gods.

[6] But who can really build him a house? The heavens, even the highest heavens, cannot contain him. Who am I to build him a house — except as a place to burn offerings before him?

[7] So send me a man skilled in gold, silver, bronze, iron, and in purple, crimson, and violet fabrics — a master engraver who can work alongside the artisans my father David already gathered here in Judah and Jerusalem.

[8] And send me cedar, cypress, and algum logs from Lebanon. I know your men are unmatched at felling Lebanon's timber. My men will work beside yours

[9] to prepare timber in massive quantities, because the house I am building must be vast and extraordinary.

[10] To your woodsmen, the men who cut the timber, I will give twenty thousand kors of crushed wheat, twenty thousand kors of barley, twenty thousand baths of wine, and twenty thousand baths of oil."

[11] Hiram king of Tyre replied in a letter to Solomon: "Because the LORD loves his people, he has made you their king."

[12] Hiram went on: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who made heaven and earth — who has given King David a wise son endowed with discernment and insight, who will build a house for the LORD and a royal palace for himself.

[13] I am sending you Huram-abi, a master craftsman, a man of skill —

[14] son of a Danite woman and a Tyrian father. He works gold, silver, bronze, iron, stone, and wood; purple, violet, fine linen, and crimson. He can engrave any design and execute any plan you set before him, alongside your craftsmen and those of my lord David, your father.

[15] So now — let my lord send his servants the wheat, barley, oil, and wine he promised.

[16] We will cut all the timber you need from Lebanon and float it as rafts by sea to Joppa, and you can haul it up to Jerusalem from there."

[17] Solomon took a census of every foreign man living in Israel — a count like the one his father David had taken — and they came to one hundred fifty-three thousand six hundred.

[18] Seventy thousand of them he assigned to haul loads, eighty thousand to quarry stone in the hills, and three thousand six hundred as overseers to keep the work moving.

[1] So Solomon broke ground on the House of the LORD in Jerusalem, on Mount Moriah, where the LORD had appeared to his father David — the very spot David had prepared on the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite.

[2] Construction began on the second day of the second month, in the fourth year of his reign.

[3] These are the foundations Solomon laid for the House of God: ninety feet long and thirty feet wide, measured by the old standard.

[4] The portico across the front matched the width of the house — thirty feet — and rose thirty feet high. Inside, he overlaid it with pure gold.

[5] The main hall he paneled with cypress, plated it with fine gold, and embossed it with palm trees and chains.

[6] He studded the house with precious stones for sheer beauty, and the gold was gold from Parvaim.

[7] He sheathed the whole structure in gold — beams, thresholds, walls, doors — and carved cherubim into the walls.

[8] Then he built the Most Holy Place: thirty feet long, matching the width of the house, and thirty feet wide. He overlaid it with six hundred talents of pure gold —

[9] the nails alone weighed fifty shekels of gold. The upper rooms he likewise covered in gold.

[10] In the Most Holy Place he sculpted two cherubim and overlaid them with gold.

[11] Their wings stretched thirty feet across: one wing of the first cherub touched the wall, seven and a half feet out; its other wing, also seven and a half feet, met the wing of the second cherub.

[12] The second cherub matched it — one wing touching the far wall, the other reaching to meet its companion in the center.

[13] So the wings of these cherubim spread thirty feet wide. They stood on their feet, faces turned toward the hall.

[14] He hung the veil — blue, purple, crimson, and fine linen — and worked cherubim into it.

[15] Out in front of the house he raised two pillars, fifty-two feet tall, each crowned with a capital seven and a half feet high.

[16] He fashioned chains like a necklace and set them on the capitals, then made a hundred pomegranates and hung them in the chains.

[17] He stood the pillars before the temple, one on the right and one on the left. The right one he named Jachin — *He establishes.* The left one, Boaz — *In him is strength.*

[1] So the work Solomon had poured into the house of the LORD was finished. He brought in everything his father David had set apart — the silver, the gold, every sacred vessel — and stored them in the treasuries of God's house.

[2] Then Solomon called Israel's elders to Jerusalem — every tribal head, every chief of the ancestral houses — to bring up the ark of the LORD's covenant from the City of David, that is, Zion.

[3] Every man of Israel showed up before the king at the festival in the seventh month.

[4] When all the elders of Israel had arrived, the Levites lifted the ark.

[5] They carried the ark up, along with the tent of meeting and every holy vessel inside it — the Levitical priests bore them all.

[6] King Solomon and the entire assembly of Israel gathered before the ark, sacrificing sheep and oxen beyond counting, beyond reckoning.

[7] The priests brought the ark of the LORD's covenant to its place — the inner sanctuary of the house, the Most Holy Place — beneath the wings of the cherubim.

[8] The cherubim spread their wings over the spot, sheltering the ark and its carrying poles from above.

[9] The poles were long enough that their ends could be seen from the Holy Place just in front of the inner sanctuary — but not from outside. They are there to this day.

[10] Inside the ark: nothing but the two tablets Moses had placed there at Horeb, where the LORD cut covenant with the Israelites after they came out of Egypt.

[11] Then the priests came out of the Holy Place. Every priest present had consecrated himself — divisions didn't matter that day.

[12] And every Levitical singer — Asaph, Heman, Jeduthun, their sons, their kin — stood east of the altar, dressed in fine linen, with cymbals, harps, and lyres. With them, a hundred and twenty priests sounding trumpets.

[13] The trumpeters and singers became one voice — one sound rising in praise and thanks to the LORD. As the trumpets and cymbals and instruments lifted, as the voices lifted — *He is good; his love never quits* — the cloud filled the house. The house of the LORD. It filled it.

[14] And the priests could not stand to do their work — because of the cloud. Because the glory of the LORD had filled the house of God.

[1] Then Solomon said: "The LORD declared he would live in thick darkness. [2] And I have built you a house of magnificence, a place for you to settle in forever."

[3] The king turned and blessed the whole assembly of Israel as they stood there. [4] He said: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who spoke with his own mouth to my father David and now with his own hand has fulfilled it, saying: [5] 'From the day I brought my people out of Egypt, I chose no city among all the tribes of Israel for a house to be built where my name would dwell, nor did I choose anyone to lead my people Israel. [6] But now I have chosen Jerusalem for my name to dwell there, and I have chosen David to rule my people Israel.'

[7] "My father David had it in his heart to build a house for the name of the LORD, the God of Israel. [8] But the LORD said to my father David: 'Your heart was right to want to build a house for my name. [9] Yet you are not the one who will build it. Your son — the one who comes from your own body — he will build the house for my name.'

[10] "The LORD has kept the promise he made. I have risen in my father David's place. I sit on the throne of Israel, just as the LORD said. I have built the house for the name of the LORD, the God of Israel. [11] And there I have placed the ark, which holds the covenant the LORD made with the people of Israel."

[12] Then Solomon stood before the altar of the LORD in front of the entire assembly of Israel and spread out his hands. [13] He had made a bronze platform — seven and a half feet long, seven and a half feet wide, four and a half feet high — and set it in the middle of the court. He stood on it, then dropped to his knees in front of the whole assembly of Israel and stretched his hands toward heaven.

[14] He prayed: "LORD, God of Israel — there is no god like you in heaven or on earth, keeping covenant and steadfast love with your servants who walk before you with their whole heart. [15] You kept your word to your servant David, my father. What you spoke with your mouth you have done with your hand — as it is this very day.

[16] "So now, LORD, God of Israel, hold to what you promised your servant David, my father, when you said: 'You will never lack a man to sit before me on the throne of Israel — if your sons guard their way and walk in my Law as you have walked before me.' [17] So now, LORD, God of Israel — let your word to your servant David stand confirmed.

[18] "But will God really dwell with humanity on earth? The heavens — even the highest heavens — cannot contain you. How much less this house I have built! [19] Yet turn toward your servant's prayer, toward his plea, LORD my God. Listen to the cry and the prayer your servant is praying before you. [20] Let your eyes stay open toward this house day and night — this place where you said you would put your name. Hear the prayer your servant prays toward this place. [21] Hear the pleas of your servant and your people Israel when they pray toward this place. Hear from the place where you dwell — from heaven. Hear, and forgive.

[22] "When someone wrongs his neighbor and is required to take an oath, and he comes and swears the oath before your altar in this house — [23] then hear from heaven and act. Judge between your servants. Pay back the guilty by bringing his ways down on his own head. Vindicate the righteous by giving him what his righteousness deserves.

[24] "When your people Israel are struck down before an enemy because they have sinned against you, and they turn back to you, confess your name, and pray and plead before you in this house — [25] hear from heaven. Forgive the sin of your people Israel. Bring them back to the land you gave them and their fathers.

[26] "When the heavens are shut and there is no rain because they have sinned against you, and they pray toward this place, confess your name, and turn from their sin because you have humbled them — [27] hear from heaven. Forgive the sin of your servants, your people Israel. Teach them the good way they should walk. Send rain on your land — the land you gave your people as their inheritance.

[28] "When famine strikes the land, or plague, or blight, or mildew, or locust, or grasshopper — when their enemies besiege them in any of their cities — whatever disaster, whatever disease — [29] whatever prayer or plea comes from anyone, or from all your people Israel, each one knowing the wound and pain in his own heart, spreading his hands toward this house — [30] hear from heaven, the place where you dwell. Forgive. Deal with each one according to his ways, since you know his heart — for you alone know the human heart — [31] so that they will fear you and walk in your ways for as long as they live in the land you gave our fathers.

[32] "Even the foreigner — the one who is not part of your people Israel — when he comes from a distant land because of your great name and your mighty hand and outstretched arm, when he comes and prays toward this house — [33] hear from heaven, the place where you dwell. Do whatever the foreigner asks of you. Then every people on earth will know your name and fear you, just as your people Israel do, and they will know that this house I have built bears your name.

[34] "When your people go out to war against their enemies, wherever you send them, and they pray to you toward this city you have chosen and toward the house I have built for your name — [35] hear from heaven their prayer and their plea, and uphold their cause.

[36] "When they sin against you — for there is no one who does not sin — and you become angry with them and hand them over to an enemy, and their captors carry them away to a land far or near — [37] if they come to their senses in the land where they are held, if they turn and plead with you in the land of their captivity, saying, 'We have sinned, we have done wrong, we have acted wickedly,' [38] if they return to you with their whole heart and soul in the land of their captivity, where they were taken, and they pray toward the land you gave their fathers, toward the city you chose, toward the house I built for your name — [39] hear from heaven, the place where you dwell, their prayer and their pleas. Uphold their cause. Forgive your people who have sinned against you.

[40] "Now, my God — let your eyes be open and your ears attentive to the prayer offered in this place.

[41] "Rise, LORD God, and come to your resting place — you and the ark of your strength. Let your priests, LORD God, be clothed with salvation. Let your faithful ones rejoice in the good. [42] LORD God, do not turn your face from your anointed. Remember the steadfast love shown to David, your servant."

[1] The moment Solomon finished praying, fire ripped down from the sky and devoured the burnt offering and the sacrifices, and the glory of the LORD filled the house.

[2] The priests could not even enter the temple of the LORD — the glory of the LORD had taken it over completely.

[3] When every Israelite saw the fire fall and the glory settle on the house, they dropped face-down on the pavement and worshiped, pouring out their thanks to the LORD: "He is good. His love never ends."

[4] Then the king and all the people offered sacrifices before the LORD.

[5] King Solomon's offering was staggering: twenty-two thousand cattle and a hundred and twenty thousand sheep. That is how the king and the people dedicated the house of God.

[6] The priests stood at their posts. The Levites stood with the instruments David had crafted for praising the LORD — the ones David himself had used when he gave thanks, singing "His love never ends." Across from them the priests blasted the trumpets, and all Israel stood.

[7] Solomon also consecrated the middle of the courtyard fronting the temple, because that is where he offered the burnt offerings and the fat of the peace offerings — the bronze altar he had built could not hold the burnt offerings, the grain offerings, and the fat.

[8] So Solomon held the festival for seven days, and all Israel with him — a vast assembly stretching from Lebo-hamath to the Wadi of Egypt.

[9] On the eighth day they held a solemn assembly. They had spent seven days dedicating the altar and seven days on the festival itself.

[10] On the twenty-third day of the seventh month he sent the people home — hearts full, joy spilling over for all the goodness the LORD had shown to David, to Solomon, and to his people Israel.

[11] So Solomon finished the house of the LORD and the royal palace. Everything he had set his heart on building — in the LORD's house and in his own — he pulled off completely.

[12] Then the LORD appeared to Solomon at night and said: "I have heard your prayer. I have chosen this place for myself as a house of sacrifice.

[13] When I shut up the sky so no rain falls, or order the locusts to strip the land, or send a plague among my people —

[14] if my people, the ones who bear my name, will humble themselves and pray and seek my face and turn from their crooked ways, then I will hear from heaven. I will forgive their sin. I will heal their land.

[15] My eyes will be wide open and my ears tuned to every prayer prayed in this place.

[16] I have chosen this house. I have made it holy. My name will live here forever. My eyes and my heart will be here, always.

[17] As for you — if you walk before me the way your father David walked, doing everything I command, keeping my statutes and my rulings,

[18] then I will lock in your royal throne, exactly as I promised your father David when I told him, 'You will never lack a man to rule Israel.'

[19] But if you turn away — if you abandon the statutes and commands I have set before you and go serve other gods and bow down to them —

[20] then I will rip Israel out of the land I gave them. This house I have consecrated to my name, I will throw out of my sight. I will make it a proverb, a punchline among all peoples.

[21] And this house, however exalted it stands now — everyone who walks past will be horrified and ask, 'Why did the LORD do this to this land, to this house?'

[22] And the answer will come back: 'Because they abandoned the LORD, the God of their fathers, who brought them out of Egypt. They grabbed hold of other gods, bowed to them, served them. That is why he brought this whole disaster down on them.'"

[1] Twenty years. That is what it took Solomon to build the house of the LORD and his own palace.

[2] After that, he turned to the towns Hiram had handed back to him and rebuilt them, settling Israelites inside their walls.

[3] Then Solomon marched on Hamath-zobah and seized it.

[4] He fortified Tadmor out in the wilderness and raised every storage city in Hamath.

[5] He rebuilt Upper Beth-horon and Lower Beth-horon — fortress towns walled tight, with gates and bars locked down.

[6] He built Baalath too, and every supply depot, every chariot city, every cavalry station. Whatever Solomon wanted to build — in Jerusalem, in Lebanon, anywhere across his domain — he built.

[7] As for the people still left in the land — Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites — none of them Israelite —

[8] descendants of nations Israel had not wiped out — Solomon conscripted them into forced labor. They serve to this day.

[9] But Israelites? Solomon made none of them slaves for his projects. They were his soldiers, his officers, his chariot commanders, his cavalry.

[10] King Solomon's chief overseers numbered two hundred and fifty, the men who ran the workforce.

[11] Solomon moved Pharaoh's daughter up from the City of David into the house he had built her. He said: "My wife will not live in the house of David, king of Israel — those places are holy ground because the ark of the LORD has entered them."

[12] Then Solomon began offering burnt offerings to the LORD on the LORD's altar — the one he had built in front of the portico —

[13] doing each day's offering as Moses commanded: the sabbaths, the new moons, the three annual festivals — Unleavened Bread, Weeks, and Booths.

[14] Following his father David's design, he stationed the priestly divisions at their duties and the Levites at their posts — to lead worship and serve alongside the priests, day by day according to schedule. He also set the gatekeepers division by division at every gate, exactly as David, the man of God, had ordered.

[15] No one deviated from the king's instructions — not the priests, not the Levites — not on any matter, not even regarding the treasuries.

[16] So Solomon's work was completed, start to finish — from the day the LORD's house was founded to the day it stood finished. The house of the LORD was perfect.

[17] Then Solomon traveled to Ezion-geber and Eloth, port cities on the coast of Edom.

[18] Hiram sent him ships — sent through his own crews — and sailors who knew the sea. They went with Solomon's men to Ophir and brought back four hundred and fifty talents of gold, delivered to King Solomon.

[1] When the queen of Sheba caught wind of Solomon's reputation, she came to Jerusalem to test him with riddles that had no easy answers. She arrived with a massive entourage — camels loaded with spices, gold by the ton, and gemstones. She came to Solomon and laid out everything weighing on her mind.

[2] Solomon answered every question. Nothing was too obscure for him; nothing stumped him.

[3] When the queen of Sheba saw the depth of Solomon's wisdom, the palace he had built,

[4] the food on his table, the seating of his officials, the choreography of his servants and their uniforms, his cupbearers and theirs, and the staircase he ascended to the temple of the Lord — it took her breath away.

[5] She said to the king, "The reports I heard in my own country about your accomplishments and your wisdom — they were true.

[6] I didn't believe a word of it until I came and saw with my own eyes. And the truth is, they didn't tell me half of it. Your wisdom and your wealth exceed everything I heard.

[7] How fortunate are your people. How fortunate are these servants who get to stand here day after day soaking in your wisdom.

[8] Blessed be the Lord your God, who delighted in you and seated you on his throne as king for the Lord your God. Because your God loves Israel and means to establish them forever, he made you their king to administer justice and righteousness."

[9] She presented the king with four and a half tons of gold, vast quantities of spices, and precious stones. No spices ever rivaled what the queen of Sheba gave to King Solomon.

[10] Meanwhile, the servants of Hiram and the servants of Solomon, who brought gold from Ophir, also brought algum wood and precious stones.

[11] The king used the algum wood to make steps for the temple of the Lord and the royal palace, and harps and lyres for the musicians. Nothing like it had ever been seen in the land of Judah.

[12] King Solomon gave the queen of Sheba whatever she desired, whatever she asked for — far more than she had brought him. Then she turned and went home, she and her servants.

[13] The gold flowing into Solomon every single year amounted to twenty-five tons —

[14] and that didn't count the revenue from traders and merchants. All the kings of Arabia and the governors of the territories brought him gold and silver too.

[15] King Solomon made two hundred large shields of hammered gold — fifteen pounds of gold went into each one.

[16] And three hundred smaller shields of hammered gold, seven and a half pounds each. The king housed them in the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon.

[17] The king built a massive ivory throne and overlaid it with pure gold.

[18] Six steps led up to the throne, with a golden footstool attached. Armrests flanked the seat, and two lions stood guard beside them.

[19] Twelve more lions stood on the six steps, one on each end of every step. Nothing like it had ever been crafted for any kingdom.

[20] Every drinking vessel in King Solomon's court was gold. Every utensil in the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon was solid gold. Silver was considered nothing in Solomon's day —

[21] because the king's ships sailed to Tarshish with Hiram's crews, and once every three years the Tarshish fleet returned hauling gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks.

[22] King Solomon outstripped every king on earth in wealth and wisdom.

[23] Every king on earth came looking for an audience with Solomon, hungry to hear the wisdom God had planted in his heart.

[24] And every one of them brought tribute — silver vessels and gold vessels, robes, weapons, spices, horses, mules — year after year after year.

[25] Solomon had four thousand stalls for horses and chariots and twelve thousand horsemen, stationed in chariot cities and with him in Jerusalem.

[26] He ruled over every king from the Euphrates to the land of the Philistines, all the way to the border of Egypt.

[27] The king made silver in Jerusalem as common as stones, and cedar as plentiful as the sycamore-fig trees in the foothills.

[28] Horses were imported for Solomon from Egypt and from every other land.

[29] The rest of Solomon's acts, from beginning to end — aren't they written in the records of Nathan the prophet, in the prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite, and in the visions of Iddo the seer concerning Jeroboam son of Nebat?

[30] Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel for forty years.

[31] Then Solomon lay down with his fathers and was buried in the city of David his father. And Rehoboam his son reigned in his place.

[1] Rehoboam went to Shechem, because all Israel had gathered there to crown him king.

[2] When Jeroboam son of Nebat heard the news—he was still in Egypt where he'd fled from King Solomon—he came back.

[3] They sent for him, and Jeroboam came with the whole assembly of Israel to confront Rehoboam:

[4] "Your father strapped a brutal yoke on us. Lighten the load—ease up on the crushing labor your father imposed—and we'll serve you."

[5] Rehoboam answered, "Give me three days. Then come back." So the people left.

[6] King Rehoboam went to the elders—the ones who had stood at his father Solomon's side while he was alive. "How do you advise me to answer these people?"

[7] They told him, "If you treat these people well today—if you please them, give them a straight, kind answer—they'll be your servants forever."

[8] But he tossed aside the counsel of the elders and turned to the young men he'd grown up with, the ones who now stood at his side.

[9] "What do you advise?" he asked them. "How should I answer these people who are demanding I lighten the yoke my father put on them?"

[10] The young men he'd grown up with said, "Here's what to tell them—these people who whined that your father made their yoke heavy and want you to lighten it—tell them this: 'My little finger is thicker than my father's waist.

[11] My father loaded you with a heavy yoke? I'll pile on more. My father disciplined you with whips? I'll discipline you with scorpions.'"

[12] So Jeroboam and all the people came back to Rehoboam on the third day, just as the king had said: "Come back to me in three days."

[13] The king answered them harshly. King Rehoboam threw away the elders' counsel

[14] and spoke to them as the young men had advised: "My father made your yoke heavy—I'll make it heavier. My father disciplined you with whips—I'll use scorpions."

[15] The king refused to listen to the people, because this turn of events was from God—so the LORD could fulfill the word he'd spoken through Ahijah of Shiloh to Jeroboam son of Nebat.

[16] When all Israel saw that the king would not listen, they fired back at him:

"What share do we have in David?
What inheritance in Jesse's son?
To your tents, Israel!
Look after your own house, David!"

And Israel went home.

[17] But as for the Israelites living in the towns of Judah—Rehoboam still ruled them.

[18] Then King Rehoboam sent out Hadoram, the man over forced labor—and the Israelites stoned him to death. King Rehoboam scrambled into his chariot and fled to Jerusalem.

[19] And so Israel has been in rebellion against the house of David ever since.

[1] When Rehoboam reached Jerusalem, he mustered the house of Judah and Benjamin — a hundred and eighty thousand picked fighters — to wage war against Israel and pull the kingdom back to himself.

[2] But the word of the LORD came to Shemaiah, the man of God:

[3] "Speak to Rehoboam son of Solomon, king of Judah, and to all Israel in Judah and Benjamin:

[4] This is what the LORD says — do not march. Do not fight your brothers. Every man, go home. This thing is from me." So they listened to the LORD's words and turned back from the campaign against Jeroboam.

[5] Rehoboam settled into Jerusalem and built up fortified cities throughout Judah.

[6] He rebuilt Bethlehem, Etam, Tekoa,

[7] Beth-zur, Soco, Adullam,

[8] Gath, Mareshah, Ziph,

[9] Adoraim, Lachish, Azekah,

[10] Zorah, Aijalon, and Hebron — the fortified cities of Judah and Benjamin.

[11] He reinforced their defenses, installed commanders, stockpiled provisions, olive oil, and wine,

[12] and stationed shields and spears in every city, making them formidable. Judah and Benjamin were his.

[13] The priests and Levites from across Israel rallied to him from every territory.

[14] The Levites abandoned their pasturelands and properties and came to Judah and Jerusalem — because Jeroboam and his sons had cut them off from serving as priests to the LORD.

[15] Jeroboam had appointed his own priests for the high places, for the goat-demons, and for the calf-idols he had made.

[16] And from every tribe of Israel, those whose hearts were set on seeking the LORD, the God of Israel, followed the Levites to Jerusalem to sacrifice to the LORD, the God of their fathers.

[17] They strengthened the kingdom of Judah and stood behind Rehoboam son of Solomon for three years — three years of walking in the way of David and Solomon.

[18] Rehoboam took Mahalath as his wife — daughter of David's son Jerimoth and of Abihail, daughter of Jesse's son Eliab.

[19] She bore him sons: Jeush, Shemariah, and Zaham.

[20] After her, he married Maacah daughter of Absalom, who bore him Abijah, Attai, Ziza, and Shelomith.

[21] Rehoboam loved Maacah daughter of Absalom more than all his other wives and concubines — eighteen wives, sixty concubines, twenty-eight sons, sixty daughters.

[22] He elevated Abijah son of Maacah as chief, prince among his brothers — because he intended to make him king.

[23] And Rehoboam played it shrewdly: he scattered his sons throughout every district of Judah and Benjamin, into every fortified city, gave them generous provisions, and arranged many wives for them.

[1] Once Rehoboam's rule was firmly established and his strength secure, he walked away from the LORD's instruction — and all Israel with him.

[2] So in his fifth year on the throne, Shishak king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem. They had been unfaithful to the LORD, and now they would learn what that costs.

[3] He came with twelve hundred chariots and sixty thousand horsemen, and the troops trailing him from Egypt were beyond counting — Libyans, Sukkites, Cushites, the whole flood of them.

[4] He took the fortified cities of Judah one after another and pressed all the way to Jerusalem.

[5] Then Shemaiah the prophet came to Rehoboam and to the leaders of Judah who had gathered in Jerusalem because of Shishak. He delivered the word: "This is what the LORD says — You abandoned me. So I have abandoned you to Shishak."

[6] The leaders of Israel and the king bowed low. "The LORD is right," they said. "We deserve this."

[7] When the LORD saw them break, his word came to Shemaiah again: "They've humbled themselves. I will not destroy them. I'll give them a measure of escape. My wrath will not pour out on Jerusalem through Shishak's hand.

[8] But they will serve him — so they can learn the difference between serving me and serving the kingdoms of the earth."

[9] So Shishak king of Egypt marched on Jerusalem. He stripped the treasures of the LORD's house and the treasures of the king's house. He took it all — including the gold shields Solomon had made.

[10] To replace them, King Rehoboam had bronze shields made and entrusted them to the captains of the guard who watched the entrance to the king's palace.

[11] Whenever the king entered the LORD's house, the guards would carry these shields, then bring them back to the guardroom afterward.

[12] Because Rehoboam humbled himself, the LORD's anger turned away from him — he was not destroyed completely. And there was still some good in Judah.

[13] So King Rehoboam consolidated power in Jerusalem and kept reigning. He was forty-one when he took the throne and he ruled seventeen years from Jerusalem, the city the LORD had chosen out of all the tribes of Israel to put his name there. His mother was Naamah the Ammonite.

[14] He did evil — because he never set his heart to seek the LORD.

[15] As for the rest of Rehoboam's story, from beginning to end — it's written in the records of Shemaiah the prophet and Iddo the seer, including the genealogies. War between Rehoboam and Jeroboam was constant.

[16] Rehoboam joined his ancestors in death and was buried in the City of David. His son Abijah took the throne.

[1] In the eighteenth year of King Jeroboam, Abijah took the throne of Judah. [2] He reigned three years in Jerusalem. His mother was Micaiah, daughter of Uriel of Gibeah.

Then war broke out between Abijah and Jeroboam.

[3] Abijah marched into battle with an elite force of four hundred thousand picked warriors. Jeroboam met him with eight hundred thousand — double the army, every man a fighter.

[4] Abijah climbed Mount Zemaraim, deep in the hill country of Ephraim, and raised his voice:

"Listen to me, Jeroboam — and all Israel, hear this. [5] Don't you know? The LORD, the God of Israel, handed kingship over Israel to David and his sons forever — sealed by a covenant of salt. [6] Yet Jeroboam son of Nebat, a servant of Solomon son of David, rose up and rebelled against his master. [7] Worthless men, scoundrels, gathered around him and bullied Rehoboam son of Solomon when Rehoboam was young and unsure of himself, too weak to stand against them.

[8] And now you think you can stand against the kingdom of the LORD — the kingdom held by David's sons? Yes, you outnumber us. Yes, you brought your golden calves — the gods Jeroboam made for you. [9] You drove out the LORD's priests, the sons of Aaron, and the Levites. You manufactured priests of your own, just like the nations around you. Anyone who shows up with a young bull and seven rams gets to be a priest of your nothing-gods.

[10] But us? The LORD is our God. We have not abandoned him. The priests who serve the LORD are sons of Aaron. The Levites do their work. [11] Every morning, every evening, they burn offerings to the LORD — fragrant incense, ordered bread on the pure table, the golden lampstand lit each night. We keep watch at the LORD's post. You walked away from him. [12] God himself leads us. His priests stand ready with battle trumpets to sound the war cry against you.

People of Israel — do not fight the LORD, the God of your fathers. You will not win."

[13] But Jeroboam had already sent a force around to ambush them from behind. His main army faced Judah; the ambush waited at their back. [14] Judah turned and saw it — battle in front, battle behind. They cried out to the LORD. The priests blasted the trumpets. [15] The men of Judah let loose a war shout — and when they shouted, God struck Jeroboam and all Israel before Abijah and Judah.

[16] Israel ran. God handed them over. [17] Abijah and his army cut them down — a slaughter. Five hundred thousand of Israel's finest fell that day. [18] The men of Israel were crushed. The men of Judah prevailed because they leaned on the LORD, the God of their fathers.

[19] Abijah chased Jeroboam down and seized cities from him: Bethel and its villages, Jeshanah and its villages, Ephron and its villages.

[20] Jeroboam never recovered his strength in Abijah's lifetime. The LORD struck him, and he died.

[21] But Abijah grew strong. He took fourteen wives and fathered twenty-two sons and sixteen daughters.

[22] The rest of Abijah's deeds — his ways, his words — are written in the commentary of the prophet Iddo.

[1] Abijah went to rest with his ancestors, and they buried him in the City of David. His son Asa took the throne, and under him the land had peace for ten years.

[2] Asa did what was good and right in the eyes of the Lord his God. [3] He tore down the foreign altars and the high places. He smashed the sacred pillars. He cut down the Asherah poles. [4] He commanded Judah to seek the Lord, the God of their ancestors, and to keep his law and his commands. [5] He swept the high places and the incense altars out of every town in Judah. The kingdom stood quiet under him.

[6] He fortified cities across Judah while the land was at peace. No war touched him in those years, because the Lord gave him rest. [7] He told Judah, "Let's build up these cities — wall them, tower them, gate them, bar them. The land is still ours because we sought the Lord our God. We sought him, and he gave us rest on every side." So they built, and they prospered.

[8] Asa's army: three hundred thousand from Judah carrying large shields and spears, and two hundred eighty thousand from Benjamin carrying smaller shields and drawing bows. All of them, hardened soldiers.

[9] Then Zerah the Cushite marched out against them — a million men and three hundred chariots — and he came as far as Mareshah. [10] Asa moved to meet him, and they drew up battle lines in the Valley of Zephathah at Mareshah.

[11] Asa cried out to the Lord his God: "Lord, no one but you can help when the strong fight the helpless. Help us, Lord our God — we lean on you, and in your name we've come against this horde. Lord, you are our God. Don't let a mortal stand against you."

[12] The Lord shattered the Cushites in front of Asa and Judah. The Cushites fled. [13] Asa and his men chased them all the way to Gerar. The Cushites went down — so many fell that the army could not regroup. They were crushed before the Lord and his troops, and Judah carried off an enormous haul of plunder. [14] They struck every village around Gerar, because the terror of the Lord had fallen on them. They stripped those villages clean — there was plunder in all of them. [15] They even raided the herdsmen's camps, hauling away droves of sheep and camels. Then they turned back to Jerusalem.

[1] The Spirit of God came over Azariah son of Oded, [2] and he went out to meet Asa and said: "Hear me, Asa, and all Judah and Benjamin. The LORD is with you when you are with him. If you seek him, he lets himself be found. If you abandon him, he abandons you. [3] For a long stretch Israel had no true God, no priest to teach, no instruction. [4] But when the pressure crushed them and they turned back to the LORD, the God of Israel — when they searched for him, he let himself be found. [5] In those days no one could travel safely. Whole populations lived in turmoil. [6] Nation collided with nation, city with city, because God himself was shaking them with every kind of trouble. [7] But you — be strong. Do not let your hands drop. Your work will be repaid."

[8] When Asa heard this — the prophecy from Oded the prophet — he steeled himself. He swept the detestable idols out of Judah and Benjamin and out of every town he had captured in the hill country of Ephraim, and he restored the LORD's altar that stood before the temple porch.

[9] Then he gathered all Judah and Benjamin, along with everyone from Ephraim, Manasseh, and Simeon who had come to live among them — masses of people had defected from Israel to Asa once they saw the LORD his God was with him. [10] They assembled in Jerusalem in the third month of the fifteenth year of Asa's reign. [11] That day they sacrificed to the LORD from the plunder they had brought back — seven hundred cattle and seven thousand sheep and goats. [12] Then they entered into a covenant: they would seek the LORD, the God of their ancestors, with everything in them — heart and soul. [13] Anyone who refused to seek the LORD, the God of Israel, would be put to death, regardless of status, age, or sex. [14] They swore the oath to the LORD with a great shout, with trumpets blasting and rams' horns sounding. [15] All Judah rejoiced in the oath. They had sworn it with their whole heart. They had sought him with everything they had, and he let himself be found. The LORD gave them rest on every side.

[16] King Asa even removed his grandmother Maacah from her position as queen mother because she had made a vile image for Asherah. Asa cut it down, smashed it to pieces, and burned it in the Kidron Valley. [17] The high places never came down out of Israel — but Asa's heart stayed fully committed to the LORD as long as he lived. [18] He brought into the temple of God the silver and gold and the sacred objects he and his father had dedicated.

[19] And there was no more war until the thirty-fifth year of Asa's reign.

[1] In the thirty-sixth year of Asa's reign, Baasha king of Israel marched against Judah and started fortifying Ramah — sealing off every road in and out of King Asa's territory.

[2] So Asa raided the treasuries of the LORD's house and the palace, pulled out silver and gold, and sent it to Ben-Hadad king of Aram in Damascus with this message:

[3] "Let's make a treaty — you and me, your father and mine. I'm sending you silver and gold. Break your treaty with Baasha king of Israel so he pulls back from me."

[4] Ben-Hadad agreed. He dispatched his commanders against the cities of Israel, and they struck Ijon, Dan, Abel-maim, and every storage city in Naphtali.

[5] When Baasha got the news, he abandoned the construction at Ramah and walked off the project.

[6] Then Asa mobilized all of Judah. They hauled away the stones and timber Baasha had been using at Ramah, and Asa used them to fortify Geba and Mizpah.

[7] At that point Hanani the seer came to Asa king of Judah and told him: "Because you leaned on the king of Aram instead of leaning on the LORD your God, the army of Aram has slipped through your fingers.

[8] Remember the Cushites and Libyans — that massive force, all those chariots and horsemen? When you leaned on the LORD back then, He handed them over to you.

[9] The eyes of the LORD sweep across the whole earth, hunting for hearts fully committed to Him so He can show Himself strong on their behalf. You played the fool here. From now on, you'll have wars."

[10] Asa's response? Rage. He threw the seer in the stocks — he was furious about what he'd heard. And around that same time, Asa started crushing some of his own people.

[11] The record of Asa's reign, from beginning to end, is written in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel.

[12] In the thirty-ninth year of his reign, Asa's feet broke down with disease — and it was severe. But even then, he didn't seek the LORD. He sought out the physicians.

[13] In the forty-first year of his reign, Asa lay down with his fathers and died.

[14] They buried him in the tomb he had carved out for himself in the City of David. They laid him on a bed piled with spices and every kind of expertly blended perfume, and they lit an enormous fire in his honor.

[1] Jehoshaphat, Asa's son, took the throne and dug in against Israel. [2] He posted troops in every fortified city of Judah and set garrisons throughout the land — in Judah itself and in the Ephraim towns his father Asa had captured.

[3] The LORD was with Jehoshaphat because he walked the early road of his ancestor David — no chasing after the Baals, [4] but seeking the God of his father and living by his commands instead of imitating Israel's drift. [5] So the LORD locked the kingdom down under his hand. All Judah brought him tribute, and wealth and honor piled up around him. [6] His heart grew bold in the ways of the LORD — bold enough to tear down the high places and the Asherah poles across Judah.

[7] In his third year he sent his officials out to teach in the cities of Judah — Ben-Hail, Obadiah, Zechariah, Nethanel, and Micaiah. [8] With them went Levites — Shemaiah, Nethaniah, Zebadiah, Asahel, Shemiramoth, Jehonathan, Adonijah, Tobijah, Tob-Adonijah — and the priests Elishama and Jehoram. [9] They carried the Book of the Law of the LORD with them, traveling town to town through Judah, teaching the people.

[10] A dread of the LORD settled over every kingdom around Judah — none of them dared make war on Jehoshaphat. [11] Philistines came bringing gifts and silver as tribute. Arabs drove him flocks: seventy-seven hundred rams, seventy-seven hundred goats.

[12] Jehoshaphat kept rising — higher and higher. He built fortresses across Judah and storage cities stocked deep. [13] His holdings in those cities ran enormous, and in Jerusalem he kept seasoned warriors at the ready.

[14] Here is the muster, by ancestral house. From Judah, the commanders of thousands: Adnah the chief, leading three hundred thousand crack troops; [15] beside him Jehohanan with two hundred eighty thousand; [16] beside him Amasiah son of Zichri — a man who volunteered himself to the LORD — with two hundred thousand. [17] From Benjamin: Eliada, a fighting man, with two hundred thousand archers and shield-bearers; [18] beside him Jehozabad with one hundred eighty thousand battle-ready men. [19] These were the king's standing force — not counting the troops he had stationed in the fortified cities throughout Judah.

[1] Jehoshaphat had wealth and honor in abundance — and he married into Ahab's family.

[2] Years later he went down to visit Ahab in Samaria. Ahab threw a feast for him and his entourage — slaughtered sheep and cattle by the hundreds — and worked him over to join an attack on Ramoth-gilead.

[3] Ahab king of Israel said to Jehoshaphat king of Judah, "Will you ride with me against Ramoth-gilead?" Jehoshaphat answered, "I'm with you. My people are your people. We'll fight beside you."

[4] But Jehoshaphat added, "First — let's hear what the LORD has to say."

[5] So Ahab assembled the prophets — four hundred of them — and put the question: "Should we march on Ramoth-gilead, or hold back?" They said in one voice, "Go up. God will hand it to you."

[6] Jehoshaphat pressed: "Isn't there one more prophet of the LORD here we can ask?"

[7] Ahab said, "There's one more — Micaiah son of Imlah. But I hate him. He never prophesies anything good about me. Only disaster." Jehoshaphat said, "Don't talk like that."

[8] So Ahab summoned an officer: "Bring Micaiah son of Imlah. Now."

[9] The two kings sat enthroned in royal robes on a threshing floor at the gate of Samaria, the prophets in full frenzy before them.

[10] Zedekiah son of Chenaanah had made himself iron horns. "This is what the LORD says," he declared. "With these you will gore the Arameans until they're finished."

[11] Every prophet sang the same tune: "Go up to Ramoth-gilead. Win. The LORD will give it to the king."

[12] Meanwhile the messenger sent to fetch Micaiah leaned in: "Listen — every prophet is predicting victory for the king. With one voice. Say what they're saying. Make it good news."

[13] Micaiah said, "As surely as the LORD lives — I will say what my God tells me to say. Nothing else."

[14] When he reached the king, Ahab said, "Micaiah — do we march on Ramoth-gilead, or stand down?" Micaiah said, "Go up. Win. They'll be handed to you."

[15] The king said, "How many times do I have to make you swear to tell me the truth in the name of the LORD?"

[16] Then Micaiah said: "I saw all Israel scattered across the hills like sheep without a shepherd. And the LORD said, 'These have no master. Let each man go home in peace.'"

[17] Ahab turned to Jehoshaphat: "Didn't I tell you? He never prophesies good for me. Only disaster."

[18] Micaiah went on: "Hear the word of the LORD. I saw the LORD seated on his throne, the whole army of heaven standing to his right and his left.

[19] And the LORD said, 'Who will lure Ahab king of Israel into attacking Ramoth-gilead — so he falls there?' One said this. Another said that.

[20] Then a spirit stepped forward, stood before the LORD, and said, 'I will lure him.' The LORD said, 'How?'

[21] He said, 'I'll go out and become a lying spirit in the mouth of every one of his prophets.' And the LORD said, 'You will lure him. You will succeed. Go. Do it.'

[22] So there it is — the LORD has put a lying spirit in the mouth of these prophets of yours. The LORD has decreed disaster for you."

[23] Zedekiah son of Chenaanah stepped up and slapped Micaiah across the face. "Which way did the Spirit of the LORD pass from me to speak to you?"

[24] Micaiah said, "You'll find out — the day you run into an inner room to hide."

[25] The king of Israel said, "Take Micaiah. Hand him over to Amon the governor of the city and to Joash my son.

[26] Tell them: This is the king's order. Throw this man in prison. Feed him bread and water — just enough to keep him alive — until I come back safe."

[27] Micaiah said, "If you come back safe, the LORD has not spoken through me." And he added, "Mark my words, every one of you."

[28] So the king of Israel and Jehoshaphat king of Judah marched up to Ramoth-gilead.

[29] Ahab said to Jehoshaphat, "I'll go into battle disguised. You wear your royal robes." So Ahab disguised himself, and they went in.

[30] Now the king of Aram had ordered his chariot commanders: "Don't engage anyone — small or great — except the king of Israel. Him alone."

[31] When the chariot commanders saw Jehoshaphat, they thought, "That's the king of Israel," and wheeled to attack him. Jehoshaphat cried out. The LORD helped him. God drew them away.

[32] When the commanders realized he wasn't the king of Israel, they broke off the pursuit.

[33] But someone drew his bow at random — and the arrow found the seam in Ahab's armor. Ahab told his driver, "Turn around. Get me out of here. I'm hit."

[34] The fighting raged all day. The king of Israel propped himself up in his chariot, facing the Arameans, until evening. At sunset he died.

[1] Jehoshaphat king of Judah made it home to Jerusalem alive and unharmed. [2] But Jehu son of Hanani the seer walked out to meet him and looked the king in the eye: "You ran to help the wicked. You threw in your lot with men who hate the LORD. For this, wrath is coming down on you from the LORD himself. [3] Still — there is good in you. You tore the Asherah poles out of the land. You set your heart on seeking God."

[4] Jehoshaphat settled back into Jerusalem, then went out again — among the people, from Beersheba in the south to the hills of Ephraim in the north — and brought them back to the LORD, the God of their fathers.

[5] He appointed judges across the land, city by city, throughout the fortified towns of Judah. [6] He charged them: "Watch what you do. You are not judging for men — you are judging for the LORD. He stands with you when you render a verdict. [7] So let the fear of the LORD sit on you. Be careful how you act. With the LORD our God there is no injustice, no favoritism, no bribe slipped under the table."

[8] In Jerusalem, Jehoshaphat also stationed Levites, priests, and heads of Israel's families to handle cases of the LORD's law and disputes brought in by the residents of the city. [9] He gave them their charge: "Do this in the fear of the LORD — faithfully, with a whole heart. [10] Whenever a case comes to you from your fellow citizens out in the towns — whether it involves bloodshed, or a question of law, command, statute, or ruling — warn them. Do not let them rack up guilt before the LORD and bring his wrath down on you and on them. Do this and you stay clear of guilt.

[11] "Amariah the chief priest will preside over you in everything that touches the LORD. Zebadiah son of Ishmael, the leader of the house of Judah, will preside in everything that touches the king. The Levites serve as your officers. Be strong. Act. And may the LORD stand with the one who does right."

[1] After this, the Moabites and Ammonites, with some of the Meunites, marched out to make war on Jehoshaphat.

[2] Word reached Jehoshaphat: "A massive army is coming against you from beyond the Dead Sea, out of Edom. They're already at Hazazon-tamar" — that is, En-gedi.

[3] Jehoshaphat was afraid. He set his face to seek the LORD and called the whole nation to fast.

[4] Judah gathered to ask help from the LORD. From every town they came, hungry for him.

[5] Jehoshaphat stood up in the assembly of Judah and Jerusalem, in the house of the LORD, in front of the new courtyard,

[6] and he prayed: "LORD, God of our fathers — you are God in the heavens, you rule every kingdom on earth. Power and strength are in your hand, and no one can stand against you.

[7] Wasn't it you, our God, who drove out the inhabitants of this land before your people Israel and gave it forever to the descendants of Abraham — your friend?

[8] They settled here. They built you a sanctuary for your name, and they said,

[9] 'If disaster comes on us — sword, judgment, plague, famine — we will stand before this house, before you, because your name is on this house. We will cry out to you in our distress, and you will hear and save.'

[10] Now look — the Ammonites, the Moabites, the people from Mount Seir. When Israel came out of Egypt, you wouldn't let us invade their lands; we turned aside and didn't destroy them.

[11] And this is how they repay us — coming to drive us out of the land you gave us as our inheritance.

[12] Our God, will you not judge them? We have no strength against this horde bearing down on us. We don't know what to do. But our eyes are on you."

[13] All Judah stood there before the LORD — every man, with their little ones, their wives, their children.

[14] Then the Spirit of the LORD came on Jahaziel son of Zechariah, son of Benaiah, son of Jeiel, son of Mattaniah — a Levite from the line of Asaph — right there in the middle of the assembly.

[15] He said: "Listen — all Judah, everyone living in Jerusalem, King Jehoshaphat. This is what the LORD says to you: Don't be afraid. Don't be shaken by this massive army. The battle isn't yours. It's God's.

[16] Tomorrow, march down against them. They'll be climbing up the Ziz Pass — you'll find them at the end of the valley, facing the wilderness of Jeruel.

[17] You won't have to fight this one. Take your positions. Stand still. Watch the LORD deliver you. Judah. Jerusalem. Don't be afraid. Don't lose heart. Tomorrow, march out to meet them — and the LORD is with you."

[18] Jehoshaphat bowed his face to the ground. All Judah and the people of Jerusalem fell down before the LORD, worshiping him.

[19] Then the Levites — from the Kohathites and the Korahites — stood up and praised the LORD, the God of Israel, with a voice loud enough to split the sky.

[20] Early in the morning they set out for the wilderness of Tekoa. As they marched, Jehoshaphat stood and said: "Listen to me, Judah, Jerusalem! Trust the LORD your God and you will hold firm. Trust his prophets and you will succeed."

[21] Then, after consulting with the people, he appointed singers to walk in front of the army, dressed in holy splendor, praising the LORD: *Give thanks to the LORD — his love endures forever.*

[22] The moment they began to sing and praise, the LORD set ambushes against the Ammonites, Moabites, and the men of Mount Seir — and they were struck down.

[23] The Ammonites and Moabites turned on the men of Mount Seir, slaughtering them, devoting them to destruction. When they finished with Seir, they turned on each other.

[24] When Judah came to the lookout over the wilderness and looked toward the army — bodies. Corpses on the ground. Not one survivor.

[25] Jehoshaphat and his army came to gather the spoils, and they found enormous quantities — equipment, valuables, precious things. They stripped so much they couldn't carry it. Three days they spent gathering plunder — there was that much.

[26] On the fourth day they gathered in the Valley of Beracah — Blessing — because that's what they did there: they blessed the LORD. The name sticks to this day.

[27] Then every man of Judah and Jerusalem, with Jehoshaphat at the front, turned back to Jerusalem with joy — because the LORD had made them rejoice over their enemies.

[28] They entered Jerusalem with harps and lyres and trumpets, marching to the house of the LORD.
