[29] The dread of God fell on every kingdom around when they heard how the LORD had fought against the enemies of Israel.

[30] And Jehoshaphat's kingdom had rest. His God gave him peace on every side.

[31] Jehoshaphat reigned over Judah. He was thirty-five when he took the throne, and he ruled twenty-five years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Azubah, daughter of Shilhi.

[32] He walked in the way of his father Asa — never turning from it — doing what was right in the LORD's eyes.

[33] But the high places weren't torn down. The people still hadn't fully turned their hearts to the God of their fathers.

[34] The rest of Jehoshaphat's deeds — beginning to end — are written in the records of Jehu son of Hanani, which are included in the Book of the Kings of Israel.

[35] Later, Jehoshaphat king of Judah made an alliance with Ahaziah king of Israel — a wicked man.

[36] He partnered with him to build ships for trade to Tarshish. They built them at Ezion-geber.

[37] Then Eliezer son of Dodavahu from Mareshah prophesied against Jehoshaphat: "Because you've allied yourself with Ahaziah, the LORD has shattered what you made." The ships broke apart. They never sailed to Tarshish.

[1] Jehoshaphat slept with his ancestors and was buried beside them in the City of David. His son Jehoram took the throne.

[2] Jehoram had brothers — sons of Jehoshaphat: Azariah, Jehiel, Zechariah, another Azariah, Michael, and Shephatiah. All of them sons of Jehoshaphat, king of Israel.

[3] Their father had loaded them with gifts — silver, gold, precious things, fortified cities throughout Judah. But the kingdom itself he handed to Jehoram, the firstborn.

[4] Once Jehoram had locked down his father's throne and felt the power settle into his hands, he drew the sword and killed every one of his brothers — and several of Israel's princes along with them.

[5] He was thirty-two when he became king, and he reigned eight years in Jerusalem.

[6] He walked the road of Israel's kings — the road the house of Ahab walked — because he had married Ahab's daughter. He did what was evil in the LORD's sight.

[7] But the LORD would not destroy the house of David. Not because of Jehoram — because of the covenant he had cut with David, his promise to keep a lamp burning for him and his sons forever.

[8] In Jehoram's days, Edom revolted against Judah's authority and set up its own king.

[9] So Jehoram crossed over with his officers and all his chariots. He moved by night and struck the Edomites who had surrounded him and the chariot commanders.

[10] Edom has been in revolt against Judah from that day until now. At the same time, Libnah broke free of him too — because he had abandoned the LORD, the God of his ancestors.

[11] More than that — he built high places in the hills of Judah. He led Jerusalem's people into spiritual prostitution and seduced Judah itself off the path.

[12] Then a letter arrived. From Elijah the prophet. It read:

*This is what the LORD, the God of David your father, says: You have not walked in the ways of Jehoshaphat your father or Asa king of Judah.*

[13] *You have walked instead in the ways of Israel's kings. You have prostituted Judah and Jerusalem just as the house of Ahab did. And worse — you murdered your own brothers, your father's house, men better than you.*

[14] *So now: the LORD is about to strike. A massive blow — against your people, your sons, your wives, everything you own.*

[15] *And you yourself — a wasting disease in your bowels, day after day, until your insides come out.*

[16] Then the LORD stirred up against Jehoram the fury of the Philistines and the Arabs who lived near the Cushites.

[17] They marched up into Judah, broke through, and looted the royal palace — carried off his sons, his wives. Not one son was left to him except Jehoahaz, the youngest.

[18] After all this, the LORD struck him in his bowels with an incurable disease.

[19] It dragged on. After two years, his intestines came out from the disease, and he died in agony. His people lit no fire for him — none of the honors they had given his ancestors.

[20] He was thirty-two when he took the throne. Eight years in Jerusalem. He departed unmourned. They buried him in the City of David — but not in the tombs of the kings.

[1] The people of Jerusalem made Ahaziah, Jehoram's youngest son, king in his place — because the raiding band that swept into the camp with the Arabs had butchered all the older sons. So Ahaziah, son of Jehoram king of Judah, took the throne.

[2] Ahaziah was twenty-two when he became king, and he ruled in Jerusalem one year. His mother's name was Athaliah, granddaughter of Omri.

[3] He too walked the road of Ahab's house, because his mother coached him in corruption.

[4] He did what the LORD called evil, just like the house of Ahab — they were his advisors after his father died, and they steered him straight off the cliff.

[5] Following their counsel, he marched out with Joram son of Ahab, king of Israel, to fight Hazael king of Aram at Ramoth-gilead. The Arameans wounded Joram in the battle,

[6] and he went back to Jezreel to recover from the wounds he had taken at Ramah fighting Hazael king of Aram. Then Ahaziah son of Jehoram, king of Judah, went down to Jezreel to visit Joram son of Ahab on his sickbed.

[7] But God had already written Ahaziah's ending into that visit. When he arrived, he went out with Joram to meet Jehu son of Nimshi — the man the LORD had anointed to wipe out the house of Ahab.

[8] And while Jehu was carrying out judgment on Ahab's house, he found the officials of Judah and the nephews of Ahaziah who served the king — and he killed them.

[9] Then he hunted down Ahaziah himself. They caught him hiding in Samaria, dragged him to Jehu, and executed him. They buried him only because they said, "He was the grandson of Jehoshaphat, who sought the LORD with his whole heart." After that, no one in the house of Ahaziah had the strength left to hold the kingdom.

[10] When Athaliah, Ahaziah's mother, saw that her son was dead, she rose up and slaughtered the entire royal line of the house of Judah.

[11] But Jehoshabeath, the king's daughter, snatched Joash son of Ahaziah out from among the doomed princes and hid him — him and his nurse — in a bedroom. So Jehoshabeath, daughter of King Jehoram and wife of Jehoiada the priest (she was Ahaziah's sister), kept the boy hidden from Athaliah, and Athaliah never killed him.

[12] He stayed with them, tucked away in the house of God, for six years — while Athaliah ruled the land.

[1] In the seventh year Jehoiada made his move. He pulled the commanders of hundreds into a covenant with him — Azariah son of Jeroham, Ishmael son of Jehohanan, Azariah son of Obed, Maaseiah son of Adaiah, and Elishaphat son of Zichri.

[2] They fanned out through Judah, gathering the Levites from every town and the heads of Israel's families, and brought them to Jerusalem.

[3] The whole assembly cut a covenant with the king inside the house of God. Jehoiada told them: "Here is the king's son. He will reign — exactly as the LORD promised concerning David's line.

[4] This is how we do it. A third of you priests and Levites coming on Sabbath duty will guard the doors.

[5] Another third will hold the royal palace. The last third will be at the Foundation Gate. Everyone else stays in the courts of the LORD's house.

[6] Nobody enters the LORD's house except the priests and the serving Levites — they're consecrated, they go in. Everyone else maintains the LORD's watch outside.

[7] Levites — surround the king, weapons in hand. Anyone who breaks into the house, kill him. Stay with the king wherever he moves."

[8] The Levites and all Judah did exactly what Jehoiada commanded. Each commander took his men — both those going on Sabbath duty and those coming off — because Jehoiada had not dismissed any of the divisions.

[9] Then Jehoiada handed the commanders of hundreds the spears and the large and small shields that had belonged to King David, stored in the house of God.

[10] He positioned every man, weapon ready, from the south side of the temple to the north side, around the altar and the temple, surrounding the king.

[11] Then they brought out the king's son. They set the crown on him, handed him the testimony, and made him king. Jehoiada and his sons anointed him. And the shout went up: "Long live the king!"

[12] Athaliah heard the noise — people running, people cheering for the king — and she came to the people at the house of the LORD.

[13] She looked. And there he was — the king, standing by his pillar at the entrance, the commanders and trumpeters beside him, all the people of the land celebrating and blasting trumpets, the singers with their instruments leading the praise. Athaliah ripped her clothes and screamed, "Treason! Treason!"

[14] Jehoiada the priest brought out the commanders of hundreds in charge of the army and said, "Take her out between the ranks. Anyone who follows her — cut him down." (The priest had said: don't kill her in the house of the LORD.)

[15] So they seized her. When she reached the entrance of the Horse Gate at the palace, they killed her there.

[16] Then Jehoiada cut a covenant between himself, all the people, and the king — that they would be the LORD's people.

[17] All the people stormed into the house of Baal and tore it apart. They smashed its altars and its images and slaughtered Mattan, Baal's priest, right in front of the altars.

[18] Jehoiada then put the oversight of the LORD's house back in the hands of the Levitical priests — the ones David had assigned to the LORD's house to offer the burnt offerings, as Moses' law required, with singing and rejoicing, just as David had organized it.

[19] He stationed gatekeepers at the gates of the LORD's house so no one ritually unclean for any reason could come in.

[20] He gathered the commanders of hundreds, the nobles, the rulers of the people, and the whole population, and brought the king down from the house of the LORD. They came through the Upper Gate to the royal palace and seated him on the throne of the kingdom.

[21] All the people of the land celebrated. The city was quiet. Athaliah — they had killed her with the sword.

[1] Joash was seven when he took the throne, and he reigned in Jerusalem for forty years. His mother was Zibiah from Beersheba.

[2] Joash did what was right in the LORD's eyes the whole time Jehoiada the priest was alive.

[3] Jehoiada chose two wives for him, and he fathered sons and daughters.

[4] Sometime later, Joash decided to restore the house of the LORD.

[5] He gathered the priests and Levites and told them: "Fan out through the towns of Judah. Collect money from every Israelite, year by year, to repair the house of your God. Move on this — now." But the Levites dragged their feet.

[6] So the king summoned Jehoiada the chief priest and confronted him: "Why have you not pressed the Levites to bring in from Judah and Jerusalem the tax that Moses, the LORD's servant, levied on the assembly of Israel for the tent of the covenant?"

[7] (The sons of that wicked woman Athaliah had broken into the house of God and used everything consecrated to the LORD for the Baals.)

[8] At the king's order, a chest was built and set outside, at the gate of the LORD's house.

[9] Then word went out across Judah and Jerusalem to bring in the tax that Moses, God's servant, had imposed on Israel back in the wilderness.

[10] The officials and all the people rejoiced. They brought their contributions and dropped them into the chest until it was full.

[11] Whenever the Levites brought the chest in for royal inspection and the officials saw it was packed with silver, the king's scribe and the high priest's officer would come, empty it, and carry it back to its place. Day after day they did this, and the silver piled up.

[12] The king and Jehoiada handed it over to the men supervising the work on the LORD's house. They hired masons and carpenters to restore the temple, and ironworkers and bronzeworkers to reinforce it.

[13] The workers labored, and the project moved forward in their hands. They rebuilt the house of God to its original specifications and made it solid.

[14] When they finished, they brought the remaining silver to the king and Jehoiada, who used it to make vessels for the LORD's house — tools for worship, bowls for offerings, gold and silver vessels. And throughout Jehoiada's life, burnt offerings rose continually in the house of the LORD.

[15] Jehoiada grew old, full of days, and he died — one hundred thirty years old.

[16] They buried him in the City of David among the kings, because of the good he had done in Israel — for God and for his house.

[17] After Jehoiada's death, the officials of Judah came and bowed before the king, and the king listened to them.

[18] They abandoned the house of the LORD, the God of their fathers, and took up the worship of Asherah poles and idols. Wrath fell on Judah and Jerusalem for this guilt.

[19] The LORD sent prophets to call them back to him. The prophets testified against them. They would not listen.

[20] Then the Spirit of God clothed Zechariah son of Jehoiada the priest. He stood before the people and declared: "This is what God says: Why do you defy the LORD's commands? You will not succeed. Because you have abandoned the LORD, he has abandoned you."

[21] They conspired against him. At the king's command, they stoned him to death in the courtyard of the LORD's house.

[22] King Joash forgot the loyalty Jehoiada — Zechariah's father — had shown him. He murdered his son. As Zechariah was dying he said, "May the LORD see this. May he call you to account."

[23] At the turn of the year, the Aramean army marched against Joash. They invaded Judah and Jerusalem, slaughtered every leader among the people, and shipped all the plunder back to the king of Damascus.

[24] The Aramean force was small, but the LORD handed over a vast army into their hands — because Judah had abandoned the LORD, the God of their fathers. So judgment fell on Joash.

[25] When the Arameans pulled back, they left him gravely wounded. His own officials conspired against him because he had murdered the son of Jehoiada the priest, and they killed him in his bed. He died, and they buried him in the City of David — but not in the tombs of the kings.

[26] These were the conspirators: Zabad son of Shimeath the Ammonite woman, and Jehozabad son of Shimrith the Moabite woman.

[27] As for his sons, the many oracles spoken against him, and the restoration of the house of God — they are recorded in the commentary on the book of the kings. His son Amaziah took the throne after him.

[1] Amaziah was twenty-five when he took the throne, and he ruled twenty-nine years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Jehoaddan, from Jerusalem.

[2] He did what was right in the LORD's eyes — but not with a whole heart.

[3] Once his grip on the kingdom was secure, he executed the officials who had assassinated his father the king.

[4] But he did not kill their children. He held to what was written in the Law of Moses, where the LORD had commanded: "Fathers shall not die for what their children did, and children shall not die for what their fathers did. Each person dies for his own sin."

[5] Amaziah pulled Judah together and organized them by clan under commanders of thousands and hundreds — every man from Judah and Benjamin. He counted those twenty and older and found three hundred thousand fighting men, fit for war, capable with spear and shield.

[6] Then he hired another hundred thousand seasoned soldiers from Israel for a hundred talents of silver.

[7] But a man of God came to him and said: "King — do not let Israel's army march with you. The LORD is not with Israel. Not with any of these Ephraimites.

[8] If you go anyway — go ahead, fight hard — God will trip you in front of the enemy. God has the power to help and the power to break."

[9] Amaziah said to the man of God, "But what about the hundred talents I paid the troops from Israel?" The man of God answered, "The LORD can give you far more than that."

[10] So Amaziah discharged the troops from Ephraim and sent them home. They went back furious — burning with rage at Judah.

[11] But Amaziah steeled himself, led his army out to the Valley of Salt, and struck down ten thousand men of Seir.

[12] Judah's army took another ten thousand alive, marched them to the top of a cliff, and threw them off. Every one of them was shattered on the rocks below.

[13] Meanwhile, the troops Amaziah had sent home — the ones not allowed to fight — raided towns of Judah from Samaria to Beth Horon. They killed three thousand and hauled off massive plunder.

[14] When Amaziah came home from slaughtering the Edomites, he brought back the gods of Seir, set them up as his own gods, bowed down to them, and burned incense to them.

[15] The LORD burned with anger against Amaziah and sent a prophet, who said: "Why are you chasing after a people's gods that could not even save their own people from your hand?"

[16] The king cut him off: "Did we make you the king's counselor? Stop — before I have you killed." The prophet stopped. But he said: "I know God has decided to destroy you, because you have done this and refused to listen to my counsel."

[17] After taking advice from others, Amaziah king of Judah sent word to Jehoash son of Jehoahaz, son of Jehu, king of Israel: "Come — let us face each other in battle."

[18] Jehoash king of Israel sent back this message to Amaziah king of Judah: "A thistle on the slopes of Lebanon sent word to a cedar of Lebanon: 'Give your daughter to my son in marriage.' Then a wild animal in Lebanon trampled the thistle flat.

[19] You say to yourself, 'Look — I crushed Edom.' Now your heart is swollen with pride. Stay home. Glory in that. Why provoke disaster — your own ruin, and Judah's with you?"

[20] But Amaziah would not listen. This was from God — God was setting them up to be handed over, because they had chased after the gods of Edom.

[21] So Jehoash king of Israel marched out, and he and Amaziah king of Judah met face to face at Beth Shemesh in Judah.

[22] Judah was crushed by Israel. Every man fled to his tent.

[23] Jehoash king of Israel captured Amaziah king of Judah, son of Joash, son of Jehoahaz, at Beth Shemesh. He brought him to Jerusalem and tore down the city wall — four hundred cubits of it, from the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate.

[24] He took all the gold and silver, every vessel in the house of God under Obed-Edom's care, and the treasures of the royal palace — and hostages too — and went back to Samaria.

[25] Amaziah son of Joash king of Judah lived fifteen years after Jehoash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel died.

[26] The rest of Amaziah's history, beginning to end — it is written in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel.

[27] From the moment Amaziah turned from following the LORD, conspiracies took shape against him in Jerusalem. He fled to Lachish. They sent men after him to Lachish and killed him there.

[28] They brought him back on horses and buried him with his ancestors in the City of Judah.

[1] Then the people of Judah took Uzziah—just sixteen years old—and crowned him in his father Amaziah's place.

[2] He was the one who rebuilt Elath and brought it back under Judah's control, after his father was laid to rest with his ancestors.

[3] Uzziah was sixteen when he took the throne, and he ruled fifty-two years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Jecoliah, from Jerusalem.

[4] He did what was right in the LORD's sight, walking the same path his father Amaziah had walked.

[5] As long as Zechariah lived—the man who taught him to fear God—Uzziah sought the LORD. And as long as he sought the LORD, God made him succeed.

[6] He marched out and went to war against the Philistines. He tore down the walls of Gath, Jabneh, and Ashdod, then built up new cities in Ashdod territory and across Philistine country.

[7] God backed him in his campaigns—against the Philistines, against the Arabs living in Gur-baal, against the Meunites.

[8] The Ammonites paid tribute to Uzziah. His reputation traveled all the way to the border of Egypt, because he had grown so powerful.

[9] In Jerusalem he built fortified towers—at the Corner Gate, the Valley Gate, and the angle in the wall—and reinforced them.

[10] He built towers in the wilderness too, and dug out cisterns by the dozens, because he ran enormous herds in the foothills and on the plains. He had farmers and vineyard workers in the hills and on the fertile land—the man loved the soil.

[11] Uzziah had an army built for war, divisions trained and ready to march, mustered by Jeiel the scribe and Maaseiah the officer, under the command of Hananiah, one of the king's chief officers.

[12] The total of the family chiefs over these elite warriors came to 2,600.

[13] Under their command stood an army of 307,500—a fighting force of real strength, ready to back the king against any enemy.

[14] Uzziah equipped the entire army with shields, spears, helmets, body armor, bows, and stones for slinging.

[15] In Jerusalem he had engineers design machines—mounted on the towers and corners—that could fire arrows and hurl massive stones. His fame spread far and wide, because he was helped beyond all measure, until he became truly powerful.

[16] But once he was strong, his heart grew arrogant—and that arrogance destroyed him. He betrayed the LORD his God by walking into the LORD's temple to burn incense on the altar of incense.

[17] Azariah the priest went in after him, with eighty other priests of the LORD—men of courage.

[18] They stood up to King Uzziah and told him straight: "This isn't yours to do, Uzziah—burning incense to the LORD belongs to the priests, the descendants of Aaron, who were consecrated for this. Get out of the sanctuary. You've crossed a line, and there's no honor here for you from the LORD God."

[19] Uzziah erupted. He was standing there with a censer in his hand, ready to burn incense, and as his rage boiled over against the priests, leprosy broke out on his forehead—right there in the LORD's house, in front of the priests, beside the incense altar.

[20] Azariah the chief priest and all the other priests spun around and saw it—the disease blooming on his forehead. They rushed him out. He himself bolted, because the LORD had struck him.

[21] King Uzziah lived as a leper until the day he died. He stayed in a separate house, cut off from the LORD's temple. His son Jotham ran the palace and governed the people of the land.

[22] The rest of Uzziah's reign, from beginning to end, was recorded by the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz.

[23] When Uzziah died, they buried him near his ancestors—but in a field belonging to the royal cemetery, not in the tombs themselves, because they said, "He had leprosy." And his son Jotham took the throne.

[1] Jotham was twenty-five when he took the throne, and he ruled in Jerusalem sixteen years. His mother was Jerushah, Zadok's daughter.

[2] He did what was right in the LORD's eyes — everything his father Uzziah had done, except he never broke into the temple. But the people kept right on with their corruption.

[3] He rebuilt the Upper Gate of the LORD's house and did extensive work on the wall at Ophel.

[4] He built cities throughout the Judean hill country, and fortresses and watchtowers in the wooded country.

[5] He went to war against the king of the Ammonites and crushed them. That year the Ammonites paid him a hundred talents of silver, ten thousand cors of wheat, and ten thousand of barley. They paid the same the second year and the third.

[6] Jotham grew strong because he ordered his life before the LORD his God.

[7] The rest of Jotham's story — every war he fought, everything he did — is written in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah.

[8] He was twenty-five when he took the throne, and he ruled in Jerusalem sixteen years.

[9] Jotham slept with his ancestors, and they buried him in the City of David. His son Ahaz took the throne after him.

[1] Ahaz was twenty when he took the throne, and he ruled in Jerusalem for sixteen years. Unlike his ancestor David, he did nothing right in the LORD's eyes.

[2] He walked the road of Israel's kings. He cast metal images for the Baals.

[3] He burned incense in the Valley of Ben-Hinnom and threw his own sons into the fire — the exact atrocity the LORD had driven the nations out for committing.

[4] He sacrificed and burned offerings at the high places, on the hilltops, under every leafy tree.

[5] So the LORD his God handed him over. The king of Aram crushed him, hauling off a vast crowd of captives to Damascus. Then Israel's king struck him too — a slaughter on a massive scale.

[6] Pekah son of Remaliah killed 120,000 Judean fighting men in a single day — every one of them a warrior — because they had abandoned the LORD, the God of their fathers.

[7] Zikri, an Ephraimite champion, killed the king's son Maaseiah, the palace administrator Azrikam, and Elkanah, second-in-command to the king.

[8] The Israelites carried off 200,000 of their own kinsmen — wives, sons, daughters — and looted enormous spoil, which they hauled back to Samaria.

[9] But a prophet of the LORD was there, a man named Oded. He went out to meet the returning army at Samaria and confronted them: "Look — the LORD, the God of your fathers, was furious with Judah, so he handed them to you. But you butchered them in a rage that reaches heaven.

[10] And now you're planning to enslave the people of Judah and Jerusalem — men and women — as your property? Tell me you don't carry your own guilt before the LORD your God.

[11] Listen to me. Send these captives back. They are your brothers. The LORD's fury is burning toward you."

[12] Then several leaders of Ephraim — Azariah son of Jehohanan, Berekiah son of Meshillemoth, Jehizkiah son of Shallum, and Amasa son of Hadlai — stood up against those returning from battle.

[13] "Don't bring those captives in here," they said. "You want to add to our guilt before the LORD on top of everything we've already done? Our guilt is already huge. His fury already burns against Israel."

[14] So the soldiers released the captives and the plunder in front of the officers and the whole assembly.

[15] The men named by name stood up, took the captives, and from the plunder they clothed everyone who was naked. They gave them clothing and sandals, fed them, gave them water, dressed their wounds with oil. The weak they put on donkeys. They led them all back to their own people at Jericho, the City of Palms. Then they returned to Samaria.

[16] During this time King Ahaz sent for help — to the kings of Assyria.

[17] The Edomites had come again and struck Judah and taken captives.

[18] The Philistines were raiding the towns of the Shephelah and the Negev — they had seized Beth Shemesh, Aijalon, Gederoth, Soco and its villages, Timnah and its villages, Gimzo and its villages — and they settled in.

[19] The LORD was bringing Judah low because of Ahaz, king of Israel, who had let the people run wild and had been utterly faithless to the LORD.

[20] Tiglath-Pileser, king of Assyria, did come — but instead of helping, he tightened the noose.

[21] Ahaz had stripped the temple of the LORD, the royal palace, and the houses of his officials to pay off the Assyrian king. None of it helped.

[22] In the very middle of his agony, this same King Ahaz only deepened his betrayal of the LORD.

[23] He sacrificed to the gods of Damascus, the gods who had defeated him. He said, "The gods of Aram's kings helped them — I'll sacrifice to them so they'll help me." Those gods were his undoing, and the undoing of all Israel.

[24] Ahaz gathered up the vessels of God's house and smashed them. He shut the doors of the LORD's temple. He set up altars on every corner of Jerusalem.

[25] In every town in Judah he built high places to burn incense to other gods — and he provoked the LORD, the God of his fathers, to fury.

[26] The rest of his deeds, every one of his ways, from first to last — they're written in the book of the kings of Judah and Israel.

[27] Ahaz slept with his ancestors. They buried him in Jerusalem — but not in the tombs of Israel's kings. His son Hezekiah took the throne in his place.

[1] Hezekiah took the throne at twenty-five and ruled twenty-nine years in Jerusalem. His mother was Abijah, Zechariah's daughter.

[2] He did what was right in the LORD's eyes — everything his ancestor David had done.

[3] First month, first year of his reign — he threw open the doors of the LORD's house and repaired them.

[4] He gathered the priests and Levites, assembled them in the eastern square,

[5] and said: "Listen to me, Levites. Consecrate yourselves now. Consecrate this house — the house of the LORD, the God of your fathers. Clear the filth out of the sanctuary.

[6] Our fathers betrayed him. They did evil in the eyes of the LORD our God. They abandoned him. They turned their faces away from the LORD's dwelling and gave him their backs.

[7] They shut the doors of the portico. They snuffed out the lamps. They stopped burning incense. They stopped offering sacrifices in the holy place to the God of Israel.

[8] So the LORD's wrath fell on Judah and Jerusalem. He made us a horror, an object of shock and contempt — you see it with your own eyes.

[9] That's why our fathers fell by the sword. That's why our sons, daughters, and wives are in captivity.

[10] Now I intend to make a covenant with the LORD, the God of Israel, so his burning anger turns away from us.

[11] My sons, don't waste another moment. The LORD chose you to stand before him, to serve him, to be his ministers and burn incense to him."

[12] So the Levites rose up: from the Kohathites — Mahath son of Amasai, Joel son of Azariah; from the Merarites — Kish son of Abdi, Azariah son of Jehallelel; from the Gershonites — Joah son of Zimmah, Eden son of Joah;

[13] from the sons of Elizaphan — Shimri and Jeiel; from the sons of Asaph — Zechariah and Mattaniah;

[14] from the sons of Heman — Jehiel and Shimei; from the sons of Jeduthun — Shemaiah and Uzziel.

[15] They rallied their brothers, consecrated themselves, and entered to purify the LORD's house — just as the king commanded, following the words of the LORD.

[16] The priests went into the interior of the LORD's house to cleanse it. Every defiled thing they found inside the temple, they hauled out to the courtyard. The Levites took it from there and carried it to the Kidron Valley.

[17] They began the consecration on the first day of the first month. By the eighth day they reached the LORD's portico. They consecrated the LORD's house for another eight days, finishing on the sixteenth of the first month.

[18] Then they came before King Hezekiah and reported: "We've cleansed the entire house of the LORD — the altar of burnt offering with all its utensils, the table of the bread of the Presence with all its utensils.

[19] Every article King Ahaz threw out during his reign of betrayal — we've restored them, consecrated them. They're standing now before the altar of the LORD."

[20] Hezekiah moved fast. The next morning he gathered the city's officials and went up to the LORD's house.

[21] They brought seven bulls, seven rams, seven lambs, and seven male goats — a sin offering for the kingdom, for the sanctuary, for Judah. He commanded the priests, Aaron's descendants, to offer them on the LORD's altar.

[22] They slaughtered the bulls; the priests caught the blood and splashed it against the altar. They slaughtered the rams and splashed the blood against the altar. They slaughtered the lambs and splashed the blood against the altar.

[23] Then they brought the goats for the sin offering forward, before the king and the assembly, and laid their hands on them.

[24] The priests slaughtered them and used the blood as a sin offering on the altar to make atonement for all Israel — because the king had ordered the burnt offering and sin offering for all Israel.

[25] He stationed the Levites in the LORD's house with cymbals, harps, and lyres — exactly as David had commanded, along with Gad the king's seer and Nathan the prophet. This was the LORD's command, given through his prophets.

[26] The Levites stood ready with David's instruments, and the priests with the trumpets.

[27] Hezekiah gave the command: offer the burnt offering on the altar. The moment the offering began, the song of the LORD broke out — trumpets blaring, instruments of David, king of Israel, joining in.

[28] The whole assembly worshiped. The singers sang. The trumpets sounded. It went on until the burnt offering was complete.

[29] When the offering was finished, the king and everyone with him dropped to their knees and worshiped.

[30] King Hezekiah and the officials told the Levites to praise the LORD using the words of David and Asaph the seer. They praised with joy, then bowed down and worshiped.

[31] Then Hezekiah said: "Now you've consecrated yourselves to the LORD. Come close. Bring sacrifices and thank offerings to the house of the LORD." The assembly brought sacrifices and thank offerings — and everyone whose heart moved them brought burnt offerings too.

[32] The count of burnt offerings the assembly brought: seventy bulls, a hundred rams, two hundred lambs — all of them for burnt offerings to the LORD.

[33] The consecrated offerings: six hundred bulls and three thousand sheep.

[34] But the priests were too few — they couldn't skin all the burnt offerings. So their brothers the Levites helped them until the work was done and more priests had consecrated themselves. The Levites had been more conscientious about consecration than the priests.

[35] Burnt offerings were abundant, with the fat of the peace offerings and the drink offerings that accompanied them. The service of the LORD's house was reestablished.

[36] Hezekiah rejoiced — and so did all the people — over what God had done for the nation. Because it all happened so suddenly.

[1] Hezekiah sent word throughout Israel and Judah, and he wrote letters to Ephraim and Manasseh as well, summoning everyone to the house of the LORD in Jerusalem to keep Passover to the LORD, the God of Israel.

[2] The king, his officials, and the whole assembly in Jerusalem had decided to hold Passover in the second month —

[3] they could not do it at the regular time because not enough priests had consecrated themselves, and the people had not yet gathered in Jerusalem.

[4] The plan looked right to the king and to the whole assembly,

[5] so they made the decree and sent it ringing through Israel from Beersheba to Dan: come to Jerusalem and keep the Passover of the LORD, the God of Israel. For too long they had not kept it the way it was written.

[6] Couriers went out across all Israel and Judah with letters from the king and his officials, and this was the message: "People of Israel, turn back to the LORD, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel — turn back, and he will turn back to the remnant of you who escaped the hand of the kings of Assyria.

[7] Don't be like your fathers and your brothers, who broke faith with the LORD, the God of their fathers, and he handed them over to ruin — you can see it with your own eyes.

[8] Don't stiffen your necks the way they did. Surrender to the LORD. Come into his sanctuary, the one he has set apart forever. Serve the LORD your God, and his burning anger will turn away from you.

[9] If you come back to the LORD, your brothers and your children will find mercy from those who took them captive, and they will come home to this land. The LORD your God is gracious and merciful; he will not turn his face away from you if you turn back to him."

[10] The couriers swept from town to town through Ephraim and Manasseh, all the way to Zebulun — and people laughed in their faces and mocked them.

[11] But some — men from Asher, Manasseh, and Zebulun — humbled themselves and came to Jerusalem.

[12] In Judah the hand of God was on the people, giving them one heart to do what the king and the officials had commanded — according to the word of the LORD.

[13] A massive crowd gathered in Jerusalem to keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread in the second month — an enormous assembly.

[14] They got to work: they tore down the altars in Jerusalem, hauled away every incense altar, and threw them into the Kidron Valley.

[15] Then on the fourteenth day of the second month they slaughtered the Passover lamb. The priests and Levites — stung into shame — consecrated themselves and brought burnt offerings into the house of the LORD.

[16] They took their stations as the law of Moses, the man of God, had laid down. The priests dashed the blood, which they received from the Levites' hands.

[17] Many in the assembly had not consecrated themselves, so the Levites took on the killing of the Passover lambs for everyone who was not clean, to consecrate them to the LORD.

[18] A large portion of the people — many from Ephraim, Manasseh, Issachar, and Zebulun — had not purified themselves; they ate the Passover anyway, against what was written. But Hezekiah prayed for them: "May the LORD, who is good, pardon

[19] everyone who has set his heart to seek God — the LORD, the God of his fathers — even if he is not clean by the standards of the sanctuary."

[20] And the LORD heard Hezekiah and healed the people.

[21] The Israelites who had come to Jerusalem kept the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days with overwhelming joy, and day after day the Levites and priests praised the LORD with the loud instruments of the LORD.

[22] Hezekiah spoke straight to the heart of every Levite who showed real skill in the service of the LORD. So they ate through the seven-day feast, offering peace offerings and giving thanks to the LORD, the God of their fathers.

[23] Then the whole assembly decided together to keep it seven more days — and they did, seven more days of joy.

[24] Hezekiah king of Judah donated a thousand bulls and seven thousand sheep to the assembly, and the officials donated another thousand bulls and ten thousand sheep. Priests consecrated themselves in great numbers.

[25] The whole assembly of Judah rejoiced — priests, Levites, the entire crowd that had come from Israel, and the foreigners who had come from Israel or were living in Judah.

[26] Jerusalem was flooded with joy. Nothing like this had happened in the city since the days of Solomon son of David, king of Israel.

[27] Then the Levitical priests stood and blessed the people, and their voice was heard, and their prayer rose to his holy dwelling — to heaven.

[1] When the whole thing wrapped, every Israelite who had come streamed out into the towns of Judah and went to work — smashing the sacred pillars, hacking down the Asherah poles, leveling the high places and altars across Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh. They demolished it all. Then the people went home, each to his own property, each to his own town.

[2] Hezekiah reinstated the priestly and Levitical divisions — each man assigned to his function, whether priest or Levite — for burnt offerings, fellowship offerings, ministry, thanksgiving, and praise at the gates of the LORD's camp.

[3] He committed a portion of his own wealth to the burnt offerings — morning and evening, Sabbaths, new moons, and the appointed feasts — exactly as the Law of the LORD prescribed.

[4] He commanded the people of Jerusalem to give the priests and Levites their rightful share, so they could pour themselves fully into the Law of the LORD.

[5] The moment the word went out, the Israelites responded with an avalanche of generosity — the firstfruits of grain, new wine, olive oil, honey, and every crop of the field. They brought in a tithe of everything, mountains of it.

[6] The Israelites and Judahites living in the towns of Judah also brought the tithe of cattle and sheep, plus a tithe of the holy things consecrated to the LORD their God — and they stacked it all in heaps.

[7] They started the heaps in the third month and finished in the seventh.

[8] When Hezekiah and his officials came and saw the heaps, they blessed the LORD and blessed his people Israel.

[9] Hezekiah questioned the priests and Levites about the heaps,

[10] and Azariah the chief priest, from the house of Zadok, answered him: "From the moment people started bringing their gifts to the house of the LORD, we have eaten our fill and there is still this much left over. The LORD has blessed his people, and what you see here is what remains."

[11] So Hezekiah ordered storerooms to be prepared in the house of the LORD, and they were prepared.

[12] Then they brought in the contributions, tithes, and consecrated gifts — faithfully. Conaniah the Levite was put in charge, with his brother Shimei as his second.

[13] Under their oversight worked Jehiel, Azaziah, Nahath, Asahel, Jerimoth, Jozabad, Eliel, Ismakiah, Mahath, and Benaiah — all appointed by King Hezekiah and Azariah, the official in charge of God's house.

[14] Kore son of Imnah the Levite, the gatekeeper of the East Gate, was over the freewill offerings to God, distributing the contributions of the LORD and the most sacred things.

[15] Under him — faithful in their assignments in the priestly cities — were Eden, Miniamin, Jeshua, Shemaiah, Amariah, and Shecaniah. They handed out portions to their fellow priests by division, to old and young alike.

[16] This included the males three years old and up listed in the genealogies — anyone who entered the house of the LORD to perform his daily duties according to his service and division.

[17] The priests were registered by family, the Levites from age twenty and up by their duties and divisions.

[18] The registration covered all the little ones, wives, sons, and daughters of the whole community — because they had faithfully consecrated themselves.

[19] As for the descendants of Aaron — the priests living on the pastureland surrounding their towns — designated men in every town distributed portions to every male among the priests and to every Levite registered in the genealogies.

[20] This is what Hezekiah did throughout Judah. He did what was good, right, and faithful before the LORD his God.

[21] Every project he took on — service in God's house, the Law, the commands — he threw himself into it with everything he had, chasing after his God. And he succeeded.

[1] After everything Hezekiah had done with such faithfulness, Sennacherib king of Assyria came. He marched into Judah, surrounded the fortified cities, and made plans to crack them open and take them for himself.

[2] When Hezekiah saw Sennacherib coming, saw that the man had set his face toward Jerusalem for war,

[3] he sat down with his officers and his warriors and decided to cut off the water supply outside the city. They backed him.

[4] A massive workforce assembled and they stopped up every spring, every stream flowing through the land. "Why should the kings of Assyria come and find water in abundance?" they said.

[5] Then Hezekiah went to work. He rebuilt every broken section of the wall, raised towers along it, threw up a second wall outside the first, reinforced the Millo in the City of David, and forged weapons and shields by the thousands.

[6] He appointed military commanders over the people, gathered them in the open square at the city gate, and spoke directly to their hearts:

[7] "Be strong. Be brave. Do not flinch before the king of Assyria or the horde he brings — because the one with us is greater than the one with him.

[8] He has an arm of flesh. We have the LORD our God to fight our battles and stand at our side." And the people leaned hard into the words of Hezekiah king of Judah.

[9] Then Sennacherib king of Assyria — while he himself was still besieging Lachish with the full weight of his army — sent his servants to Jerusalem, to Hezekiah king of Judah and to every man of Judah there, with this message:

[10] "This is what Sennacherib king of Assyria says: What are you trusting in, that you sit here under siege in Jerusalem?

[11] Hezekiah is leading you to die — by famine, by thirst — when he tells you, 'The LORD our God will rescue us from the king of Assyria.'

[12] Isn't this the same Hezekiah who tore down the LORD's own high places and altars, who told Judah and Jerusalem, 'You will worship at one altar and burn incense only there'?

[13] Don't you know what I and my fathers have done to every people of every land? Were any of the gods of those nations able to save their lands from my hand?

[14] Name one — from all the gods of all the nations my fathers wiped out — one who could rescue his people from me. So why would your god rescue you?

[15] Don't let Hezekiah deceive you. Don't let him fool you like this. Don't believe a word of it — because no god of any nation or kingdom has ever been able to save his people from me or from my fathers. How much less your god, from my hand?"

[16] And his servants kept piling on, speaking against the LORD God and against his servant Hezekiah.

[17] Sennacherib even wrote letters to insult the LORD, the God of Israel, mocking him: "Just as the gods of the nations of the earth did not rescue their people from my hand, neither will the god of Hezekiah rescue his people from my hand."

[18] Then they shouted it out in Hebrew at the top of their lungs to the people of Jerusalem standing on the wall — to frighten them, to terrify them, to break the city without a fight.

[19] They spoke of the God of Jerusalem the way they spoke of the gods of the peoples of the earth — handmade things, the work of human fingers.

[20] So King Hezekiah and the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz prayed about this. They cried out to heaven.

[21] And the LORD sent an angel. He cut down every warrior, every commander, every officer in the camp of the king of Assyria. Sennacherib went home in disgrace, back to his own land. And when he entered the house of his god, his own sons — flesh of his flesh — struck him down with the sword right there.

[22] So the LORD saved Hezekiah and the people of Jerusalem from the hand of Sennacherib king of Assyria, and from every enemy. He gave them rest on every side.

[23] Many brought gifts to the LORD in Jerusalem and treasures to Hezekiah king of Judah. From that moment on, he was exalted in the eyes of all the nations.

[24] In those days Hezekiah fell sick — sick to the point of death. He prayed to the LORD, and the LORD answered him and gave him a sign.

[25] But Hezekiah did not return the favor for what had been done for him. His heart swelled with pride. So wrath came upon him and upon Judah and Jerusalem.

[26] Then Hezekiah humbled himself for the pride of his heart — he and the people of Jerusalem together — and the wrath of the LORD did not fall on them during the days of Hezekiah.

[27] Hezekiah had wealth and honor beyond measure. He built himself treasuries for silver, for gold, for precious stones, for spices, for shields, for every kind of beautiful thing.

[28] He built storehouses for grain, for new wine, for oil — stalls for every kind of livestock, pens for the flocks.

[29] He built cities for himself, and he had flocks and herds in abundance, because God had given him enormous wealth.

[30] This same Hezekiah blocked the upper outlet of the Gihon spring and channeled the water straight down to the west side of the City of David. Everything he set his hand to, he succeeded.

[31] Even when envoys came from the rulers of Babylon to ask about the sign that had happened in the land, God left him to himself — to test him, to see everything that was in his heart.

[32] The rest of Hezekiah's story, and his acts of loyal love — it is all written in the vision of Isaiah the prophet, son of Amoz, and in the book of the kings of Judah and Israel.

[33] Hezekiah lay down with his fathers, and they buried him on the upper slope, where the tombs of David's descendants are. All Judah and the people of Jerusalem gave him honor at his death. And Manasseh his son ruled after him.

[1] Manasseh was twelve when he took the throne, and he ruled fifty-five years in Jerusalem.

[2] He did what the LORD calls evil — the same vile practices of the nations the LORD had driven out before Israel.

[3] He rebuilt the high places his father Hezekiah had torn down. He raised altars to the Baals, planted Asherah poles, and bowed before the whole host of heaven, serving them.

[4] He built altars inside the LORD's own house — the place God had said, "My name will live in Jerusalem forever."

[5] In both courtyards of the temple, he built altars to every star in the sky.

[6] He passed his own sons through fire in the Valley of Ben-Hinnom. He read omens, practiced sorcery, dabbled in witchcraft, consulted mediums and spiritists. He poured evil into the LORD's sight until it overflowed, and he provoked him to fury.

[7] He took a carved idol he had made and set it in the house of God — the very house God had told David and his son Solomon: "In this house, in Jerusalem, the city I chose from all the tribes of Israel, I will plant my name forever.

[8] I will never again drag Israel's feet from the land I assigned their fathers — if only they will do everything I commanded through Moses: the whole law, the statutes, the rulings."

[9] But Manasseh seduced Judah and Jerusalem off the path. They became more depraved than the nations the LORD had wiped out before the Israelites.

[10] The LORD spoke to Manasseh and his people. They refused to listen.

[11] So the LORD brought the Assyrian commanders down on them. They hooked Manasseh, shackled him in bronze, and dragged him to Babylon.

[12] In his agony he begged the LORD his God for mercy. He humbled himself — deeply — before the God of his ancestors.

[13] He prayed, and God listened. God heard his plea and brought him back to Jerusalem, back to his throne. That was when Manasseh knew: the LORD is God.

[14] Afterward he built an outer wall for the City of David, west of the Gihon spring in the valley, running up to the Fish Gate and circling Ophel — he built it high. And he stationed military commanders in every fortified city of Judah.

[15] He hauled the foreign gods and the idol out of the LORD's house. He tore down every altar he had built on the temple mount and across Jerusalem and dumped them outside the city.

[16] He restored the LORD's altar and offered fellowship and thanksgiving sacrifices on it. He ordered Judah to serve the LORD, the God of Israel.

[17] The people still sacrificed at the high places — but only to the LORD their God.

[18] The rest of Manasseh's story — his prayer to his God, the words of the seers who spoke to him in the name of the LORD, the God of Israel — it's all in the records of Israel's kings.

[19] His prayer, how God was moved by it, every sin, every act of treachery, the locations where he built high places and set up Asherah poles and idols before he humbled himself — it's written in the records of the seers.

[20] Manasseh joined his ancestors in death and was buried at his palace. His son Amon took the throne.

[21] Amon was twenty-two when he became king, and he ruled two years in Jerusalem.

[22] He did what the LORD calls evil, just like his father Manasseh. He sacrificed to every idol Manasseh had made, and he worshiped them.

[23] But he never humbled himself before the LORD the way his father finally had. He just kept piling on guilt.

[24] His own officials conspired against him and killed him in his palace.

[25] Then the people of the land struck down everyone who had plotted against King Amon, and they made his son Josiah king in his place.

[1] Josiah was eight years old when he took the throne, and he reigned in Jerusalem for thirty-one years.

[2] He did what was right in the LORD's eyes. He walked the road his ancestor David walked — never veering right, never veering left.

[3] In the eighth year of his reign, while he was still a boy, he began to seek the God of his ancestor David. In the twelfth year he started the cleansing — scrubbing Judah and Jerusalem clean of the high places, the Asherah poles, the carved gods, the cast-metal idols.

[4] They tore down the Baal altars right in front of him. He smashed the incense altars perched above them. He shattered the Asherah poles, the carved images, the cast idols — pulverized them, then scattered the dust over the graves of the people who had sacrificed to them.

[5] He burned the bones of their priests on their own altars. That is how he purged Judah and Jerusalem.

[6] Then he carried the purge into the cities of Manasseh, Ephraim, Simeon — all the way to Naphtali — and into the ruins surrounding them.

[7] He demolished the altars, ground the Asherah poles and idols to powder, hacked down every incense altar across the whole land of Israel. Then he came back to Jerusalem.

[8] In the eighteenth year of his reign, after he had purified the land and the temple, he sent Shaphan son of Azaliah, Maaseiah the governor of the city, and Joah son of Joahaz the recorder, to repair the house of the LORD his God.

[9] They came to Hilkiah the high priest and handed over the money that had been brought into God's house — money the Levite gatekeepers had collected from Manasseh and Ephraim, from the remnant of Israel, from all Judah and Benjamin, and from everyone living in Jerusalem.

[10] They put it into the hands of the foremen overseeing the work on the LORD's house, and these men paid the workers doing the restoration and repair —

[11] paid the carpenters and builders to buy quarried stone and timber for joists, to put beams on the buildings the kings of Judah had let fall into ruin.

[12] The men worked with integrity. Over them were Jahath and Obadiah, Levites from the line of Merari, and Zechariah and Meshullam from the line of Kohath — all of them supervising. The Levites — every one skilled with instruments —

[13] also kept watch over the haulers and directed every worker, whatever the task. Some of the Levites served as scribes, officers, and gatekeepers.

[14] As they were pulling out the money brought into the LORD's house, Hilkiah the priest found the Book of the Law of the LORD — the one given through Moses.

[15] Hilkiah turned to Shaphan the scribe: "I found the Book of the Law in the house of the LORD." And he handed it to Shaphan.

[16] Shaphan brought the book to the king and delivered his report: "Your servants are doing everything you put in their hands.

[17] They've emptied out the silver from the LORD's house and placed it in the hands of the overseers and workmen."

[18] Then Shaphan added, "Hilkiah the priest gave me a book." And he began to read it aloud before the king.

[19] When the king heard the words of the Law, he tore his clothes.

[20] He gave the order — to Hilkiah, to Ahikam son of Shaphan, to Abdon son of Micah, to Shaphan the scribe, and to Asaiah the king's servant:

[21] "Go. Inquire of the LORD for me, and for what is left of Israel and Judah, about the words of this book that has been found. The fury of the LORD poured out on us must be enormous — because our ancestors did not keep the word of the LORD, did not do what is written in this book."

[22] So Hilkiah and the king's men went to Huldah the prophetess, wife of Shallum son of Tokhath son of Hasrah, keeper of the wardrobe. She lived in Jerusalem, in the Second Quarter. They laid the matter before her.

[23] She said to them: "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — tell the man who sent you to me:

[24] This is what the LORD says. I am bringing disaster on this place and on the people who live here — every curse written in the book they read before the king of Judah.

[25] Because they abandoned me. Because they burned incense to other gods, provoking me to fury with everything their hands have made — my wrath is going to pour out on this place, and nothing will quench it.

[26] But to the king of Judah, who sent you to inquire of the LORD — tell him this: The LORD, the God of Israel, says about the words you heard —

[27] because your heart was tender and you humbled yourself before God when you heard his words against this place and its people, because you humbled yourself before me and tore your clothes and wept in my presence — I have heard you. This is the LORD's word.

[28] I am going to gather you to your ancestors. You will be laid in your tomb in peace. Your eyes will not see all the disaster I am bringing on this place and its people."

They carried her answer back to the king.

[29] The king sent word and assembled all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.

[30] He went up to the house of the LORD — every man of Judah, every resident of Jerusalem, the priests, the Levites, all the people, great and small. And he read in their hearing every word of the Book of the Covenant that had been found in the house of the LORD.

[31] The king stood in his place and cut the covenant before the LORD — to follow the LORD, to keep his commands, his testimonies, his statutes, with his whole heart and his whole soul, to perform the words of the covenant written in this book.

[32] And he made everyone in Jerusalem and Benjamin take their stand with it. The people of Jerusalem lived by the covenant of God, the God of their ancestors.

[33] Josiah swept every detestable thing out of every territory belonging to the people of Israel. He required everyone in Israel to serve the LORD their God. And as long as he lived, they did not turn away from following the LORD, the God of their ancestors.

[1] Josiah threw open Passover to the LORD in Jerusalem, and they slaughtered the Passover lamb on the fourteenth day of the first month.

[2] He stationed the priests at their posts and rallied them for service in the house of the LORD.

[3] To the Levites — the ones who taught all Israel, the consecrated ones — he said: "Place the sacred ark in the house Solomon son of David, king of Israel, built. No more hauling it on your shoulders. Now serve the LORD your God and his people Israel.

[4] Take your positions by ancestral house, by your divisions, following the written instructions of David king of Israel and the writings of his son Solomon.

[5] Stand in the sanctuary by clans, organized so that for every ancestral house among your kin — the common people — there's a corresponding division of Levites.

[6] Slaughter the Passover lamb. Consecrate yourselves. Prepare it for your kin, doing exactly what the LORD spoke through Moses."

[7] Josiah donated from his own flocks for the common people: thirty thousand lambs and young goats for the Passover offerings, plus three thousand bulls — all of it from the king's own holdings.

[8] His officials gave freely too — to the people, to the priests, to the Levites. Hilkiah, Zechariah, and Jehiel, overseers of God's house, gave the priests twenty-six hundred Passover animals and three hundred bulls.

[9] Conaniah and his brothers Shemaiah and Nethanel, along with Hashabiah, Jeiel, and Jozabad — chiefs of the Levites — gave the Levites five thousand Passover animals and five hundred bulls.

[10] The service was set. The priests stood at their stations, the Levites in their divisions — exactly as the king had ordered.

[11] They slaughtered the Passover lamb. The priests caught the blood from the Levites' hands and splashed it; the Levites did the skinning.

[12] They set aside the burnt offerings to distribute by ancestral division to the common people, so they could present them to the LORD as written in the book of Moses. Same with the bulls.

[13] They roasted the Passover lamb over fire, by regulation, and boiled the consecrated portions in pots, cauldrons, and pans — then rushed it to all the people.

[14] After that, they prepared portions for themselves and for the priests, since the priests — descendants of Aaron — were busy past nightfall offering up the burnt offerings and the fat pieces. So the Levites prepared for themselves and for Aaron's sons.

[15] The Asaphite singers held their posts as David, Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun the king's seer had ordered. The gatekeepers stayed at each gate. None of them had to leave their service — their fellow Levites prepared for them.

[16] So the whole service of the LORD was set in motion that day — Passover kept, burnt offerings ascending on the LORD's altar — just as King Josiah commanded.

[17] The Israelites present celebrated the Passover at that time, and the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days.

[18] No Passover like this had been kept in Israel since the days of Samuel the prophet. None of the kings of Israel had ever pulled off a Passover like the one Josiah staged — with the priests, the Levites, all of Judah and Israel who were present, and the people of Jerusalem.

[19] This Passover happened in the eighteenth year of Josiah's reign.

[20] After all this — after Josiah had set the temple in order — Neco king of Egypt marched up to fight at Carchemish on the Euphrates. Josiah went out to intercept him.

[21] Neco sent messengers ahead: "What's between you and me, king of Judah? I'm not coming against you today. My war is with another house, and God has told me to hurry. Back off from the God who's with me, or he'll destroy you."

[22] But Josiah wouldn't turn aside. He disguised himself for battle. He refused to listen to what Neco said at God's command, and he went down to fight on the plain of Megiddo.

[23] The archers shot King Josiah. He told his servants, "Get me out — I'm badly wounded."

[24] They lifted him from his chariot, transferred him to another one, and brought him to Jerusalem. There he died. They buried him in the tombs of his ancestors, and all Judah and Jerusalem mourned for Josiah.

[25] Jeremiah composed a lament for him, and to this day all the singers — men and women — invoke Josiah in their laments. It became a tradition in Israel, written down in the Book of Laments.

[26] The rest of Josiah's story — his acts of devotion, performed by the book of the LORD's law —

[27] from start to finish, is recorded in the chronicle of the kings of Israel and Judah.

[1] The people of the land took Jehoahaz, son of Josiah, and made him king in Jerusalem in his father's place. [2] He was twenty-three when he took the throne, and he ruled in Jerusalem for three months. [3] Then the king of Egypt stripped him of his crown in Jerusalem and laid a tribute on the land — a hundred talents of silver, one talent of gold. [4] The king of Egypt installed Eliakim, the brother of Jehoahaz, as king over Judah and Jerusalem, and renamed him Jehoiakim. As for Jehoahaz — Neco seized him and dragged him off to Egypt.

[5] Jehoiakim was twenty-five when he took the throne, and he ruled in Jerusalem for eleven years. He did what was evil in the eyes of the LORD his God. [6] Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, marched against him, bound him in bronze shackles, and hauled him off to Babylon. [7] Nebuchadnezzar also carried away some of the vessels of the LORD's house and set them in his own palace in Babylon. [8] The rest of Jehoiakim's deeds — the abominations he committed, everything found against him — it is all written in the scroll of the kings of Israel and Judah. His son Jehoiachin took his place.

[9] Jehoiachin was eighteen when he took the throne, and he ruled in Jerusalem for three months and ten days. He did what was evil in the eyes of the LORD. [10] When the year turned, King Nebuchadnezzar sent for him and had him brought to Babylon, along with the treasures of the LORD's house. He set Zedekiah, a relative of Jehoiachin, on the throne over Judah and Jerusalem.

[11] Zedekiah was twenty-one when he took the throne, and he ruled in Jerusalem for eleven years. [12] He did what was evil in the eyes of the LORD his God. He would not bow himself before Jeremiah the prophet, who spoke straight from the mouth of the LORD. [13] He even revolted against King Nebuchadnezzar, who had made him swear loyalty by God. He stiffened his neck and hardened his heart against turning back to the LORD, the God of Israel. [14] And the leaders of the priests, and the people themselves — they piled treachery on treachery, following every disgusting practice of the nations, defiling the very house of the LORD that he had made holy in Jerusalem.

[15] The LORD, the God of their fathers, kept sending word to them through his messengers — again and again, tirelessly — because he had compassion on his people and on his dwelling place. [16] But they mocked God's messengers. They despised his words. They sneered at his prophets — until the fury of the LORD rose against his people, and there was no remedy left.

[17] So he brought up against them the king of the Babylonians, who cut down their young men with the sword inside the sanctuary itself, sparing no one — not the young man, not the young woman, not the old, not the gray-haired. God handed them all over. [18] Every vessel of God's house — large and small — and the treasures of the LORD's house and the king's treasures and his officials' — Nebuchadnezzar carted it all to Babylon. [19] They torched God's house. They tore down the walls of Jerusalem. They burned every palace to the ground. Every object of value — destroyed. [20] Those who survived the sword — he marched them off to Babylon, where they became slaves to him and his sons until the Persian kingdom rose to power. [21] All of this fulfilled what the LORD spoke through Jeremiah: the land would have its sabbaths. As long as it lay desolate, it kept sabbath — until seventy years were done.

[22] In the first year of Cyrus, king of Persia — to make good on the word of the LORD spoken through Jeremiah — the LORD stirred up the spirit of Cyrus, king of Persia, and he sent a proclamation through every corner of his kingdom, and also put it in writing:

[23] "This is what Cyrus, king of Persia, says: The LORD, the God of heaven, has handed me every kingdom on earth, and he has charged me to build him a house in Jerusalem, in Judah. Anyone among you who belongs to his people — may the LORD his God be with him. Let him go up."

[1] Paul — sent as an envoy of Christ Jesus by God's own choosing — and Timothy, our brother, to God's gathered people in Corinth, and to all the holy ones scattered across Achaia:

[2] Grace to you, and peace, from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

[3] Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ — the Father whose mercy runs deep, the God from whom every comfort flows.

[4] He meets us in every crushing moment, holds us through it, so that we can turn around and hold others through theirs — passing on the same comfort God poured into us.

[5] Here's the pattern: the more Christ's sufferings overflow into our lives, the more his comfort overflows right alongside them.

[6] When we're put through the wringer, it's for your healing and rescue. When we're carried through, it's so you can be carried through too — the same endurance, the same suffering, the same way out.

[7] Our hope for you stands rock-solid. We know that since you share the suffering, you'll share the comfort.

[8] Brothers and sisters, I won't keep you in the dark about what happened to us in Asia. The pressure crushed us beyond what we thought we could bear — we honestly didn't expect to come out alive.

[9] In our own minds, we'd already been handed the death sentence. But that was the point: to stop us from leaning on ourselves and force us to lean on the God who raises the dead.

[10] He pulled us out of a deadly trap. He'll do it again. And we've staked everything on the certainty that he'll keep doing it.

[11] You help us by praying — and when many voices lift up thanks on our behalf for the gift of our rescue, the praise multiplies.

[12] Here's what we're proud of, and our conscience backs it up: we've conducted ourselves in this world — and especially toward you — with the simplicity and sincerity that comes from God. Not propped up by clever human strategy, but by sheer grace.

[13] We don't write you anything coded. What you read is what we mean — and I hope you'll grasp it fully,

[14] just as you've already grasped part of it. On the day the Lord Jesus returns, you'll have reason to be proud of us, and we of you.

[15] Banking on that confidence, I planned to come to you first — a double dose of grace —

[16] swinging through your city on the way to Macedonia, then circling back to you on the way home, so you could send me off to Judea.

[17] When I had to change those plans, was I being flaky? Do I make decisions like someone with no spine, saying yes and no in the same breath?

[18] As God is faithful — our word to you isn't yes and no.

[19] The Son of God, Jesus Christ — the one Silvanus, Timothy, and I preached to you — was never a wavering yes-and-no. In him it has always been Yes.

[20] Every promise God ever made finds its Yes in him. That's why through him we say Amen — to the glory of God.

[21] God is the one anchoring both us and you firmly in Christ. He's the one who anointed us,

[22] stamped his seal on us, and deposited his Spirit into our hearts as the down payment on everything still to come.

[23] I'll call God himself as witness — stake my life on it: the reason I haven't come back to Corinth is to spare you.

[24] Not that we're trying to control your faith — you're standing firm in that. We're working alongside you, for your joy.

[1] So I made up my mind: I would not come to you again carrying grief. [2] Because if I'm the one who wounds you, who's left to lift my spirits? Only the very people I just crushed. [3] That's exactly why I wrote what I wrote — I didn't want to show up and be devastated by the very ones who should have been my joy. I trust you enough to believe that my joy is your joy too. [4] I wrote that letter out of anguish, with a clenched heart and tears running down my face — not to break you, but so you'd know how fiercely I love you.

[5] If someone has caused pain, he hasn't really pained me — he's pained all of you, at least in part, and I don't want to pile it on. [6] The discipline the majority handed down has done its work. That's enough. [7] Now flip it: forgive him. Comfort him. Otherwise the man will drown in his own grief. [8] So I'm asking you — make love the public verdict. [9] Part of why I wrote was to test you, to see whether you'd follow through on everything. [10] Whoever you forgive, I forgive. And whatever I've forgiven — if there was anything to forgive — I did it for your sake, in the presence of Christ, [11] so Satan doesn't outmaneuver us. We know his playbook.

[12] When I got to Troas to preach the news of Christ, the Lord threw a door wide open for me — [13] but I couldn't rest. My spirit was restless because I hadn't found Titus, my brother. So I said my goodbyes and pushed on into Macedonia.

[14] But thank God — who hauls us along in Christ's triumphal parade and through us releases the fragrance of knowing him everywhere we go. [15] We are the aroma of Christ rising up to God, drifting through both the saved and the perishing — [16] to the one, the stench of death leading to more death; to the other, the breath of life leading to more life. And who's adequate for this? [17] We're not like the crowd hawking God's word for profit. We speak with integrity, sent from God, standing before God, in Christ.

[1] Are we starting up with self-promotion again? Do we need letters of recommendation to you—or from you—like some people do?

[2] You are our letter. Written on our hearts. Known and read by everyone who looks at us.

[3] You make it obvious: you are a letter from the Messiah, delivered through us. Not written in ink but with the Spirit of the living God. Not chiseled into stone tablets but inscribed on the living tissue of human hearts.

[4] That is the kind of confidence we carry before God through the Messiah.

[5] Not that we have anything in ourselves to draw from—as if any of this competence originated with us. Our competence comes from God.

[6] He is the one who made us competent to serve a new covenant—not the covenant of the written code, but of the Spirit. Because the written code kills. The Spirit gives life.

[7] Think about it: that old arrangement—death chiseled letter by letter into stone—came with such glory that the Israelites could not even look at Moses's face. His skin was radiant, though that radiance was already fading.

[8] If that ministry blazed, how much more will the ministry of the Spirit blaze?

[9] If the ministry that handed down condemnation had glory, the ministry that hands down righteousness overflows with glory beyond measure.

[10] In fact, what once seemed glorious has lost its glow entirely—eclipsed by something so much brighter.

[11] If what was fading came with glory, what endures comes with glory that will not quit.

[12] So with a hope like that, we speak with total boldness.

[13] We are not like Moses, who kept pulling a veil across his face so the Israelites would not watch the radiance fade out.

[14] But their minds went hard. And to this day, when the old covenant is read aloud, that same veil hangs in place. Unlifted. Because only in the Messiah does it come off.

[15] Even now, whenever Moses is read, a veil drapes their hearts.

[16] But the moment anyone turns to the Lord, the veil is yanked away.

[17] The Lord is the Spirit. And where the Spirit of the Lord is—freedom.

[18] All of us, with faces uncovered, catching the glory of the Lord like mirrors, are being transformed into that same image—glory feeding into glory—by the Lord, who is the Spirit.

[1] So since we've been handed this ministry — mercy, sheer mercy — we don't cave. [2] We've renounced the underhanded things people hide in shame. We don't operate by manipulation. We don't doctor God's word. We just lay the truth out in the open and let it commend us to every human conscience before God's own eyes.

[3] If our message is veiled, it's veiled to those already perishing — [4] the ones whose minds the god of this present age has blinded, so they can't see the light streaming from the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the very image of God. [5] We don't preach ourselves. We preach Jesus Christ as Lord, and ourselves as your servants for his sake. [6] Because the same God who said, "Let light blaze out of the darkness," has flooded our hearts with light — the radiance of knowing God's glory in the face of Jesus Christ.

[7] But we carry this treasure in jars of clay. That's the point: the staggering power belongs to God and not to us. [8] Crushed on every side — but not broken. Bewildered — but not in despair. [9] Hunted — but not abandoned. Struck down — but not destroyed. [10] We carry the dying of Jesus around in our bodies, every day, so that his life might also be made visible in us. [11] We who live are constantly being handed over to death for Jesus' sake, so that his life might break through this mortal flesh of ours. [12] So death does its work in us — and life does its work in you.

[13] But we have the same spirit of faith the psalm speaks of: "I believed, therefore I spoke." We too believe, and therefore we speak — [14] knowing that the One who raised the Lord Jesus will raise us with Jesus too, and bring us with you into his presence. [15] All of this is for your sake — so that grace, spreading to more and more people, will overflow into thunderous thanksgiving to the glory of God.

[16] So we don't lose heart. Even as our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed day after day. [17] This light, momentary affliction is forging for us a weight of glory beyond all comparison, beyond all measuring. [18] We fix our eyes not on what's seen but on what's unseen. Because what's seen is temporary. What's unseen is forever.

[1] We know this: if the tent we live in—this earthly body—gets torn down, we have a building from God. A house not made by human hands. Eternal. In the heavens.

[2] And right now we groan, aching to be clothed with our heavenly dwelling—

[3] because once we've put it on, we won't be found naked.

[4] While we're still in this tent, we groan under the weight of it. Not because we want to be stripped bare, but because we want to be further clothed—so that what is mortal gets swallowed up by life itself.

[5] God himself shaped us for exactly this, and he handed us the Spirit as the down payment.

[6] So we walk with confidence—always. We know that as long as we're at home in this body, we're away from the Lord.

[7] We move by faith, not by what we see.

[8] Yes, confident. And honestly? We'd rather pack up and leave this body to be at home with the Lord.

[9] So whether we're here or there, we have one ambition: to please him.

[10] Because every one of us will stand exposed before the judgment seat of Christ. Each will receive what's due—measured by what we did in this body, whether good or worthless.

[11] Knowing the fear of the Lord, we work to persuade people. What we are is laid bare before God—and I hope, laid bare to your conscience too.

[12] We're not recommending ourselves to you again. We're giving you ammunition—reason to be proud of us—so you have an answer for those who measure by appearance instead of by the heart.

[13] If we've lost our minds, it was for God. If we're level-headed, it's for you.

[14] The love of Christ has us in its grip. We've reached this conclusion: one died for all, therefore all died.

[15] And he died for all so that those who live would no longer live for themselves but for the one who died for them and was raised.

[16] So from now on, we evaluate no one by surface appearances. Even if we once sized up Christ that way, we don't anymore.

[17] If anyone is in Christ—new creation. The old has vanished. Look: everything is new.

[18] And all of it comes from God, who reconciled us to himself through Christ and handed us the work of reconciliation.

[19] Here it is: God was in Christ, drawing the world back to himself, refusing to count their sins against them, and entrusting to us the message of reconciliation.

[20] So we are Christ's ambassadors—God himself making his appeal through us. We plead on Christ's behalf: be reconciled to God.

[21] The one who never knew sin—God made him to be sin for us, so that in him we would become the righteousness of God.

[1] Working alongside him, we beg you: don't let the grace you've received from God amount to nothing. [2] He says it himself: *In the moment of favor I heard you, on the day of rescue I came to your aid.* Look — now is that moment of favor. Now is that day of rescue.

[3] We refuse to put a stumbling block in anyone's path. Nothing must discredit this work. [4] Instead, in every way we prove ourselves to be God's servants — through staggering endurance: in pressure, in hardship, in dead-end situations, [5] under the lash, in prison cells, through mob violence, in grinding labor, in nights without sleep, in days without food. [6] Through purity, through clear sight, through patience that won't break, through kindness, through the Holy Spirit, through love with no pretense, [7] through truthful speech, through the raw power of God — armed with righteousness in the right hand and the left, [8] whether the world honors us or spits on us, slanders us or sings our praises. They call us frauds, yet we speak only truth. [9] Treated as nobodies, yet known everywhere. Dying, and look — still alive. Beaten down, and not yet killed. [10] Heartbroken, yet always rejoicing. Penniless, yet making many rich. Owning nothing, yet holding everything.
