[11] Corinthians — we've spoken to you with nothing held back. Our heart is wide open. [12] You're not cramped in us. You've cramped yourselves inside your own affections. [13] So return the favor — I'm speaking to you as my children — open wide.

[14] Stop yoking yourselves to people who don't believe. What does righteousness share with lawlessness? What fellowship does light have with darkness? [15] What harmony exists between Christ and Belial? What does a believer have in common with someone who doesn't believe? [16] What agreement can God's temple reach with idols? And we — *we* — are the temple of the living God. As he himself said: *I will live among them and walk among them. I will be their God, and they will be my people.* [17] So: *Come out from them. Separate yourselves,* says the Lord. *Don't touch what is unclean, and I will welcome you in.* [18] *I will be a Father to you, and you will be sons and daughters to me, says the Lord, the All-Powerful.*

[1] So with promises like these in our hands, dear friends, let's scrub ourselves clean of everything that defiles body or spirit, completing the work of holiness because we stand in awe of God.

[2] Make room for us in your hearts. We've wronged no one, ruined no one, taken advantage of no one.

[3] I'm not saying this to condemn you—I already told you, you live in our hearts. We're in this together: living or dying, side by side.

[4] I speak to you with total candor. I boast about you constantly. I'm filled with encouragement. In all our suffering, joy floods over me.

[5] Even when we arrived in Macedonia, our bodies got no rest. Pressure hit us from every direction—battles outside, fears inside.

[6] But God, who comforts the downcast, comforted us by sending Titus—

[7] and not just by his arrival, but by the comfort he himself had drawn from you. He told us how you longed to see me, how you grieved, how fiercely you defended me. It made me happier than ever.

[8] Yes, my letter caused you pain, and I don't regret it—though I did regret it for a moment when I saw how deeply it cut you, even if only briefly.

[9] Now I'm glad. Not glad that you hurt, but glad that the hurt drove you to change. You felt the pain God intended, so we didn't damage you in any way.

[10] The grief God works in us produces a turnaround that leads to rescue—no regrets there. The world's kind of grief just kills you.

[11] Look at what this godly sorrow has done in you: an urgency, a clearing of your name, a fire of indignation, a holy fear, longing, zeal, a readiness to set things right. At every point, you've shown yourselves clean in this matter.

[12] So when I wrote, it wasn't really about the one who did the wrong or the one who was wronged. I wrote so that your devotion to us would stand exposed before God in your own sight.

[13] That's why we feel encouraged. And on top of our own encouragement, we were overjoyed to see how thrilled Titus was—you'd set his spirit at ease, all of you.

[14] I had bragged about you to him, and you didn't make me look foolish. Everything I told you was true, and everything I told Titus about you turned out true as well.

[15] His affection for you runs deeper than ever as he remembers how every one of you welcomed him—with reverence, with trembling, ready to obey.

[16] I'm overjoyed. I can trust you completely.

[1] Now, brothers, I want you to know about the grace God has poured out on the churches of Macedonia.

[2] In the middle of a brutal trial, their overflowing joy and their crushing poverty somehow combined to produce extravagant generosity.

[3] I'm a witness: they gave according to what they had — no, beyond what they had. And they did it entirely on their own.

[4] They actually begged us — pleaded — for the privilege of joining this ministry to God's people.

[5] And they went further than we ever expected. They gave themselves first to the Lord, and then to us, exactly as God wanted.

[6] So we urged Titus, since he started this work among you, to also bring it to completion — this act of grace.

[7] You excel at everything — faith, speech, knowledge, passion of every kind, and the love we kindled in you — so excel at this grace too.

[8] I'm not issuing orders. I'm just holding up the earnestness of others as a mirror to test whether your love is the real thing.

[9] You know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ: though he was rich, for your sake he became poor, so that through his poverty you might become rich.

[10] Here's my advice on this — and it's in your best interest: a year ago you were the first not only to act but to want to act.

[11] So finish what you started. Let the eagerness you showed at the beginning be matched by what you actually deliver, according to what you have.

[12] Because if the willingness is there, the gift is measured by what someone has — not by what they don't.

[13] The point isn't to relieve others by crushing you. The point is balance.

[14] Right now your abundance meets their need, so that one day their abundance can meet yours. That's balance.

[15] As it's written: "The one who gathered much had no surplus, and the one who gathered little had no shortage."

[16] Thanks be to God, who put into Titus's heart the same earnest concern I have for you.

[17] He welcomed our appeal — and more than that, he's coming to you on his own initiative, eager to do it.

[18] With him we're sending the brother whose work for the gospel has earned him praise across every church.

[19] Even more — he's been chosen by the churches to travel with us as we carry this gift, which we administer for the glory of the Lord himself and to show our eagerness to help.

[20] We're taking every precaution so that no one can criticize how we handle this generous offering.

[21] Because we're determined to do what's right — not just before the Lord, but before people too.

[22] And with them we're sending our brother, whose earnestness we've verified again and again, in many situations — and now even more so, because of his deep confidence in you.

[23] As for Titus — he's my partner, working alongside me for your good. As for the other brothers — they're representatives of the churches, the glory of Christ on display.

[24] So show them — out in the open, in front of all the churches — the proof of your love and the reason for our pride in you.

[1] About this relief effort for God's people in Jerusalem — honestly, I don't even need to write to you about it.

[2] I know your heart. I've been bragging about you to the Macedonians, telling them, "Achaia has been ready since last year." Your enthusiasm has lit a fire under most of them.

[3] But I'm sending the brothers anyway — just to make sure my boasting about you doesn't end up hollow on this one point. I want you actually ready, the way I've been claiming you are.

[4] Imagine if some Macedonians show up with me and find you unprepared. The embarrassment — I won't even say yours — would be ours, after we've talked you up so confidently.

[5] So I thought I had to send the brothers ahead to get your promised gift in order before I arrive. That way it stands as a genuine blessing, freely given — not something squeezed out of you under pressure.

[6] Here's the principle: scatter seed stingily and you'll harvest stingily. Scatter generously and you'll harvest generously.

[7] Each person should give what they've settled on in their own heart — not reluctantly, not under compulsion. God loves a giver who's genuinely glad to give.

[8] And God has the power to pour every kind of grace over you in abundance, so that in every situation, at every moment, you have everything you need — and overflow with more to pour into every good work.

[9] As Scripture says: *He scattered, he gave to the poor; his righteousness stands forever.*

[10] The One who supplies seed to the sower and bread for eating will supply and multiply your seed, and grow the harvest of your righteousness.

[11] You'll be made rich in every way so you can be generous in every way — and through us, that generosity will rise up as thanksgiving to God.

[12] Because this act of service doesn't just meet the needs of God's people — it spills over into a flood of gratitude reaching all the way to God.

[13] When they see the proof of your service, they will glorify God: for your faithful submission to the gospel of Christ that you confess, and for the open-handed generosity you've shown them and everyone else.

[14] And they'll pray for you, their hearts drawn out toward you because of the extraordinary grace of God resting on your lives.

[15] Thank God for his gift — a gift no words can hold.

[1] Now I, Paul, appeal to you personally—by the gentleness and kindness of Christ—I, the one who is "humble when face-to-face with you but bold from a distance," as they say.

[2] I'm begging you: don't force me to be bold when I arrive. Because I am ready to bring the full weight of my courage against those who think we operate by worldly motives.

[3] Yes, we live in the flesh. But we don't fight like the world fights.

[4] The weapons we wield aren't the weapons of this age. They carry divine power to demolish fortresses. We tear down arguments,

[5] we level every proud tower built against the knowledge of God, we take every thought captive and march it into obedience to Christ.

[6] And we stand ready to punish every act of disobedience the moment your own obedience is complete.

[7] Look at what's right in front of you. If someone is convinced he belongs to Christ, let him think again—because we belong to Christ just as surely as he does.

[8] Even if I boast a little more than necessary about the authority the Lord gave us—authority meant to build you up, not tear you down—I won't be ashamed of it.

[9] I don't want to seem like I'm trying to scare you with my letters.

[10] Because—so they say—"His letters are weighty and forceful, but in person he's unimpressive and his speech is nothing."

[11] Whoever says that should understand: what we are in our letters from a distance, we will be in our actions when we're there.

[12] We wouldn't dare put ourselves in the same league as those who promote themselves. They measure themselves by themselves, compare themselves with themselves—and miss the point entirely.

[13] We, however, will not boast beyond our limits. We'll stay within the field God himself marked out for us—a field that includes you.

[14] We're not overreaching when we count you in. We got all the way to you with the gospel of Christ. You're within our boundaries.

[15] We don't boast about other people's labor, claiming territory that isn't ours. Our hope is that as your faith matures, our sphere of work among you will expand dramatically,

[16] so we can carry the gospel into regions beyond you—without ever planting our flag in someone else's field and bragging about work already done.

[17] "Let the one who boasts boast in the Lord."

[18] Because the one who passes the test isn't the one who recommends himself. It's the one the Lord recommends.

[1] Bear with me in a little foolishness—please, bear with me. [2] I'm jealous over you with God's own jealousy. I promised you to one husband, to present you to Christ as a pure virgin. [3] But I'm afraid. Just as the serpent seduced Eve with his cunning, your minds might get pulled away from the pure, single-hearted devotion to Christ. [4] Because if someone shows up preaching a different Jesus than the one we preached, or offering a different spirit than the one you received, or a different gospel than the one you accepted—you put up with it just fine. [5] I don't think I rank one bit below these "super-apostles" of yours. [6] Maybe I'm no polished orator, but I'm not lacking in knowledge—and we've made that plain to you in every possible way.

[7] Was it a sin to humble myself so you could be lifted up? To preach God's gospel to you free of charge? [8] I robbed other churches—took their pay—just to serve you. [9] When I was with you and ran short, I didn't lean on anyone. The brothers from Macedonia covered what I lacked. I kept myself from being a burden to you, and I'll keep it that way. [10] As surely as Christ's truth lives in me, no one in the regions of Achaia is going to silence this boast of mine. [11] Why? Because I don't love you? God knows I do.

[12] And I'll keep doing what I'm doing—to cut the ground out from under those who want a chance to be seen as our equals in what they brag about. [13] These people are counterfeit apostles. Deceitful workers. Masquerading as apostles of Christ. [14] No surprise there. Satan himself masquerades as an angel of light. [15] So it's nothing remarkable if his servants masquerade as servants of righteousness. Their end will match their work.

[16] Let me say it again: don't take me for a fool. But if you do, then accept me as a fool—let me boast a little too. [17] What I'm about to say isn't from the Lord. I'm talking like a fool in this confident boasting. [18] Since plenty of people boast in worldly terms, I'll boast too. [19] After all, you're so wise—you happily put up with fools. [20] You tolerate it when someone enslaves you, devours you, exploits you, puts on airs, slaps you in the face. [21] To my shame, I admit—we were too weak for that.

But whatever anyone else dares to boast about—I'm talking like a fool now—I'll dare it too. [22] Are they Hebrews? So am I. Israelites? So am I. Descendants of Abraham? So am I. [23] Servants of Christ? I'm out of my mind to talk this way—I'm more. I've worked harder. Been in prison more often. Beaten beyond counting. Faced death again and again.

[24] Five times the Jews gave me forty lashes minus one. [25] Three times beaten with rods. Once stoned. Three times shipwrecked. A night and a day adrift on the open sea. [26] Constant journeys. Rivers threatening to drown me. Bandits threatening to kill me. Danger from my own people. Danger from Gentiles. Danger in the city. Danger in the wilderness. Danger at sea. Danger from false brothers. [27] Labor and exhaustion. Sleepless nights. Hunger and thirst. Going without food. Cold and exposure.

[28] And on top of all that—the daily pressure on me, my anxiety for every single church. [29] Who is weak, and I don't feel it? Who falls into sin, and I don't burn?

[30] If I have to boast, I'll boast about the things that show my weakness. [31] The God and Father of the Lord Jesus, who is blessed forever, knows I'm not lying. [32] In Damascus, the governor under King Aretas had the city locked down to arrest me, [33] and I was lowered in a basket through a window in the wall—and slipped through his fingers.

[1] I have to keep boasting. There's nothing to gain by it, but I'll move on to visions and revelations from the Lord.

[2] I know a man in Christ who, fourteen years ago, was caught up to the third heaven. Whether in the body or out of it, I cannot tell you. God knows.

[3] And I know that this man — in the body or apart from it, I cannot say, God knows —

[4] was seized and carried into paradise, where he heard words that cannot be spoken, things a human tongue is not permitted to repeat.

[5] About a man like that, I'll boast. About myself, I'll boast in nothing except my weaknesses.

[6] If I did want to boast, I wouldn't be a fool — I'd be telling the truth. But I hold back, so no one credits me with more than what they see in me or hear from me.

[7] And to keep me from inflating myself over the sheer magnitude of those revelations, I was given a thorn in the flesh — a messenger from Satan to batter me, to keep me from rising too high.

[8] Three times I begged the Lord to take it from me.

[9] His answer: "My grace is enough for you. Power reaches its full strength in weakness." So I'll boast all the more gladly about my weaknesses — so that the power of Christ can pitch its tent over me.

[10] That's why I take pleasure in weaknesses, in insults, in hardships, in persecutions, in being cornered — all for Christ. Because when I'm weak, that's when I'm strong.

[11] I've made a fool of myself. You forced me to it. You should have been the ones speaking up for me. Because I am in no way inferior to those "super-apostles" — even if I am nothing.

[12] The marks of a true apostle were on display among you — sustained endurance, signs, wonders, acts of power.

[13] What did you get less of than the other churches? Nothing — except that I never became a financial burden to you. Forgive me that injustice.

[14] Here I am, ready for a third visit, and I still won't be a burden. I'm not after what you have. I'm after you. Children don't save up for their parents — parents save up for their children.

[15] And I will gladly spend everything I have, and be spent myself, for your souls. If I love you more, am I to be loved less?

[16] Granted, I didn't lean on you. But — clever man that I am — I caught you by some trick? Is that the accusation?

[17] Did I exploit you through any of the men I sent your way?

[18] I urged Titus to go, and I sent the brother with him. Did Titus take advantage of you? Didn't we walk by the same Spirit, in the same footprints?

[19] You've been thinking all along that we're defending ourselves to you. No — we speak before God, in Christ. And everything, dear friends, is for building you up.

[20] Because I'm afraid that when I come, I won't find you the way I want, and you won't find me the way you want. I'm afraid of finding fights, jealousy, flares of temper, factions, slander, gossip, swollen egos, and chaos.

[21] I'm afraid that when I come back, my God will humble me again in front of you, and I'll be left grieving over many who sinned before and still haven't turned from the filth, the sexual immorality, the unrestrained appetites they've given themselves to.

[1] This is my third trip to you. Every charge stands or falls on the testimony of two or three witnesses. [2] I warned you when I was there the second time, and I'm warning you now while I'm away—those who kept sinning before, and everyone else: when I come back, I will not go easy on anyone. [3] You want proof that Christ speaks through me? You'll get it. He is not weak in dealing with you. He is powerful, right in the middle of you. [4] Yes, he was crucified in weakness—but he lives by the power of God. And we share that weakness in him, but you will see us alive with him, powered by God, when we deal with you.

[5] Take a hard look at yourselves. Are you actually in the faith? Test yourselves. Don't you know that Jesus Christ is in you—unless you fail the test? [6] I hope you'll discover that we haven't failed it. [7] We pray to God that you'll do no wrong—not so we can look vindicated, but so you'll do what's right, even if we end up looking like failures. [8] We can't move against the truth, only for it. [9] We're glad to be the weak ones if you're the strong ones. This is what we pray for: that you'll be made whole. [10] That's why I'm writing this from a distance—so that when I show up, I won't have to come down hard, using the authority the Lord gave me. He gave it to build you up, not tear you down.

[11] Final word, brothers and sisters: celebrate. Get yourselves in order. Listen to what I've said. Live in agreement. Live in peace. And the God of love and peace will be right there with you.

[12] Greet each other with a holy kiss. [13] Every one of God's people here sends their greetings.

[14] The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all.

[1] From the elder—to the chosen lady and her children, whom I love in the truth. And not only me—everyone who has come to know the truth loves you,

[2] because of the truth that lives in us and will be with us forever.

[3] Grace, mercy, and peace will be with us from God the Father and from Jesus Christ, the Father's Son—in truth and in love.

[4] I cannot tell you how glad I was to find some of your children walking in the truth, exactly as the Father commanded us.

[5] And now, dear lady, I'm asking you something—not a new command, just the one we've had from the start: let's love one another.

[6] This is what love is: walking according to his commands. And this is the command, as you heard it from the beginning: walk in it.

[7] Because many deceivers have gone out into the world—people who refuse to confess Jesus Christ coming in the flesh. This is the deceiver. This is the antichrist.

[8] Watch yourselves. Don't lose what we've worked for. Aim for the full reward.

[9] Anyone who runs ahead and doesn't stay in the teaching of Christ does not have God. Whoever stays in the teaching—that person has both the Father and the Son.

[10] If someone shows up and isn't bringing this teaching, don't welcome them into your house. Don't even greet them.

[11] Because to greet them is to share in their poisonous work.

[12] I have a lot more to say to you, but I'd rather not put it on paper with ink. I'm hoping to come see you and talk face to face—so our joy can be full.

[13] The children of your chosen sister send their greetings.

[1] After Ahab died, Moab broke from Israel.

[2] Ahaziah fell through the lattice of his upper room in Samaria and lay there, broken. So he sent messengers: "Go. Ask Baal-zebub, the god of Ekron, whether I'll recover."

[3] But the angel of the LORD spoke to Elijah the Tishbite: "Get up. Intercept the king's messengers from Samaria. Ask them: Is there no God in Israel, that you go inquiring of Baal-zebub, the god of Ekron?

[4] So this is what the LORD says: The bed you've climbed into — you will not climb out of it. You will die." And Elijah went.

[5] The messengers turned back to Ahaziah. "Why are you back already?" he asked.

[6] They said, "A man came up to meet us. He said: Go back to the king who sent you and tell him — this is what the LORD says: Is there no God in Israel, that you send to inquire of Baal-zebub, the god of Ekron? The bed you've climbed into — you will not climb out of it. You will die."

[7] Ahaziah demanded, "This man who came to meet you — what did he look like?"

[8] "A man wearing hair," they said, "with a leather belt cinched at his waist."

"Elijah the Tishbite," the king said.

[9] He dispatched a captain with fifty soldiers. The captain found Elijah sitting on a hilltop. "Man of God," he called up, "the king says: Come down."

[10] Elijah answered, "If I am a man of God, may fire fall from heaven and devour you and your fifty." And fire fell from heaven and devoured them — the captain and his fifty.

[11] The king sent another captain with another fifty. He called up, "Man of God, the king says: Come down. Now."

[12] Elijah answered, "If I am a man of God, may fire fall from heaven and devour you and your fifty." And the fire of God fell from heaven and devoured them — the captain and his fifty.

[13] The king sent a third captain with fifty men. This captain climbed the hill, fell on his knees before Elijah, and pleaded: "Man of God — please. Let my life and the lives of these fifty servants of yours mean something to you.

[14] Fire fell from heaven and devoured the first two captains with all their men. Let my life count for something now."

[15] The angel of the LORD said to Elijah, "Go down with him. Don't be afraid." So Elijah got up and went down with him to the king.

[16] He told Ahaziah, "This is what the LORD says: Because you sent messengers to inquire of Baal-zebub, the god of Ekron — as though there were no God in Israel whose word you could seek — therefore the bed you've climbed into, you will not climb out of. You will die."

[17] And he died, exactly as the LORD had spoken through Elijah. Since he had no son, Jehoram succeeded him in the second year of Jehoram son of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah.

[18] The rest of Ahaziah's reign — everything he did — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[1] The day came when the LORD was about to sweep Elijah up to heaven in a whirlwind. Elijah and Elisha were walking out of Gilgal together.

[2] Elijah said, "Stay here. The LORD is sending me on to Bethel."

Elisha answered, "As the LORD lives, as you yourself live—I will not leave you." So they went down to Bethel together.

[3] The company of prophets at Bethel came out to Elisha and said, "Do you know? Today the LORD is taking your master away from you."

"I know," he said. "Keep quiet about it."

[4] Then Elijah said, "Elisha, stay here. The LORD is sending me on to Jericho."

He answered, "As the LORD lives, as you yourself live—I will not leave you." So they went on to Jericho.

[5] The company of prophets at Jericho approached Elisha and said, "Do you know? Today the LORD is taking your master away from you."

"I know," he said. "Keep quiet about it."

[6] Elijah said to him, "Stay here. The LORD is sending me on to the Jordan."

He answered, "As the LORD lives, as you yourself live—I will not leave you." So the two of them kept walking.

[7] Fifty men from the company of prophets followed at a distance and stopped to watch as the two of them stood at the Jordan.

[8] Elijah took off his cloak, rolled it up, and struck the water. The river split right and left, and the two of them crossed over on dry ground.

[9] When they reached the other side, Elijah said to Elisha, "Ask. What do you want me to do for you before I am taken?"

Elisha said, "Let me inherit a double portion of your spirit."

[10] "You've asked a hard thing," Elijah said. "But if you see me when I'm taken from you, it will be yours. If not, it won't."

[11] They were still walking, still talking, when suddenly—a chariot of fire, horses of fire, splitting the air between them. And Elijah went up to heaven in the whirlwind.

[12] Elisha saw it. He cried out, "My father! My father! The chariots and horsemen of Israel!" And then he could see him no more. He took hold of his own clothes and tore them in two.

[13] He picked up the cloak Elijah had dropped, walked back, and stood at the bank of the Jordan.

[14] He took the cloak and struck the water. "Where is the LORD, the God of Elijah—where is he now?" And when he struck the water, it split right and left, and Elisha crossed over.

[15] The prophets from Jericho saw it from across the river and said, "The spirit of Elijah is resting on Elisha." They came out to meet him and bowed to the ground before him.

[16] They said, "Look, your servants—fifty strong men. Let them go search for your master. Maybe the Spirit of the LORD has carried him off and dropped him on some mountain or in some valley."

"No," he said. "Don't send them."

[17] But they pressed him until he was embarrassed to refuse. "Send them, then." They sent fifty men, who searched for three days and found nothing.

[18] When they returned to Elisha, who was staying in Jericho, he said, "Didn't I tell you not to go?"

[19] The men of the city came to Elisha. "Look, sir—our town sits in a good location, as you can see. But the water is poisoned, and the land miscarries."

[20] He said, "Bring me a new bowl. Put salt in it." They brought it to him.

[21] He walked out to the spring, threw the salt in, and said, "This is what the LORD says: I have healed this water. From it, no more death, no more barrenness."

[22] And the water has stayed pure to this day, just as Elisha said.

[23] From there he went up to Bethel. As he was walking up the road, a gang of boys came out of the town and started mocking him: "Go on up, baldy! Go on up, baldy!"

[24] He turned around, looked at them, and cursed them in the name of the LORD. Two bears came out of the woods and mauled forty-two of them.

[25] From there he went on to Mount Carmel, and from there back to Samaria.

[1] Joram, son of Ahab, took Israel's throne in Samaria in Jehoshaphat's eighteenth year as king of Judah, and he ruled for twelve years.

[2] He did evil in the LORD's eyes — though not like his father and mother. He tore down the sacred pillar of Baal his father had built.

[3] Still, he clung to the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat — the sins that dragged Israel down. He would not let them go.

[4] Mesha king of Moab raised sheep. Every year he paid Israel a tribute of a hundred thousand lambs and the wool of a hundred thousand rams.

[5] But the moment Ahab died, the king of Moab broke with Israel.

[6] So King Joram marched out of Samaria and mustered the whole of Israel.

[7] He sent word to Jehoshaphat king of Judah: "The king of Moab has rebelled against me. Will you march with me to fight him?" Jehoshaphat answered, "I'll go. My people are your people, my horses your horses."

[8] "Which way do we attack?" he asked. Joram said, "Through the wilderness of Edom."

[9] So the three kings set out — Israel, Judah, and Edom. They circled for seven days through the wasteland, and there was no water — none for the army, none for the animals trailing behind.

[10] The king of Israel groaned, "It's over. The LORD has dragged us three kings out here just to hand us to Moab."

[11] But Jehoshaphat said, "Is there no prophet of the LORD here? Through him we could ask the LORD." One of Israel's officers spoke up: "Elisha son of Shaphat is here — the one who used to pour water on Elijah's hands."

[12] Jehoshaphat said, "The word of the LORD is with him." So the three kings went down to him.

[13] Elisha looked at the king of Israel and said, "What do you want from me? Go to your father's prophets, your mother's prophets." Joram answered, "No — the LORD is the one who summoned us three kings out here to be handed over to Moab."

[14] Elisha said, "As surely as the LORD of Armies lives — the One I stand before — if it weren't for Jehoshaphat king of Judah, I wouldn't even look at you. I wouldn't see you.

[15] Now bring me a musician." And when the musician played, the hand of the LORD came on Elisha.

[16] He said: "This is what the LORD says — Dig this valley full of trenches. Dig.

[17] You won't see wind. You won't see rain. But this valley will fill with water, and you will drink — you, your livestock, your animals.

[18] And this is a small thing in the LORD's sight. He will also hand Moab over to you.

[19] You will smash every fortified city, every fine town. You will fell every good tree, stop up every spring, ruin every good field with stones."

[20] Morning came — the hour of the grain offering — and water came pouring in from the direction of Edom. The land flooded.

[21] Meanwhile every man in Moab who could lift a weapon had been called up and mustered at the border.

[22] At dawn, when the sun rose over the water, the Moabites looked across and the water in front of them flashed red as blood.

[23] "Blood!" they shouted. "The kings have turned on each other and slaughtered themselves. To the plunder, Moab!"

[24] But when they reached the Israelite camp, Israel rose up and cut them down. Moab fled, and Israel hunted them through their own land, killing as they went.

[25] They tore down the cities. They buried every good field under stones — every man hurling his stone. They stopped up every spring, felled every good tree. Only Kir Hareseth's stone walls were left standing, and the slingers ringed it and battered it too.

[26] The king of Moab saw the battle was lost. He took seven hundred swordsmen and tried to cut through to the king of Edom — but he couldn't break through.

[27] So he took his firstborn son — the one who was to reign after him — and offered him as a burnt sacrifice on the city wall. A great wrath came against Israel. They pulled back and returned to their own land.

[1] One of the wives in the prophets' guild came crying to Elisha: "Your servant my husband is dead. You know he revered the LORD. Now the creditor is here to seize my two boys as slaves."

[2] Elisha said, "What can I do for you? Tell me — what do you have in the house?" She said, "Your servant has nothing in the house. Just a flask of oil."

[3] He said, "Go. Borrow vessels from every neighbor you have. Empty ones. And not just a few.

[4] Then go inside, shut the door behind you and your sons, and start pouring oil into all those vessels. As each one fills, set it aside."

[5] So she left him. She shut the door behind herself and her sons, and they kept bringing the vessels to her while she poured.

[6] When the vessels were full, she said to her son, "Bring me another." He said, "There aren't any more." And the oil stopped.

[7] She went and told the man of God. He said, "Go. Sell the oil. Pay your debt. You and your sons can live on what's left."

[8] One day Elisha was passing through Shunem. A prominent woman there pressed him to stay for a meal. From then on, whenever he came through, he would stop in to eat.

[9] She said to her husband, "Listen — I'm certain the man who keeps passing this way is a holy man of God.

[10] Let's build a small room on the roof. We'll put a bed in it, a table, a chair, a lamp. When he comes, he'll have a place."

[11] So one day he came, went up to the room, and lay down.

[12] He said to Gehazi, his servant, "Call the Shunammite." Gehazi called her, and she stood before him.

[13] Elisha said to Gehazi, "Tell her this: 'You've gone to all this trouble for us. What can be done for you? Should we speak to the king on your behalf? Or the commander of the army?'" She answered, "I live among my own people. I'm fine."

[14] Later he said, "Then what can be done for her?" Gehazi said, "Well — she has no son. And her husband is old."

[15] "Call her," Elisha said. So he called her, and she stood in the doorway.

[16] Elisha said, "This time next year, you'll be holding a son in your arms." She said, "No — please, my lord, man of God, don't lie to your servant."

[17] But the woman conceived. And she bore a son the next year at that very season, just as Elisha had said.

[18] The boy grew. One day he went out to his father, who was with the harvesters.

[19] Suddenly he cried, "My head — my head!" The father told a servant, "Carry him to his mother."

[20] He carried him in. The boy sat on his mother's lap until noon — then he died.

[21] She carried him up and laid him on the bed of the man of God. She shut the door and left.

[22] She called her husband: "Send me one of the servants and a donkey. I need to get to the man of God and back, quickly."

[23] He said, "Why today? It's not the New Moon. It's not the Sabbath." She said, "It's all right."

[24] She saddled the donkey and told her servant, "Drive. Don't slow down for me unless I tell you."

[25] So she went, and came to the man of God on Mount Carmel. When Elisha saw her coming in the distance, he said to Gehazi, "Look — the Shunammite.

[26] Run to meet her. Ask her: 'Are you well? Is your husband well? Is the child well?'" She said, "All is well."

[27] But when she reached the man of God on the mountain, she grabbed his feet. Gehazi moved to push her away, but the man of God said, "Leave her alone. She's in bitter pain, and the LORD has hidden it from me — he didn't tell me."

[28] Then she said, "Did I ask my lord for a son? Didn't I say, 'Don't deceive me'?"

[29] Elisha said to Gehazi, "Tuck your cloak into your belt. Take my staff and go. If you meet anyone, don't greet them. If anyone greets you, don't answer. Lay my staff on the boy's face."

[30] But the boy's mother said, "As surely as the LORD lives and you live — I am not leaving without you." So he got up and followed her.

[31] Gehazi went ahead and laid the staff on the boy's face. No sound. No response. He came back to Elisha and reported, "The boy did not wake."

[32] When Elisha arrived at the house, there was the boy — dead, laid out on his bed.

[33] He went in, shut the door on the two of them, and prayed to the LORD.

[34] Then he climbed onto the bed and lay on the child — mouth to mouth, eyes to eyes, hands to hands. He stretched himself over him, and the boy's flesh grew warm.

[35] He got down, walked across the room once, then climbed back on and stretched over him again. The boy sneezed — seven times — and opened his eyes.

[36] Elisha called Gehazi: "Call the Shunammite." He called her. When she came, Elisha said, "Take your son."

[37] She came in, fell at his feet, bowed to the ground, picked up her son, and walked out.

[38] Elisha returned to Gilgal. There was famine in the land. The prophets' guild was sitting before him, and he said to his servant, "Set the large pot on the fire. Boil some stew for them."

[39] One of them went out to the field to gather herbs. He found a wild vine and stripped off its gourds — filled the folds of his cloak. He came back and chopped them into the pot, not knowing what they were.

[40] They served it up for the men to eat. But as soon as they tasted the stew, they cried out, "Man of God — there's death in the pot!" They couldn't eat it.

[41] He said, "Bring me some flour." He threw it into the pot and said, "Pour it out. Let them eat." And there was nothing harmful in the pot.

[42] A man came from Baal-shalishah, bringing the man of God twenty loaves of barley bread from the first of the harvest, along with fresh grain in his sack. Elisha said, "Give it to the men. Let them eat."

[43] His attendant said, "How am I to set this in front of a hundred men?" Elisha said, "Give it to them. Let them eat. For this is what the LORD says: 'They will eat, and they will have some left.'"

[44] So he set it before them. They ate, and they had leftovers — just as the LORD had said.

[1] Naaman was commander of the Aramean army — a giant of a man in his king's eyes, decorated, respected, the one through whom the LORD had handed Aram its victories. A mighty warrior. And a leper.

[2] Now Aram had sent out raiding parties, and on one of those raids they'd taken captive a young Israelite girl. She ended up serving Naaman's wife.

[3] One day she said to her mistress, "If only my master could stand before the prophet in Samaria. He would cure him of this leprosy."

[4] Naaman went to his king and told him what the girl from Israel had said.

[5] "Go then," the king of Aram said. "I'll send a letter to the king of Israel myself." So Naaman set out, taking with him seven hundred fifty pounds of silver, a hundred fifty pounds of gold, and ten changes of clothing.

[6] He delivered the letter to the king of Israel. It read: *With this letter I am sending my servant Naaman to you, so that you may cure him of his leprosy.*

[7] The king of Israel read it and tore his clothes. "Am I God?" he cried. "Do I deal in death and life, that this man sends me someone to be cured of leprosy? Watch — he's looking for a fight. He's hunting for an excuse."

[8] When Elisha the man of God heard that the king had torn his clothes, he sent word: "Why have you torn your clothes? Send the man to me. He'll learn there's a prophet in Israel."

[9] So Naaman came — horses, chariots, the whole entourage — and stopped at Elisha's door.

[10] Elisha didn't even come out. He sent a messenger: "Go wash in the Jordan seven times. Your flesh will return. You'll be clean."

[11] Naaman walked off in a rage. "I thought — I really thought — he would come out to me. Stand there. Call on the name of the LORD his God. Wave his hand over the spot. Cure the leprosy.

[12] Aren't the Abana and the Pharpar, the rivers of Damascus, better than every water in Israel? Couldn't I wash in them and be clean?" And he turned and stormed away.

[13] But his servants came up to him and said, "Father — if the prophet had told you to do something difficult, wouldn't you have done it? How much more when all he said was, *Wash, and be clean*?"

[14] So he went down. He plunged into the Jordan seven times, exactly as the man of God had said. And his skin came back — soft as a child's. He was clean.

[15] He returned to the man of God, his whole company with him, and stood before him. "Now I know," he said. "There is no God in all the earth except in Israel. Please — take a gift from your servant."

[16] But Elisha said, "As the LORD lives, the One I serve — I will take nothing." Naaman pressed him. Elisha refused.

[17] "Then please," Naaman said, "let your servant be given as much soil as two mules can carry. Because from now on your servant will offer no burnt offering, no sacrifice, to any god but the LORD.

[18] One thing only — may the LORD forgive me for this: when my master enters the temple of Rimmon to bow down, and he's leaning on my arm so I have to bow down with him — when I bow in the house of Rimmon, may the LORD forgive your servant for that one thing."

[19] "Go in peace," Elisha said. And Naaman set out. He had gone only a short distance

[20] when Gehazi, the servant of Elisha the man of God, said to himself, *My master let this Aramean off too easy. He wouldn't take what the man brought. As the LORD lives, I'll run after him and get something out of him.*

[21] So Gehazi went chasing after Naaman. When Naaman saw someone running behind him, he jumped down from the chariot to meet him. "Is everything all right?" he asked.

[22] "Everything's fine," Gehazi said. "My master sent me to say: *Just now two young men from the prophets' guild have arrived from the hill country of Ephraim. Please give them seventy-five pounds of silver and two changes of clothes.*"

[23] "By all means — take a hundred fifty," Naaman said. He pressed it on him, tied up the silver in two bags with two sets of clothes, and handed them to two of his own servants to carry ahead of Gehazi.

[24] When they reached the hill, Gehazi took the bags from their hands and stashed them in the house. He sent the men away, and they left.

[25] Then he went in and stood before his master. "Where have you been, Gehazi?" Elisha asked. "Nowhere, master. Your servant didn't go anywhere."

[26] Elisha said, "Did my heart not go with you when that man stepped down from his chariot to meet you? Is this the moment to take silver — to take clothes, olive groves, vineyards, sheep, oxen, slaves?

[27] Naaman's leprosy will cling to you and your descendants forever." And Gehazi walked out of his presence — leprous, white as snow.

[1] The company of prophets came to Elisha and said, "Look — the place where we live and meet with you is too cramped for us. [2] Let us go down to the Jordan, each of us cut a beam, and build ourselves a new place there."

He said, "Go."

[3] One of them said, "Please — come with your servants."

"I'll come," he answered.

[4] So he went with them. When they reached the Jordan, they started felling trees. [5] As one of them was bringing down a beam, the iron axhead flew off into the water. He cried out, "No — master! It was borrowed!"

[6] The man of God said, "Where did it fall?" He showed him the spot. Elisha cut a stick, threw it in, and the iron floated up. [7] "Lift it out," he said. The man reached out and took it.

[8] The king of Aram was at war with Israel. He'd consult his officers and say, "I'll set up camp at such-and-such a place." [9] Then the man of God would send word to the king of Israel: "Don't pass that place — Aram is coming down there." [10] So the king of Israel kept sending scouts to the place the man of God had warned him about. Again and again Elisha saved him — not once or twice.

[11] This unsettled the king of Aram. He summoned his officers and demanded, "Tell me — which of us is leaking to the king of Israel?"

[12] One of his officers said, "No one, my lord king. It's Elisha, the prophet in Israel. He tells the king of Israel the very words you speak in your bedroom."

[13] He said, "Go find out where he is, and I'll send men to seize him."

Word came back: "He's in Dothan."

[14] So the king dispatched horses and chariots and a heavy force. They moved in by night and surrounded the city.

[15] Early in the morning, the man of God's servant got up, went outside — and there it was: an army with horses and chariots ringing the city. "Oh no, master," he said. "What do we do?"

[16] "Don't be afraid," Elisha answered. "The ones with us outnumber the ones with them."

[17] Then Elisha prayed, "Lord, open his eyes — let him see." And the Lord opened the servant's eyes. He looked: the hills were ablaze with horses and chariots of fire all around Elisha.

[18] As the Arameans came down at him, Elisha prayed, "Strike these men with blindness." And he struck them blind, just as Elisha asked.

[19] Then Elisha said to them, "This isn't the road. This isn't the city. Follow me — I'll lead you to the man you're hunting." And he led them straight to Samaria.

[20] When they entered Samaria, Elisha said, "Lord, open their eyes — let them see." The Lord opened their eyes, and there they were: standing in the middle of Samaria.

[21] When the king of Israel saw them, he said to Elisha, "Father — should I strike them down? Should I?"

[22] "Don't," he said. "Would you cut down men you'd taken with your own sword and bow? Set bread and water in front of them. Let them eat and drink and go back to their master."

[23] So he laid out a great feast for them. They ate and drank, and he sent them on their way, and they went home to their master. And Aramean raiders stopped invading Israel — for a while.

[24] Later, Ben-Hadad king of Aram mustered his entire army, marched up, and laid siege to Samaria. [25] The siege ground on until famine gripped the city — until a donkey's head sold for eighty shekels of silver, and a cup of dove's dung for five.

[26] The king of Israel was walking the wall when a woman cried out to him, "Help me, my lord king!"

[27] He said, "If the Lord won't help you, where am I going to get help — from the threshing floor? From the winepress?" [28] Then he asked, "What is it?"

She said, "This woman told me, 'Hand over your son — we'll eat him today, and tomorrow we'll eat mine.' [29] So we boiled my son and ate him. The next day I said, 'Hand over your son so we can eat him' — and she's hidden her son."

[30] When the king heard what the woman said, he tore his robes. He was walking the wall, and the people saw — underneath, against his skin, he was wearing sackcloth. [31] He said, "May God deal with me, and worse, if the head of Elisha son of Shaphat stays on his shoulders by the end of this day."

[32] Elisha was sitting in his house with the elders sitting around him. The king sent a man ahead — but before the messenger arrived, Elisha said to the elders, "Do you see how this murderer's son has sent someone to take off my head? Watch — when the messenger comes, shut the door. Hold it against him. Isn't the sound of his master's feet right behind him?"

[33] He was still speaking when the messenger came down to him, and the king's word with him: "This disaster is from the Lord. Why should I wait on the Lord any longer?"

[1] Elisha answered, "Hear what the LORD says: Tomorrow, this time, at the Samaria gate — a measure of fine flour will go for a shekel, two measures of barley for the same."

[2] The captain on whose arm the king leaned shot back at the man of God: "Even if the LORD ripped open the windows of heaven, could this happen?" Elisha said: "You'll see it with your own eyes. But you won't taste a bite of it."

[3] Four men with leprosy sat at the entrance of the gate. They said to each other, "Why are we sitting here waiting to die?

[4] If we go into the city, the famine kills us. If we sit here, we die anyway. So — let's defect to the Aramean camp. If they let us live, we live. If they kill us, we die."

[5] At dusk they rose and walked toward the Aramean camp. When they reached the edge of it — no one. Not a soul.

[6] Because the Lord had made the Aramean army hear it: the thunder of chariots, the thunder of horses, the roar of a vast army bearing down on them. They said to each other, "The king of Israel has hired the Hittite kings and the Egyptian kings to come at us — "

[7] So they ran. At twilight they bolted, leaving everything where it stood — tents, horses, donkeys, the whole camp intact — running for their lives.

[8] The lepers reached the edge of the camp, entered one tent, ate, drank, hauled out silver and gold and clothes, hid them. Came back, entered another tent, hauled out more, hid it.

[9] Then they stopped. "We're doing this wrong. This is a day of good news, and we're keeping it quiet. If we wait till morning, punishment will find us. Come on — let's go tell the palace."

[10] They went and called out to the city gatekeepers: "We went to the Aramean camp — no one there. Not a voice. Just horses tied up, donkeys tied up, the tents standing exactly as they were."

[11] The gatekeepers shouted the news. It traveled inside, all the way to the king's house.

[12] The king got up in the dark and told his officers: "Let me tell you what the Arameans have done. They know we're starving. They've left the camp to hide in the open country, thinking, 'When the Israelites come out, we'll take them alive and walk into the city.'"

[13] One of his officers said, "Take five of the horses still left — they'll die anyway like everyone else here — and send men out to look."

[14] So they took two chariot teams and the king sent them after the Aramean army, saying, "Go. See."

[15] They followed the trail all the way to the Jordan — the whole road littered with clothes and gear the Arameans had thrown off in their panic. The messengers came back and reported to the king.

[16] The people poured out and plundered the Aramean camp. A measure of fine flour sold for a shekel, two measures of barley for a shekel — exactly as the LORD had said.

[17] The king had stationed the captain — the one on whose arm he leaned — at the gate. The crowd trampled him there. He died. Just as the man of God had said when the king came down to him.

[18] It happened exactly as the man of God had told the king: "Two measures of barley for a shekel, a measure of fine flour for a shekel — tomorrow, this time, at the gate of Samaria."

[19] And the captain had answered the man of God: "Even if the LORD ripped open the windows of heaven, could this happen?" And Elisha had said: "You'll see it with your own eyes. But you won't taste a bite of it."

[20] And that's what happened to him. The people trampled him at the gate. He died.

[1] Elisha had said to the woman whose son he'd brought back from death: "Get up. Take your household and go — live wherever you can. The LORD has called for a famine, and it's coming for the land. Seven years of it."

[2] The woman did exactly what the man of God said. She took her family and lived as a foreigner in Philistine territory for seven years.

[3] When the seven years ended, she came back from Philistia and went straight to the king to plead for her house and her land.

[4] The king happened to be talking with Gehazi, the servant of the man of God. "Tell me," he said, "all the great things Elisha has done."

[5] Right as Gehazi was telling him how Elisha had raised the dead — that very moment — the woman whose son had been raised walked in to plead for her house and land. Gehazi said, "My lord the king, this is the woman. And this is her son — the one Elisha brought back to life."

[6] The king questioned the woman, and she confirmed it. So he assigned her an official: "Restore everything that belonged to her, plus all the income from her fields, from the day she left until now."

[7] Elisha went to Damascus. Ben-Hadad king of Aram was sick, and word reached him: "The man of God has come up here."

[8] The king said to Hazael, "Take a gift, go meet the man of God, and consult the LORD through him. Ask: Will I recover from this illness?"

[9] So Hazael went to meet him, carrying a gift — every fine thing from Damascus, forty camel-loads. He came and stood before Elisha and said, "Your son Ben-Hadad king of Aram sent me to ask: Will I recover from this illness?"

[10] Elisha said to him, "Go tell him, 'You will certainly recover.' But the LORD has shown me — he will absolutely die."

[11] Then Elisha fixed him with a stare, held it until Hazael was unnerved. And the man of God wept.

[12] "Why is my lord weeping?" Hazael asked. Elisha answered: "Because I know what you're going to do to Israel. You'll torch their fortified cities. You'll put their young men to the sword. You'll dash their infants to pieces. You'll rip open their pregnant women."

[13] Hazael said, "What is your servant — a dog — that he should do such monstrous things?" Elisha said, "The LORD has shown me: you will be king over Aram."

[14] Hazael left Elisha and returned to his master, who asked, "What did Elisha say to you?" He answered, "He told me you would certainly recover."

[15] But the next day Hazael took a thick cloth, dipped it in water, and pressed it over the king's face until he died. And Hazael became king in his place.

[16] In the fifth year of Joram son of Ahab, king of Israel — while Jehoshaphat was still king of Judah — Jehoram son of Jehoshaphat became king of Judah.

[17] He was thirty-two when he took the throne, and he reigned eight years in Jerusalem.

[18] He walked the road of the kings of Israel — the road of Ahab's house, because he had married Ahab's daughter. He did what was evil in the LORD's eyes.

[19] But the LORD refused to destroy Judah, for David's sake — his servant — because he had promised David a lamp for his sons forever.

[20] In Jehoram's days Edom broke free of Judah's grip and set up its own king.

[21] So Jehoram crossed over to Zair with all his chariots. At night he rose and struck the Edomites surrounding him and the chariot commanders — but his army fled home.

[22] So Edom has stayed in revolt against Judah's rule to this day. Libnah revolted at the same time.

[23] The rest of Jehoram's story — everything he did — it's written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah.

[24] Jehoram slept with his fathers and was buried with them in the City of David. His son Ahaziah became king.

[25] In the twelfth year of Joram son of Ahab, king of Israel, Ahaziah son of Jehoram became king of Judah.

[26] Ahaziah was twenty-two when he took the throne, and he reigned one year in Jerusalem. His mother was Athaliah, granddaughter of Omri king of Israel.

[27] He walked the road of Ahab's house. He did what was evil in the LORD's eyes — just like Ahab's house — because he had married into it.

[28] He went with Joram son of Ahab to fight Hazael king of Aram at Ramoth-gilead. The Arameans wounded Joram.

[29] King Joram returned to Jezreel to recover from the wounds the Arameans had given him at Ramah, in the fight with Hazael king of Aram. And Ahaziah son of Jehoram, king of Judah, went down to Jezreel to visit Joram son of Ahab, because he was wounded.

[1] Elisha the prophet called one of the sons of the prophets aside. "Tuck up your robe," he said. "Take this flask of oil and go to Ramoth-gilead.

[2] When you get there, find Jehu son of Jehoshaphat, son of Nimshi. Walk in, pull him away from his fellow officers, and take him into an inner room.

[3] Then pour the oil over his head and say: 'This is what the LORD says — I have anointed you king over Israel.' After that, open the door and run. Don't linger."

[4] So the young prophet went to Ramoth-gilead.

[5] He arrived to find the army commanders sitting together. "I have a message for you, Commander," he said. Jehu asked, "For which one of us?" The young man said, "For you, Commander."

[6] Jehu stood up and followed him into the house. The prophet poured the oil over his head and said: "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, declares — I anoint you king over the LORD's people, over Israel.

[7] You will strike down the house of Ahab your master. I will avenge the blood of my servants the prophets and the blood of every servant of the LORD spilled by Jezebel.

[8] The whole house of Ahab will perish. I will cut off every male in Ahab's line — slave or free — from Israel.

[9] I will make Ahab's house like the house of Jeroboam son of Nebat, and like the house of Baasha son of Ahijah.

[10] And Jezebel? Dogs will eat her in the field of Jezreel — no one will bury her." Then he opened the door and ran.

[11] When Jehu came back out to his fellow officers, one of them asked, "Everything all right? What did that lunatic want with you?" Jehu said, "You know the type — you know how they talk."

[12] "Don't play games," they said. "Tell us." So he said: "Here's what he told me. He said, 'This is what the LORD says — I have anointed you king over Israel.'"

[13] Instantly every man stripped off his cloak and laid it under Jehu's feet on the bare steps. They blew the trumpet and shouted, "Jehu is king!"

[14] So Jehu son of Jehoshaphat, son of Nimshi, plotted against Joram. (Joram and all Israel had been defending Ramoth-gilead against Hazael king of Aram,

[15] but King Joram had returned to Jezreel to recover from the wounds the Arameans had given him in the fight against Hazael.) Jehu said, "If you're with me, don't let anyone slip out of this city to carry word to Jezreel."

[16] Then Jehu mounted his chariot and rode hard for Jezreel, where Joram lay wounded — and where Ahaziah king of Judah had come down to visit him.

[17] The watchman on the tower at Jezreel saw Jehu's company approaching and called out, "I see a company coming." Joram said, "Send a rider to meet them and ask, 'Do you come in peace?'"

[18] The rider went out: "The king asks — do you come in peace?" Jehu said, "Peace? What do you know about peace? Fall in behind me." The watchman reported, "The messenger reached them but he's not coming back."

[19] So Joram sent a second rider. He reached them and said the same thing. Jehu answered, "Peace? What do you know about peace? Fall in behind me."

[20] The watchman reported again, "He reached them but he's not coming back. And the driving — it's like the driving of Jehu son of Nimshi. He drives like a madman."

[21] "Hitch up!" Joram ordered. They harnessed his chariot, and Joram king of Israel and Ahaziah king of Judah rode out, each in his own chariot, to meet Jehu. They met him on the field that had belonged to Naboth the Jezreelite.

[22] When Joram saw Jehu, he called out, "Do you come in peace, Jehu?" Jehu shot back, "What peace — with your mother Jezebel's whorings and witchcrafts still spreading?"

[23] Joram wheeled his chariot around to flee, shouting to Ahaziah, "Treachery, Ahaziah!"

[24] Jehu drew his bow with full strength and shot Joram between the shoulders. The arrow tore through his heart, and he slumped down in his chariot.

[25] Jehu said to Bidkar, his officer: "Pick him up. Throw him onto the field that belonged to Naboth the Jezreelite. Remember? You and I rode together behind his father Ahab when the LORD pronounced this oracle against him:

[26] 'As surely as I saw the blood of Naboth and the blood of his sons yesterday, declares the LORD, I will repay you on this very plot of ground.' So pick him up. Throw him onto that field. The LORD's word stands."

[27] When Ahaziah king of Judah saw what was happening, he fled toward Beth-haggan. Jehu pursued him, shouting, "Shoot him too!" They wounded him in his chariot on the ascent to Gur near Ibleam. He made it to Megiddo and died there.

[28] His servants carried his body by chariot to Jerusalem and buried him with his ancestors in the City of David.

[29] (Ahaziah had become king over Judah in the eleventh year of Joram son of Ahab.)

[30] Then Jehu came to Jezreel. Jezebel heard he was coming, so she painted her eyes, fixed her hair, and stood at the window.

[31] As Jehu came through the gate she called down, "Is it peace, you Zimri — you murderer of your master?"

[32] He looked up at the window and said, "Who is with me? Who?" Two or three eunuchs looked down at him.

[33] "Throw her down," he said. They threw her down. Her blood spattered the wall and the horses, and he trampled her under hoof.

[34] Then he went inside, ate and drank, and said, "See to that cursed woman. Bury her — she was a king's daughter, after all."

[35] But when they went to bury her, all they found was her skull, her feet, and the palms of her hands.

[36] They came back and told him. Jehu said: "This is exactly what the LORD spoke through his servant Elijah the Tishbite — 'On the plot of Jezreel the dogs will devour the flesh of Jezebel.

[37] Her corpse will lie like dung on the field at Jezreel, so that no one will be able to say: This was Jezebel.'"

[1] Ahab had seventy sons in Samaria. Jehu wrote letters and dispatched them to Samaria—to the city officials, the elders, and the guardians of Ahab's heirs. The message read:

[2] "The moment this letter reaches you—seeing that you have your master's sons with you, plus chariots, horses, a fortified city, and weapons—

[3] pick the best and most capable of your master's sons, set him on his father's throne, and fight for the house of your master."

[4] They were terrified. They said, "Two kings couldn't stand against him. How can we?"

[5] So the palace administrator, the city governor, the elders, and the guardians sent word to Jehu: "We are your servants. Whatever you say, we'll do. We won't make anyone king. Do what seems right to you."

[6] Jehu wrote them a second letter: "If you're with me, if you'll obey my voice, then take the heads of your master's sons and come to me at Jezreel by this time tomorrow."

Now the king's seventy sons were being raised by the city's leading men.

[7] When the letter arrived, they took the king's sons—all seventy—and slaughtered them. They piled the heads in baskets and sent them to Jehu at Jezreel.

[8] A messenger came and told him, "They've brought the heads of the king's sons."

Jehu said, "Stack them in two heaps at the city gate. Leave them till morning."

[9] In the morning he went out, stood before all the people, and said: "You are innocent. Yes—I conspired against my master and killed him. But who struck down all these?

[10] Know this: not one word the LORD spoke against the house of Ahab will fall to the ground. The LORD has done what he promised through his servant Elijah."

[11] Then Jehu killed everyone left of Ahab's house in Jezreel—all his great men, his close friends, his priests. He left no survivor.

[12] Then he set out for Samaria. On the way, at Beth-eked of the Shepherds,

[13] Jehu came across the relatives of Ahaziah, king of Judah. He asked, "Who are you?"

They said, "Relatives of Ahaziah. We came down to greet the royal family."

[14] He said, "Take them alive." They took them alive and slaughtered them at the pit of Beth-eked—forty-two men. He left none.

[15] When he left there, he met Jehonadab son of Rechab coming to meet him. Jehu greeted him and said, "Is your heart right with mine, as mine is with yours?"

Jehonadab said, "It is."

"Then give me your hand." He gave it, and Jehu pulled him up into the chariot.

[16] He said, "Come with me. Watch my zeal for the LORD." So they rode together in his chariot.

[17] When he reached Samaria, he killed everyone left of Ahab's family there. He wiped them out—exactly as the LORD had told Elijah.

[18] Then Jehu gathered all the people and said: "Ahab served Baal a little. Jehu will serve him much.

[19] Now—summon every prophet of Baal, every worshiper, every priest. Let no one be missing. I'm holding a great sacrifice for Baal. Anyone who doesn't show up dies."

But Jehu was acting with cunning—to destroy the worshipers of Baal.

[20] Jehu said, "Consecrate a solemn assembly for Baal." They proclaimed it.

[21] Jehu sent word throughout Israel, and every worshiper of Baal came. Not a man stayed home. They poured into the temple of Baal until it was packed wall to wall.

[22] He told the keeper of the wardrobe, "Bring out vestments for every worshiper of Baal." So he brought them out.

[23] Then Jehu went into the temple of Baal with Jehonadab son of Rechab and said to the worshipers of Baal: "Search carefully. Make sure no servant of the LORD is here with you—only worshipers of Baal."

[24] They went in to offer sacrifices and burnt offerings.

Now Jehu had stationed eighty men outside with this warning: "If a single one of these men I'm placing in your hands escapes, your life will pay for his."

[25] The moment Jehu finished the burnt offering, he said to the guards and officers: "Go in. Kill them. Let no one out."

They cut them down with the sword. The guards and officers dumped the bodies outside and pressed on into the inner shrine of Baal's temple.

[26] They dragged out the sacred pillar of Baal and burned it.

[27] They smashed the pillar of Baal. They tore down the temple of Baal and turned it into a latrine—which it remains to this day.

[28] So Jehu wiped Baal out of Israel.

[29] But the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat—the ones that dragged Israel into sin—Jehu wouldn't let those go. The golden calves at Bethel and Dan stayed standing.

[30] The LORD said to Jehu: "Because you have done well what is right in my eyes—because you carried out against the house of Ahab everything that was in my heart—your sons will sit on Israel's throne to the fourth generation."

[31] But Jehu didn't watch his step. He didn't walk in the law of the LORD, the God of Israel, with all his heart. He didn't turn from the sins of Jeroboam that pulled Israel down.

[32] In those days the LORD began to cut Israel down. Hazael struck them along every border—

[33] from the Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead, the Gadites, the Reubenites, the Manassites; from Aroer by the Arnon Valley, through Gilead, through Bashan.

[34] The rest of Jehu's story—everything he did, all his might—it's written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[35] Jehu lay down with his fathers. They buried him in Samaria. Jehoahaz his son reigned in his place.

[36] Jehu reigned over Israel in Samaria twenty-eight years.

[1] When Athaliah, Ahaziah's mother, saw her son was dead, she rose up and butchered the entire royal bloodline.

[2] But Jehosheba — King Joram's daughter, Ahaziah's sister — snatched Joash son of Ahaziah out of the lineup of princes scheduled for slaughter. She hid him and his nurse away in a bedroom, out of Athaliah's reach. He lived.

[3] For six years he stayed buried in the temple of the LORD with her while Athaliah ruled the land.

[4] In the seventh year, Jehoiada made his move. He sent for the captains of the Carites and the palace guard, brought them into the temple of the LORD, cut a covenant with them, made them swear an oath inside the sanctuary — and then he showed them the king's son.

[5] His orders: "Here's how we do this. One third of you who come on duty Sabbath morning will guard the palace.

[6] Another third at the Sur Gate, another third at the gate behind the guards. You'll cover the house in shifts.

[7] The two divisions of you who go off duty Sabbath morning — you stay. You guard the temple of the LORD around the king.

[8] Surround him. Weapons drawn. Anyone breaks the ranks — dead. Stay tight on the king wherever he moves."

[9] The captains followed Jehoiada's orders to the letter. Each one rounded up his men — both those coming on Sabbath duty and those going off — and brought them to Jehoiada.

[10] The priest handed out to the captains the spears and shields that had belonged to King David, still kept in the temple of the LORD.

[11] The guards took their positions, weapons in hand, forming a wall from the south side of the temple to the north, ringing the altar and the sanctuary, the king in the middle.

[12] Then Jehoiada brought out the king's son. He set the crown on his head, handed him the testimony, anointed him king. The people clapped and shouted, "Long live the king!"

[13] Athaliah heard the noise — the guards, the crowd — and came running to the temple of the LORD where the people were gathered.

[14] She looked, and there he stood: the king, by the pillar, as tradition demanded. The officers and trumpeters flanked him. The whole country was celebrating, trumpets blaring. Athaliah ripped her clothes and screamed, "Treason! Treason!"

[15] Jehoiada the priest gave the order to the captains in charge of the troops: "Get her out of here — between the ranks. Anyone who follows her, kill them." He had said, "Don't kill her in the temple of the LORD."

[16] So they seized her. They led her through the horse entrance to the palace grounds. There they killed her.

[17] Then Jehoiada cut a covenant — between the LORD and the king and the people — that they would be the LORD's people. And another between the king and the people.

[18] The whole country marched to the temple of Baal and tore it down. Altars smashed. Images shattered. Mattan, Baal's priest, killed right there in front of his own altars. Then Jehoiada posted guards over the temple of the LORD.

[19] He took the captains, the Carites, the guards, and the whole country with him. They brought the king down from the temple of the LORD, through the guards' gate, to the palace. He sat on the throne of the kings.

[20] The whole country celebrated. The city was quiet. Athaliah was dead — killed by the sword at the palace.

[21] Joash was seven years old when he became king.

[1] Jehoash took the throne in the seventh year of Jehu, and he reigned in Jerusalem for forty years. His mother was Zibiah, from Beersheba.

[2] Jehoash did right in the LORD's eyes throughout the years Jehoiada the priest mentored him.

[3] But the high places stayed standing. The people kept sacrificing and burning incense there.

[4] Jehoash said to the priests: "Take every sacred contribution that comes into the LORD's house — the census money, the personal vow money, all the freewill offerings —

[5] let each priest collect it from his contacts, and use it to repair whatever's broken in the temple, wherever damage shows."

[6] But by year twenty-three of King Jehoash, the priests still had not done the repairs.

[7] So the king summoned Jehoiada and the other priests and said: "Why aren't you fixing the temple? From now on, stop taking money from your contacts — hand it over for the repairs."

[8] The priests agreed: they would take no more silver from the people, and they would not be responsible for the repairs themselves.

[9] Jehoiada took a chest, drilled a hole in the lid, and set it beside the altar, on the right as you enter the LORD's house. The priests guarding the threshold dropped into it every coin brought to the temple.

[10] Whenever they saw the chest was full, the royal scribe and the high priest would come, count out the silver, and bag it up.

[11] Then they handed the weighed silver to the foremen overseeing the temple work, who paid the carpenters and builders,

[12] the masons and stonecutters, and bought timber and dressed stone — everything needed to repair the LORD's house, every expense.

[13] But none of the silver brought to the temple was made into silver basins, snuffers, bowls, trumpets, or any gold or silver vessels for the LORD's house.

[14] It all went to the workmen, who used it to restore the temple.

[15] No one audited the men handling the money to pay the workers — they were trusted.

[16] Money from guilt offerings and sin offerings did not go into the LORD's house. It belonged to the priests.

[17] Around this time, Hazael king of Aram marched on Gath and took it. Then he set his sights on Jerusalem.

[18] So Jehoash king of Judah took every sacred object dedicated by his ancestors — Jehoshaphat, Jehoram, Ahaziah, kings of Judah — and his own dedicated objects, along with all the gold he could find in the treasuries of the LORD's house and the palace, and shipped it to Hazael king of Aram. Hazael pulled back from Jerusalem.

[19] The rest of the record of Jehoash — everything he did — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah.

[20] His own officials conspired against him and struck him down at Beth-millo, on the road going down to Silla.

[21] The officers who killed him were Jozabad son of Shimeath and Jehozabad son of Shomer. He died and was buried with his ancestors in the City of David. His son Amaziah took the throne after him.

[1] In the twenty-third year of Joash son of Ahaziah, king of Judah, Jehoahaz son of Jehu took the throne of Israel in Samaria, and he ruled for seventeen years.

[2] He did what was evil in the LORD's sight. He chased the same sins Jeroboam son of Nebat had dragged Israel into, and he refused to let them go.

[3] So the LORD's anger flared against Israel, and he handed them over — first to Hazael king of Aram, then to his son Ben-hadad — for as long as those kings drew breath.

[4] Then Jehoahaz begged the LORD for mercy, and the LORD listened. He had seen what Aram was doing to Israel — the crushing weight of it.

[5] So the LORD gave Israel a deliverer, and they slipped out from under Aram's hand. The people of Israel went back to living in their own homes, the way they had before.

[6] But they still would not turn from the sins of the house of Jeroboam, the sins that had infected the whole nation. They walked in them. And the Asherah pole still stood in Samaria.

[7] Jehoahaz was left with nothing but fifty horsemen, ten chariots, and ten thousand foot soldiers. The king of Aram had ground the rest into dust — like wheat at threshing time.

[8] The rest of what Jehoahaz did — all his exploits, all his strength — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[9] Jehoahaz lay down with his ancestors and was buried in Samaria, and his son Joash took the throne.

[10] In the thirty-seventh year of Joash king of Judah, Jehoash son of Jehoahaz became king of Israel in Samaria. He ruled sixteen years.

[11] He did what was evil in the LORD's sight. He never broke away from the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat — the sins that had poisoned Israel. He walked the same road.

[12] The rest of what Joash did — his accomplishments, his war with Amaziah king of Judah — is written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[13] Joash lay down with his ancestors, and Jeroboam took his seat on the throne. Joash was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel.

[14] Now Elisha had fallen sick — the illness that would kill him. Joash king of Israel came down to him, wept over his face, and cried, "My father, my father! The chariots of Israel and its horsemen!"

[15] Elisha said, "Bring a bow and some arrows." He brought them.

[16] Then Elisha told the king of Israel, "Put your hand on the bow." He did. And Elisha laid his own hands over the king's hands.

[17] "Open the east window," Elisha said. He opened it. "Shoot!" And he shot. "The LORD's arrow of victory," Elisha said. "The arrow of victory over Aram. You will strike Aram at Aphek until you have finished them."

[18] Then he said, "Take the arrows." He took them. "Strike the ground," Elisha told the king of Israel. He struck three times and stopped.

[19] The man of God burned with anger at him. "You should have struck five times, six times — then you would have hammered Aram into the ground, finished them. Now you will defeat Aram only three times."

[20] Then Elisha died, and they buried him. Now Moabite raiding bands used to invade the land every spring.

[21] Some men were burying a body when they spotted a raiding party. They threw the corpse into Elisha's tomb and ran. The moment the body touched Elisha's bones, the man came back to life and stood on his feet.

[22] Hazael king of Aram crushed Israel for the whole reign of Jehoahaz.

[23] But the LORD took pity on them. He showed them compassion and turned toward them — all because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. He refused to destroy them. He would not cast them away from his presence — not yet.

[24] When Hazael king of Aram died, his son Ben-hadad took the throne.

[25] Then Jehoash son of Jehoahaz took back the cities Hazael had stripped from his father Jehoahaz in war. Three times Jehoash struck him down, and three times he recovered the cities of Israel.

[1] In the second year of Joash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel, Amaziah son of Joash became king of Judah.

[2] He was twenty-five when he took the throne, and he ruled twenty-nine years in Jerusalem. His mother was Jehoaddin from Jerusalem.

[3] He did what was right before the LORD — though not on the scale of David, his ancestor. He governed the way his father Joash had governed.

[4] The high places stayed standing. People kept sacrificing and burning incense on them.

[5] Once the throne was firmly his, he hunted down the men who had assassinated his father.

[6] But he did not touch their children. He held the line Moses had drawn in the scroll of the law, where the LORD commanded: fathers will not die for what their sons did, and sons will not die for what their fathers did. Each person dies for his own sin.

[7] He destroyed ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt and took Sela by storm. He renamed it Joktheel — the name it carries to this day.

[8] Then Amaziah sent messengers to Jehoash son of Jehoahaz son of Jehu, king of Israel: "Come. Let us look each other in the face."

[9] Jehoash king of Israel sent his answer back to Amaziah king of Judah: "A thistle in Lebanon sent word to a cedar in Lebanon — 'Give your daughter to my son as a wife.' Then a wild beast from Lebanon walked by and trampled the thistle flat.

[10] You crushed Edom and now your head is swollen. Bask in your glory — at home. Why pick a fight you cannot win, dragging Judah down with you?"

[11] But Amaziah would not listen. So Jehoash king of Israel marched out, and the two kings faced each other at Beth Shemesh in Judah.

[12] Judah was routed by Israel. Every man ran for home.

[13] Jehoash king of Israel captured Amaziah king of Judah, son of Joash son of Ahaziah, at Beth Shemesh. He marched to Jerusalem and tore down four hundred cubits of the city wall — from the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate.

[14] He hauled away every gold and silver vessel he could find, every treasure in the house of the LORD and in the palace storerooms — and hostages. Then he went back to Samaria.

[15] The rest of what Jehoash did, all his exploits, his war with Amaziah king of Judah — it is all written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.

[16] Jehoash slept with his ancestors and was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel. His son Jeroboam took the throne.

[17] Amaziah son of Joash king of Judah lived fifteen years past the death of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel.

[18] The rest of what Amaziah did is written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah.

[19] A conspiracy formed against him in Jerusalem. He fled to Lachish — they sent men after him to Lachish and killed him there.

[20] They brought him back on horses and buried him in Jerusalem with his ancestors, in the City of David.

[21] Then all the people of Judah took Azariah, sixteen years old, and made him king in place of his father Amaziah.

[22] Azariah was the one who rebuilt Elath and brought it back to Judah after his father was laid to rest.

[23] In the fifteenth year of Amaziah son of Joash king of Judah, Jeroboam son of Jehoash became king of Israel in Samaria. He ruled forty-one years.

[24] He did evil in the LORD's eyes. He never turned away from any of the sins Jeroboam son of Nebat had pulled Israel into.

[25] But he was the one who restored Israel's borders — from Lebo Hamath all the way down to the Sea of the Arabah — fulfilling what the LORD God of Israel had spoken through his servant Jonah son of Amittai, the prophet from Gath Hepher.

[26] The LORD had seen Israel's anguish — bitter, unbearable — no one left, slave or free, no one to come to their rescue.

[27] And the LORD had not said he would blot Israel's name out from under heaven. So he saved them by the hand of Jeroboam son of Jehoash.

[28] The rest of what Jeroboam did, all his exploits, his wars, how he recovered Damascus and Hamath for Israel — what once belonged to Judah — it is all written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel.
