[6] I haven't lived in a house from the day I brought Israel up out of Egypt to this very day. I've been moving — a tent for my dwelling, a tabernacle for my home.

[7] Everywhere I've traveled with the people of Israel, did I ever once say a word to any of the tribal leaders I appointed to shepherd my people — did I ever ask, 'Why haven't you built me a house of cedar?'

[8] So now — tell my servant David: This is what the LORD of armies says. I took you from the pasture, from trailing the flock, and made you ruler over my people Israel.

[9] I've been with you wherever you went. I've cut down every enemy in your path. And I'm going to make your name great — a name to rank with the greatest names on earth.

[10] I'll plant my people Israel in a place of their own. They'll put down roots and live undisturbed. No more torment. No more violent men grinding them down the way they did at the beginning,

[11] from the days I set judges over my people Israel. I'm giving you rest from all your enemies. And hear this: the LORD himself will build you a house.

[12] When your days are done and you lie down with your fathers, I'll raise up your offspring after you — your own flesh and blood — and I'll establish his kingdom.

[13] He's the one who will build a house for my name. And I'll set the throne of his kingdom in place — forever.

[14] I'll be his father. He'll be my son. When he goes wrong, I'll discipline him — with a rod the way any man does, with the strokes anyone deserves.

[15] But my steadfast love? I will never tear it away from him the way I tore it from Saul, whom I removed before you.

[16] Your house and your kingdom will stand secure before me forever. Your throne will be established forever."

[17] Nathan delivered the whole vision, every word of it, to David.

[18] Then King David went in and sat before the LORD. He said: "Who am I, Lord GOD, and what is my house, that you have brought me this far?

[19] And as if this were not enough in your eyes, Lord GOD, you've spoken about the future of your servant's house — far into the distance. Is this how you deal with a man, Lord GOD?

[20] What more can David say to you? You know your servant, Lord GOD.

[21] For the sake of your word, and because your heart willed it, you've done this enormous thing and let your servant in on it.

[22] So you are great, Lord GOD. There is none like you. There is no God but you — everything we've heard with our own ears confirms it.

[23] And who is like your people Israel — one nation on earth that God himself went out to redeem as his own, to make a name for himself, to do for them great and awe-inspiring things by driving out nations and their gods before your people, whom you redeemed for yourself out of Egypt?

[24] You established Israel as your people forever. And you, LORD, became their God.

[25] So now, LORD God, take the word you've spoken about your servant and his house and make it stand forever. Do exactly what you said.

[26] Your name will be magnified forever — they'll say, 'The LORD of armies is God over Israel.' And the house of your servant David will stand secure before you.

[27] Because you, LORD of armies, God of Israel, have opened your servant's ear and said, 'I will build you a house' — that's why your servant has found the courage to pray this prayer to you.

[28] And now, Lord GOD, you are God. Your words are true. And you've promised this good thing to your servant.

[29] So now — be pleased to bless the house of your servant, that it may go on before you forever. You yourself, Lord GOD, have spoken it. And by your blessing the house of your servant will be blessed forever."

[1] After this, David struck the Philistines hard. He broke them. He wrenched the reins of power right out of their hands.

[2] Then he turned on Moab. He made them lie down on the ground and measured them off with a rope—two lengths to die, one length to live. Moab became his, paying tribute.

[3] He hit Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah, when Hadadezer marched out to reclaim his grip on the Euphrates.

[4] David seized seventeen hundred horsemen and twenty thousand foot soldiers. He hamstrung all the chariot horses except for a hundred he kept for himself.

[5] When the Arameans of Damascus came to bail Hadadezer out, David cut down twenty-two thousand of them.

[6] He planted garrisons throughout Aram of Damascus, and the Arameans became his, paying tribute. Wherever David went, the LORD made him win.

[7] David stripped the gold shields off Hadadezer's officers and carried them back to Jerusalem.

[8] From Betah and Berothai, Hadadezer's cities, he hauled away staggering loads of bronze.

[9] When Toi, king of Hamath, heard that David had crushed Hadadezer's entire army—

[10] he sent his son Joram to David with greetings and congratulations, because Hadadezer had been Toi's enemy too, and Toi had been at war with him. Joram brought articles of silver, gold, and bronze,

[11] which David dedicated to the LORD, along with the silver and gold he had set apart from every nation he had subdued—

[12] Edom, Moab, the Ammonites, the Philistines, Amalek, and the plunder from Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah.

[13] David's name thundered after he came back from striking down eighteen thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt.

[14] He stationed garrisons throughout Edom, all of it, and every Edomite became David's subject. Wherever David went, the LORD made him win.

[15] So David reigned over all Israel. He governed his people with justice and made things right.

[16] Joab son of Zeruiah commanded the army. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud kept the records.

[17] Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar were priests. Seraiah was the scribe.

[18] Benaiah son of Jehoiada led the Kerethites and the Pelethites. And David's sons served as priests.

[1] One day David asked, "Is anyone from Saul's house still alive? I want to show kindness for Jonathan's sake."

[2] There was a servant of Saul's household named Ziba. They summoned him to David, and the king said, "Are you Ziba?" "At your service," he answered.

[3] The king pressed him: "Is there anyone left from Saul's family I can show God's own kindness to?" Ziba said, "There's still a son of Jonathan — crippled in both feet."

[4] "Where is he?" David asked. Ziba answered, "At the house of Machir son of Ammiel, in Lo Debar."

[5] So King David sent for him and brought him out of Machir's house in Lo Debar.

[6] When Mephibosheth, son of Jonathan, grandson of Saul, came before David, he fell on his face in homage. David said, "Mephibosheth." "Your servant," he answered.

[7] "Don't be afraid," David told him. "I'm going to show you kindness for your father Jonathan's sake. I'll restore to you every acre of land that belonged to your grandfather Saul, and you'll eat at my table — always."

[8] Mephibosheth bowed low and said, "What am I, that you'd even look at a dead dog like me?"

[9] Then the king called Ziba, Saul's servant, and told him: "Everything that belonged to Saul and his household — I'm giving it all to your master's grandson.

[10] You and your sons and your servants will work the land for him and bring in the harvest, so that your master's grandson has food on his table. But Mephibosheth himself will always eat at my table." Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.

[11] Ziba said to the king, "Whatever my lord the king commands, your servant will do." So Mephibosheth ate at David's table like one of the king's own sons.

[12] Mephibosheth had a young son named Mica, and every member of Ziba's household became Mephibosheth's servants.

[13] And Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem, eating at the king's table every day — crippled in both feet.

[1] Some time later, the king of Ammon died, and his son Hanun took the throne. [2] David said, "I'll show kindness to Hanun son of Nahash, the way his father showed kindness to me." So David sent a delegation to console him over his father.

But when David's men arrived in Ammonite territory, [3] the Ammonite nobles whispered to Hanun their lord: "You think David is honoring your father by sending these condolers? No — he sent these men to case the city, to scout it, to bring it down."

[4] So Hanun seized David's envoys, shaved off half of each man's beard, sliced their robes off at the buttocks, and sent them away exposed.

[5] When David heard, he sent messengers to meet them — these were men deeply humiliated — and the king said, "Stay in Jericho until your beards grow back. Then come home."

[6] The Ammonites realized they had made themselves a stench to David. So they hired Aramean mercenaries — twenty thousand foot soldiers from Beth-rehob and Zobah, a thousand men from the king of Maacah, and twelve thousand from Tob.

[7] David heard and dispatched Joab with the entire army — every fighting man he had.

[8] The Ammonites marched out and formed their battle line at the city gate. The Arameans from Zobah and Rehob, with the men from Tob and Maacah, took position separately in the open field.

[9] Joab saw the trap — battle lines in front of him and behind him. He picked Israel's best troops and arrayed them against the Arameans. [10] The rest he handed to his brother Abishai, who set them against the Ammonites. [11] Joab said, "If the Arameans overpower me, you come rescue me. If the Ammonites overpower you, I'll come rescue you. [12] Be strong. Let's fight like men — for our people, for the cities of our God. And let the LORD do what seems right to him."

[13] Joab advanced with his troops against the Arameans, and they broke and ran. [14] When the Ammonites saw the Arameans fleeing, they fled too — straight from Abishai back into the city. So Joab pulled back from the Ammonites and returned to Jerusalem.

[15] The Arameans saw they had been routed by Israel, and they regrouped. [16] Hadadezer sent for the Arameans across the Euphrates, and they came to Helam under Shobach, commander of Hadadezer's army.

[17] Word reached David. He gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan, and arrived at Helam. The Arameans drew up against him, and the battle began. [18] But the Arameans broke again before Israel. David cut down seven hundred chariot drivers and forty thousand horsemen. He struck down Shobach the commander, and Shobach died there.

[19] When all the kings who served Hadadezer saw they had been beaten by Israel, they made peace with Israel and submitted to them. And the Arameans were finished helping the Ammonites — they were too afraid.

[1] Spring came around — the season when kings march out to war. David sent Joab out with his officers and the whole army of Israel. They shredded the Ammonites and laid siege to Rabbah. David stayed in Jerusalem.

[2] One evening David got up from his bed and walked the roof of the palace. From up there he saw a woman bathing. She was stunning.

[3] He sent someone to find out who she was. The report came back: "That's Bathsheba, daughter of Eliam, wife of Uriah the Hittite."

[4] David sent messengers to bring her. She came. He slept with her — she had just purified herself from her period — and then she went home.

[5] She got pregnant. She sent word to David: "I'm pregnant."

[6] David sent a message to Joab: "Send me Uriah the Hittite." Joab sent him.

[7] When Uriah arrived, David asked him how Joab was doing, how the troops were doing, how the war was going.

[8] Then David said, "Go home. Wash your feet. Relax." Uriah left the palace, and the king sent a gift after him.

[9] But Uriah didn't go home. He slept at the palace gate with the king's servants.

[10] When David heard Uriah hadn't gone home, he asked him, "You've been on the road. Why didn't you go home?"

[11] Uriah said, "The ark is in a tent. Israel and Judah are camped in the open field. My commander Joab and the king's men are sleeping on the ground. And I'm supposed to go home, eat, drink, and sleep with my wife? By your life, I will not do such a thing."

[12] David said, "Stay here one more day. Tomorrow I'll send you back." So Uriah stayed in Jerusalem that day and the next.

[13] David invited him to dinner. He got him drunk. But that night Uriah still went out and slept among the king's servants — he did not go home.

[14] In the morning David wrote a letter to Joab and sent it by Uriah's own hand.

[15] The letter said: "Put Uriah on the front line where the fighting is fiercest. Then pull back from him so he gets struck down and dies."

[16] So Joab, watching the city, posted Uriah at the spot where he knew the toughest defenders were.

[17] The men of the city came out and fought Joab. Some of David's soldiers fell. Uriah the Hittite died too.

[18] Joab sent David a full report of the battle.

[19] He instructed the messenger: "When you finish telling the king everything about the battle,

[20] if his temper flares and he says, 'Why did you go so close to the city? Didn't you know they'd shoot from the wall?

[21] Who killed Abimelech son of Jerubbesheth? Wasn't it a woman who dropped a millstone on him from the wall at Thebez? So why did you go that close?' — then say, 'Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead.'"

[22] The messenger went. He told David everything Joab had sent him to say.

[23] He said, "The enemy pushed us back into the open field, but we drove them back to the city gate.

[24] Then the archers shot at us from the wall. Some of the king's men died. Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead."

[25] David told the messenger, "Tell Joab this: 'Don't let it shake you. The sword devours one as well as another. Press the attack on the city. Take it down.' Encourage him."

[26] When Uriah's wife heard her husband was dead, she mourned for him.

[27] When the mourning was over, David sent for her and brought her into his house. She became his wife and bore him a son.

But what David had done was evil in the eyes of the LORD.

[1] So the LORD sent Nathan to David. Nathan walked in and said: "Two men lived in the same city. One was rich. One was poor.

[2] The rich man had flocks and herds — more than he could count.

[3] The poor man had nothing. Nothing but a single lamb, small, that he had bought. He raised it. It grew up alongside his children. It ate from his plate, drank from his cup, slept in his arms. It was like a daughter to him.

[4] One day a traveler showed up at the rich man's door. But the rich man couldn't bear to slaughter one of his own animals to feed his guest. So he took the poor man's lamb — and served it to the traveler."

[5] David's blood went hot. He erupted at Nathan: "As the LORD lives, the man who did this deserves to die.

[6] He'll pay for that lamb four times over — because he did this thing, because he had no pity."

[7] Nathan looked at him. "You are the man. This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I anointed you king over Israel. I rescued you from Saul's hand.

[8] I handed you your master's house. I put your master's wives in your arms. I gave you Israel and Judah. And if that wasn't enough, I would have given you more, and more again.

[9] So why did you despise the word of the LORD by doing this evil thing? You struck down Uriah the Hittite with the sword and took his wife to be yours. You killed him with the sword of the Ammonites.

[10] Now the sword will never leave your house — because you despised me and took the wife of Uriah the Hittite to be your own.

[11] This is what the LORD says: I am raising disaster against you from inside your own house. I will take your wives before your eyes and give them to a man close to you. He will sleep with them in broad daylight.

[12] You did it in secret. I will do it in front of all Israel, under the sun."

[13] David said to Nathan, "I have sinned against the LORD." And Nathan said to David, "The LORD has put your sin away. You will not die.

[14] But because by this you have given the LORD's enemies every reason to mock him — the child born to you will die."

[15] Nathan went home. And the LORD struck the child Uriah's wife had borne to David, and the child grew gravely ill.

[16] David begged God for the boy. He fasted. He went into his house and lay on the ground through the night.

[17] The elders of his household stood over him, trying to get him up off the floor. He wouldn't move. He wouldn't eat with them.

[18] On the seventh day the child died. David's servants were afraid to tell him. They whispered to each other, "Look — while the child was alive we spoke to him and he wouldn't listen. How can we tell him the child is dead? He might do something terrible."

[19] But David saw his servants whispering. He understood. He asked them, "Is the child dead?" They said, "He's dead."

[20] David got up off the ground. He washed. He put on oil. He changed his clothes. He went into the house of the LORD and worshiped. Then he went home and asked for food, and they set it before him, and he ate.

[21] His servants said, "What are you doing? While the child was alive, you fasted and wept. Now that he's dead, you get up and eat?"

[22] He said, "While the child was alive I fasted and wept. I thought: Who knows? Maybe the LORD will be gracious to me and let the child live.

[23] But now he's dead. Why should I fast? Can I bring him back? I am going to him. He is not coming back to me."

[24] Then David comforted Bathsheba his wife. He went to her and slept with her, and she gave birth to a son. They named him Solomon. The LORD loved him,

[25] and sent word through Nathan the prophet to call him Jedidiah — Beloved of the LORD — for the LORD's sake.

[26] Meanwhile Joab was fighting against Rabbah of the Ammonites. He captured the royal city.

[27] Joab sent messengers to David: "I have fought against Rabbah. I have taken the city of waters.

[28] Now gather the rest of the army, lay siege to the city, and capture it — before I take it myself and it gets named after me."

[29] So David gathered the whole army, marched to Rabbah, attacked it, and took it.

[30] He lifted the crown from the head of their king — a crown of gold weighing a talent, set with a precious stone — and it was placed on David's head. He hauled out plunder from the city, mountains of it.

[31] He brought out the people and put them to work with saws and iron picks and axes, and set them to making bricks. He did this to every Ammonite city. Then David and the whole army returned to Jerusalem.

[1] Some time later, this happened: Absalom son of David had a beautiful sister named Tamar. And Amnon, David's son, became obsessed with her.

[2] Amnon was so tormented over his sister Tamar that he made himself sick. She was a virgin, and it seemed impossible to Amnon that he could do anything to her.

[3] But Amnon had a friend named Jonadab, son of David's brother Shimeah. Jonadab was a shrewd man.

[4] He asked Amnon, "Why are you, the king's son, looking more haggard every morning? Tell me." Amnon said, "I'm in love with Tamar, my brother Absalom's sister."

[5] Jonadab said, "Lie down in bed. Pretend to be sick. When your father comes to check on you, say, 'Please let my sister Tamar come and feed me. Let her prepare the food right in front of me so I can watch — and eat from her hand.'"

[6] So Amnon lay down and faked illness. When the king came to see him, Amnon said, "Please — let my sister Tamar come and make a couple of cakes in front of me, and I'll eat from her hand."

[7] David sent word to Tamar at the palace: "Go to your brother Amnon's house and prepare him some food."

[8] Tamar went to Amnon's house, where he was lying down. She took dough, kneaded it, shaped the cakes in his sight, and baked them.

[9] Then she took the pan and served him — but he refused to eat. "Send everyone out of here," Amnon said. So every servant left.

[10] Amnon said to Tamar, "Bring the food into the bedroom so I can eat from your hand." Tamar took the cakes she had made and brought them to her brother Amnon in his bedroom.

[11] But when she brought them close so he could eat, he grabbed her. "Come to bed with me, sister," he said.

[12] "No, brother!" she cried. "Don't force me. This isn't done in Israel. Don't do this disgraceful thing.

[13] Where could I take my shame? And you — you'd be counted among the worst fools in Israel. Please — speak to the king. He won't keep me from you."

[14] But he wouldn't listen. He overpowered her and raped her.

[15] Then Amnon hated her — with a hatred more violent than the lust he'd felt before. "Get up," he said. "Get out."

[16] "No!" she said. "Sending me away is a worse wrong than what you've already done." But he wouldn't listen.

[17] He called his attendant. "Get this woman out of here. Throw her out and bolt the door behind her."

[18] (She was wearing an ornate robe, the kind the king's virgin daughters wore.) The servant put her out and bolted the door.

[19] Tamar poured ashes on her head, tore the ornate robe she was wearing, put her hand on her head, and walked away weeping aloud.

[20] Her brother Absalom said to her, "Has Amnon, your brother, been with you? Keep quiet for now, sister. He's your brother. Don't take this to heart." So Tamar stayed in her brother Absalom's house — a desolate woman.

[21] When King David heard all of this, he was furious — but he did nothing.

[22] Absalom said nothing to Amnon, good or bad. But he hated Amnon for violating his sister Tamar.

[23] Two years later, Absalom was shearing sheep at Baal-hazor near Ephraim, and he invited all the king's sons.

[24] Absalom went to the king and said, "Your servant is shearing sheep. Would the king and his court honor me by coming?"

[25] "No, my son," the king said. "We shouldn't all come — we'd be a burden to you." Absalom pressed him, but David wouldn't go, though he gave him his blessing.

[26] Then Absalom said, "If not you, then please let my brother Amnon come with us." "Why him?" the king asked.

[27] But Absalom pressed him, so David sent Amnon and the rest of the king's sons.

[28] Absalom gave his men their orders: "Watch carefully. When Amnon's heart is light with wine and I say, 'Strike Amnon down' — kill him. Don't be afraid. I'm the one giving the order. Be strong. Be brave."

[29] So Absalom's men did to Amnon what Absalom commanded. Then every one of the king's sons jumped on his mule and fled.

[30] While they were still on the road, the news reached David: "Absalom has killed every one of the king's sons. Not one is left."

[31] The king stood up, tore his clothes, and threw himself on the ground. All his servants stood by, their clothes torn too.

[32] But Jonadab, son of David's brother Shimeah, spoke up: "Don't let my lord think they've killed all the young men, the king's sons. Only Amnon is dead. Absalom has been planning this from the day Amnon raped his sister Tamar.

[33] So don't let my lord the king take this report to heart, thinking all his sons are dead. Only Amnon is dead."

[34] Meanwhile, Absalom had fled. The watchman on duty looked up and saw a crowd of people coming down the road from the west, along the side of the hill.

[35] Jonadab said to the king, "Look — the king's sons. It's just as I said."

[36] As he finished speaking, the king's sons arrived, wailing aloud. And the king and all his servants wept too — wept hard.

[37] Absalom fled to Talmai son of Ammihud, king of Geshur. And David mourned for his son day after day.

[38] After Absalom fled to Geshur, he stayed there three years.

[39] And King David's spirit longed to go out to Absalom, because he had come to terms with Amnon's death.

[1] Joab son of Zeruiah could read the king. He saw it: David's heart was leaning toward Absalom.

[2] So Joab sent to Tekoa and brought back a clever woman. He told her, "Play the mourner. Dress in grief, no oil, no perfume — be a woman who has wept for the dead a long time.

[3] Go to the king and say what I tell you to say." And he put the words in her mouth.

[4] When the woman from Tekoa came before the king, she dropped face-down to the ground and cried out, "Save me, O king!"

[5] The king said, "What's wrong?" She said, "I'm a widow. My husband is dead.

[6] Your servant had two sons. They quarreled out in the field. No one to pull them apart — one struck the other and killed him.

[7] Now the whole clan has risen against me, demanding, 'Hand over the one who struck his brother. We'll kill him for the life he took — and finish off the heir while we're at it.' They mean to snuff out the last ember I have left, leaving my husband no name, no remnant on the face of the earth."

[8] The king said to the woman, "Go home. I'll take care of it."

[9] But the woman from Tekoa said, "My lord the king, let the guilt fall on me and on my father's house. Let the king and his throne be innocent of it."

[10] The king said, "If anyone gives you trouble, bring him to me — he won't lay a hand on you again."

[11] She said, "Let the king swear by the LORD his God that the blood-avenger won't multiply destruction by killing my son." And he said, "As the LORD lives, not one hair of your son's head will fall to the ground."

[12] Then the woman said, "Let your servant speak one more word to my lord the king." He said, "Speak."

[13] And she said, "Then why have you plotted exactly this kind of thing against God's people? In what you just declared, the king has convicted himself — because he hasn't brought home the son he banished.

[14] We all die. We're like water spilled on the ground, gone, ungathered. But God does not simply take a life. He devises ways — He works it out so the exile is not cast off forever.

[15] I came to say this to my lord the king because the people frightened me. Your servant thought, 'Let me speak to the king. Maybe he'll do what I ask.

[16] Maybe the king will listen and rescue his servant from the hand of the man who would cut me and my son out of God's inheritance.'

[17] And your servant thought, 'May the word of my lord the king bring me rest. For my lord the king is like the angel of God, weighing good and evil.' May the LORD your God be with you."

[18] Then the king answered the woman: "Don't hide anything from me when I ask you this." She said, "Let my lord the king speak."

[19] The king said, "Is Joab's hand in all of this?" The woman answered, "As surely as you live, my lord the king — no one slips left or right of what my lord the king has said. Yes. Your servant Joab is the one. He told me to come. He put every word in my mouth.

[20] He did it to turn the situation around. But my lord has wisdom like the angel of God — he knows everything that happens in the land."

[21] The king said to Joab, "All right. I'll do it. Go. Bring the young man Absalom back."

[22] Joab fell face-down to the ground and blessed the king. "Today your servant knows he has found favor in your eyes, my lord the king — because the king has granted his servant's request."

[23] Then Joab got up, went to Geshur, and brought Absalom back to Jerusalem.

[24] But the king said, "Let him go to his own house. He is not to see my face." So Absalom went to his own house and did not see the king.

[25] In all Israel there was no one praised for his looks like Absalom. From the sole of his foot to the crown of his head, no flaw.

[26] When he cut his hair — he cut it once a year because it grew heavy on him — he weighed it: two hundred shekels by the royal weight.

[27] Absalom had three sons and a daughter named Tamar. She became a beautiful woman.

[28] Absalom lived in Jerusalem two full years without ever seeing the king's face.

[29] So Absalom sent for Joab to send him to the king. Joab wouldn't come. He sent a second time. Still he wouldn't come.

[30] Absalom said to his servants, "See Joab's field next to mine? He's got barley there. Go burn it." And Absalom's servants set the field on fire.

[31] Then Joab got up and came to Absalom at his house. "Why did your servants set my field on fire?"

[32] Absalom said, "Listen — I sent for you. I said come here, I want to send you to the king to ask, 'Why did I come back from Geshur? Better for me to still be there.' Now let me see the king's face. If there's guilt in me, let him put me to death."

[33] So Joab went to the king and told him. The king summoned Absalom. He came to the king and bowed face-down to the ground before him. And the king kissed Absalom.

[1] Time passed, and Absalom got himself a chariot, horses, and fifty men to run ahead of him.

[2] He made it his habit to rise early and station himself beside the road leading to the city gate. Whenever someone came along with a dispute to bring before the king for judgment, Absalom would call him over and ask, "What town are you from?" The man would answer, "Your servant is from one of the tribes of Israel."

[3] Then Absalom would say, "Look — your case is solid, airtight. But there's no one from the king who'll hear you out."

[4] And he'd add, "If only I were the judge in this land! Anyone with a complaint or a case could come to me, and I'd see that justice was done."

[5] Whenever someone approached to bow before him, Absalom would reach out, take hold of him, and kiss him.

[6] This is how Absalom treated every Israelite who came to the king for judgment. And so he stole the hearts of the people of Israel.

[7] Four years went by. Then Absalom said to the king, "Let me go to Hebron to make good on a vow I swore to the LORD.

[8] When I was living in Geshur in Aram, I made this vow: 'If the LORD really does bring me back to Jerusalem, I'll worship him in Hebron.'"

[9] The king said, "Go in peace." So Absalom got up and went to Hebron.

[10] But Absalom had already sent secret messengers throughout the tribes of Israel: "The moment you hear the trumpet, shout — 'Absalom is king in Hebron!'"

[11] Two hundred men from Jerusalem went with Absalom. They'd been invited as guests and went in good faith — they had no idea what was happening.

[12] While he was offering the sacrifices, Absalom also summoned Ahithophel the Gilonite — David's own counselor — from his hometown of Giloh. The conspiracy gathered strength, and the people streaming to Absalom kept multiplying.

[13] A messenger reached David: "Israel's heart has gone over to Absalom."

[14] David said to all his officials in Jerusalem with him, "On your feet — we have to run. There's no escape from Absalom otherwise. Move fast, before he overtakes us, brings disaster down on us, and puts this city to the sword."

[15] The king's officials answered him, "Whatever our lord the king decides — we're with you."

[16] So the king left, with his whole household behind him. He left ten concubines to look after the palace.

[17] The king went out with all the people following him, and they stopped at the last house.

[18] All his servants marched past him — every Kerethite, every Pelethite, and all six hundred Gittites who had followed him from Gath — filing by the king.

[19] The king said to Ittai the Gittite, "Why are you coming with us? Turn back. Stay with the new king. You're a foreigner, an exile from your own land.

[20] You only got here yesterday — should I drag you up and down with us today when I don't even know where I'm going? Go back. Take your countrymen with you. May steadfast love and faithfulness go with you."

[21] But Ittai answered the king, "As the LORD lives, and as my lord the king lives — wherever my lord the king is, whether it means death or life, that's where your servant will be."

[22] David said, "Then come on — march." So Ittai the Gittite marched on, with all his men and all the families with them.

[23] The whole countryside wept aloud as the people passed by. The king crossed the Kidron Valley, and all the people moved out toward the wilderness road.

[24] And there was Zadok too, with all the Levites carrying the ark of the covenant of God. They set the ark of God down — and Abiathar came up — until all the people had finished filing out of the city.

[25] Then the king said to Zadok, "Take the ark of God back to the city. If I find favor in the LORD's eyes, he'll bring me back and let me see it and its dwelling place again.

[26] But if he says, 'I'm not pleased with you' — here I am. Let him do to me whatever seems good to him."

[27] The king also told Zadok the priest, "Aren't you a seer? Return to the city in peace — and take your son Ahimaaz with you, and Jonathan son of Abiathar. Both your sons go with you.

[28] I'll wait at the fords of the wilderness until word comes from you."

[29] So Zadok and Abiathar carried the ark of God back to Jerusalem and stayed there.

[30] David climbed the Mount of Olives, weeping as he went, head covered, feet bare. And every person with him covered his head, and they climbed weeping.

[31] Someone told David, "Ahithophel is among the conspirators with Absalom." So David prayed: "O LORD, turn Ahithophel's counsel into foolishness."

[32] When David reached the summit, where people worshiped God — there came Hushai the Arkite to meet him, his robe torn and dirt on his head.

[33] David said to him, "If you come with me, you'll only be a burden.

[34] But if you go back to the city and say to Absalom, 'I'm your servant, O king — I used to serve your father, but now I'll serve you' — then you can frustrate Ahithophel's counsel for me.

[35] Won't Zadok and Abiathar the priests be there with you? Whatever you hear from inside the king's palace, pass it on to them.

[36] Their two sons are there with them — Ahimaaz, Zadok's son, and Jonathan, Abiathar's son. Send word to me through them about whatever you learn."

[37] So Hushai, David's friend, slipped into the city just as Absalom was arriving in Jerusalem.

[1] David had just crested the ridge when Ziba — Mephibosheth's steward — met him there, leading a string of donkeys saddled and loaded down with two hundred loaves, a hundred raisin cakes, a hundred summer fruits, and a skin of wine.

[2] The king turned to him. "What are these for?"

Ziba answered, "The donkeys are for your household to ride. The bread and fruit are for the young men to eat. The wine is for whoever collapses in the wilderness."

[3] "And your master's son — where is he?" the king asked.

Ziba said, "Sitting back in Jerusalem. He told himself, 'Today Israel will hand my grandfather's kingdom back to me.'"

[4] David said, "Then everything that belonged to Mephibosheth is now yours."

Ziba bowed low. "I am your servant, my lord the king. May I always find favor in your sight."

[5] As David approached Bahurim, a man came storming out — a relative of Saul's house named Shimei, son of Gera — cursing as he came.

[6] He started hurling stones at David and at every one of his servants, even though warriors and bodyguards lined both sides of the king.

[7] And he kept shouting his curse: "Get out! Get out, you man of blood, you worthless man!

[8] The LORD has dropped on your head every drop of Saul's blood — Saul, whose throne you stole. The LORD has handed the kingdom to your son Absalom. And here you are, caught in your own evil, you murderer."

[9] Abishai son of Zeruiah said to the king, "Why is this dead dog allowed to curse my lord the king? Let me cross over and take his head off."

[10] But the king said, "What business of mine is yours, sons of Zeruiah? If he is cursing because the LORD told him, 'Curse David,' then who has any right to ask why?"

[11] David turned to Abishai and to all his servants. "Look — my own son, who came from my body, is hunting my life. How much more, then, this Benjaminite. Leave him alone. Let him curse. The LORD told him to.

[12] Maybe the LORD will see the wrong being done to me and pay me back with good in place of his cursing today."

[13] So David and his men kept walking the road, while Shimei moved along the ridge above them, cursing as he went, throwing stones and kicking up dust the whole way.

[14] The king and everyone with him arrived exhausted at their destination, and there they finally rested.

[15] Meanwhile Absalom marched into Jerusalem with the men of Israel behind him — and Ahithophel with him.

[16] When Hushai the Archite, David's friend, came before Absalom, he cried out, "Long live the king! Long live the king!"

[17] Absalom said to Hushai, "Is this your loyalty to your friend? Why didn't you go with David?"

[18] Hushai answered, "No — the one chosen by the LORD and by this people and by every man of Israel, his I will be, and with him I will stay.

[19] And besides — whom should I serve, if not the son? As I served your father, so I will serve you."

[20] Then Absalom turned to Ahithophel. "Give us your counsel. What should we do?"

[21] Ahithophel said, "Go in to your father's concubines — the ones he left to watch the house. Then all Israel will hear that you have made yourself reek in your father's nostrils, and every hand with you will be strengthened."

[22] So they pitched a tent for Absalom on the roof, and Absalom went in to his father's concubines in full view of all Israel.

[23] In those days, a word from Ahithophel landed like a word straight from God. That was its weight — both for David and for Absalom.

[1] Then Ahithophel said to Absalom, "Let me handpick twelve thousand men and move out tonight, right now, after David. [2] I'll hit him while he's worn out and weak, terrify him, and his whole crew will scatter. I'll take down only the king— [3] and bring the rest of the people back to you. The man you're hunting— when he falls, everyone comes home. The whole nation, intact, yours."

[4] The plan sounded right to Absalom and to every elder of Israel.

[5] But Absalom said, "Call Hushai the Archite too. Let's hear what he has to say."

[6] When Hushai came in, Absalom laid it out: "Ahithophel has spoken. Do we go with his plan? If not— speak."

[7] Hushai answered, "This time, Ahithophel's counsel is wrong."

[8] He pressed on: "You know your father. You know his men. They're warriors. Right now they're savage— a bear in the wild whose cubs have been stolen. And your father is a soldier. He won't be sleeping with the troops. [9] He's already hidden— some cave, some hole in the ground. The moment a few of your men drop in the first skirmish, word spreads: 'Absalom's army is being slaughtered.' [10] Then even your bravest fighter— a man with a heart like a lion— will melt. Because all Israel knows your father is a fighter, and the men with him are killers.

[11] "Here's what I say: Gather all Israel to yourself— from Dan to Beersheba— sand-on-the-shore numbers. And you yourself lead them into battle. [12] We'll find him wherever he hides and fall on him like dew falling on the ground. Not one of them— not him, not any man with him— will survive. [13] And if he pulls back into a city, all Israel will bring ropes to that city and drag it into the valley until not a pebble remains."

[14] Absalom and every man of Israel said, "Hushai's counsel beats Ahithophel's." Because the LORD had ordered it this way— had decided to wreck Ahithophel's solid advice— to bring disaster on Absalom.

[15] Hushai went straight to Zadok and Abiathar, the priests. "Here's what Ahithophel told Absalom and the elders. Here's what I told them. [16] Now— move. Send word to David, fast: 'Don't camp at the fords of the wilderness tonight. Cross. Cross now. Or the king and everyone with him will be swallowed up.'"

[17] Jonathan and Ahimaaz were waiting at En-rogel— couldn't risk being seen entering the city. A servant girl would slip out, brief them, and they'd run word to King David. [18] But a boy spotted them and ran to tell Absalom. So the two of them moved fast— made it to a man's house in Bahurim. He had a well in his courtyard. They climbed down. [19] The man's wife spread a cloth over the well's mouth, scattered grain on top— nothing to see.

[20] Absalom's men reached the house: "Where are Ahimaaz and Jonathan?" The woman said, "They crossed the brook." The men searched, found nothing, and headed back to Jerusalem.

[21] After they left, the two climbed out of the well and ran to David. "Get up. Cross the water. Now. This is what Ahithophel said against you." [22] David got up, every man with him, and crossed the Jordan. By dawn— not one of them was left on the wrong side.

[23] When Ahithophel saw his counsel had been ignored, he saddled his donkey, rode home to his own town, set his house in order— and hanged himself. They buried him in his father's tomb.

[24] David made it to Mahanaim. Absalom crossed the Jordan— all the men of Israel with him. [25] He'd put Amasa over the army in place of Joab. Amasa was the son of a man named Ithra the Israelite, who'd slept with Abigail daughter of Nahash, sister of Zeruiah, Joab's mother. [26] Israel and Absalom camped in the land of Gilead.

[27] When David arrived at Mahanaim, three men were waiting: Shobi son of Nahash from Rabbah of the Ammonites, Machir son of Ammiel from Lo-debar, and Barzillai the Gileadite from Rogelim. [28] They brought beds, bowls, pottery— wheat, barley, flour, roasted grain, beans, lentils, [29] honey, curds, sheep, cheese from the herd— food for David and his people. They said, "The men out there are starving, exhausted, parched in the wilderness."

[1] David mustered the troops with him and set commanders over them — captains of thousands, captains of hundreds. [2] He divided the army into three columns: one third under Joab, one third under Joab's brother Abishai son of Zeruiah, and one third under Ittai the Gittite. Then David told them, "I'm going out with you myself."

[3] But the men refused. "You can't go. If we run, no one cares. If half of us die, no one cares. But you're worth ten thousand of us. Better that you stay back in the city and send support from there."

[4] "Whatever seems best to you," the king said. So he stood at the gate while the army marched out by hundreds and thousands. [5] And the king gave orders to Joab, Abishai, and Ittai — every soldier heard it — "Deal gently with the young man Absalom. For my sake."

[6] The army poured into open country to engage Israel, and the battle was fought in the forest of Ephraim. [7] There Israel was shattered before David's men — a slaughter that day of twenty thousand. [8] The fighting spread across the whole region, and the forest devoured more men than the sword did.

[9] Absalom blundered straight into David's troops. He was riding his mule, and the mule passed under the tangled branches of a great oak — and his head caught fast in the tree. The mule kept going. He was left hanging there, between heaven and earth.

[10] A man saw it and reported to Joab: "I just saw Absalom hanging in an oak."

[11] "You saw him?" Joab said. "Then why didn't you cut him down on the spot? I'd have given you ten pieces of silver and a soldier's belt."

[12] The man answered, "Even if you laid a thousand pieces of silver in my hand, I wouldn't lift a finger against the king's son. We all heard the king order you and Abishai and Ittai — 'Protect the young man Absalom, for my sake.' [13] If I'd betrayed his life — and nothing escapes the king — you yourself would have stood back and let me hang."

[14] "I'm not wasting time with you," Joab said. He grabbed three javelins and drove them into Absalom's heart while he was still alive, still pinned in the oak. [15] Then ten of Joab's armor-bearers closed in and finished him.

[16] Joab sounded the horn, and the troops broke off the chase. He pulled them back — he wouldn't let them keep killing Israel. [17] They took Absalom, threw him into a deep pit in the forest, and piled a massive heap of stones over him. And all Israel scattered, every man to his tent.

[18] In life, Absalom had taken a pillar and set it up for himself in the King's Valley. "I have no son to carry my name," he said. He named the pillar after himself. It stands there to this day — Absalom's Monument.

[19] Ahimaaz son of Zadok said, "Let me run and bring the king the news — the LORD has delivered him from his enemies."

[20] "Not today," Joab told him. "Bring news another day. Not today. The king's son is dead."

[21] Joab turned to a Cushite. "Go. Tell the king what you saw." The Cushite bowed and took off running.

[22] But Ahimaaz son of Zadok pressed again. "Whatever happens — let me run too, behind the Cushite."

"Why, son?" Joab said. "There's no reward in this message for you."

[23] "Whatever happens — I'm running."

"Run, then." So Ahimaaz took the plain road and outran the Cushite.

[24] David sat between the inner and outer gates. The watchman climbed to the roof of the gate, up onto the wall, and looked out — and there: a man, running alone. [25] He shouted down to the king. The king said, "If he's alone, he carries news." The runner came on, closer and closer.

[26] Then the watchman saw another man running. He called to the gatekeeper: "Look — another man, running alone."

"He brings news too," the king said.

[27] "I can see how the first one runs," the watchman said. "It's the run of Ahimaaz son of Zadok."

"A good man," said the king. "He comes with good news."

[28] Ahimaaz called out to the king, "All is well!" He bowed his face to the ground. "Blessed be the LORD your God, who has delivered up the men who raised their hands against my lord the king."

[29] "And the young man Absalom?" the king asked. "Is he safe?"

Ahimaaz said, "When Joab sent your servant — and me, your servant — I saw a great commotion, but I don't know what it was."

[30] "Step aside," the king said. "Stand there." He stepped aside and stood.

[31] Then the Cushite arrived. "News for my lord the king. The LORD has delivered you today from the hand of all who rose against you."

[32] "And the young man Absalom?" the king asked. "Is he safe?"

The Cushite said, "May the enemies of my lord the king — and every man who rises to harm you — end up like that young man."

[33] The king came apart. He climbed to the room above the gate, weeping as he went, crying out, "My son Absalom — my son, my son Absalom — if only I had died instead of you — Absalom, my son, my son."

[1] Word reached Joab: "The king is weeping. He's in mourning for Absalom."

[2] So the day's victory curdled into grief for the whole army, because the troops heard it said: "The king is broken over his son."

[3] The men slipped back into the city that day like soldiers slinking home in shame after running from a battle.

[4] The king had covered his face. He was crying out at full voice: "My son Absalom! Absalom, my son, my son!"

[5] Joab marched into the king's quarters and let him have it: "Today you've humiliated every man who saved your life — your sons, your daughters, your wives, your concubines.

[6] You love the people who hate you and hate the people who love you. You've made it clear today: your commanders and soldiers mean nothing to you. I see now — if Absalom were alive and all of us were dead, you'd be content.

[7] Get up. Go out there. Speak to your men. Encourage them. Because I swear by the LORD — if you don't go out, not one man will stay with you tonight. And that disaster will be worse than every disaster you've faced from your youth until now."

[8] So the king got up and took his seat at the gate. Word spread through the ranks: "The king is sitting at the gate." And the whole army came and stood before him.

Meanwhile Israel had scattered — every man back to his tent.

[9] Across all the tribes of Israel, people were arguing: "The king rescued us from our enemies. He saved us from the Philistines. And now he's fled the country because of Absalom.

[10] And Absalom — the one we anointed to rule us — is dead in battle. So why is nobody saying anything about bringing the king back?"

[11] King David sent word to the priests Zadok and Abiathar: "Speak to the elders of Judah. Ask them: 'Why are you the last to bring the king home? The talk of all Israel has already reached the king at his residence.

[12] You're my brothers. My own bone and flesh. Why are you the last to bring the king back?'

[13] And tell Amasa: 'Aren't you my own flesh and blood? May God do worse than the worst to me if you don't become my permanent army commander in place of Joab.'"

[14] He won over every man of Judah as if they were one heart. They sent word to the king: "Come back — you and all your men."

[15] So the king started home. He reached the Jordan, and Judah came down to Gilgal to meet him and escort him across.

[16] Shimei son of Gera — the Benjaminite from Bahurim — hurried down with the men of Judah to meet King David.

[17] A thousand Benjaminites came with him. Ziba, the steward of Saul's house, was there too, along with his fifteen sons and twenty servants. They rushed to the Jordan ahead of the king,

[18] crossing back and forth to ferry the king's household across and do whatever he wanted.

Shimei son of Gera threw himself down before the king as he was about to cross the Jordan.

[19] He said, "My lord, don't hold my guilt against me. Don't remember what your servant did wrong the day you left Jerusalem. Don't let the king carry that against me.

[20] I know I sinned. So here I am — the first one from the entire house of Joseph to come down and meet my lord the king."

[21] Abishai son of Zeruiah broke in: "Shouldn't Shimei be executed for this? He cursed the LORD's anointed!"

[22] David said, "What business is this of yours, sons of Zeruiah? You're acting like my enemies today. Should anyone in Israel be put to death today? Don't I know I'm king over Israel again?"

[23] Then the king said to Shimei, "You will not die." And he swore it to him.

[24] Mephibosheth, Saul's grandson, came down to meet the king. He hadn't cared for his feet, trimmed his mustache, or washed his clothes from the day the king left until the day he came home in peace.

[25] When he arrived from Jerusalem to meet the king, the king asked him, "Why didn't you come with me, Mephibosheth?"

[26] He answered, "My lord the king — my servant betrayed me. I told him, 'Saddle a donkey for me so I can ride out with the king' — because I'm lame, you know.

[27] But he slandered me to my lord the king. Still, my lord the king is like an angel of God. Do what seems right to you.

[28] My father's entire house deserved nothing but death from my lord the king. Yet you set me at your own table. What right do I have to ask anything more of the king?"

[29] The king said, "Why keep talking? I've decided: you and Ziba will split the estate."

[30] Mephibosheth said, "Let him have all of it. My lord the king has come home in peace — that's enough for me."

[31] Barzillai the Gileadite came down from Rogelim and crossed the Jordan with the king to send him on his way.

[32] Barzillai was very old — eighty years. He'd kept the king supplied while he stayed at Mahanaim, because he was a man of immense wealth.

[33] The king said to Barzillai, "Cross over with me. I'll take care of you in Jerusalem at my side."

[34] But Barzillai answered the king, "How many years do I have left, that I should go up to Jerusalem with the king?

[35] I'm eighty years old. Can I tell good from bad anymore? Can your servant taste what he eats and drinks? Can I still hear singers, men or women? Why should your servant be one more burden to my lord the king?

[36] Your servant will just cross the Jordan with the king a short way. Why repay me with a reward like that?

[37] Let your servant turn back so I can die in my own town near the graves of my father and mother. But here is Kimham, your servant — let him cross over with my lord the king. Do for him whatever seems good to you."

[38] The king said, "Kimham will cross over with me. I'll do for him whatever pleases you. And anything you want from me, I'll do for you."

[39] So all the people crossed the Jordan, and then the king crossed. The king kissed Barzillai and blessed him, and Barzillai returned home.

[40] The king went on to Gilgal, and Kimham crossed with him. All the troops of Judah escorted the king across, along with half the troops of Israel.

[41] Then all the men of Israel came to the king and complained: "Why have our brothers, the men of Judah, stolen you away? Why did they bring the king and his household across the Jordan, and all David's men with him?"

[42] The men of Judah shot back at the men of Israel: "Because the king is our close relative. Why are you so worked up over this? Have we eaten at the king's expense? Have we taken anything for ourselves?"

[43] The men of Israel answered Judah: "We have ten shares in the king — and we have more claim to David than you do. So why have you despised us? Weren't we the first to talk about bringing our king back?"

But the men of Judah came back harder than the men of Israel.

[1] A troublemaker happened to be there — a Benjamite named Sheba son of Bichri. He blew the ram's horn and shouted, "We have no share in David, no inheritance in Jesse's son! Every man to his tent, Israel!"

[2] So every man of Israel walked away from David and followed Sheba son of Bichri. But the men of Judah stayed glued to their king, all the way from the Jordan to Jerusalem.

[3] David came home to his palace in Jerusalem. He took the ten concubines he had left behind to keep the house and put them under guard. He provided for them but never slept with them again. They lived shut away until the day they died — widows of a living husband.

[4] The king said to Amasa, "Muster the men of Judah for me. Three days. Be back here."

[5] Amasa went to call up Judah, but he took longer than the deadline David had set.

[6] So David said to Abishai, "Sheba son of Bichri is going to hurt us worse than Absalom did. Take my men — go after him — before he finds himself fortified cities and slips through our fingers."

[7] Joab's men marched out behind Abishai — the Kerethites, the Pelethites, every hardened soldier — out of Jerusalem in pursuit of Sheba son of Bichri.

[8] When they reached the great rock at Gibeon, Amasa came to meet them. Joab was wearing his uniform, and over it a belt with a sheathed sword strapped to his hip. As he stepped forward, the sword slipped out.

[9] "How are you, my brother?" Joab said to Amasa. And Joab's right hand reached for Amasa's beard, as if to kiss him.

[10] Amasa never saw the sword in Joab's other hand. Joab drove it into his belly — his insides spilled onto the ground — and he didn't need a second blow. He was dead. Then Joab and his brother Abishai went on after Sheba son of Bichri.

[11] One of Joab's young men stood over the body and called out, "Whoever sides with Joab, whoever's for David — follow Joab!"

[12] But Amasa lay there twitching in his own blood in the middle of the road, and the man saw that everyone passing stopped to stare. So he dragged Amasa off the road into a field and threw a cloak over him — because every man who came up and saw him just stood there.

[13] Once the body was off the road, the troops moved on past, following Joab to hunt down Sheba son of Bichri.

[14] Sheba ran through every tribe of Israel all the way to Abel Beth Maacah, and all the Bichrites rallied to him and followed him in.

[15] Joab's army caught up and laid siege to him at Abel Beth Maacah. They threw up a ramp against the city, right against the outer wall, and began pounding the wall to bring it down.

[16] Then a wise woman called out from inside the city: "Listen! Listen! Tell Joab to come over here. I want to speak with him."

[17] He came over. "Are you Joab?" she asked. "I am," he said. "Hear what your servant has to say," she said. "I'm listening," he answered.

[18] She said, "People used to have a saying — 'Ask at Abel,' and that settled it.

[19] We're the peaceful ones, the faithful ones in Israel. You're trying to destroy a mother city in Israel. Why would you devour what belongs to the LORD?"

[20] "Never," Joab said. "Never. I have no desire to devour or destroy.

[21] That's not what this is. A man from the hill country of Ephraim — Sheba son of Bichri — has lifted his hand against King David. Hand him over, just him, and I'll pull back from the city." The woman told Joab, "His head will come over the wall to you."

[22] Then the woman went to all the people with her wisdom. They cut off the head of Sheba son of Bichri and threw it over to Joab. He blew the ram's horn, and his men scattered from the city, each to his own home. And Joab returned to the king in Jerusalem.

[23] Joab commanded all the army of Israel. Benaiah son of Jehoiada commanded the Kerethites and Pelethites.

[24] Adoram was over forced labor. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the recorder.

[25] Sheva was the scribe. Zadok and Abiathar were the priests.

[26] And Ira the Jairite was David's personal priest.

[1] Famine struck during David's reign — three years of it, year after grinding year. David sought the LORD's face, and the LORD answered: "It's because of Saul. Because of that blood-soaked house. He killed the Gibeonites."

[2] So David summoned the Gibeonites. (These weren't Israelites — they were what remained of the Amorites. Israel had sworn an oath to spare them, but Saul, in his fevered zeal for Israel and Judah, had tried to wipe them out.)

[3] David asked them, "What can I do for you? How do I make this right, so you'll bless the LORD's inheritance?"

[4] The Gibeonites answered, "It's not about silver or gold from Saul's house. And it's not our place to execute anyone in Israel." David said, "Whatever you say, I'll do it."

[5] They said, "The man who tried to destroy us — who plotted to erase us from every corner of Israel —

[6] give us seven of his sons. We'll execute them before the LORD at Gibeah of Saul, the LORD's chosen one." The king said, "Done."

[7] But David spared Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan, Saul's grandson — because of the oath sworn between David and Jonathan in the LORD's name.

[8] So the king took the two sons of Rizpah daughter of Aiah, whom she had borne to Saul — Armoni and Mephibosheth — and the five sons of Saul's daughter Merab, whom she had borne to Adriel son of Barzillai the Meholathite.

[9] He handed them over to the Gibeonites, who executed them on the mountain before the LORD. All seven fell together. They died in the first days of harvest, when the barley was just being cut.

[10] Then Rizpah daughter of Aiah took sackcloth and spread it on a rock for herself. From the beginning of harvest until the rains finally came and fell on the bodies, she did not let the birds of the sky touch them by day or the wild animals by night.

[11] Word reached David — what Rizpah, Saul's concubine, had done.

[12] So David went and retrieved the bones of Saul and his son Jonathan from the men of Jabesh-gilead. (They had stolen the bodies from the public square of Beth-shan, where the Philistines had hung them after striking Saul down on Gilboa.)

[13] He brought up Saul's bones and Jonathan's bones from there, and they gathered up the bones of the seven who had been executed.

[14] They buried them in the land of Benjamin at Zela, in the tomb of Saul's father Kish. Everything the king commanded, they did. And after that, God answered the cry of the land.

[15] War broke out again — Philistines against Israel. David went down with his men to fight, and David grew exhausted.

[16] Then Ishbi-benob, one of the giants' brood — his bronze spear weighed three hundred shekels, and he wore a brand-new sword — declared he would kill David.

[17] But Abishai son of Zeruiah came to David's rescue. He struck the Philistine down and killed him. Then David's men swore an oath to him: "You're not going out to battle with us again. You will not snuff out the lamp of Israel."

[18] Later, another battle with the Philistines erupted at Gob. That's where Sibbecai the Hushathite killed Saph, another of the giants' brood.

[19] At Gob there was yet another battle with the Philistines. Elhanan son of Jair the Bethlehemite killed Goliath the Gittite — the one whose spear shaft was thick as a weaver's beam.

[20] And in another battle, at Gath, there was a massive man with six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot — twenty-four in all. He too was descended from the giants.

[21] When he taunted Israel, Jonathan — son of David's brother Shimei — struck him down.

[22] These four were born to the giants of Gath, and they all fell at the hands of David and his men.

[1] When the LORD pulled David out from under Saul's hand and every other enemy that hunted him, David sang this song back to him:

[2] The LORD is my cliff, my fortress, my way out.
[3] My God is the rock I run to —
my shield, the horn of my salvation,
my stronghold, my refuge,
the one who saves me from violence.

[4] I call on the LORD — the one worthy of praise —
and I am rescued from my enemies.

[5] The waves of death curled around me.
Torrents of destruction roared in my ears.
[6] The ropes of the grave wound tight around me.
The snares of death sprang shut in my face.

[7] In my desperation I called the LORD's name.
I cried out to my God.
From his temple he heard my voice —
my scream reached his ears.

[8] Then the earth bucked and shuddered.
The foundations of the heavens trembled,
shaken because his fury was up.
[9] Smoke poured from his nostrils.
Fire from his mouth — consuming.
Burning coals blazed out of him.
[10] He bent the sky low and came down,
storm-dark beneath his feet.
[11] He mounted a cherub and flew.
He soared on the wings of the wind.
[12] He pulled darkness around himself like a tent —
a canopy of dark water, of thick clouds.
[13] From the brightness in front of him
burning coals broke into flame.
[14] The LORD thundered from heaven.
The Most High let his voice loose.
[15] He shot arrows and scattered them.
Lightning — and they were routed.
[16] The seabeds were exposed.
The foundations of the world stood naked
at the LORD's rebuke,
at the blast of breath from his nostrils.

[17] He reached down from the heights and took hold of me.
He hauled me out of deep water.
[18] He rescued me from my enemy — too strong for me —
from those who hated me, when they had me outmatched.
[19] They jumped me on my worst day,
but the LORD was the wall at my back.
[20] He brought me out into open country.
He rescued me because he delighted in me.

[21] The LORD has dealt with me according to my righteousness.
The cleanness of my hands — he has paid me back for it.
[22] Because I kept to the LORD's ways.
I did not turn from my God toward evil.
[23] All his rulings stayed before me.
I did not push his statutes aside.
[24] I was blameless before him.
I kept myself from doing wrong.
[25] So the LORD repaid me according to my righteousness,
according to my cleanness in his sight.

[26] With the loyal, you are loyal.
With the blameless, you are blameless.
[27] With the pure, you are pure —
but with the twisted, you outmaneuver them.
[28] You save the humbled, lowered crowd,
but your eyes track down the arrogant to bring them down.

[29] You, LORD, are my lamp.
The LORD lights up my darkness.
[30] With you I can charge a battalion.
With my God I can vault a wall.

[31] This God — his way is flawless.
The LORD's word is fire-tested.
He is a shield to everyone who takes shelter in him.

[32] Who is God besides the LORD?
Who is a rock except our God?
[33] God is the fortress wrapped around me,
the one who clears the road in front of me.
[34] He makes my feet like a deer's —
sure-footed on heights that would kill another man.
[35] He trains my hands for war.
My arms can bend a bow of bronze.

[36] You hand me the shield of your salvation.
Your gentleness has made me great.
[37] You widen the path beneath me
so my ankles do not buckle.

[38] I chased my enemies and ran them down.
I did not turn back until they were finished.
[39] I crushed them. They could not get up.
They fell beneath my feet.
[40] You strapped strength on me for battle.
You bent my attackers to the ground beneath me.
[41] You made my enemies turn tail —
those who hated me — I cut them off.
[42] They looked, but no one came to save them.
They cried to the LORD — he did not answer.
[43] I ground them fine as dust on the ground.
Like mud in the streets I stamped them down.

[44] You rescued me from a feuding people.
You set me at the head of nations.
A people I did not know now serves me.
[45] Foreigners come crawling to me.
The instant they hear my name they obey.
[46] Foreigners lose their nerve.
They come trembling out of their strongholds.

[47] The LORD lives! Blessed be my rock!
Exalted be God, the rock of my salvation —
[48] the God who hands me my vengeance,
who brings nations down beneath me,
[49] who pulls me clear of my enemies.
You lifted me above those who rose against me.
You snatched me from the violent man.

[50] So I will praise you, LORD, among the nations.
I will sing your name.
[51] He delivers his king with overwhelming victories.
He shows loyal love to his anointed —
to David and his descendants, forever.

[1] These are David's last words.

The oracle of David son of Jesse,
the oracle of the man God lifted high,
the anointed of the God of Jacob,
Israel's sweet singer:

[2] The Spirit of the LORD spoke through me;
his word was on my tongue.

[3] The God of Israel said it,
the Rock of Israel said to me:
When a man rules people with justice,
when he rules in the fear of God,

[4] he is light at sunrise on a cloudless morning,
the brightness after rain
that draws green shoots from the earth.

[5] Is my house not exactly like this with God?
He cut an eternal covenant with me,
fixed and guaranteed in every detail.
Will he not bring every salvation,
every desire, to its full flowering?

[6] But the worthless — all of them —
are thorns thrown aside,
untouchable by hand.

[7] Anyone who handles them
needs iron and a spear shaft,
and they are burned to nothing
right where they lie.

[8] These are the names of David's warriors.

Josheb-basshebeth the Tahchemonite, chief of the Three. He swung his spear against eight hundred men and killed them in a single engagement.

[9] After him, Eleazar son of Dodo the Ahohite, one of the three mighty men with David when they taunted the Philistines gathered for battle there. Israel's men pulled back,

[10] but Eleazar stood his ground and cut Philistines down until his hand cramped frozen to his sword. The LORD delivered a massive victory that day, and the troops came back behind him — only to strip the dead.

[11] After him, Shammah son of Agee the Hararite. The Philistines massed at a field full of lentils, and the Israelite troops fled before them.

[12] Shammah planted himself in the middle of that field, defended it, and cut the Philistines down. The LORD delivered a massive victory.

[13] During harvest, three of the thirty chiefs came down to David at the cave of Adullam while a Philistine raiding company was camped in the Valley of Rephaim.

[14] David was holed up in the stronghold; a Philistine garrison occupied Bethlehem.

[15] David let slip a longing: "If only someone would bring me a drink of water from the well by the gate of Bethlehem."

[16] So the three broke through the Philistine camp, drew water from the well by the gate of Bethlehem, and carried it back to David. But he would not drink it. He poured it out to the LORD

[17] and said, "Far be it from me, LORD, to drink this. This is the blood of men who walked into death to get it." So he refused to drink.

These were the deeds of the three warriors.

[18] Abishai brother of Joab, son of Zeruiah, was chief of the Thirty. He swung his spear against three hundred and killed them, earning a name beside the Three.

[19] He was the most honored of the Thirty and became their commander, but he never reached the rank of the Three.

[20] Benaiah son of Jehoiada from Kabzeel was a fighter of valor with a record of exploits. He cut down the two champions of Moab. On a snowy day he climbed down into a pit and killed a lion.

[21] He killed an Egyptian — a massive man. The Egyptian had a spear; Benaiah went at him with nothing but a club, ripped the spear out of the Egyptian's hand, and killed him with his own weapon.

[22] These were the deeds of Benaiah son of Jehoiada. He had a name among the three warriors.

[23] He was honored above the Thirty but never reached the Three. David put him in charge of his personal guard.

[24] Asahel brother of Joab was one of the Thirty. Also Elhanan son of Dodo of Bethlehem,

[25] Shammah the Harodite, Elika the Harodite,

[26] Helez the Paltite, Ira son of Ikkesh of Tekoa,

[27] Abiezer of Anathoth, Mebunnai the Hushathite,

[28] Zalmon the Ahohite, Maharai of Netophah,

[29] Heleb son of Baanah the Netophathite, Ittai son of Ribai from Gibeah of Benjamin,

[30] Benaiah of Pirathon, Hiddai from the wadis of Gaash,

[31] Abi-albon the Arbathite, Azmaveth of Bahurim,

[32] Eliahba the Shaalbonite, the sons of Jashen, Jonathan,

[33] Shammah the Hararite, Ahiam son of Sharar the Hararite,

[34] Eliphelet son of Ahasbai the Maacathite, Eliam son of Ahithophel the Gilonite,

[35] Hezro of Carmel, Paarai the Arbite,

[36] Igal son of Nathan from Zobah, Bani the Gadite,

[37] Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai of Beeroth — armor-bearer to Joab son of Zeruiah,

[38] Ira the Ithrite, Gareb the Ithrite,

[39] Uriah the Hittite.

Thirty-seven in all.

[1] The LORD's anger flared against Israel again, and he incited David against them: "Go. Count Israel and Judah."

[2] So the king said to Joab, the commander of his army with him: "Sweep through every tribe of Israel, from Dan to Beersheba. Register the people. I want the number."

[3] But Joab pushed back: "May the LORD your God multiply this people a hundred times over, and may my lord the king live to see it. But why does my lord the king want this thing?"

[4] The king's word overrode Joab and the army commanders. So they left the king's presence to take the census of Israel.

[5] They crossed the Jordan and camped near Aroer, south of the town in the valley, then headed toward Gad and on to Jazer.

[6] They went to Gilead and the region of Tahtim-hodshi, then to Dan-jaan and around to Sidon.

[7] They reached the fortress of Tyre and every town of the Hivites and Canaanites, and finally pushed south to the Negev of Judah at Beersheba.

[8] After traversing the whole land, they returned to Jerusalem nine months and twenty days later.

[9] Joab handed the king the tally: in Israel, eight hundred thousand fighting men who could draw a sword; in Judah, five hundred thousand.

[10] Then David's heart struck him after he had counted the people. He said to the LORD, "I have sinned — deeply — in what I did. Now, LORD, take away your servant's guilt. I have been a fool."

[11] When David rose the next morning, the word of the LORD had already come to the prophet Gad, David's seer:

[12] "Go and tell David: This is what the LORD says — I am offering you three things. Choose one, and I will do it to you."

[13] So Gad came to David and laid it out: "Seven years of famine across your land? Or three months of running from your enemies while they chase you down? Or three days of plague in your land? Think it over. Decide what answer I bring back to the one who sent me."

[14] David said to Gad, "I am in agony. Let us fall into the hand of the LORD — his mercy runs deep. But do not let me fall into human hands."

[15] So the LORD unleashed plague on Israel from that morning until the appointed time. From Dan to Beersheba, seventy thousand of the people died.

[16] When the angel stretched his hand toward Jerusalem to destroy it, the LORD relented from the catastrophe and said to the angel devastating the people, "Enough. Drop your hand." The angel of the LORD was standing at the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

[17] When David saw the angel striking down the people, he said to the LORD, "I am the one who sinned. I am the one who did wrong. These — these sheep — what have they done? Let your hand fall on me and on my father's house."

[18] That day Gad came to David and said, "Go up. Build an altar to the LORD on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite."

[19] David went up as Gad commanded, as the LORD had ordered.

[20] When Araunah looked out and saw the king and his servants coming toward him, he hurried out and bowed face-down to the ground before the king.

[21] Araunah asked, "Why has my lord the king come to his servant?" David answered, "To buy your threshing floor — to build an altar to the LORD, so the plague on the people will be lifted."

[22] Araunah said to David, "Let my lord the king take it and offer whatever seems right. Look — here are the oxen for the burnt offering, the threshing sledges and ox-yokes for wood.

[23] All of it, O king, Araunah gives to the king." And he added, "May the LORD your God accept you."

[24] But the king said to Araunah, "No. I will buy it from you at a price. I will not offer to the LORD my God burnt offerings that cost me nothing." So David bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.

[25] David built an altar there to the LORD and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. The LORD answered the plea for the land, and the plague over Israel stopped.

[1] From Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy — to the church of the Thessalonians, rooted in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ:

[2] Grace to you, and peace, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

[3] We owe God constant thanks for you, brothers and sisters — and rightly so — because your faith is surging forward, and the love each of you has for one another keeps multiplying.

[4] So we boast about you in God's churches. We tell them about your endurance, your faith holding firm through every persecution and every pressure you're enduring.

[5] All of this is evidence — clear evidence — of God's just judgment, proof that you'll be counted worthy of the kingdom of God, the very kingdom you're suffering for.

[6] God is just. He will pay back trouble to those troubling you,

[7] and to you who are being crushed — relief, alongside us — when the Lord Jesus is unveiled from heaven with his mighty angels,

[8] wrapped in blazing fire, dealing out justice to those who refuse to know God and refuse to obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus.

[9] Their sentence: ruin without end, exiled from the presence of the Lord and from the splendor of his power,

[10] on the day he comes to be glorified in his holy ones and marveled at by all who believed — because our witness to you was believed.

[11] This is why we pray for you constantly: that our God will count you worthy of the calling he gave you, and that by his power he will bring to completion every good intention and every act your faith reaches for —

[12] so the name of our Lord Jesus will be glorified in you, and you in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ.

[1] Now about the arrival of our Lord Jesus the Anointed and our gathering to him — we're asking you, brothers and sisters: [2] don't lose your heads. Don't let anyone shake you or panic you — not through some so-called prophecy, not through a sermon, not through a letter supposedly from us — claiming the Day of the Lord has already come.

[3] Don't let anyone con you. That Day doesn't arrive until the great defection happens and the man of lawlessness steps onto the stage — the son of ruin, [4] the one who sets himself against everything called god or sacred, who hoists himself above it all, who plants himself in God's own temple and proclaims himself divine.

[5] Don't you remember? I told you all this when I was still with you. [6] And you know what's holding him back right now, keeping him offstage until his moment comes. [7] The mystery of lawlessness is already at work under the surface — only there's something restraining it, and someone restraining it, until that restraint is pulled away. [8] Then the lawless one breaks into the open — and the Lord Jesus will incinerate him with the breath of his mouth and obliterate him with the brilliance of his coming.

[9] The lawless one shows up with all the firepower of Satan behind him — counterfeit miracles, signs, wonders, [10] every kind of wicked deception aimed at those on their way to destruction. They're perishing because they refused to love the truth that could have saved them. [11] So God hands them over to a delusion that swallows them whole — they'll believe the lie. [12] Every last one of them — those who took no pleasure in truth and reveled in evil — will stand condemned.

[13] But you, brothers and sisters loved by the Lord — we owe God thanks for you, always. He chose you from the beginning for rescue, through the sanctifying work of the Spirit and your faith in what is true. [14] He called you to this through the good news we preached — called you to share in the glory of our Lord Jesus the Anointed.

[15] So plant your feet. Hold tight to what we passed on to you, whether by sermon or by letter.

[16] May our Lord Jesus the Anointed himself — and God our Father, who loved us and out of sheer grace gave us an unshakable comfort and a hope that won't quit — [17] steady your hearts and reinforce you in every good word and every good work.

[1] One last thing, brothers — pray for us. Pray that the message of the Lord runs unhindered and is honored everywhere, the way it was honored among you. [2] And pray we get clear of the warped and malicious people out there — because not everyone has faith. [3] But the Lord is faithful. He will anchor you. He will guard you against the evil one. [4] We're confident in the Lord about you — confident that you're doing what we told you, and that you'll keep doing it. [5] May the Lord steer your hearts straight into the love of God and the endurance of Christ.

[6] Now hear this, brothers — in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ: keep your distance from any believer living like a drifter, ignoring the tradition you received from us. [7] You know exactly how to imitate us. We weren't lazy when we were with you. [8] We didn't sponge a single meal off anyone. We worked. Day and night, grinding, exhausted — so we wouldn't be a weight on any of you. [9] Not because we didn't have the right to be supported — we did. But we wanted to hand you a pattern to follow. [10] Even back then we laid down the rule: if someone refuses to work, they don't eat. [11] Because word has reached us that some of you are loafing around — not busy, just busybodies. [12] To people like that, we issue this directly in the Lord Jesus Christ: settle down, do your work, and earn the bread you eat. [13] As for the rest of you, brothers — don't get tired of doing good.

[14] If anyone won't listen to what we're saying in this letter, mark them. Cut off contact. Let them feel the weight of it and come to their senses. [15] But don't treat them like an enemy. Warn them like family.

[16] May the Lord of peace himself give you peace — every moment, every situation. The Lord be with all of you.

[17] This greeting is in my own hand — Paul. This is the mark on every letter I send. This is how I write. [18] The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with all of you.

[1] Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, sent to announce the promise of life that lives in Christ Jesus—

[2] To Timothy, my beloved son: grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.

[3] I thank God—the God my ancestors served and the God I serve with a clean conscience—every time I remember you in my prayers, which is constantly, night and day.

[4] When I recall your tears, I ache to see you again, and seeing you would fill me with joy.

[5] I keep thinking about the genuine faith inside you—the faith that first lived in your grandmother Lois, then in your mother Eunice, and now, I'm certain of it, lives in you too.
