[6] So here is what I'm telling you: fan the flame. Stir up the gift God placed in you the day I laid my hands on your head.

[7] God did not hand us a spirit of fear. He gave us power. He gave us love. He gave us a mind that holds steady.

[8] So don't be embarrassed about testifying to our Lord, and don't be embarrassed about me, his prisoner. Take your share of the suffering for the gospel—God will supply the strength.

[9] He saved us. He called us to a holy life—not because of anything we did, but because of his own purpose and his own grace, grace handed to us in Christ Jesus before time even began,

[10] and now blazing into the open through the appearing of our Savior Christ Jesus, who shattered death and dragged life and immortality into the light through the gospel.

[11] And to that gospel I was appointed—herald, apostle, teacher.

[12] That's why I'm suffering what I'm suffering. But I'm not ashamed. I know the One I've trusted, and I am convinced he can guard what I've entrusted to him until that Day.

[13] Hold tight to the pattern of sound words you heard from me, with the faith and love that come from Christ Jesus.

[14] Guard the treasure that was entrusted to you. The Holy Spirit who lives in us will help you guard it.

[15] You know what happened—everyone in Asia abandoned me. Phygelus and Hermogenes among them.

[16] May the Lord show mercy to the household of Onesiphorus. He refreshed me again and again. He wasn't ashamed of my chains—

[17] in fact, when he reached Rome, he hunted for me until he found me.

[18] May the Lord grant him mercy on that Day. And you know better than anyone how much he served us back in Ephesus.

[1] So you, my son — find your strength in the grace that flows from Christ Jesus. [2] Take everything you heard me teach in front of many witnesses and hand it off to people you can trust — people with the capacity to teach others in turn. [3] Take your share of the suffering. Be a good soldier of Christ Jesus. [4] No soldier on active duty gets tangled up in civilian affairs — he wants to please the one who enlisted him. [5] No athlete wins the prize unless he competes by the rules. [6] The farmer doing the actual work is the one who deserves first crack at the harvest. [7] Sit with what I'm saying. The Lord will give you insight into all of it.

[8] Keep your eyes on Jesus Christ — risen from the dead, descended from David — this is the gospel I preach, [9] the gospel I'm suffering for, chained up like a criminal. But the word of God is not chained. [10] So I endure all of it for the sake of the chosen ones — that they too might lay hold of the salvation in Christ Jesus, with eternal glory attached.

[11] Here is a saying you can stake your life on:
   If we died with him, we will live with him.
[12] If we endure, we will reign with him.
   If we disown him, he will disown us.
[13] If we are faithless, he remains faithful —
   he cannot deny himself.

[14] Keep reminding people of these things. Charge them before God: stop fighting over words. It accomplishes nothing and wrecks the people listening. [15] Throw yourself into the work of presenting yourself to God as someone tested and approved — a worker who has nothing to be ashamed of, who handles the word of truth with a straight edge. [16] Steer clear of godless chatter. It only drives people deeper into godlessness, [17] and the talk spreads like gangrene. Hymenaeus and Philetus are in that crowd — [18] they've gone off the rails on the truth, claiming the resurrection has already happened, and they're capsizing people's faith.

[19] Still, God's foundation stands rock-solid, sealed with this inscription: *The Lord knows who belongs to him*, and *Let everyone who names the name of the Lord turn away from evil.*

[20] In a large house there are not only vessels of gold and silver but also of wood and clay — some for honored use, some for disgrace. [21] So if a person scrubs himself clean of all that, he becomes a vessel set apart for honor — useful to the Master, ready for every good work.

[22] Run from the cravings of youth. Chase righteousness, faith, love, peace — alongside those who call on the Lord with a clean heart. [23] Refuse to entertain stupid, ignorant arguments — you know they only breed fights. [24] The Lord's servant has no business fighting. He must be kind to everyone, able to teach, patient when wronged, [25] gently correcting those who push back — hoping God will grant them a change of heart leading to knowledge of the truth, [26] so they wake up and slip out of the devil's snare, where they've been held captive to do his will.

[1] Know this: the final days will be brutal. [2] People will love only themselves and their money. They will brag, strut, sneer, defy their parents, forget gratitude, abandon anything sacred. [3] No natural affection. No willingness to reconcile. Slanderers. No self-control. Savage. Allergic to goodness. [4] Betrayers. Reckless. Drunk on themselves. In love with pleasure instead of in love with God— [5] keeping the outward shape of faith while gutting it of any real power. Stay away from people like that.

[6] These are the kind who slither into homes and capture vulnerable women weighed down by guilt, women yanked around by every craving, [7] always learning, never actually arriving at the truth. [8] Just like Jannes and Jambres stood against Moses, these men stand against the truth—minds corroded, faith proven counterfeit. [9] But they will not get far. Their foolishness will become obvious to everyone, just as it did with those two.

[10] You, though—you have tracked with me through all of it: my teaching, my way of life, my purpose, my faith, my patience, my love, my endurance, [11] the persecutions, the suffering—what happened to me in Antioch, Iconium, Lystra. The persecutions I absorbed. And out of every one of them, the Lord pulled me clear.

[12] In fact, anyone determined to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will be persecuted. Count on it. [13] Meanwhile, evil people and con artists will spiral further down, deceiving and being deceived.

[14] But you—hold your ground. Stay in what you have learned and believed. You know who taught you. [15] From childhood you have known the sacred writings, which are able to make you wise toward salvation through faith in Christ Jesus.

[16] Every word of Scripture is God-breathed and useful—for teaching, for confronting, for course-correcting, for training in what is right, [17] so that the person who belongs to God stands fully equipped, ready for every good work.

[1] Before God and before Christ Jesus — the One who will judge the living and the dead, the One whose appearing brings his kingdom — I charge you:

[2] Preach the word. Stay ready, in season and out. Correct. Rebuke. Encourage. Do it with relentless patience and careful teaching.

[3] Because a time is coming when people will not tolerate sound teaching. They will scratch their itching ears, hunting down teachers who tell them what they want to hear.

[4] They will turn their ears from the truth and chase after myths.

[5] You — keep your head. Endure the hard road. Do the work of a herald of the good news. Finish what you were given to do.

[6] As for me — I am already being poured out. The time of my departure has arrived.

[7] I fought the good fight. I finished the race. I kept the faith.

[8] Now the crown is waiting — the crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the just Judge, will hand me on that Day. And not just me, but everyone who has loved his appearing.

[9] Hurry. Come to me as soon as you can.

[10] Demas abandoned me — he fell in love with this present world and left for Thessalonica. Crescens went to Galatia. Titus to Dalmatia.

[11] Only Luke is with me. Get Mark and bring him with you — he is useful to me for the work.

[12] I sent Tychicus to Ephesus.

[13] When you come, bring the cloak I left at Carpus's place in Troas. And the scrolls. Especially the parchments.

[14] Alexander the coppersmith did me serious harm. The Lord will repay him for what he did.

[15] Watch out for him yourself — he stood in fierce opposition to our message.

[16] At my first defense, no one stood with me. Everyone deserted me. May it not be held against them.

[17] But the Lord stood by me. He gave me strength so the message would be preached in full and every gentile would hear it. And I was rescued from the lion's mouth.

[18] The Lord will rescue me from every evil attack and bring me safely into his heavenly kingdom. To him be the glory forever and ever. Amen.

[19] Greet Prisca and Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus.

[20] Erastus stayed in Corinth. Trophimus I left sick in Miletus.

[21] Do everything you can to get here before winter. Eubulus sends his greetings. So do Pudens, Linus, Claudia, and all the brothers and sisters.

[22] The Lord be with your spirit. Grace be with you all.

[1] From the elder, to my dear Gaius — the one I love in truth.

[2] Beloved, I'm praying that everything goes well for you and that your body holds up as strongly as your soul is holding up.

[3] Nothing makes me happier than when brothers show up and tell me the truth about you — how you're walking in it, living it out.

[4] No joy hits deeper than hearing my children are walking in the truth.

[5] Beloved, you are doing faithful work in how you care for the brothers — even the ones who are strangers to you.

[6] They've testified to your love in front of the whole assembly. Send them on their way in a manner worthy of God, and you'll do beautifully.

[7] They went out for the sake of the Name, taking nothing from the pagans.

[8] So we ought to support people like this — to become fellow workers with the truth.

[9] I wrote something to the church, but Diotrephes — who has to be first in everything — refuses to acknowledge us.

[10] So when I come, I'm going to bring up what he's been doing: spewing malicious nonsense about us. And that's not enough for him — he refuses to welcome the brothers himself, blocks anyone who tries, and throws them out of the church.

[11] Beloved, don't imitate evil — imitate good. The one who does good belongs to God. The one who does evil has never even seen Him.

[12] Demetrius gets glowing testimony from everyone — even from the truth itself. We vouch for him too, and you know our witness holds up.

[13] I have a lot more to write, but I'm not going to do it with ink and pen.

[14] I'm hoping to see you soon, and then we'll talk face to face.

[15] Peace to you. The friends here greet you. Greet the friends there, each one by name.

[1] Theophilus—my first account covered everything Jesus began doing and teaching [2] right up to the day he was taken up, after he'd given his final instructions through the Holy Spirit to the apostles he'd chosen.

[3] After he suffered, he showed himself alive to them with proof you couldn't argue with. Over forty days he kept appearing, talking about the kingdom of God. [4] During one of those meals together, he gave them an order: don't leave Jerusalem. Wait. Wait for what the Father promised—what you heard me talk about. [5] John baptized with water. In a few days, you'll be baptized in the Holy Spirit.

[6] So when they were together, they pressed him: "Lord—is this it? Are you finally restoring the kingdom to Israel?"

[7] He told them: "The when and the timing—that's not yours to know. The Father holds those in his own authority. [8] But power is coming. The Holy Spirit will fall on you, and you'll be my witnesses—in Jerusalem, throughout Judea and Samaria, all the way to the ends of the earth."

[9] And as he said it, he was lifted up right in front of them. A cloud swallowed him, and they couldn't see him anymore. [10] They stood there staring at the sky, watching him go—and suddenly two men in white were standing next to them.

[11] "Galileans—why are you standing here staring at the sky? This Jesus, the one taken from you up into heaven—he'll come back the same way you watched him leave."

[12] So they made their way back to Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives—a Sabbath's walk from the city. [13] When they got there, they went up to the room where they'd been staying. Peter, John, James, Andrew. Philip, Thomas, Bartholomew, Matthew. James son of Alphaeus, Simon the Zealot, Judas son of James. [14] They devoted themselves to prayer, all of them together—with the women, with Mary the mother of Jesus, with his brothers.

[15] During those days Peter stood up among the believers—about a hundred and twenty of them gathered together—and said:

[16] "Brothers and sisters, the Scripture had to be fulfilled—what the Holy Spirit spoke long ago through David about Judas, the one who guided the mob that arrested Jesus. [17] He was one of us. He had a share in this ministry."

[18] (With the money he got for that betrayal, Judas bought a field. He fell headfirst there—his body burst open, his guts spilled out. [19] Everyone in Jerusalem heard about it, so they named the place Akeldama in their own language. Field of Blood.)

[20] "It's written in the Psalms: *Let his place become a wasteland; let no one live there.* And: *Let someone else take his position.*

[21] "So—one of the men who's been with us the whole time the Lord Jesus moved among us, [22] from John's baptism all the way to the day he was taken up—one of these has to become a witness with us of the resurrection."

[23] They put forward two: Joseph called Barsabbas, also known as Justus, and Matthias. [24] Then they prayed: "Lord, you know every heart. Show us which of these two you've chosen [25] to take the place in this ministry and apostleship that Judas abandoned to go where he belonged."

[26] They cast lots. The lot fell to Matthias. He was added to the eleven apostles.

[1] When the day of Pentecost arrived, they were all together in one place. [2] Suddenly a sound tore out of the sky — like a hurricane bearing down — and it filled the entire house where they were sitting. [3] Tongues appeared among them, fire-shaped, splitting apart and settling on each one of them. [4] Every one of them was filled with the Holy Spirit, and they began to speak in other languages — whatever languages the Spirit gave them to speak.

[5] Now Jerusalem was packed with devout Jews from every nation under heaven. [6] When the sound erupted, a crowd rushed in, stunned — because each person was hearing their own native language come out of these mouths. [7] They couldn't believe it. "Wait — aren't all these people Galileans?" they kept asking. [8] "Then how is it that we're each hearing our own mother tongue? [9] Parthians, Medes, Elamites — people from Mesopotamia, Judea, Cappadocia, Pontus, Asia, [10] Phrygia, Pamphylia, Egypt, the regions of Libya around Cyrene, visitors from Rome — [11] Jews and converts alike, Cretans, Arabs — we hear them telling, in our own languages, the magnificent things God has done." [12] They stood there bewildered, asking each other, "What does this mean?" [13] Others sneered: "They've had too much wine."

[14] Then Peter stood up with the Eleven, raised his voice, and addressed them: "Fellow Jews, every one of you visiting Jerusalem — listen carefully. Let me explain what's happening. [15] These people aren't drunk, like you assume. It's only nine in the morning. [16] No — this is what the prophet Joel was talking about:

[17] 'In the last days, God says, I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh. Your sons and daughters will prophesy, your young will see visions, your old will dream dreams. [18] Even on my servants — men and women alike — I will pour out my Spirit in those days, and they will prophesy. [19] I will set wonders in the sky above and signs on the earth below: blood and fire and billowing smoke. [20] The sun will go black, the moon turn to blood, before the great and luminous Day of the Lord arrives. [21] And everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.'

[22] "Israelites, hear me. Jesus of Nazareth was a man God authenticated to you with miracles and wonders and signs — God working through him, right in front of you, as you well know. [23] This man was handed over according to God's deliberate plan and foreknowledge — and you, through the hands of lawless men, nailed him to a cross and killed him. [24] But God raised him up, breaking the chains of death — because death had no power to keep its grip on him. [25] David said it about him:

'I saw the Lord always before me, at my right hand — I will not be shaken. [26] So my heart is glad and my tongue sings out; even my body will rest in hope. [27] You will not abandon my soul to the grave, nor let your Holy One see decay. [28] You have shown me the paths of life; you will fill me with joy in your presence.'

[29] "Brothers, let me speak plainly about our father David: he died, he was buried, his tomb is still here among us to this day. [30] But he was a prophet. He knew God had sworn an oath to seat one of his descendants on his throne. [31] Seeing what was coming, he spoke about the resurrection of the Messiah — that he would not be abandoned to the grave, nor would his body see decay. [32] This Jesus — God raised him up. And all of us are witnesses to it. [33] Lifted to the right hand of God, having received from the Father the promised Holy Spirit, he has poured out what you are now seeing and hearing. [34] David never ascended to the heavens, but he himself said:

'The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand [35] until I make your enemies a footstool beneath your feet.'

[36] "So let every house in Israel know this for certain: God has made him both Lord and Messiah — this Jesus, the one you crucified."

[37] The words hit them like a blade to the heart. They turned to Peter and the other apostles: "Brothers — what do we do?"

[38] Peter answered, "Repent. Every one of you — be baptized in the name of Jesus the Messiah for the forgiveness of your sins. And you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit. [39] The promise is for you, and for your children, and for everyone far off — for as many as the Lord our God calls to himself."

[40] He kept testifying, kept pleading with them: "Save yourselves from this twisted generation." [41] Those who took his message to heart were baptized — and that day about three thousand people were added to their number.

[42] They committed themselves to the apostles' teaching, to shared life, to breaking bread, to prayer. [43] A holy awe settled over everyone, and many wonders and signs were happening through the apostles. [44] All the believers were together, holding everything in common. [45] They sold their possessions and property, distributing the proceeds to anyone in need. [46] Day after day they were together in the temple courts, breaking bread in their homes, eating with joyful and uncomplicated hearts, [47] praising God and enjoying the favor of all the people. And the Lord kept adding to their number day by day — those who were being saved.

[1] Peter and John were heading up to the temple at three in the afternoon — the hour of prayer.

[2] A man was being carried in, lame from the womb. They set him down every day at the temple gate called Beautiful so he could beg from the people streaming inside.

[3] When he saw Peter and John about to enter, he asked them for money.

[4] Peter locked eyes with him. So did John. "Look at us," Peter said.

[5] The man fixed his attention on them, expecting something.

[6] Then Peter: "Silver and gold I don't have. But what I do have, I give you — in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk."

[7] He grabbed the man's right hand and pulled him up. Instantly the feet and ankles snapped into strength.

[8] He sprang up, stood, and started walking. Then he went into the temple with them — walking, leaping, shouting praise to God.

[9] Everyone saw him walking and praising God,

[10] and they recognized him — this was the man who sat begging at the Beautiful Gate. Astonishment hit them like a wave. They couldn't comprehend what had just happened to him.

[11] As he clung to Peter and John, the whole crowd came running, dumbstruck, to where they stood in Solomon's Colonnade.

[12] Peter saw it and spoke: "Men of Israel — why are you stunned by this? Why stare at us as if we made him walk by our own power, our own piety?

[13] The God of Abraham, of Isaac, of Jacob — the God of our fathers — has glorified his servant Jesus. The one you handed over. The one you rejected to Pilate's face when Pilate had decided to release him.

[14] You rejected the Holy One, the Righteous One, and demanded a murderer be given to you instead.

[15] You killed the Author of life. God raised him from the dead — we are witnesses.

[16] And it is his name — faith in his name — that has made this man strong, this man you see and recognize. The faith that comes through Jesus has given him this complete wholeness in front of all of you.

[17] Now, brothers — I know you acted in ignorance, as did your rulers.

[18] But God was fulfilling what he had announced through every prophet: that his Messiah would suffer.

[19] So turn. Repent. Let your sins be wiped away, so that seasons of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord,

[20] and he may send the Messiah appointed for you — Jesus.

[21] Heaven must hold him until the time comes to restore everything, just as God promised long ago through the mouths of his holy prophets.

[22] Moses said: 'The Lord your God will raise up a prophet like me for you from among your own people. Listen to him in everything he tells you.

[23] Anyone who refuses to listen to that prophet will be cut off from the people.'

[24] And every prophet who spoke — from Samuel on down — announced these days.

[25] You are the heirs of the prophets and of the covenant God made with your fathers when he said to Abraham: 'Through your offspring all the families of the earth will be blessed.'

[26] God raised up his servant and sent him first to you — to bless you by turning each of you from your wickedness."

[1] While Peter and John were still speaking to the crowd, they were interrupted — the priests, the captain of the temple guard, and the Sadducees descended on them, [2] furious that these men were teaching the people and announcing — through Jesus — that the dead rise. [3] They seized them and threw them in jail overnight, since it was already evening. [4] But the word had landed. Many who heard it believed, and the number of men swelled to about five thousand.

[5] The next morning Jerusalem's power structure assembled — the rulers, the elders, the scribes, [6] Annas the high priest, Caiaphas, John, Alexander, and everyone of high-priestly blood. [7] They stood Peter and John in the center and demanded: "By what power? In whose name did you do this?"

[8] Then Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, spoke: "Rulers of the people and elders, [9] if we're on trial today for an act of kindness to a crippled man — if you want to know how he was healed — [10] then know this, all of you, and let all Israel know: it was the name of Jesus the Messiah of Nazareth, the one you crucified, the one God raised from the dead. By that name this man stands here before you, whole. [11] He is the stone you builders threw out — and he has become the cornerstone. [12] There is salvation in no one else. Under heaven, no other name has been given to humanity by which we can be saved."

[13] They saw Peter's nerve, and John's, and realized these were untrained men, ordinary men — and it stunned them. Then it clicked: these men had been with Jesus. [14] And with the healed man standing right there beside them, there was nothing they could say. [15] So they ordered them out of the chamber and conferred among themselves: [16] "What do we do with these men? Everyone in Jerusalem knows a real miracle happened through them. We can't deny it. [17] But to keep it from spreading any further — let's threaten them. Tell them never to speak that name to anyone again."

[18] They called them back in and ordered them flatly: no speaking, no teaching, in the name of Jesus. Ever.

[19] Peter and John answered: "You decide — is it right in God's eyes to listen to you instead of him? [20] Because we cannot — we will not — stop speaking about what we have seen and heard."

[21] After more threats, they let them go. They couldn't find a way to punish them — the people were glorifying God for what had happened. [22] The man miraculously healed was over forty years old.

[23] Released, Peter and John went straight to their own and reported everything — what the chief priests and elders had said. [24] When they heard it, they lifted their voices together to God: "Sovereign Lord — you made the heavens and the earth and the sea and everything in them. [25] You spoke through the Holy Spirit through the mouth of our father David, your servant:

*Why do the nations rage,
why do the peoples plot in vain?
[26] The kings of the earth take their stand,
the rulers gather as one —
against the Lord
and against his Anointed.*

[27] And that's exactly what happened — right here in this city. Herod and Pontius Pilate, with the Gentiles and the people of Israel, gathered against your holy servant Jesus, the one you anointed, [28] to do whatever your hand and your plan had already decided would happen. [29] And now, Lord — look at their threats. Give your servants the boldness to keep speaking your word. [30] Stretch out your hand to heal. Let signs and wonders happen through the name of your holy servant Jesus."

[31] When they finished praying, the place where they were gathered shook. They were all filled with the Holy Spirit, and they spoke God's word with fearless courage.

[32] The whole company of believers had one heart, one soul. No one claimed anything as their own. Everything was held in common. [33] With tremendous power the apostles kept testifying to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus, and grace rested heavy on them all. [34] No one among them lacked a thing. Those who owned land or houses sold them, brought the proceeds, [35] and laid the money at the apostles' feet — and it was distributed to anyone in need.

[36] Joseph — the one the apostles called Barnabas, which means "son of encouragement" — a Levite from Cyprus, [37] sold a field he owned, brought the money, and laid it at the apostles' feet.

[1] But a man named Ananias, along with his wife Sapphira, sold a piece of property [2] and skimmed off part of the price for himself — his wife was in on it — then brought the rest and laid it at the apostles' feet, like it was the whole sum.

[3] Peter said, "Ananias, why has Satan filled your heart to lie to the Holy Spirit and hold back part of the proceeds from the land? [4] Before you sold it, wasn't it yours? After you sold it, wasn't the money yours to do with as you pleased? What possessed you to plot this in your heart? You haven't lied to people. You've lied to God."

[5] The moment Ananias heard those words, he collapsed and died. Terror swept through everyone who heard. [6] The young men got up, wrapped him, carried him out, and buried him.

[7] About three hours later, his wife walked in, oblivious to what had happened. [8] Peter said to her, "Tell me — did you sell the land for this amount?"

"Yes," she said. "That's the amount."

[9] Peter said, "Why did the two of you agree to test the Spirit of the Lord? Listen — the footsteps of the men who buried your husband are at the door. They'll carry you out too."

[10] Instantly she dropped at his feet, dead. The young men came in, found her gone, and carried her out and buried her beside her husband. [11] Awe — the trembling kind — fell on the whole church and on everyone who heard about it.

[12] Meanwhile the apostles were working signs and wonders in plain sight among the people. The believers all gathered together in Solomon's Colonnade. [13] No outsider dared attach himself to them — but the people held them in awe. [14] And still, more and more believed in the Lord — crowds of men and women — they kept piling on.

[15] It got to the point where people carried the sick out into the streets and laid them on cots and mats, hoping that as Peter passed by even his shadow might fall on one of them. [16] Crowds streamed in from the towns around Jerusalem, bringing the sick and those tormented by unclean spirits — and every one of them was healed.

[17] Then the high priest moved. He and his crew — the Sadducee party — were eaten alive with jealousy. [18] They seized the apostles and threw them in the public jail.

[19] But during the night an angel of the Lord opened the prison doors, led them out, [20] and said, "Go. Stand in the temple courts and tell the people everything about this Life."

[21] At daybreak they walked into the temple and started teaching, exactly as told. Meanwhile the high priest and his men convened the Sanhedrin — the full council of Israel's elders — and sent to the jail to have the prisoners brought in.

[22] But when the officers got there, the cell was empty. They went back and reported: [23] "We found the jail locked tight, guards posted at the doors — but when we opened up, no one was inside."

[24] When the captain of the temple guard and the chief priests heard this, they were utterly thrown — what was this turning into?

[25] Then someone burst in: "The men you locked up — they're standing in the temple right now, teaching the people!"

[26] The captain went with his officers and brought them in — gently, no force — because they were afraid the crowd would stone them.

[27] They marched the apostles in and stood them before the council. The high priest interrogated them: [28] "We gave you strict orders not to teach in this name. And look — you've filled Jerusalem with your teaching, and you mean to bring this man's blood down on us."

[29] Peter and the apostles answered: "We must obey God rather than men. [30] The God of our fathers raised Jesus — the one you killed by hanging him on a tree. [31] God exalted him to his right hand as Prince and Savior, to give Israel repentance and forgiveness of sins. [32] We are witnesses of these things — and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey him."

[33] When they heard that, they were cut to the core and wanted them dead.

[34] But a Pharisee in the council named Gamaliel — a teacher of the law, respected by everyone — stood up and ordered the apostles taken outside for a moment. [35] Then he addressed them: "Men of Israel, think carefully about what you're about to do to these men. [36] Some time back Theudas turned up, claiming to be somebody. About four hundred men rallied to him. He was killed, his followers scattered, and the whole thing came to nothing. [37] After him, Judas the Galilean rose up during the census and pulled a crowd after him. He died too, and his followers were scattered. [38] So here's my counsel: leave these men alone. Let them go. If this plan or this movement is human, it will collapse on its own. [39] But if it's from God — you won't be able to stop them. You might just find yourselves fighting God."

They were persuaded. [40] They called the apostles back in, had them flogged, ordered them not to speak in the name of Jesus, and let them go.

[41] The apostles walked out of the Sanhedrin rejoicing — counted worthy to suffer disgrace for the Name. [42] And every single day, in the temple and house to house, they never stopped — teaching and announcing the good news: Jesus is the Messiah.

[1] The disciples were multiplying fast, and friction surfaced. The Greek-speaking Jews started complaining against the Hebrew-speaking ones: their widows were getting overlooked in the daily food distribution.

[2] So the Twelve gathered the whole community of disciples and said, "It's not right for us to step away from preaching God's word to wait tables.

[3] Brothers, pick seven men from among yourselves — men with solid reputations, full of the Spirit and wisdom. We'll hand this responsibility to them.

[4] That frees us to give ourselves entirely to prayer and the ministry of the word."

[5] The whole assembly liked the proposal. They chose Stephen — a man saturated with faith and the Holy Spirit — along with Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolas, a convert from Antioch.

[6] They brought these seven before the apostles, who prayed and laid hands on them.

[7] God's word kept spreading. The number of disciples in Jerusalem kept multiplying. Even a large company of priests surrendered to the faith.

[8] Stephen, brimming with grace and power, was working stunning signs and wonders among the people.

[9] But opposition rose up — men from the synagogue called "The Freedmen," along with Jews from Cyrene, Alexandria, Cilicia, and Asia. They started debating with Stephen,

[10] but they couldn't stand against the wisdom and the Spirit that spoke through him.

[11] So they got desperate. They secretly recruited men to claim, "We heard him blaspheming Moses and God."

[12] They stirred up the people, the elders, and the scribes. They came at Stephen, seized him, and dragged him before the Sanhedrin.

[13] They produced false witnesses who said, "This man won't stop attacking this holy place and the Law.

[14] We've heard him say that Jesus of Nazareth will tear this place down and overturn the customs Moses handed us."

[15] Everyone sitting in the council stared at him — and saw his face shining like the face of an angel.

[1] The high priest said, "Is this true?"

[2] Stephen answered: "Brothers, fathers, listen. The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham when he was still in Mesopotamia, before he ever settled in Haran,

[3] and said: 'Leave your country, leave your relatives, and go to the land I'll show you.'

[4] So he left the land of the Chaldeans and settled in Haran. After his father died, God moved him from there into this land where you now live.

[5] God gave him no inheritance here — not even a foothold. But he promised to give it to him as a possession, and to his descendants after him, though at the time Abraham had no child.

[6] God told him this: his descendants would be strangers in a foreign land, enslaved and mistreated for four hundred years.

[7] 'But I will judge the nation that enslaves them,' God said, 'and afterward they'll come out and worship me in this place.'

[8] Then God gave him the covenant of circumcision. And so Abraham fathered Isaac and circumcised him on the eighth day. Isaac fathered Jacob. Jacob fathered the twelve patriarchs.

[9] The patriarchs, eaten up with jealousy, sold Joseph into Egypt. But God was with him.

[10] God pulled him out of every affliction. He gave him favor and wisdom before Pharaoh, king of Egypt — who made him governor over Egypt and over his entire household.

[11] Then famine struck all of Egypt and Canaan, terrible suffering, and our fathers couldn't find food.

[12] When Jacob heard there was grain in Egypt, he sent our fathers — that was the first trip.

[13] On the second, Joseph revealed himself to his brothers, and Pharaoh learned about Joseph's family.

[14] Joseph sent for his father Jacob and brought down the whole clan — seventy-five people in all.

[15] Jacob came down into Egypt. He died there. So did our fathers.

[16] Their bones were carried back to Shechem and laid in the tomb Abraham had bought for a sum of silver from the sons of Hamor in Shechem.

[17] As the time drew near for God to fulfill what he'd sworn to Abraham, our people in Egypt multiplied and grew strong —

[18] until a new king rose over Egypt who knew nothing of Joseph.

[19] He played our people for fools, brutalized our ancestors, and forced them to abandon their infants so they wouldn't survive.

[20] In that hour Moses was born — beautiful in God's sight. He was nursed for three months in his father's house.

[21] When he was put outside, Pharaoh's daughter took him in and raised him as her own son.

[22] Moses was schooled in all the wisdom of Egypt — powerful in his words and in his deeds.

[23] When he turned forty, it came into his heart to visit his brothers, the sons of Israel.

[24] He saw one of them being mistreated, and he intervened. He avenged the oppressed man by striking down the Egyptian.

[25] He assumed his brothers would understand that God was using his hand to deliver them. They didn't understand.

[26] The next day he came upon two Israelites fighting and tried to reconcile them. 'Men, you're brothers,' he said. 'Why are you hurting each other?'

[27] But the one in the wrong shoved him aside. 'Who made you ruler and judge over us?

[28] Are you going to kill me the way you killed that Egyptian yesterday?'

[29] At that word, Moses ran. He lived as a foreigner in Midian, where he fathered two sons.

[30] Forty years later, in the wilderness near Mount Sinai, an angel appeared to him in the flame of a burning bush.

[31] Moses stared, astonished. As he moved closer to look, the voice of the Lord came:

[32] 'I am the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, of Isaac, of Jacob.' Moses trembled. He didn't dare look.

[33] The Lord said: 'Take the sandals off your feet — the ground you're standing on is holy.

[34] I have seen — really seen — how my people are being crushed in Egypt. I've heard their groaning. I've come down to rescue them. Now go. I'm sending you to Egypt.'

[35] This same Moses — the one they rejected, saying, 'Who made you ruler and judge?' — God sent as ruler and deliverer, by the hand of the angel who appeared to him in the bush.

[36] He led them out, performing wonders and signs in Egypt, at the Red Sea, and through forty years in the wilderness.

[37] This is the same Moses who told the Israelites: 'God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your own brothers.'

[38] This is the one who was in the assembly in the wilderness — with the angel who spoke to him at Sinai and with our fathers — the one who received living words to pass on to us.

[39] But our fathers refused to obey him. They pushed him away. In their hearts, they turned back toward Egypt.

[40] They told Aaron: 'Make us gods to walk in front of us. This Moses who led us out of Egypt — we don't know what's happened to him.'

[41] And that's when they made the calf. They offered sacrifices to the idol and threw a party for the work of their own hands.

[42] So God turned away. He handed them over to worship the host of heaven — just as it's written in the book of the prophets: 'Did you offer me sacrifices and offerings those forty years in the wilderness, house of Israel?

[43] No — you carried the tent of Moloch and the star of your god Rephan, the images you made for yourselves to worship. So I will deport you beyond Babylon.'

[44] Our fathers had the tent of witness in the wilderness, built exactly as the One who spoke to Moses ordered — according to the pattern he'd seen.

[45] Our fathers received it and brought it in with Joshua when they dispossessed the nations God drove out before them. It stayed until the days of David.

[46] David found favor with God and asked permission to provide a dwelling for the God of Jacob.

[47] But it was Solomon who built him a house.

[48] Yet the Most High doesn't live in structures made by human hands. As the prophet says:

[49] 'Heaven is my throne. The earth is the footstool under my feet. What kind of house would you build for me? — says the Lord — Where could I possibly rest?

[50] Didn't my own hand make all of this?'

[51] Stiff-necked. Uncircumcised in heart and ears. You always — always — fight the Holy Spirit. Just like your fathers, so you.

[52] Which prophet did your fathers not persecute? They killed the ones who announced ahead of time the coming of the Righteous One — and now you have become his betrayers and murderers.

[53] You received the law as it was delivered by angels — and you didn't keep it."

[54] When they heard this, it cut them to the heart. They ground their teeth at him.

[55] But Stephen, full of the Holy Spirit, looked up into heaven and saw the glory of God — and Jesus standing at God's right hand.

[56] "Look," he said. "I see the heavens torn open and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God."

[57] At that they screamed, covered their ears, and rushed him as one.

[58] They dragged him outside the city and started hurling stones. The witnesses laid their coats at the feet of a young man named Saul.

[59] As the stones came down, Stephen called out: "Lord Jesus, receive my spirit."

[60] He dropped to his knees and cried with everything he had: "Lord, don't hold this sin against them." And with that word, he fell asleep.

[1] Saul stood there giving his full approval to the killing. That same day, a violent persecution broke out against the church in Jerusalem, and everyone scattered across Judea and Samaria — everyone except the apostles.

[2] Some devout men buried Stephen and mourned him with deep, public grief.

[3] But Saul was tearing the church apart. He went house to house, dragging out men and women alike and hauling them off to prison.

[4] Those who scattered, though — they went everywhere announcing the message.

[5] Philip headed down to a city in Samaria and started proclaiming the Messiah to them.

[6] The crowds locked in on what he was saying. They listened as one, watching the signs he was doing.

[7] Unclean spirits screamed as they tore out of the people they had been holding. The paralyzed walked. The lame walked.

[8] And a wave of joy washed through that city.

[9] Now there was a man named Simon already in the city, a practicing magician who had Samaria spellbound. He passed himself off as someone extraordinary,

[10] and everyone — from the nobodies to the somebodies — was hanging on his every word, saying, "This man is the power of God. The Great Power."

[11] They'd been under his spell for a long time, mesmerized by his sorcery.

[12] But once they believed Philip — who was bringing them good news about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus the Messiah — they were baptized, men and women alike.

[13] Even Simon himself believed. After his baptism, he stuck close to Philip, awestruck by the signs and miracles unfolding in front of him.

[14] When the apostles back in Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received God's word, they sent Peter and John down to them.

[15] The two arrived and prayed that the Samaritans might receive the Holy Spirit,

[16] because the Spirit had not yet fallen on any of them. They had only been baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.

[17] So Peter and John laid hands on them, and they received the Holy Spirit.

[18] When Simon saw that the Spirit was given through the laying on of the apostles' hands, he pulled out money

[19] and said, "Give me this power too. I want to lay my hands on people and have them receive the Holy Spirit."

[20] Peter's reply cut: "May your silver go to hell with you — for thinking the gift of God could be bought.

[21] You have no part in this, no share in it. Your heart is twisted before God.

[22] Repent of this evil. Beg the Lord. Maybe — maybe — the thought of your heart will be forgiven.

[23] Because I can see what you are: bitter poison and a prisoner of corruption."

[24] Simon answered, "You pray to the Lord for me — that none of what you said comes down on me."

[25] After they had testified and spoken the word of the Lord, Peter and John headed back to Jerusalem, announcing the good news through village after village of Samaria along the way.

[26] An angel of the Lord spoke to Philip: "Get up. Head south, to the desert road that runs from Jerusalem down to Gaza."

[27] So he got up and went. And there on the road was an Ethiopian — a eunuch, a court official, the treasurer of Candace the queen of Ethiopia. He had come to Jerusalem to worship

[28] and was now on his way home, sitting in his chariot, reading the prophet Isaiah.

[29] The Spirit said to Philip, "Go. Catch up to that chariot."

[30] Philip ran up alongside it and heard the man reading Isaiah out loud. He asked, "Do you actually understand what you're reading?"

[31] The eunuch said, "How could I — unless someone guides me?" And he invited Philip up to sit with him.

[32] The passage of Scripture he was reading was this:
"Like a sheep he was led to slaughter.
Like a lamb silent before its shearer,
he did not open his mouth.

[33] In his humiliation, justice was denied him.
Who can describe his generation?
For his life is taken from the earth."

[34] The eunuch turned to Philip: "Tell me — who is the prophet talking about? Himself? Or someone else?"

[35] Philip opened his mouth, started from that very passage, and told him the good news about Jesus.

[36] As they traveled on, they came to some water. The eunuch said, "Look — water. What's stopping me from being baptized?"

[37]

[38] He ordered the chariot stopped. Both of them — Philip and the eunuch — went down into the water, and Philip baptized him.

[39] When they came up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord caught Philip away. The eunuch never saw him again — but he went on his way, rejoicing.

[40] Philip turned up in Azotus. From there he traveled through every town along the coast, announcing the good news, until he reached Caesarea.

[1] Saul was still breathing threats — still hungry for the blood of the Lord's disciples. He went to the high priest [2] and got letters of authorization for the synagogues in Damascus, so that if he found anyone there following the Way — men or women — he could drag them back to Jerusalem in chains.

[3] He was almost to Damascus when it happened. Suddenly a light from heaven exploded around him. [4] He hit the ground. A voice: "Saul. Saul. Why are you hunting me?"

[5] "Who are you, Lord?"

"I am Jesus. The one you're hunting. [6] Get up. Go into the city. You'll be told what to do."

[7] The men traveling with him stood there speechless — they heard the voice but saw no one. [8] Saul got up off the ground. His eyes were open but he couldn't see a thing. They had to lead him by the hand into Damascus. [9] For three days he sat in the dark — no food, no water.

[10] In Damascus there was a disciple named Ananias. The Lord spoke to him in a vision: "Ananias."

"Here I am, Lord."

[11] "Get up. Go to Straight Street, to Judas's house, and ask for a man from Tarsus named Saul. He's praying right now. [12] He's seen — in a vision — a man named Ananias come in and lay hands on him so he can see again."

[13] "Lord — I've heard about this man. I've heard plenty. The damage he's done to your people in Jerusalem. [14] And he's here now with authority from the chief priests to arrest everyone who calls on your name."

[15] But the Lord said, "Go. This man is my chosen instrument. He will carry my name to the nations and to their kings, and to the children of Israel. [16] I'll show him myself how much he'll have to suffer for my name."

[17] So Ananias went. He entered the house, laid his hands on Saul, and said, "Brother Saul — the Lord, Jesus, the one who appeared to you on the road — he sent me. So you can see again. So you can be filled with the Holy Spirit." [18] Instantly something like scales fell from his eyes. He could see. He got up. He was baptized. [19] He ate. His strength returned.

He spent some days with the disciples in Damascus. [20] And right away he started preaching in the synagogues: Jesus is the Son of God.

[21] Everyone who heard him was stunned. "Isn't this the man who tore through Jerusalem destroying everyone who called on this name? Didn't he come here for exactly that — to drag them in chains to the chief priests?"

[22] But Saul only grew stronger. He dismantled the arguments of the Damascus Jews, proving Jesus was the Messiah. [23] After enough time had passed, the Jews plotted to kill him. [24] But Saul learned of it. They were watching the city gates day and night, waiting for him. [25] So his disciples took him by night and lowered him through an opening in the wall — in a basket.

[26] When he reached Jerusalem he tried to join the disciples. They were all afraid of him. They didn't believe he was really a disciple. [27] But Barnabas took him in, brought him to the apostles, and told them the whole story — how Saul had seen the Lord on the road, how the Lord had spoken to him, how he had preached fearlessly in Damascus in the name of Jesus. [28] So he stayed with them, moving freely in and out of Jerusalem, speaking boldly in the name of the Lord. [29] He debated the Hellenist Jews. They tried to kill him. [30] When the brothers found out, they got him down to Caesarea and sent him off to Tarsus.

[31] So the church — across Judea, Galilee, Samaria — had peace. It was being built up. It walked in the fear of the Lord and in the comfort of the Holy Spirit, and it multiplied.

[32] Peter was traveling through the whole region. He came down to the believers in Lydda. [33] There he found a man named Aeneas — paralyzed, bedridden for eight years. [34] Peter said to him, "Aeneas — Jesus Christ heals you. Get up. Make your bed." And he got up — immediately. [35] Everyone in Lydda and Sharon saw him. And they turned to the Lord.

[36] In Joppa there was a disciple named Tabitha — Dorcas in Greek. Her life was full of good works and acts of mercy. [37] In those days she got sick and died. They washed her body and laid her in an upstairs room. [38] Lydda was close to Joppa, and when the disciples heard Peter was there, they sent two men to him: "Come. Don't delay."

[39] Peter went with them. When he arrived they took him up to the room. The widows crowded around him, weeping, holding up the tunics and robes Dorcas had made while she was with them. [40] Peter sent them all out. He knelt. He prayed. Then he turned to the body and said, "Tabitha — get up." Her eyes opened. She saw Peter. She sat up. [41] He gave her his hand and lifted her to her feet. Then he called in the believers and the widows and presented her — alive.

[42] Word spread through all of Joppa. Many came to believe in the Lord. [43] Peter stayed in Joppa a good while, with a tanner named Simon.

[1] In Caesarea there lived a man named Cornelius, a centurion in what they called the Italian Regiment.

[2] He was devout. So was his whole household. He gave generously to the Jewish poor and prayed to God constantly.

[3] One afternoon, around three o'clock, he saw it clearly in a vision — an angel of God walking into his house and calling his name: "Cornelius."

[4] He stared, terrified. "What is it, Lord?"

The angel answered: "Your prayers and your generosity have risen up before God. He has not forgotten.

[5] Now send men to Joppa. Bring back a man named Simon — the one they call Peter.

[6] He's staying with another Simon, a tanner, whose house sits by the sea."

[7] When the angel was gone, Cornelius called two of his household servants and a soldier — one of his devout aides —

[8] told them everything, and sent them off to Joppa.

[9] The next day, around noon, while they were closing in on the city, Peter climbed up to the roof to pray.

[10] He got hungry. He wanted food. But while it was being prepared, he slipped into a trance.

[11] He saw the sky cracked open and something descending — a vast sheet, lowered to the ground by its four corners.

[12] Inside it: every kind of four-footed animal, every creature that crawls, every bird in the air.

[13] And a voice: "Get up, Peter. Kill. Eat."

[14] "Absolutely not, Lord," Peter said. "I have never eaten anything unclean or impure in my life."

[15] The voice came again: "What God has made clean, you do not call filthy."

[16] Three times this happened. Then the sheet was pulled back up into the sky.

[17] Peter was sitting there trying to make sense of it when the men sent by Cornelius — having asked around for Simon's house — showed up at the gate.

[18] They called out, asking whether Simon Peter was staying there.

[19] Peter was still chewing on the vision when the Spirit spoke: "Three men are looking for you.

[20] Get up. Go down. Go with them. No hesitation. I sent them."

[21] Peter went down. "I'm the man you're looking for. What's this about?"

[22] They told him: "Cornelius, the centurion — a righteous man, God-fearing, respected by the entire Jewish community — was instructed by a holy angel to bring you to his house and listen to what you have to say."

[23] So Peter invited them in and put them up for the night. The next morning he set out with them, and some of the brothers from Joppa came along.

[24] The following day they reached Caesarea. Cornelius was waiting — he'd already gathered his relatives and closest friends.

[25] The moment Peter walked in, Cornelius met him, dropped to his feet, and began to worship.

[26] Peter pulled him up. "Stand up. I'm just a man."

[27] Talking with him, Peter went inside and found a crowd assembled.

[28] He said to them: "You know perfectly well that it's off-limits for a Jew to mix with or visit a foreigner. But God showed me — I cannot call any human being filthy or unclean.

[29] That's why I came without protest when you sent for me. So tell me — why did you send for me?"

[30] Cornelius answered: "Four days ago at this exact hour, three in the afternoon, I was praying in my house when suddenly a man in dazzling clothes stood in front of me

[31] and said, 'Cornelius, your prayer has been heard. Your generosity has been remembered before God.

[32] Send to Joppa for Simon, called Peter. He's staying at the house of Simon the tanner, by the sea.'

[33] So I sent for you immediately, and you were good enough to come. Now we are all here, in the presence of God, ready to hear everything the Lord has told you to say."

[34] Peter opened his mouth. "Now I see it — truly. God plays no favorites.

[35] In every nation, anyone who fears him and does what is right is welcome before him.

[36] This is the message he sent to the people of Israel — the good news of peace through Jesus Christ, who is Lord of all.

[37] You know the story — what unfolded across Judea, starting in Galilee after the baptism John preached:

[38] how God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power, how he went everywhere doing good, healing everyone the devil had crushed — because God was with him.

[39] We are witnesses to all of it — everything he did in the Judean countryside and in Jerusalem. They killed him. Hung him on a tree.

[40] God raised him on the third day and let him be seen —

[41] not by everyone, but by witnesses God had chosen in advance. By us. We ate with him. We drank with him. After he came back from the dead.

[42] He ordered us to preach to the people and to testify that this is the one God appointed as judge of the living and the dead.

[43] Every prophet says the same thing about him: everyone who trusts in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name."

[44] Peter was still mid-sentence when the Holy Spirit fell on everyone listening.

[45] The Jewish believers who'd come with Peter were stunned — the gift of the Holy Spirit had been poured out on Gentiles.

[46] They could hear it — these people were speaking in tongues and exalting God.

Then Peter said:

[47] "Can anyone here object to water? Can anyone refuse to baptize these people, who've received the Holy Spirit just as we did?"

[48] So he ordered them baptized in the name of Jesus Christ. Then they asked him to stay a few more days.

[1] News traveled fast. The apostles and the believers across Judea heard it: Gentiles — Gentiles — had received the word of God.

[2] So when Peter finally came up to Jerusalem, the circumcision party was waiting for him.

[3] "You went into the house of uncircumcised men. You ate with them."

[4] Peter walked them through it from the beginning, step by step.

[5] "I was in Joppa, praying. And right there in the middle of prayer I fell into a trance and saw it — something like an enormous sheet, lowered down from the sky by its four corners, coming straight to me.

[6] I stared into it. Every kind of four-footed animal, wild beast, reptile, bird — all of it.

[7] Then a voice: 'Get up, Peter. Kill. Eat.'

[8] I said, 'No. Absolutely not, Lord. Nothing unclean or defiled has ever crossed my lips.'

[9] The voice came back a second time, straight from heaven: 'What God has made clean — don't you dare call defiled.'

[10] Three times this happened. Then the whole thing was hauled back up into the sky.

[11] At that exact moment — three men showed up at the house where I was staying. Sent from Caesarea. Sent for me.

[12] The Spirit told me to go with them, no second-guessing. These six brothers came with me, and we walked into the man's house.

[13] He told us how he'd seen an angel standing in his home, telling him, 'Send to Joppa. Get Simon, the one they call Peter.

[14] He'll speak words that will save you — you and everyone under your roof.'

[15] And the moment I started speaking, the Holy Spirit fell on them — exactly the way He fell on us at the beginning.

[16] Then I remembered what the Lord said: 'John baptized with water, but you — you'll be baptized with the Holy Spirit.'

[17] So if God gave them the same gift He gave us when we believed in the Lord Jesus Christ — who am I? Who am I to stand in God's way?"

[18] That silenced them. And then they began to praise God. "So God has given even the Gentiles repentance — and life."

[19] Meanwhile, the believers scattered by the persecution that erupted over Stephen had pushed all the way to Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch — speaking the word to Jews and only Jews.

[20] But some of them — men from Cyprus and Cyrene — got to Antioch and started speaking to Greeks too, announcing the good news of the Lord Jesus.

[21] And the Lord's hand was on them. A staggering number believed and turned to the Lord.

[22] Word of it reached the church in Jerusalem, and they sent Barnabas out to Antioch.

[23] When he arrived and saw the grace of God in action, he was overjoyed. He urged them all: stay with the Lord. Stay, heart and soul.

[24] He was a good man — full of the Holy Spirit, full of faith. And more people, many more, were brought to the Lord.

[25] Then Barnabas left for Tarsus to track down Saul.

[26] He found him, brought him back to Antioch, and for a whole year the two of them gathered with the church and taught a huge crowd. Antioch — that's where the disciples were first called Christians.

[27] During those days, prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch.

[28] One of them, Agabus, stood up and signaled through the Spirit that a massive famine was coming over the whole Roman world — which is exactly what happened, under Claudius.

[29] So the disciples decided, each according to what he had, to send relief to the brothers and sisters living in Judea.

[30] And they did. They sent it through Barnabas and Saul, straight to the elders.

[1] Around that time, King Herod went on the offensive against believers in the church. [2] He had James, John's brother, executed by the sword. [3] When he saw how this played with the Jewish leadership, he went further and arrested Peter too. This was during the festival of Unleavened Bread. [4] He seized him, threw him in prison, and assigned four squads of four soldiers each to guard him in rotation, planning to put him on public trial after Passover. [5] So Peter sat in prison. But the church was praying for him — relentlessly, urgently, lifting him up to God.

[6] The night before Herod was going to drag him out, Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, chained to both of them, with guards posted at the door. [7] Suddenly an angel of the Lord stood there. Light flooded the cell. The angel jabbed Peter in the side and woke him up. "Get up — quick." The chains dropped off his wrists. [8] "Belt your tunic. Strap on your sandals." Peter did. "Throw on your coat. Follow me." [9] So Peter followed him out — though he had no idea this was actually happening. He thought he was seeing a vision. [10] They passed the first guard post, then the second, and came to the iron gate that led into the city. It swung open on its own. They walked out, went down one street, and just like that, the angel was gone.

[11] Peter came to himself. "Now I get it. The Lord sent his angel and pulled me out of Herod's hand — out of everything the Jewish leaders were hoping would happen." [12] Once he had his bearings, he headed to the house of Mary, mother of John (also called Mark), where a crowd had gathered to pray. [13] He knocked at the outer gate. A servant girl named Rhoda came to answer. [14] When she heard Peter's voice, she was so overjoyed she forgot to actually open the gate — she ran back inside shouting that Peter was standing outside. [15] "You're out of your mind," they told her. She kept insisting. So they said, "It must be his angel." [16] Meanwhile, Peter kept knocking. When they finally opened up and saw him, they were stunned. [17] He waved them quiet, walked them through how the Lord had broken him out of prison, and said, "Tell James and the brothers." Then he left and went somewhere else.

[18] When morning came, the soldiers were in chaos — what in the world had happened to Peter? [19] Herod launched a manhunt. When he couldn't find him, he interrogated the guards and ordered them executed. Then he left Judea for Caesarea and stayed there.

[20] Now Herod was furious with the people of Tyre and Sidon. They sent a united delegation to him, having first won over Blastus, the king's chamberlain, and sued for peace — because their region depended on the king's country for food. [21] On the appointed day, Herod put on his royal robes, took his seat on the throne, and delivered a public address. [22] The crowd kept shouting, "This is the voice of a god, not a man!" [23] Instantly, an angel of the Lord struck him down — because he had taken the glory that belonged to God. He was eaten by worms and died.

[24] Meanwhile, the word of God kept spreading and multiplying.

[25] Barnabas and Saul, having completed their mission in Jerusalem, headed back, bringing John (also called Mark) with them.

[1] In the church at Antioch, there were prophets and teachers: Barnabas, Simeon called Niger, Lucius from Cyrene, Manaen (who'd grown up with Herod the tetrarch), and Saul.

[2] While they were worshiping the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit spoke: "Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul. I have work for them."

[3] So they fasted, prayed, laid hands on them, and sent them off.

[4] Sent by the Spirit, they made their way down to Seleucia and sailed from there to Cyprus.

[5] Landing at Salamis, they began proclaiming God's word in the Jewish synagogues. John was with them as their assistant.

[6] They traveled the whole island, all the way to Paphos, where they ran into a Jewish sorcerer and false prophet named Bar-Jesus.

[7] He was attached to the proconsul, Sergius Paulus — an intelligent man — who summoned Barnabas and Saul because he wanted to hear God's word.

[8] But Elymas the sorcerer (that's what his name means) pushed back against them, trying to twist the proconsul away from the faith.

[9] Then Saul — also called Paul — filled with the Holy Spirit, fixed his eyes on him

[10] and said: "You son of the devil, enemy of everything right, stuffed with every kind of fraud and trickery — will you never stop bending the straight paths of the Lord?

[11] Look — the Lord's hand is on you now. You'll be blind. You won't see the sun for a while." Instantly a fog, a darkness, came down on him, and he stumbled around groping for someone to lead him by the hand.

[12] When the proconsul saw what happened, he believed — stunned by the teaching about the Lord.

[13] Paul and his companions set sail from Paphos and came to Perga in Pamphylia. There John left them and went back to Jerusalem.

[14] They pushed on from Perga and arrived at Antioch in Pisidia. On the Sabbath they walked into the synagogue and sat down.

[15] After the reading from the Law and the Prophets, the synagogue leaders sent word: "Brothers, if you have a word of encouragement for the people, speak."

[16] Paul stood, motioned with his hand, and began: "Israelites — and you Gentiles who fear God — listen.

[17] The God of this people Israel chose our ancestors. He raised them into a great people during their exile in Egypt, and with a mighty arm he brought them out.

[18] For about forty years he put up with them in the wilderness.

[19] He destroyed seven nations in Canaan and handed their land over as inheritance —

[20] all this stretching out about four hundred and fifty years. After that he gave them judges, until the prophet Samuel.

[21] Then they demanded a king, and God gave them Saul son of Kish, from the tribe of Benjamin — forty years' worth.

[22] After removing him, God raised up David as their king. He testified about him: 'I have found David son of Jesse, a man after my own heart. He'll do everything I want done.'

[23] From this man's line, exactly as promised, God brought to Israel a Savior — Jesus.

[24] Before Jesus arrived, John had already announced a baptism of repentance to all Israel.

[25] As John was finishing his course, he said: 'What do you think I am? I'm not the one. But he's coming after me — and I'm not worthy to untie the sandals on his feet.'

[26] Brothers — children of Abraham's line, and you Gentiles who fear God — this message of salvation has been sent to us.

[27] The people of Jerusalem and their rulers didn't recognize him. They didn't understand the prophets they hear read every Sabbath. So in condemning him, they fulfilled those very prophets.

[28] They found no grounds for a death sentence, but still demanded Pilate execute him.

[29] When they had completed everything written about him, they took him down from the tree and laid him in a tomb.

[30] But God raised him from the dead.

[31] For many days he appeared to those who had traveled with him from Galilee to Jerusalem — they are now his witnesses to the people.

[32] And we are bringing you the good news: what God promised our ancestors —

[33] he has fulfilled for us, their children, by raising Jesus. As it's written in the second Psalm: 'You are my Son. Today I have fathered you.'

[34] As for raising him from the dead, never to return to decay, God said it like this: 'I will give you the holy and sure blessings promised to David.'

[35] And in another psalm: 'You will not let your Holy One see decay.'

[36] Now David, after serving God's purpose in his own generation, fell asleep, was buried with his ancestors, and saw decay.

[37] But the one God raised did not see decay.

[38] So know this, brothers: through this man, forgiveness of sins is being announced to you.

[39] And through him, everyone who believes is justified from everything the law of Moses could never justify you from.

[40] Make sure what the prophets warned about doesn't fall on you:

[41] 'Look, you scoffers — be astonished, and vanish. I am doing something in your days you would never believe, even if someone spelled it out for you.'"

[42] As they were leaving, the people begged them to come back the next Sabbath and say more.

[43] When the meeting broke up, many Jews and devout converts followed Paul and Barnabas, who urged them to keep holding on to the grace of God.

[44] The next Sabbath, nearly the whole city showed up to hear the word of the Lord.

[45] But when the Jewish leaders saw the crowds, jealousy burned in them. They started contradicting Paul and slandering him.

[46] Paul and Barnabas answered with steel: "The word of God had to be spoken to you first. Since you push it away and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life — fine. We turn to the Gentiles.

[47] The Lord commanded us: 'I have made you a light to the nations, to carry salvation to the ends of the earth.'"

[48] The Gentiles heard this and lit up with joy, glorifying the word of the Lord. And all who were appointed for eternal life believed.

[49] The word of the Lord spread across the whole region.

[50] But the Jewish leaders stirred up the religious upper-class women and the leading men of the city, kicked off a persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and drove them out of their territory.

[51] So Paul and Barnabas shook the dust off their feet at them and moved on to Iconium.

[52] And the disciples were flooded with joy and the Holy Spirit.

[1] In Iconium, the same pattern: they walked into the synagogue and spoke with such force that a massive crowd — Jews and Greeks alike — believed. [2] But the Jews who refused to believe stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds against the brothers. [3] So Paul and Barnabas stayed a long time, speaking boldly for the Lord, who confirmed the message of his grace by letting signs and wonders pour through their hands. [4] The city split in two. Some sided with the Jews, others with the apostles. [5] When word reached them of a plot — Gentiles and Jews together, with their rulers behind it, planning to abuse them and stone them — [6] they caught wind of it and fled to the Lycaonian cities of Lystra and Derbe and the surrounding country, [7] and there they kept preaching the good news.

[8] In Lystra, a man sat who couldn't use his feet — crippled from birth, never once had he walked. [9] He was listening to Paul speak. Paul locked eyes with him, saw he had the faith to be healed, [10] and called out, "Stand up on your feet." The man leaped — and walked.

[11] When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they erupted, shouting in Lycaonian: "The gods have come down to us in human form!" [12] They called Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, because Paul was the one carrying the words. [13] The priest of Zeus — whose temple stood at the city gates — brought out bulls and garlands and was ready, with the crowds, to offer sacrifice.

[14] When the apostles Barnabas and Paul caught on, they tore their clothes and rushed into the crowd, shouting, [15] "Men — what are you doing? We're human, same as you. We've come with good news, to turn you away from these empty things to the living God — the one who made heaven and earth and sea and everything in them. [16] In past generations he let every nation walk its own road. [17] Yet he never left himself without witness: he gave you good gifts — rain from heaven, harvests in their season, food for your tables, gladness to fill your hearts." [18] Even with all this, they barely managed to stop the crowd from sacrificing to them.

[19] Then Jews arrived from Antioch and Iconium and turned the crowd. They stoned Paul and dragged him out of the city, certain he was dead. [20] But the disciples gathered around him, and he got up and walked back into the city. The next day he left with Barnabas for Derbe.

[21] After preaching the good news in that city and making many disciples, they doubled back — to Lystra, to Iconium, to Antioch — [22] strengthening the souls of the disciples, urging them to stay in the faith, telling them straight: the road into God's kingdom runs through hardship. [23] In every church they appointed elders, and with prayer and fasting they entrusted them to the Lord they had come to trust.

[24] They passed through Pisidia and reached Pamphylia, [25] preached the word in Perga, then went down to Attalia. [26] From there they sailed back to Antioch — the city where they'd first been handed over to the grace of God for the work they had now completed. [27] When they arrived, they gathered the church and told the whole story: everything God had done through them, and how he had thrown open the door of faith to the Gentiles. [28] And they stayed there with the disciples a good long while.

[1] Then some men came down from Judea and started teaching the brothers: "Unless you get circumcised according to the custom Moses laid down, you cannot be saved."

[2] Paul and Barnabas pushed back hard. The dispute got sharp. So the church decided Paul and Barnabas, along with a few others, should go up to Jerusalem and put the question to the apostles and elders.

[3] The church sent them off, and as they passed through Phoenicia and Samaria they kept telling the story of the Gentiles turning to God — and everywhere they went, the brothers erupted with joy.

[4] When they reached Jerusalem, the church welcomed them, along with the apostles and elders, and they reported everything God had done through them.

[5] But some believers from the Pharisee party stood up and said, "They have to be circumcised. They have to be ordered to keep the law of Moses."

[6] So the apostles and elders gathered to settle the matter.

[7] After a long debate, Peter stood up and said: "Brothers, you know that in the early days God chose me, out of all of you, to be the mouth through which the Gentiles would hear the gospel and believe.

[8] And God, who reads the heart, vouched for them — He gave them the Holy Spirit, just as He gave it to us.

[9] He made no distinction between us and them. He cleansed their hearts by faith.

[10] So why are you now testing God by laying on the necks of these disciples a yoke that neither we nor our fathers could carry?

[11] No — we believe we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus. Just like them."

[12] The whole assembly went quiet. They listened as Barnabas and Paul recounted the signs and wonders God had performed among the Gentiles through them.

[13] When they finished, James spoke: "Brothers, listen to me.

[14] Simeon has explained how God first reached into the Gentile world to take from it a people for His name.

[15] This matches what the prophets said. As it is written:

[16] 'After this I will return and rebuild the fallen tent of David. I will rebuild its ruins and raise it up,

[17] so that the rest of humanity may seek the Lord — every Gentile who bears my name — says the Lord, who does these things' —

[18] things known from of old.

[19] So here is my judgment: we should not make it hard on the Gentiles who are turning to God.

[20] We write to them only this: stay away from food polluted by idols, from sexual immorality, from the meat of strangled animals, and from blood.

[21] After all, Moses has been preached in every city for generations. He is read aloud in the synagogues every Sabbath."

[22] Then the apostles and elders, with the whole church, decided to choose men from among themselves and send them to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas. They picked Judas (called Barsabbas) and Silas — both leaders among the brothers.

[23] They sent this letter with them:

"From the apostles and elders, your brothers,
To the Gentile brothers in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia: Greetings.

[24] We have heard that some who went out from us — without our authorization — have been unsettling you with their words and shaking your souls.

[25] So we all agreed to choose men and send them to you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul —

[26] men who have put their lives on the line for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.

[27] We are sending Judas and Silas, who will tell you the same thing in person.

[28] It seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to lay no further burden on you beyond these essentials:

[29] abstain from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality. Keep clear of these things, and you will do well. Farewell."

[30] So they were sent off. They came down to Antioch, gathered the congregation, and delivered the letter.

[31] The people read it and rejoiced at the encouragement.

[32] Judas and Silas — being prophets themselves — said much to strengthen and steady the brothers.

[33] After spending some time there, they were sent off in peace by the brothers, back to those who had sent them.

[34] —

[35] Paul and Barnabas stayed in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord alongside many others.

[36] Some days later Paul said to Barnabas, "Let's go back. Let's visit the brothers in every city where we preached the word of the Lord and see how they're doing."

[37] Barnabas wanted to take John, called Mark, with them.

[38] Paul refused. Mark had bailed on them in Pamphylia — walked away from the work — and Paul would not bring him along.

[39] The disagreement grew so sharp they split up. Barnabas took Mark and sailed for Cyprus.

[40] Paul chose Silas and headed out, commended by the brothers to the grace of the Lord.

[41] He traveled through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches.

[1] Paul made it to Derbe, then Lystra. There was a disciple there named Timothy — Jewish mother, a believer; Greek father. [2] The brothers in Lystra and Iconium spoke highly of him, [3] and Paul wanted him on the road with them. So he circumcised him, because of the Jews in the region — everyone knew his father was Greek. [4] As they traveled town to town, they delivered the decisions handed down by the apostles and elders in Jerusalem, telling the believers to live by them. [5] The churches grew stronger in faith and larger in number every single day.

[6] They pushed through Phrygia and Galatia after the Holy Spirit blocked them from speaking the word in Asia. [7] When they reached the border of Mysia and tried to enter Bithynia, the Spirit of Jesus refused them. [8] So they skirted past Mysia and came down to Troas. [9] That night Paul saw it — a vision. A man from Macedonia, standing there, begging him: *Come across. Help us.* [10] The moment Paul saw the vision, we packed up for Macedonia, certain God had called us to bring them the good news.

[11] We set sail from Troas and ran a straight course to Samothrace, then Neapolis the next day, [12] and from there to Philippi — a leading city in that district of Macedonia, a Roman colony. We stayed a few days.

[13] On the Sabbath we walked outside the city gate down to the river, where we figured there'd be a place of prayer. We sat down and started talking with the women who had gathered there. [14] One of them — Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth from Thyatira, a worshiper of God — was listening. And the Lord opened her heart wide to take in what Paul was saying. [15] After she and her household were baptized, she practically pulled us in: "If you've decided I'm faithful to the Lord, then come stay at my house." She wouldn't take no for an answer.

[16] One day on our way to the place of prayer, we ran into a slave girl with a spirit of divination — she made her owners a fortune telling fortunes. [17] She started trailing Paul and the rest of us, shouting, "These men are slaves of the Most High God! They're announcing the way of salvation to you!" [18] She kept it up for days. Finally Paul had enough. He turned and said to the spirit, "In the name of Jesus Christ — out. Now." And out it came, that same hour.

[19] When her owners realized their cash cow was gone, they grabbed Paul and Silas and dragged them into the marketplace in front of the authorities. [20] They hauled them before the magistrates and said, "These men are Jews — they're throwing our city into chaos, [21] pushing customs we Romans have no business adopting or practicing."

[22] The crowd piled on. The magistrates ripped the clothes off Paul and Silas and ordered them beaten with rods. [23] After a brutal flogging, they were thrown in prison, and the jailer was told to keep them locked down tight. [24] He took the order seriously — threw them into the inner cell and clamped their feet in stocks.

[25] Around midnight, Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God. The other prisoners were listening. [26] Suddenly the earth convulsed. The prison shook to its foundations. Every door flew open. Every chain came loose. [27] The jailer jolted awake, saw the doors hanging open, drew his sword, and was about to kill himself — sure the prisoners were gone.
