[28] But Paul shouted, "Don't! We're all still here!"

[29] The jailer called for torches, ran in, and fell trembling at the feet of Paul and Silas. [30] He brought them out and asked, "Sirs — what do I have to do to be saved?" [31] They said, "Trust the Lord Jesus. You and your whole household." [32] Then they spoke the word of the Lord to him and everyone in his house. [33] At that hour of the night, he took them and washed their wounds, and then he and his whole household were baptized — right there, right then. [34] He brought them up to his house, set food in front of them, and the whole household was celebrating — they had come to believe in God.

[35] When morning came, the magistrates sent officers with the order: "Release those men." [36] The jailer relayed it to Paul: "The magistrates say you can go. Walk out in peace." [37] But Paul said to the officers, "Hold on. They beat us publicly without trial — Roman citizens, both of us — threw us in prison, and now they want to push us out quietly? No. Let them come down here themselves and escort us out."

[38] The officers reported this back to the magistrates, who panicked when they heard the word *Roman.* [39] They came down, apologized, escorted them out, and asked them — please — to leave the city. [40] Paul and Silas left the prison, stopped by Lydia's house, encouraged the brothers and sisters there, and then hit the road.

[1] They traveled through Amphipolis and Apollonia and arrived in Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue. [2] Paul went in, as was his custom, and for three Sabbaths in a row he reasoned with them from the Scriptures, [3] opening them up, laying it out: the Messiah had to suffer and rise from the dead. "This Jesus I'm proclaiming to you — he is the Messiah."

[4] Some were persuaded and threw in their lot with Paul and Silas — a large crowd of God-fearing Greeks and quite a few of the leading women.

[5] But the other Jews boiled over with jealousy. They rounded up some thugs from the marketplace, whipped up a mob, and set the whole city in an uproar. They stormed Jason's house, hunting for Paul and Silas to drag them out to the assembly. [6] When they couldn't find them, they hauled Jason and some other believers before the city officials, shouting, "These men who have turned the world upside down have shown up here too — [7] and Jason has taken them in! Every one of them defies Caesar's decrees, claiming there's another king — Jesus." [8] The crowd and the officials were thrown into turmoil at this. [9] They made Jason and the others post bond and then released them.

[10] As soon as night fell, the believers sent Paul and Silas away to Berea. On arrival, they went straight to the Jewish synagogue. [11] These Bereans were of a finer cast than the Thessalonians. They received the message eagerly and combed through the Scriptures every day to see if what Paul said held up. [12] Many of them believed — including a good number of prominent Greek women and men.

[13] But when the Jews in Thessalonica got word that Paul was preaching the word of God in Berea too, they came down to stir up the crowds there as well. [14] The believers immediately got Paul out — sent him toward the coast — while Silas and Timothy stayed behind. [15] Those escorting Paul took him all the way to Athens. Then they headed back, carrying instructions for Silas and Timothy to join him as quickly as possible.

[16] While Paul waited for them in Athens, the sight of the city — idols everywhere, on every corner — provoked him to his core. [17] So he reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and the God-fearers, and in the marketplace day after day with anyone who happened by.

[18] Some Epicurean and Stoic philosophers crossed swords with him. "What's this scavenger trying to say?" some sneered. Others: "He seems to be peddling foreign gods" — because he was preaching Jesus and the resurrection. [19] So they took him to the Areopagus and said, "Tell us — what is this new teaching you're presenting? [20] You're bringing strange ideas to our ears. We want to know what they mean." [21] (All the Athenians and the foreigners who lived there spent their time doing nothing but chasing the latest idea.)

[22] Paul stood up in the middle of the Areopagus and began:

"Men of Athens — I see you are religious to the bone in every way. [23] As I walked through your city and studied your objects of worship, I came across an altar with this inscription: TO AN UNKNOWN GOD. The one you worship without knowing — that is the one I'm here to announce.

[24] "The God who made the world and everything in it — Lord of heaven and earth — does not live in temples built by human hands. [25] Nor is he served by human hands as if he needed something. He himself gives life and breath and everything to everyone. [26] From one man he made every nation of humankind to live across the whole face of the earth. He set the times for them, drew the boundaries of where they would live, [27] so they would seek God — maybe even reach out and find him — though he's not far from any one of us. [28] 'In him we live and move and have our being.' As some of your own poets have said, 'We are his offspring.'

[29] "So then — if we are God's offspring, we shouldn't imagine the divine being is like gold or silver or stone shaped by human craft and imagination. [30] God overlooked such ignorance in times past, but now he commands all people everywhere to turn around. [31] He has set a day when he will judge the world with justice through a man he appointed — and he has given proof of it to everyone by raising him from the dead."

[32] When they heard "resurrection of the dead," some laughed him off. Others said, "We'll hear you on this some other time." [33] And Paul walked out of their assembly. [34] But some joined him and believed — among them Dionysius the Areopagite, a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

[1] After this, Paul left Athens and made his way to Corinth.

[2] There he found a Jewish man named Aquila, born in Pontus, who had recently arrived from Italy with his wife Priscilla — Claudius had just ordered every Jew out of Rome. Paul went to see them,

[3] and because they shared his trade, he stayed and worked alongside them. They were tentmakers.

[4] Every Sabbath he reasoned in the synagogue, working to persuade both Jews and Greeks.

[5] When Silas and Timothy finally arrived from Macedonia, Paul threw himself completely into the word, testifying to the Jews that Jesus was the Messiah.

[6] When they pushed back and started slandering him, he shook out his clothes and told them: "Your blood is on your own heads. I am clean. From here on, I go to the Gentiles."

[7] He walked out and moved next door — to the house of a man named Titius Justus, a Gentile who worshipped God. His house sat right against the synagogue.

[8] And Crispus, the synagogue ruler himself, came to believe in the Lord — his whole household with him. Many Corinthians heard, believed, and were baptized.

[9] One night the Lord spoke to Paul in a vision: "Do not be afraid. Keep speaking. Do not go silent.

[10] I am with you. No one is going to lay a hand on you to harm you. This city is full of people who belong to me."

[11] So Paul settled in for a year and a half, teaching them the word of God.

[12] But when Gallio became proconsul of Achaia, the Jews united in an attack on Paul and dragged him before the tribunal.

[13] "This man," they charged, "is persuading people to worship God in ways that violate the law."

[14] Paul was about to open his mouth when Gallio cut in, speaking to the Jews: "If this were a case of wrongdoing or criminal mischief, I would have reason to hear you out.

[15] But since it is a quarrel about words and names and your own law — handle it yourselves. I refuse to judge such matters."

[16] And he drove them away from the tribunal.

[17] So they grabbed Sosthenes, the new synagogue ruler, and beat him right there in front of the bench. Gallio could not have cared less.

[18] Paul stayed on in Corinth a good while longer. Then he said goodbye to the believers and sailed for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila. At Cenchreae, he cut his hair — he had taken a vow.

[19] They reached Ephesus, where he left the others behind. He himself went into the synagogue and reasoned with the Jews.

[20] When they begged him to stay longer, he refused,

[21] but as he left he told them, "I will come back to you, God willing." And he set sail from Ephesus.

[22] Landing at Caesarea, he went up and greeted the church, then went down to Antioch.

[23] After spending some time there, he set out again, traveling through the Galatian region and Phrygia in order, strengthening every disciple along the way.

[24] Now a Jew named Apollos arrived in Ephesus — born in Alexandria, eloquent, powerful in the Scriptures.

[25] He had been instructed in the way of the Lord and spoke with fire in his spirit, teaching accurately about Jesus — though he knew only the baptism of John.

[26] He began speaking boldly in the synagogue. When Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they pulled him aside and laid out the way of God for him more precisely.

[27] When he wanted to cross over to Achaia, the believers encouraged him and wrote ahead, asking the disciples to welcome him. Once there, he was a tremendous help to those who through grace had believed,

[28] because he publicly demolished the Jews in debate, proving from the Scriptures that Jesus was the Messiah.

[1] While Apollos was in Corinth, Paul traveled the inland route and arrived in Ephesus. There he found some disciples [2] and asked them, "Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed?"

"Holy Spirit?" they said. "We haven't even heard there is one."

[3] "Then what baptism did you receive?"

"John's baptism."

[4] Paul said, "John's baptism was a baptism of repentance. He told the people to believe in the one coming after him — that is, Jesus."

[5] When they heard this, they were baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus. [6] Paul laid his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came over them, and they spoke in tongues and prophesied. [7] There were about twelve men in all.

[8] For three months Paul went into the synagogue and spoke boldly, reasoning and persuading them about the kingdom of God. [9] But when some hardened themselves and refused to believe, slandering the Way in front of the crowd, Paul left them. He took the disciples with him and held daily discussions in the lecture hall of Tyrannus. [10] This went on for two years, until every person living in the province of Asia — Jew and Greek alike — had heard the word of the Lord.

[11] God did extraordinary miracles through Paul's hands — [12] so extraordinary that handkerchiefs and aprons that had touched his skin were carried to the sick, and the diseases left them and the evil spirits fled.

[13] Some Jewish exorcists wandering the area tried to use the name of the Lord Jesus over those with evil spirits. They would say, "I command you by the Jesus that Paul preaches." [14] Seven sons of a Jewish chief priest named Sceva were doing this. [15] One day the evil spirit answered them, "Jesus I know. Paul I know. But you — who are you?" [16] Then the possessed man lunged at them, overpowered them all, and beat them so violently that they ran out of the house naked and bleeding.

[17] Word spread through Ephesus — to Jews and Greeks alike — and a wave of fear swept the city. The name of the Lord Jesus was lifted high. [18] Many believers came forward, openly confessing what they had been doing. [19] A number of those who had practiced sorcery brought their scrolls and burned them in front of everyone. When they totaled the value, it came to fifty thousand silver coins. [20] So the word of the Lord kept spreading and growing in power.

[21] After all this, Paul resolved in his spirit to pass through Macedonia and Achaia and head for Jerusalem. "After I've been there," he said, "I must also see Rome." [22] He sent two of his helpers, Timothy and Erastus, on ahead to Macedonia, while he stayed a little longer in Asia.

[23] It was around this time that a serious disturbance broke out over the Way. [24] A silversmith named Demetrius, who made silver shrines of Artemis, was bringing in significant business for the craftsmen. [25] He called them together — along with workers in related trades — and said:

"Gentlemen, you know our prosperity depends on this work. [26] And you can see and hear for yourselves what's happening — not just in Ephesus but across nearly all of Asia — this Paul has convinced and turned away crowds of people, telling them that gods made by human hands are not gods at all. [27] The danger isn't only that our trade will be discredited, but that the temple of the great goddess Artemis will be dismissed as nothing — and she herself, whom all Asia and the world worship, will be stripped of her majesty."

[28] When they heard this, rage took them. They began shouting, "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!" [29] The whole city was thrown into chaos. The crowd seized Gaius and Aristarchus, Paul's traveling companions from Macedonia, and rushed together into the theater. [30] Paul wanted to go in and face the assembly, but the disciples wouldn't let him. [31] Even some of the officials of the province, who were friends of Paul, sent word begging him not to risk himself in the theater.

[32] Inside, it was bedlam. Some shouted one thing, some another. The crowd was in confusion, and most of them didn't even know why they were there. [33] The Jews pushed Alexander forward, and some in the crowd were coaching him. He raised his hand for silence, wanting to make a defense to the people. [34] But the moment they realized he was a Jew, they erupted, and for two solid hours every voice rose as one: "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!"

[35] Finally the city clerk quieted the crowd. "Men of Ephesus," he said, "what person anywhere doesn't know that the city of Ephesus is the guardian of the temple of great Artemis and of her sacred stone that fell from heaven? [36] These facts are undeniable, so you need to calm down and not do anything reckless. [37] You've brought these men here, though they have neither robbed our temple nor blasphemed our goddess. [38] If Demetrius and his fellow craftsmen have a complaint against anyone, the courts are open and there are proconsuls — let them press charges there. [39] If you want anything beyond that, it will have to be settled in a legal assembly. [40] As it stands, we're in real danger of being charged with rioting today, since there's no justification we can offer for this commotion." [41] With that, he dismissed the assembly.

[1] Once the uproar died down, Paul gathered the disciples, encouraged them, said his goodbyes, and set out for Macedonia.

[2] He traveled through those regions, speaking words that strengthened them at every stop, and finally arrived in Greece,

[3] where he stayed three months. He was about to sail for Syria when he caught wind of a Jewish plot against him, so he changed course and headed back through Macedonia.

[4] Traveling with him: Sopater son of Pyrrhus from Berea, Aristarchus and Secundus from Thessalonica, Gaius from Derbe, Timothy, and Tychicus and Trophimus from Asia.

[5] They went ahead and waited for us at Troas.

[6] We sailed from Philippi after the Feast of Unleavened Bread, met up with them in Troas five days later, and stayed there a week.

[7] On the first day of the week, we gathered to break bread. Paul was leaving the next morning, so he kept talking — and kept talking — until midnight.

[8] The upstairs room where we were meeting was lit by many lamps.

[9] A young man named Eutychus was sitting on the window ledge. As Paul went on and on, sleep crept over him — deeper and deeper — until he toppled out of the third-story window. They picked him up dead.

[10] Paul ran down, threw himself over the body, and wrapped his arms around him. "Don't panic," he said. "He's alive."

[11] Then Paul went back upstairs, broke bread, ate, and talked until dawn. Then he left.

[12] They took the boy home alive — and the comfort of it was immeasurable.

[13] We went on ahead to the ship and sailed for Assos, where we were planning to pick up Paul. He had arranged it that way — he wanted to go by land.

[14] When he met us at Assos, we took him aboard and sailed for Mitylene.

[15] The next day we sailed past Chios, the day after we crossed to Samos, and the day after that we reached Miletus.

[16] Paul had decided to sail past Ephesus so he wouldn't get stuck in Asia. He was racing to be in Jerusalem by Pentecost, if possible.

[17] From Miletus, he sent word to Ephesus and called the elders of the church to come to him.

[18] When they arrived, he said this: "You know how I lived among you from the first day I set foot in Asia.

[19] I served the Lord with everything I had — humbly, often in tears, through trials that came at me from every Jewish plot.

[20] I held nothing back that would have helped you. I taught you publicly and house to house.

[21] I declared to both Jews and Greeks the same message: turn to God, trust in our Lord Jesus.

[22] And now — compelled by the Spirit — I'm going to Jerusalem. I don't know what waits for me there.

[23] Only this: in town after town, the Holy Spirit warns me that chains and suffering are coming.

[24] But my life means nothing to me. What matters is finishing the race, completing the work the Lord Jesus handed me — to testify to the good news of God's grace.

[25] And now listen: not one of you among whom I've walked, preaching the kingdom, will see my face again.

[26] So hear me clearly today — I'm clean. No one's blood is on my hands.

[27] I didn't flinch from telling you the whole counsel of God.

[28] Watch yourselves. Watch the flock the Holy Spirit has placed under your care. Shepherd the church of God, which he purchased with his own blood.

[29] I know that after I leave, wolves will move in. Vicious ones. They will not spare the flock.

[30] Even from among yourselves, men will rise up twisting the truth, dragging disciples away to follow them.

[31] So stay alert. Remember: for three years, night and day, I never stopped warning each one of you — with tears.

[32] And now I commit you to God and to the word of his grace, which can build you up and give you the inheritance reserved for all who are made holy.

[33] I haven't coveted anyone's silver, gold, or clothing.

[34] You know these hands — they worked to provide for me and for those with me.

[35] In everything I've shown you that by working hard like this we must help the weak, remembering the words of the Lord Jesus himself: 'There is more blessing in giving than in receiving.'"

[36] When he finished, he knelt down with all of them and prayed.

[37] They wept — all of them — wept hard, throwing their arms around Paul and kissing him,

[38] grieving most over what he had said: that they would never see his face again. Then they walked him to the ship.

[1] After tearing ourselves away from them, we set sail and ran a straight course to Cos, then Rhodes the next day, then on to Patara. [2] There we found a ship crossing to Phoenicia, boarded, and put out to sea. [3] We sighted Cyprus, left it to port, and sailed on toward Syria, landing at Tyre — the ship had cargo to unload there.

[4] We tracked down the disciples and stayed with them seven days. Through the Spirit they kept warning Paul not to set foot in Jerusalem. [5] When our time was up, we left and went on our way, and every one of them — wives, children, all — walked us out of the city. We knelt down together on the beach and prayed, [6] said our goodbyes, and boarded the ship while they turned back home.

[7] From Tyre we finished the voyage at Ptolemais, greeted the brothers there, and spent a day with them. [8] The next day we left and came to Caesarea. We went to the house of Philip the evangelist — one of the Seven — and stayed with him. [9] He had four unmarried daughters who prophesied.

[10] We had been there several days when a prophet named Agabus came down from Judea. [11] He walked over to us, took Paul's belt, bound his own feet and hands with it, and said: "Thus says the Holy Spirit — 'The man who owns this belt — the Jews in Jerusalem will tie him up just like this and hand him over to the Gentiles.'"

[12] When we heard that, we — and the locals — begged Paul not to go up to Jerusalem. [13] Paul answered, "What are you doing? Weeping like this and breaking my heart? I'm ready — not just to be bound, but to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus." [14] He wouldn't be moved, so we let it go and said, "The Lord's will be done."

[15] After those days we packed up and headed up to Jerusalem. [16] Some disciples from Caesarea came with us and brought us to the house of a man named Mnason — a Cypriot, one of the early disciples — where we would lodge.

[17] When we arrived in Jerusalem, the brothers welcomed us warmly. [18] The next day Paul went with us to see James, and all the elders were there. [19] After greeting them, Paul laid out in detail everything God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry.

[20] When they heard it, they glorified God. Then they said to him: "You see, brother, how many thousands of Jews have believed — and every one of them is zealous for the law. [21] They've been told that you teach all the Jews living among the Gentiles to abandon Moses — telling them not to circumcise their children, not to follow the customs. [22] So what now? They're going to hear you've come. [23] Do what we tell you. We have four men here who've taken a vow. [24] Take them, go through the purification rite with them, and cover their expenses so they can shave their heads. Then everyone will know there's nothing to the rumors — that you yourself walk in line with the law. [25] As for the Gentile believers, we've already written to them with our decision: they should keep clear of food sacrificed to idols, of blood, of strangled meat, and of sexual immorality."

[26] So the next day Paul took the men, went through the purification with them, entered the temple, and gave notice of when the days of purification would end and the offering be made for each of them.

[27] When the seven days were almost over, Jews from the province of Asia spotted him in the temple. They stirred up the whole crowd and grabbed him, [28] shouting, "Men of Israel, help! This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against our people, against the law, against this place — and now he's brought Greeks into the temple and defiled this holy ground!" [29] They had seen Trophimus the Ephesian with him earlier in the city and assumed Paul had brought him into the temple.

[30] The whole city erupted. People came running from everywhere. They seized Paul, dragged him out of the temple, and slammed the gates shut. [31] As they were trying to kill him, word reached the commander of the Roman cohort that all of Jerusalem was in chaos. [32] He moved fast — grabbed soldiers and centurions and charged down at them. The moment the crowd saw the commander and the troops, they stopped beating Paul.

[33] The commander stepped in, arrested him, ordered him bound with two chains, then demanded to know who he was and what he'd done. [34] Some in the crowd shouted one thing, some another. He couldn't get a clear answer through the uproar, so he ordered Paul taken to the barracks. [35] When Paul reached the steps, the violence of the mob was such that the soldiers had to carry him. [36] The whole crowd kept following, screaming, "Away with him!"

[37] As they were about to take him inside, Paul said to the commander, "May I say something to you?"

"You speak Greek?" the commander said. [38] "Aren't you the Egyptian who started that revolt a while back and led the four thousand assassins out into the wilderness?"

[39] Paul answered, "I'm a Jew — from Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no insignificant city. Please — let me speak to the people."

[40] The commander gave permission. Paul stood on the steps and motioned with his hand to the crowd. A deep hush fell over them, and he addressed them in Hebrew.

[1] "Brothers. Fathers. Hear me out."

[2] When they heard him speaking Aramaic, the crowd went still. He continued:

[3] "I am a Jew. Born in Tarsus of Cilicia, raised right here in this city, trained at the feet of Gamaliel in the exact discipline of our ancestral law. I was zealous for God — every bit as zealous as all of you are today.

[4] I hunted down this Way to the death. Men, women — I dragged them in chains and threw them into prison.

[5] The high priest himself will back me up on this, along with the whole council of elders. They gave me letters to take to the brothers in Damascus, and I was on my way to arrest the believers there and march them back to Jerusalem for punishment.

[6] But here's what happened. I was nearly to Damascus, around noon, when suddenly a blinding light from heaven flashed all around me.

[7] I hit the ground. And I heard a voice: 'Saul, Saul, why are you hunting me?'

[8] I said, 'Who are you, Lord?' He said, 'I am Jesus of Nazareth, the one you are hunting.'

[9] The men with me saw the light, but they did not catch the voice of the one speaking to me.

[10] I asked, 'What should I do, Lord?' The Lord said, 'Get up. Go into Damascus. There you will be told everything you have been appointed to do.'

[11] I could not see — the brilliance of that light had blinded me. So the men with me led me by the hand into Damascus.

[12] There was a man named Ananias — devout, faithful to the law, well respected by every Jew living there.

[13] He came and stood over me and said, 'Brother Saul, see again.' And in that instant I looked up and saw him.

[14] Then he said: 'The God of our fathers chose you — chose you to know his will, to see the Righteous One, to hear the voice from his own mouth.

[15] You are going to be his witness to everyone, telling them what you have seen and heard.

[16] So what are you waiting for? Get up. Be baptized. Wash your sins away, calling on his name.'

[17] Later I returned to Jerusalem. I was in the temple praying, and I fell into a trance.

[18] I saw him. He said to me, 'Hurry. Get out of Jerusalem now. They will not accept your testimony about me.'

[19] I said, 'Lord, they know what I did. In every synagogue I was rounding up and beating those who believed in you.

[20] When the blood of your witness Stephen was being spilled, I was standing right there, giving my approval, guarding the coats of the men who killed him.'

[21] But he said to me: 'Go. I am sending you far from here — to the Gentiles.'"

[22] They listened up to that word. Then the crowd erupted: "Wipe him off the earth! He has no right to live!"

[23] They were screaming, ripping off their cloaks, hurling dust into the air.

[24] The commander ordered him brought into the barracks and said to interrogate him under the lash — he wanted to know what could make a crowd howl like this.

[25] As they stretched him out for the whips, Paul said to the centurion standing there, "Is it legal for you to flog a Roman citizen who has not even been convicted?"

[26] The centurion went straight to the commander. "What are you about to do? This man is a Roman."

[27] The commander came over. "Tell me. Are you a Roman citizen?" "Yes," Paul said.

[28] The commander said, "I paid a fortune for my citizenship." Paul said, "I was born with mine."

[29] The men about to interrogate him backed off immediately. And the commander was shaken when he realized he had put a Roman citizen in chains.

[30] The next day, wanting to get to the bottom of why the Jews were accusing him, he released him and called together the chief priests and the whole council. Then he brought Paul down and stood him in front of them.

[1] Paul locked eyes with the Sanhedrin and said, "Brothers, I have lived before God with a clear conscience right up to this day."

[2] At that, the high priest Ananias ordered the men standing near Paul to strike him across the mouth.

[3] Paul shot back: "God is going to strike you, you whitewashed wall. You sit there judging me by the law, then you violate the law by ordering me beaten?"

[4] Those standing nearby said, "You dare insult God's high priest?"

[5] Paul answered, "I didn't know, brothers, that he was the high priest. The Scripture does say, 'Do not speak evil of a ruler of your people.'"

[6] Then Paul, realizing half the council were Sadducees and the other half Pharisees, raised his voice: "Brothers, I am a Pharisee, son of Pharisees. I am on trial because of the hope of the resurrection of the dead."

[7] The moment those words left his mouth, the room split. Pharisees and Sadducees turned on each other.

[8] (Sadducees say there's no resurrection, no angels, no spirits. Pharisees affirm all of it.)

[9] The shouting escalated. Some of the scribes from the Pharisee side jumped up and argued hard: "We find nothing wrong with this man. What if a spirit or an angel really did speak to him?"

[10] The conflict turned violent — they were ready to tear Paul apart. The commander ordered his troops down to extract him by force and bring him back to the barracks.

[11] That night the Lord stood beside Paul and said, "Courage. Just as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must testify in Rome."

[12] When morning came, a group of Jews bound themselves by oath — no food, no water — until they had killed Paul.

[13] More than forty men were in on this conspiracy.

[14] They went to the chief priests and elders and said, "We've sworn a binding oath to taste nothing until we kill Paul.

[15] So here's what you do: have the Sanhedrin request that the commander bring him down to you, as if you want a more thorough examination of his case. We'll be ready to take him out before he ever arrives."

[16] But Paul's nephew got wind of the ambush. He went into the barracks and told Paul.

[17] Paul called one of the centurions over. "Take this young man to the commander. He has something to report."

[18] The centurion brought him in. "The prisoner Paul called me over and asked me to bring this young man to you. He has something to tell you."

[19] The commander took the boy aside, away from anyone else, and said quietly, "What is it you need to tell me?"

[20] The boy said, "The Jewish leaders have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the Sanhedrin tomorrow, supposedly for a more careful inquiry.

[21] Don't fall for it. More than forty of them are lying in wait. They've sworn off food and water until they've killed him. They're ready right now, just waiting for your answer."

[22] The commander dismissed the young man with a warning: "Don't breathe a word to anyone that you told me this."

[23] Then he summoned two centurions. "Get two hundred soldiers ready to march to Caesarea at the third hour of the night. Seventy cavalry. Two hundred spearmen.

[24] And provide mounts for Paul. Get him safely to Governor Felix."

[25] He wrote a letter that read:

[26] "Claudius Lysias, to His Excellency Governor Felix: Greetings.

[27] This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed when I arrived with my troops and rescued him, having learned he was a Roman citizen.

[28] Wanting to understand what they were charging him with, I brought him before their council.

[29] I found the charges had to do with disputes over their own law — nothing deserving death or imprisonment.

[30] When I was informed of a plot against the man, I sent him to you immediately, and told his accusers to bring their case before you."

[31] So the soldiers, following orders, took Paul by night and brought him as far as Antipatris.

[32] The next day the infantry returned to the barracks and the cavalry went on with him.

[33] When they reached Caesarea, they delivered the letter to the governor and presented Paul to him.

[34] The governor read it, asked what province Paul was from, and learning he was from Cilicia,

[35] said, "I'll hear your case when your accusers arrive." Then he ordered Paul kept under guard in Herod's headquarters.

[1] Five days later, the high priest Ananias came down with some of the elders and a lawyer named Tertullus. They laid out their case against Paul before the governor.

[2] When Paul was called in, Tertullus opened the prosecution: "Most excellent Felix, thanks to you we enjoy lasting peace, and through your foresight reforms have come to this nation.

[3] In every way and everywhere, we acknowledge this with the deepest gratitude.

[4] But I will not detain you any longer. I ask only that you hear us briefly, with your usual graciousness.

[5] Here is what we have found: this man is a plague. He stirs up unrest among the Jews across the entire empire. He is a ringleader of the Nazarene sect.

[6] He even tried to desecrate the temple — that is when we seized him.

[7] —

[8] Examine him yourself, and you will see for yourself that everything we accuse him of is true."

[9] The other Jews joined in, swearing it was all true.

[10] When the governor motioned for Paul to speak, he answered: "Knowing that you have judged this nation for many years, I make my defense gladly.

[11] You can verify this easily: it has been no more than twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem to worship.

[12] No one found me arguing with anyone in the temple, or stirring up a crowd in the synagogues, or anywhere in the city.

[13] They cannot produce a shred of evidence for what they now accuse me of.

[14] But I will admit this: I worship the God of our ancestors according to the Way — what they call a sect. I believe everything written in the Law and the Prophets,

[15] and I share the same hope in God that they themselves hold — that there will be a resurrection of both the just and the unjust.

[16] So I work at it constantly: keeping my conscience clear before God and before people.

[17] After being away for years, I came back to bring gifts to my people and to present offerings.

[18] That is what I was doing in the temple — purified, no crowd around me, no disturbance — when they found me.

[19] But it was certain Jews from Asia who found me — and they are the ones who should be standing here before you, if they actually have anything against me.

[20] Or let these men say what crime they found me guilty of when I stood before the Sanhedrin —

[21] unless it was that one sentence I shouted as I stood there: 'It is over the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today.'"

[22] Felix, who knew the Way well, adjourned the hearing. "When Lysias the commander comes down," he said, "I will decide your case."

[23] He ordered the centurion to keep Paul in custody but to give him some freedom and not to prevent his friends from taking care of him.

[24] A few days later, Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish. He sent for Paul and listened to him talk about faith in Christ Jesus.

[25] But as Paul pressed on about righteousness, self-control, and the judgment coming, Felix grew uneasy. "That is enough for now," he said. "When I find a convenient time, I will send for you."

[26] He was also hoping Paul would offer him a bribe, so he kept sending for him to talk.

[27] Two years passed. Porcius Festus replaced Felix, and Felix — wanting to do the Jews a favor — left Paul in chains.

[1] Three days after Festus took office in the province, he traveled up from Caesarea to Jerusalem.

[2] The chief priests and the leading men of the Jews came to him with their case against Paul, pressing him hard,

[3] asking as a personal favor that he transfer Paul to Jerusalem — they were already setting up an ambush to kill him on the road.

[4] Festus answered that Paul was being held in Caesarea, and that he himself would be heading back there shortly.

[5] "So let your authorities come down with me," he said. "If the man has done anything wrong, they can press charges there."

[6] Festus stayed with them no more than eight or ten days, then went down to Caesarea. The next day he convened the court and ordered Paul brought in.

[7] The moment Paul walked in, the Jews who had come down from Jerusalem surrounded him, hurling one serious accusation after another — none of which they could actually prove.

[8] Paul defended himself: "I have done nothing against the law of the Jews, nothing against the temple, nothing against Caesar."

[9] Festus, angling for favor with the Jews, turned to Paul: "Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and stand trial before me on these charges?"

[10] Paul answered: "I am standing right now before Caesar's tribunal. This is where I should be tried. I have done the Jews no wrong — and you know it perfectly well.

[11] If I am guilty — if I have done something deserving death — I am not trying to escape death. But if there is nothing to their accusations, no one can hand me over to them as a favor. I appeal to Caesar."

[12] Festus conferred with his council, then delivered his verdict: "You have appealed to Caesar. To Caesar you will go."

[13] Some days later King Agrippa and Bernice arrived in Caesarea to pay their respects to Festus.

[14] Since they were staying for several days, Festus laid out Paul's case before the king. "There's a man here," he said, "left in custody by Felix.

[15] When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews came to me about him, demanding a verdict of condemnation.

[16] I told them it is not the Roman way to hand a man over before he has faced his accusers in person and had a chance to answer the charges.

[17] So when they came down here with me, I wasted no time — the next day I sat in court and had him brought in.

[18] But when his accusers stood up, they didn't lay out the kind of crimes I was expecting.

[19] Their argument with him was about points of their own religion, and about some dead man named Jesus whom Paul kept insisting was alive.

[20] I was out of my depth on questions like these, so I asked if he wanted to go to Jerusalem to be tried there.

[21] Paul appealed instead to be kept in custody for the decision of the Emperor — so I ordered him held until I could send him to Caesar."

[22] Agrippa said to Festus, "I would like to hear the man myself." "Tomorrow," Festus replied. "You will hear him."

[23] So the next day Agrippa and Bernice swept in with full ceremony and entered the audience hall, accompanied by the tribunes and the city's most prominent men. At Festus's order, Paul was brought in.

[24] Festus spoke: "King Agrippa, and everyone present with us — here is the man. The entire Jewish community has petitioned me about him, in Jerusalem and here, shouting that he should not be allowed to live another day.

[25] But I have found he has done nothing deserving death. And since he himself appealed to the Emperor, I decided to send him.

[26] The trouble is, I have nothing definite to write to my sovereign about him. That is why I have brought him before all of you — and especially before you, King Agrippa — so that after this examination I might have something to put down on paper.

[27] It seems absurd to me to send a prisoner on without specifying the charges against him."

[1] Agrippa turned to Paul: "You have the floor. Speak for yourself."

Paul stretched out his hand and began his defense:

[2] "King Agrippa, I count myself fortunate that it's before you I get to answer everything the Jews have charged me with today — [3] especially since you're an expert on every Jewish custom and controversy. So I ask you to hear me out.

[4] "Every Jew knows the life I've led from my youth — first among my own people, then in Jerusalem. [5] They've known me a long time, and if they're willing to admit it, they can testify that I lived as a Pharisee — the strictest sect of our religion. [6] And now here I stand on trial for one thing: hope. Hope in the promise God made to our fathers — [7] the same promise our twelve tribes are still straining toward, worshiping night and day, hoping to see it fulfilled. It's for this hope, King Agrippa, that the Jews accuse me. [8] Why should any of you find it incredible that God raises the dead?

[9] "I once thought it was my duty to do everything I could against the name of Jesus of Nazareth. [10] And that's exactly what I did in Jerusalem. I locked up many of God's people — I had authority from the chief priests. And when they were executed, I cast my vote against them. [11] I went from synagogue to synagogue punishing them, trying to force them to blaspheme. My rage against them was so out of control that I hunted them down even in foreign cities.

[12] "I was on one such mission to Damascus — armed with authority and orders from the chief priests — [13] when at midday, on the road, I saw it, King: a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, blazing all around me and the men traveling with me. [14] We all hit the ground. And I heard a voice speaking to me in Aramaic: 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? You're only bloodying yourself kicking at the goad.'

[15] "I said, 'Who are you, Lord?'

"And the Lord said: 'I am Jesus, the one you are persecuting. [16] Now stand up. Get on your feet. I've appeared to you for a reason — to make you my servant and my witness, both to what you've seen of me and to what I'm going to show you. [17] I will rescue you from your own people and from the Gentiles. I'm sending you to them — [18] to pry open their eyes, to turn them from darkness to light, from the grip of Satan to God, so they can receive forgiveness for their sins and a place among those made holy by trusting in me.'

[19] "So, King Agrippa, I did not disobey the vision from heaven. [20] I started in Damascus, then Jerusalem, then throughout Judea, then to the Gentiles — telling them all to repent, to turn to God, to live lives that prove it. [21] That's why the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me. [22] But God has been my help — to this very day. So here I stand, testifying to the small and the great, saying nothing beyond what the prophets and Moses said would happen: [23] that the Messiah would suffer, that he would be the first to rise from the dead, and that he would proclaim light — light to our people and light to the Gentiles."

[24] At this point Festus broke in, shouting: "Paul, you're out of your mind! All this learning has driven you insane!"

[25] "I am not insane, Your Excellency Festus," Paul replied. "What I'm telling you is true and sober. [26] The king knows what I'm talking about — that's why I can speak freely to him. None of this has escaped his notice. It wasn't done in some corner. [27] King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you do."

[28] Agrippa said to Paul: "A little more and you'll have me convinced to become a Christian."

[29] Paul replied: "Little or much — I pray to God that not only you but everyone listening to me today would become what I am — except for these chains."

[30] The king stood. So did the governor, and Bernice, and everyone seated with them. [31] As they walked out, they were saying to each other: "This man has done nothing that deserves death or prison."

[32] And Agrippa said to Festus: "He could have been set free — if he hadn't appealed to Caesar."

[1] Once the decision was made to ship us off to Italy, they handed Paul and a few other prisoners over to a centurion named Julius, of the Augustan Cohort. [2] We boarded a ship out of Adramyttium that was set to work the ports along the coast of Asia, and we put to sea. Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, was with us. [3] The next day we docked at Sidon, and Julius — treating Paul with real kindness — let him visit his friends and be cared for. [4] From there we put out again, hugging the lee of Cyprus because the winds were against us. [5] We crossed the open sea off Cilicia and Pamphylia and put in at Myra in Lycia. [6] There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship bound for Italy and loaded us aboard.

[7] For days we crawled forward, barely making Cnidus. The wind would not let us go where we wanted, so we slipped down past Salmone into the lee of Crete. [8] Clawing along the coast, we somehow reached a place called Fair Havens, near the town of Lasea.

[9] We had burned through a lot of time, and sailing was now dangerous — the Fast had already come and gone. So Paul warned them: [10] "Gentlemen, I can see this voyage is going to end in disaster — heavy losses, not just of cargo and ship, but of our own lives." [11] The centurion, however, trusted the pilot and the ship's owner more than anything Paul had to say. [12] And since the harbor was a poor place to winter, most of them voted to push on — to try to reach Phoenix, a Cretan harbor open to the southwest and northwest, and winter there.

[13] When a soft south wind began to blow, they thought they had what they wanted. They weighed anchor and slid along the Cretan coast, close in. [14] But not long after, a violent wind — the kind they call the Northeaster — came tearing down off the island. [15] It seized the ship. We could not turn her into it, so we gave way and let her run. [16] Running under the lee of a small island called Cauda, we managed — barely — to get the ship's boat under control. [17] After hauling it aboard, they used ropes to undergird the hull itself. Then, terrified of being driven aground on the Syrtis sandbanks, they lowered the gear and let her drift. [18] The next day the storm was hammering us so hard they started throwing cargo overboard. [19] On the third day they heaved the ship's tackle over the side with their own hands. [20] No sun. No stars. Day after day. The storm did not let up, and finally every last hope of being saved drained out of us.

[21] By then no one had eaten in a long while. Paul stood up in the middle of them and said: "Gentlemen, you should have listened to me. You should not have left Crete. You would have spared yourselves this damage and this loss. [22] But now — take heart. Not one of you will lose his life. Only the ship. [23] Last night an angel stood beside me — the angel of the God I belong to, the God I serve — [24] and he said: 'Do not be afraid, Paul. You must stand before Caesar. And listen — God has granted you the lives of every man sailing with you.' [25] So lift your heads, gentlemen. I trust God. It will happen exactly as he said. [26] But we have to run aground on some island."

[27] On the fourteenth night, as we were being driven across the Adriatic, the sailors sensed around midnight that land was near. [28] They took a sounding: twenty fathoms. A little further, another sounding: fifteen. [29] Afraid we would smash on rocks, they threw out four anchors from the stern and prayed for daylight. [30] Then the sailors tried to abandon ship — lowering the boat into the sea under the pretense of running out anchors from the bow. [31] Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers: "Unless these men stay on board, you cannot be saved." [32] So the soldiers cut the ropes to the boat and let it fall away.

[33] Just before dawn, Paul urged everyone to eat. "This is the fourteenth day," he said, "you have been holding on, waiting, taking nothing. [34] Eat something — please. It is for your survival. Not one hair from any of your heads will be lost." [35] Then he took bread, gave thanks to God in front of them all, broke it, and began to eat. [36] All of them took heart and ate too. [37] In all there were two hundred and seventy-six souls on that ship. [38] When they had eaten their fill, they lightened the ship by dumping the grain into the sea.

[39] When daylight came, they did not recognize the land, but they saw a bay with a beach, and they decided to try to run the ship onto it. [40] They cut away the anchors and left them in the sea, untied the rudders, hoisted the foresail to the wind, and made for the beach. [41] But they struck a reef where two seas met and ran the ship aground. The bow stuck fast and would not move; the stern began to break apart in the surf.

[42] The soldiers' plan was to kill the prisoners — keep any of them from swimming away and escaping. [43] But the centurion, wanting to save Paul, stopped them. He ordered those who could swim to jump first and head for shore, [44] and the rest to follow on planks or pieces of the ship. And so it happened — every last one of them reached land alive.

[1] Once we were safe ashore, we learned the island was called Malta. [2] The locals showed us extraordinary kindness. Rain had started falling and the cold was biting, so they built a fire and welcomed every one of us. [3] Paul gathered an armful of brushwood, and as he laid it on the fire, a viper — driven out by the heat — latched onto his hand. [4] When the islanders saw the snake hanging from him, they said to each other, "This man must be a murderer. He survived the sea, but Justice won't let him live." [5] Paul shook the creature off into the fire and felt no harm. [6] They watched, waiting for him to swell up or suddenly drop dead. They watched a long time. Nothing happened. So they flipped their verdict and decided he was a god.

[7] Nearby were the estates of the island's chief official, a man named Publius. He took us in and hosted us generously for three days. [8] His father was bedridden, burning with fever and dysentery. Paul went in, prayed, laid hands on him — and the man was healed. [9] After that, the rest of the sick on the island came, and they too were healed. [10] They honored us in every way possible, and when we finally set sail, they loaded us up with everything we needed.

[11] Three months later we boarded an Alexandrian ship that had wintered at the island — the Twin Brothers carved on her prow. [12] We put in at Syracuse and stayed three days. [13] From there we worked our way up to Rhegium. A south wind came up the next day, and in two days we made Puteoli, [14] where we found believers who begged us to stay a week with them. And then — Rome. [15] Word had reached the believers there, and they came out to meet us — some as far as the Forum of Appius, others as far as the Three Taverns. When Paul saw them, he thanked God and took courage.

[16] When we entered Rome, Paul was allowed to live by himself, with a single soldier guarding him.

[17] Three days in, Paul called together the leading Jews of the city. When they had assembled, he told them: "Brothers, I've done nothing against our people or the customs of our fathers, yet I was handed over in Jerusalem as a prisoner to the Romans. [18] They examined me and wanted to release me — there was no capital case against me. [19] But when the Jewish leaders objected, I was forced to appeal to Caesar. Not that I have any charge to bring against my own people. [20] That's why I asked to see you and speak with you. It's because of the hope of Israel that I'm bound in this chain."

[21] They answered, "We've received no letters about you from Judea, and none of the brothers who've come here has reported anything bad about you. [22] But we'd like to hear what you think. We know this sect — everywhere people are speaking against it."

[23] They set a date and came to his lodging in even larger numbers. From morning until evening Paul laid it out for them — bearing witness to the kingdom of God, working from Moses and the Prophets to convince them about Jesus. [24] Some were persuaded. Others refused to believe. [25] They left arguing among themselves, and Paul fired one last word at them:

"The Holy Spirit nailed it through Isaiah the prophet when he said to your ancestors: [26] *Go to this people and tell them — you will listen and listen and never understand, you will look and look and never see.* [27] *This people's heart has gone numb. Their ears are stopped, their eyes shut tight — otherwise they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts, and turn — and I would heal them.*

[28] "So know this: God's salvation has been sent to the Gentiles. They will listen."

[29]

[30] For two full years Paul stayed there in his own rented quarters, welcoming everyone who came to him, [31] proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ — bold, unhindered, no one shutting him down.

[1] These are the words of Amos — a shepherd from Tekoa — what he saw concerning Israel two years before the earthquake, when Uzziah ruled Judah and Jeroboam son of Joash ruled Israel.

[2] He said:

The LORD roars from Zion,
thunders from Jerusalem —
and the pastures of the shepherds wither,
the summit of Carmel goes brown.

[3] This is what the LORD says:

For three sins of Damascus —
for four — I will not turn back the verdict.
They threshed Gilead with iron sledges,
ground a people into dust.

[4] So I will hurl fire into the house of Hazael,
and it will devour the fortresses of Ben-Hadad.

[5] I will splinter the gate-bar of Damascus,
cut down the one enthroned in the Valley of Aven
and the scepter-holder in Beth-Eden,
and the people of Aram will march into exile at Kir.
The LORD has spoken.

[6] This is what the LORD says:

For three sins of Gaza —
for four — I will not turn back the verdict.
They rounded up entire villages
and sold them off to Edom.

[7] So I will hurl fire onto the walls of Gaza,
and it will devour her fortresses.

[8] I will cut down the one enthroned in Ashdod
and the scepter-holder in Ashkelon.
I will turn my hand against Ekron,
and the last of the Philistines will perish.
So says the Sovereign LORD.

[9] This is what the LORD says:

For three sins of Tyre —
for four — I will not turn back the verdict.
They handed over whole populations to Edom,
ignoring the covenant of brothers.

[10] So I will hurl fire onto the walls of Tyre,
and it will devour her fortresses.

[11] This is what the LORD says:

For three sins of Edom —
for four — I will not turn back the verdict.
He chased his own brother with the sword
and smothered every flicker of mercy.
His anger kept tearing without end;
his fury he nursed forever.

[12] So I will hurl fire onto Teman,
and it will devour the fortresses of Bozrah.

[13] This is what the LORD says:

For three sins of Ammon —
for four — I will not turn back the verdict.
They ripped open pregnant women in Gilead
to enlarge their borders.

[14] So I will set fire to the walls of Rabbah,
and it will devour her fortresses —
with war cries on the day of battle,
with a whirlwind on the day of the storm.

[15] Their king will go into exile,
he and his officials together.
The LORD has spoken.

[1] This is what the LORD says: For three crimes of Moab, for four, I will not turn back the verdict. They burned the bones of Edom's king to lime — desecration, pure and simple. [2] So I will hurl fire into Moab, and it will devour the fortresses of Kerioth. Moab will die in the chaos — war cries, the blast of the ram's horn. [3] I will cut down her ruler from the throne and slaughter every official with him. The LORD has spoken.

[4] This is what the LORD says: For three crimes of Judah, for four, I will not turn back the verdict. They threw away the LORD's instruction and refused to keep his commands. The lies their ancestors chased led them astray. [5] So I will hurl fire into Judah, and it will devour the fortresses of Jerusalem.

[6] This is what the LORD says: For three crimes of Israel, for four, I will not turn back the verdict. They sell the righteous for silver and the desperate for the price of a pair of sandals. [7] They trample the heads of the poor into the dust of the earth and shove the humble out of the way. A father and son use the same girl — and so they drag my holy name through the mud. [8] They lounge beside every altar on clothes seized as collateral, and in the house of their God they drink wine bought with fines they extracted.

[9] Yet I was the one who wiped out the Amorite before them — towering like cedars, strong as oaks — I destroyed his fruit above and his roots below. [10] I was the one who brought you up out of Egypt and led you through the wilderness forty years to take possession of the Amorite's land. [11] I raised up prophets from your sons and Nazirites from your young men. Is this not so, people of Israel? — the LORD's declaration. [12] But you forced wine on the Nazirites and ordered the prophets, "Shut up."

[13] So watch — I am about to press down on you the way a cart loaded with sheaves crushes the ground beneath it. [14] The swift will have nowhere to run. The strong will lose his strength. The warrior will not save his own skin. [15] The archer will not stand his ground. The fast runner will not escape. The horseman will not save his life. [16] On that day, even the bravest fighter will throw down his weapons and run naked for his life. The LORD's declaration.

[1] Listen to this, Israel — every word of it. This is what the LORD speaks against you, against the whole family I pulled out of Egypt:

[2] Of all the families on earth, you are the one I chose to know. That is exactly why I will hold you accountable for every wrong.

[3] Do two people walk the same road unless they've agreed to meet?

[4] Does a lion roar in the forest with no prey in sight? Does a young lion growl from its den if it has caught nothing?

[5] Does a bird drop into a trap on the ground if no bait is set? Does a snare spring up from the dirt when there's nothing to catch?

[6] When the war horn blasts in a city, do people not tremble? When disaster strikes a city, has the LORD not done it?

[7] The Sovereign LORD does nothing without first revealing his plan to his servants the prophets.

[8] The lion has roared — who will not be afraid? The Sovereign LORD has spoken — who can do anything but prophesy?

[9] Announce it from the fortresses of Ashdod, from the fortresses of Egypt. Say: "Gather on the mountains of Samaria — come see the uproar inside her, the violence churning at her core."

[10] They have forgotten how to do what is right, says the LORD. They stockpile their citadels with what they've stolen, with what they've crushed out of others.

[11] So this is what the Sovereign LORD says: An enemy will surround the land. He will tear down your defenses. Your fortresses will be looted clean.

[12] This is what the LORD says: As a shepherd rescues from the lion's mouth two leg-bones or a scrap of an ear — that is all that will be saved of the Israelites who sit in Samaria on the corner of their couches, on their imported cushions.

[13] Hear this, and testify against the house of Jacob, declares the Sovereign LORD, the God of Armies:

[14] On the day I bring Israel to account for her crimes, I will bring the altars of Bethel to account too. The horns of the altar will be hacked off and fall to the ground.

[15] I will smash the winter house along with the summer house. The ivory mansions will be ruined. The great houses will be swept away, declares the LORD.

[1] Listen up, you cows of Bashan grazing on Samaria's hill — women who crush the poor, grind the needy under your heel, who tell your husbands, "Bring me another drink." [2] The Lord Yahweh has sworn by his holiness: the day is coming when they'll drag you out with hooks, every last one of you with fishhooks. [3] You'll stumble out through breaches in the wall, each woman shoved straight through, and tossed toward Harmon. So says Yahweh.

[4] Go ahead — head to Bethel and rebel. Go to Gilgal and rebel some more. Bring your sacrifices every morning, your tithes every three days. [5] Burn your leavened thank-offerings, broadcast your freewill gifts, announce them everywhere — because that's what you love, you Israelites. So says the Lord Yahweh.

[6] I gave you empty bellies in every city, bare cupboards in every town — and still you didn't come back to me. So says Yahweh.

[7] I held back the rain when harvest was three months out. I rained on one city and not another. One field got drenched; the next field withered. [8] People staggered from town to town hunting water, never finding enough — and still you didn't come back to me. So says Yahweh.

[9] I struck your crops with blight and mildew. Your gardens, your vineyards, your fig trees, your olives — the locusts ate them down to nothing. And still you didn't come back to me. So says Yahweh.

[10] I sent plague among you the way I did in Egypt. I killed your best young men by the sword, hauled off your horses, filled your nostrils with the stench of your own dead camps. And still you didn't come back to me. So says Yahweh.

[11] I overthrew you the way I overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah. You were a stick yanked from the fire — barely. And still you didn't come back to me. So says Yahweh.

[12] So this is what I'll do to you, Israel — and because I'm going to do this to you, brace yourself to meet your God, Israel.

[13] Because look — the One who shapes mountains and creates the wind, who tells a man what his own thoughts are, who turns dawn to darkness and walks the high places of the earth — Yahweh, God of Armies, is his name.

[1] Listen to this word I am lifting over you — a funeral song, house of Israel:

[2] She has fallen. Virgin Israel will not rise again. Abandoned on her own soil. No one to lift her up.

[3] Here is what the Lord God says: The city that marches out a thousand strong will come back with a hundred. The one that musters a hundred will return with ten. That is what's left of the house of Israel.

[4] Listen to what the Lord says to the house of Israel: Seek me — and live.

[5] But do not go looking at Bethel. Do not enter Gilgal. Do not cross into Beersheba. Because Gilgal is going into exile, and Bethel will come to nothing.

[6] Seek the Lord — and live — or he will rip through the house of Joseph like fire, and there will be no one in Bethel to put it out.

[7] You who turn justice into wormwood and grind righteousness into the dirt —

[8] the One who made the Pleiades and Orion, who flips deep darkness into morning and turns day black as night, who summons the waters of the sea and pours them across the face of the earth — the Lord is his name.

[9] He flashes destruction down on the strong and brings ruin to the fortress.

[10] You hate the man who calls out wrong at the city gate. You despise the one who speaks the truth.

[11] So — because you trample the poor and extract their grain from them — those mansions you built from cut stone? You will not live in them. Those lush vineyards you planted? You will not drink their wine.

[12] I know how many your crimes are. I know how massive your sins. You harass the righteous, you take bribes, you push the needy aside at the gate.

[13] So the prudent stay silent in such a time. It is an evil time.

[14] Seek good, not evil — and live. Then the Lord, the God of armies, will be with you, the way you keep claiming he already is.

[15] Hate evil. Love good. Set up justice at the gate. Maybe — maybe — the Lord, the God of armies, will show mercy to what is left of Joseph.

[16] So this is what the Lord, the God of armies, the Sovereign, says: Wailing in every public square. Cries of "Disaster! Disaster!" in every street. They will drag the farmer in to mourn and call the professional mourners to lament.

[17] Wailing in every vineyard. Because I am coming through you. The Lord has spoken.

[18] You who long for the day of the Lord — what are you thinking? The day of the Lord is darkness, not light.

[19] It will be like a man running from a lion only to meet a bear — or stumbling home, leaning a hand against the wall, only to be bitten by a snake.

[20] The day of the Lord — darkness, not light. Pitch dark. Not a single ray.

[21] I hate your festivals. I refuse them. I will not breathe in the smoke of your solemn assemblies.

[22] Bring me your burnt offerings, your grain offerings — I will not accept them. Your fattened peace offerings — I will not look at them.

[23] Take your noisy songs away from me. I will not listen to the strumming of your harps.

[24] Let justice roll on like a river, righteousness like a stream that never runs dry.

[25] Did you really bring me sacrifices and offerings out in the wilderness those forty years, house of Israel?

[26] No — you carried Sikkuth your king and Kiyyun your idol, the star-god you made for yourselves.

[27] So I am sending you into exile, past Damascus. The Lord — the God of armies is his name — has spoken.

[1] Disaster waits for you who lounge in Zion, you who feel untouchable on Samaria's hill — the celebrities of the premier nation, the ones Israel runs to for answers.

[2] Take a walk over to Calneh and look. Go on to Hamath the great. Drop down to Philistine Gath. Are you better than these kingdoms? Is your territory wider than theirs?

[3] You shove the day of reckoning out of your mind while you pull violence closer, enthroning it.

[4] You stretch out on ivory beds, sprawl across your couches, feast on lamb from the flock and veal from the stall.

[5] You strum away on your harps, fancying yourselves Davids, inventing new instruments to amuse you.

[6] You drink wine by the bowlful, anoint yourselves with the finest oils — and the wreckage of Joseph doesn't trouble you for a moment.

[7] So you'll be first — first in line for exile. The party's over. The lounging is finished.

[8] The Lord GOD has sworn it by his own life — this is the declaration of the LORD, the God of Armies: I despise Jacob's pride. I hate his fortresses. I'm handing the city over, everything in it.

[9] And if ten men are left standing in one house, they'll die too.

[10] When a relative comes to carry out the corpses for burning and calls to whoever's hiding in the back of the house — "Anyone with you?" — and the answer comes back "No" — he'll say, "Quiet! Don't even whisper the name of the LORD."

[11] Because the LORD has given the order: the great house will be smashed to splinters, the small house to dust.

[12] Do horses gallop on cliff faces? Does anyone plow the sea with oxen? Yet you've turned justice into poison and the fruit of righteousness into bitter weeds.

[13] You celebrate your nothing-victories. You boast, "Didn't we take Karnaim by our own strength?"

[14] Watch this, house of Israel — the declaration of the LORD, the God of Armies: I am raising up a nation against you. They will grind you down from Lebo-hamath to the Wadi of the Arabah.

[1] Here is what the Lord God showed me: he was forming a swarm of locusts just as the late crop was sprouting — the second growth, after the king's share had already been cut.

[2] When the locusts had stripped the land bare, I cried out, "Lord God, forgive! How can Jacob survive? He is so small."

[3] The Lord relented. "It will not happen," he said.

[4] Then the Lord God showed me this: he was summoning fire to deliver judgment. It devoured the great deep and was eating up the land.

[5] I cried, "Lord God, stop! How can Jacob survive? He is so small."

[6] The Lord relented again. "This too will not happen," the Lord God said.

[7] Then he showed me this: the Lord was standing beside a wall built true to plumb, a plumb line in his hand.

[8] The Lord asked me, "Amos, what do you see?"

"A plumb line," I said.

The Lord said, "I am setting a plumb line in the middle of my people Israel. I will not pass them by again.

[9] The high places of Isaac will be wrecked. Israel's sanctuaries will lie in ruins. And I will rise against the house of Jeroboam with a sword."

[10] Amaziah, the priest at Bethel, sent word to Jeroboam king of Israel: "Amos is plotting against you in the heart of Israel. The land cannot hold all his words.

[11] This is what Amos is saying: 'Jeroboam will die by the sword, and Israel will go into exile, far from its own soil.'"

[12] Then Amaziah turned to Amos: "Get out, seer. Run back to Judah. Earn your bread there. Prophesy there.

[13] But never prophesy again at Bethel — this is the king's sanctuary, the royal temple."

[14] Amos answered, "I was no prophet. I was no prophet's son. I was a herdsman, a tender of sycamore figs.

[15] But the Lord took me from behind the flock and said to me, 'Go. Prophesy to my people Israel.'

[16] So listen now to the word of the Lord. You say, 'Do not prophesy against Israel. Stop preaching against the house of Isaac.'

[17] Here is what the Lord says: 'Your wife will become a prostitute in the city. Your sons and daughters will fall by the sword. Your land will be measured out and divided. You yourself will die on unclean soil. And Israel will go into exile, far from its own land.'"

[1] This is what the Lord Yahweh showed me: a basket of summer fruit, ripe and ready.

[2] "Amos, what do you see?"

"A basket of ripe fruit."

Then Yahweh said to me: "The end is ripe for my people Israel. I will not pass them by again.

[3] On that day the temple songs will turn to wailing — declaration of the Lord Yahweh. Corpses everywhere. Thrown wherever. Silence."

[4] Listen, you who trample the needy, you who erase the poor from the land —

[5] you who mutter, "When will the new moon be over so we can sell grain? When will the Sabbath end so we can open the wheat? Let's shrink the measure, inflate the price, rig the scales —

[6] buy the poor for silver, the destitute for a pair of sandals, and pass off the floor-sweepings as wheat."

[7] Yahweh has sworn by the Pride of Jacob: I will never forget a single thing they have done.

[8] Won't the land convulse for this? Won't every soul who lives on it mourn? The whole ground heaves like the Nile — rises, churns, sinks back down like the river of Egypt.

[9] On that day — declaration of the Lord Yahweh — I will make the sun go down at noon. I will darken the earth in broad daylight.

[10] I will turn your festivals into funerals, every song into a dirge. I will wrap sackcloth around every waist, shave every head. I will make it like mourning for an only son — and the end of it, a bitter day.

[11] Listen — the days are coming, declaration of the Lord Yahweh, when I will send a famine on the land — not hunger for bread, not thirst for water, but for hearing the words of Yahweh.

[12] They will stagger from sea to sea, wander from north to east, searching, searching for the word of Yahweh — and they will not find it.

[13] On that day the beautiful young women and the strong young men will faint from thirst —

[14] those who swear by the sin of Samaria, who say, "By the life of your god, Dan," and "By the way of Beersheba" — they will fall, and never get up again.

[1] I saw the Lord standing beside the altar, and he said:

Strike the tops of the pillars until the thresholds shake. Bring them down on the heads of all of them. The last survivors I will kill with the sword. Not one runner will get away. Not one fugitive will escape.

[2] If they dig down to the grave, my hand will drag them up. If they climb to the sky, I will pull them down.

[3] If they hide on the peak of Carmel, I will hunt them and seize them. If they bury themselves on the seafloor, I will command the serpent and it will bite them.

[4] If their enemies march them into exile, I will command the sword and it will cut them down. I have fixed my eyes on them — for ruin, not for good.

[5] The Lord, the God of armies — he touches the earth and it melts, and everyone on it mourns. The whole land swells like the Nile and sinks again like the river of Egypt.

[6] He builds his upper rooms in the heavens and founds his vault on the earth. He summons the waters of the sea and pours them over the face of the ground. The Lord is his name.

[7] Are you anything more to me than the Ethiopians, Israel? — declares the Lord. Yes, I brought you up from Egypt. I also brought the Philistines from Caphtor and the Arameans from Kir.

[8] The eyes of the Sovereign Lord are on this guilty kingdom. I will wipe it off the face of the earth — but I will not utterly destroy the house of Jacob, says the Lord.

[9] Look — I am giving the order. I will shake the house of Israel through every nation the way grain is shaken in a sieve, and not one pebble will fall through.

[10] All the sinners among my people will die by the sword — all who say, "Disaster won't catch us. It won't even come close."

[11] On that day I will raise up the fallen shelter of David. I will patch its cracks, lift up its ruins, and rebuild it as it was in the old days —

[12] so that they may possess what remains of Edom and every nation that bears my name — declares the Lord, who will do this.

[13] The days are coming, says the Lord, when the one plowing will catch up to the one reaping, and the one treading grapes will catch up to the one sowing seed. The mountains will drip with sweet wine, and every hill will run with it.

[14] I will bring back my people Israel from exile. They will rebuild the ruined cities and live in them. They will plant vineyards and drink the wine. They will make gardens and eat the fruit.

[15] I will plant them in their own soil, and they will never again be uprooted from the land I have given them, says the Lord your God.

[1] Paul, sent by God's own choosing as an emissary of Christ Jesus — and Timothy, our brother — [2] to the holy ones in Colossae, the faithful family in Christ: grace to you, and peace, from God our Father.

[3] We never stop thanking God — the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ — when we pray for you. [4] We've heard about your faith in Christ Jesus and the love you pour out for everyone who belongs to him, [5] all of it anchored in the hope stored up for you in heaven. That hope first reached you through the true message — the gospel — [6] which is now spreading across the whole world, bearing fruit and multiplying everywhere it goes, just as it has among you from the day you first heard it and understood the grace of God for what it really is. [7] You learned it from Epaphras, our beloved fellow servant, a faithful minister of Christ on your behalf, [8] who told us about the love the Spirit has stirred up in you.

[9] So from the day we heard, we haven't stopped praying for you. We're asking God to fill you with deep knowledge of his will — the kind that comes wrapped in spiritual wisdom and understanding — [10] so that the way you live actually matches the Lord you serve. We want you fruitful in every good work, growing in your knowledge of God, [11] strengthened with everything his glorious power can supply, so you can hold up under anything with patience — and even joy. [12] Give thanks to the Father, who has qualified you to share in what belongs to his holy ones in the kingdom of light. [13] He rescued us from the dominion of darkness and moved us into the kingdom of his beloved Son, [14] in whom we have liberation — the forgiveness of our sins.

[15] He is the image of the invisible God,
the firstborn over all creation.
[16] In him everything was made —
things in heaven, things on earth,
visible, invisible,
thrones, powers, rulers, authorities —
every last one of them was created through him and for him.
[17] He is before all things,
and in him every atom holds together.
[18] He is the head of the body — the church.
He is the beginning,
the firstborn from among the dead,
so that in everything he comes out first.
[19] Because God was pleased to let the whole fullness of himself dwell in him,
[20] and through him to reconcile everything back to himself —
whether on earth or in the heavens —
making peace through the blood of his cross.

[21] And you — once strangers, hostile in your minds, your lives evidence of it — [22] he has now reconciled, through Christ's physical body, through his death, in order to present you before himself holy, spotless, beyond accusation — [23] if you stay grounded in the faith, settled and steady, refusing to drift from the hope of the gospel you heard, the gospel that has been announced to every creature under heaven, the gospel I, Paul, became a servant of.

[24] Right now I'm rejoicing in what I'm suffering for you, and in my own body I'm filling up what's still lacking in Christ's afflictions — for the sake of his body, the church. [25] I became its servant when God handed me a stewardship aimed squarely at you: to deliver the word of God in full — [26] the mystery hidden across ages and generations, now unveiled to his holy ones. [27] To them God chose to make known how staggering this mystery's glory is among the nations: Christ in you, the hope of glory.

[28] He is the one we proclaim. We warn everyone. We teach everyone — with all the wisdom we have — so that we can present every person mature in Christ. [29] That's what I'm working for, struggling for, with all the energy he sets blazing inside me.

[1] I want you to know how hard I'm fighting for you — for the believers in Laodicea, for everyone who's never even seen my face. [2] I want your hearts braced and bound together in love, rich with the full assurance that comes from understanding — knowing the mystery of God himself: Christ. [3] In him every treasure of wisdom and knowledge is buried.

[4] I'm telling you this so nobody can talk you out of it with smooth arguments. [5] Because even though I'm not there in person, I'm with you in spirit — and I'm thrilled to see how disciplined you are, how solid your trust in Christ.

[6] So then — you received Christ Jesus as Lord. Now walk in him. [7] Sink your roots into him. Let him build you up. Stand firm in the faith you were taught, and let gratitude spill out of you.

[8] Watch out — don't let anyone drag you off as a prize through hollow philosophy, slick deception built on human tradition and the elemental forces of this world rather than on Christ. [9] Because in him the entire fullness of deity lives — in a body. [10] And in him, you've been filled. He is the head over every ruler, every authority.

[11] In him you were circumcised — not with a blade, but with the stripping away of your old flesh-self. This is the circumcision of Christ. [12] You were buried with him in baptism, and you were raised with him through faith in the power of God who lifted him from the dead.

[13] You were dead — corpses in your sins, in the uncircumcision of your flesh — and he made you alive together with him. He forgave us everything. [14] He erased the IOU that stood against us, the legal record with all its damning fine print. He took it out of the way. He nailed it to the cross. [15] He stripped the rulers and authorities, paraded them through the streets, made a public spectacle of them — triumphing over them in the cross.

[16] So don't let anyone judge you over what you eat or drink, or about a festival, a new moon, a Sabbath. [17] These were shadows of what was coming. The body casting the shadow is Christ.

[18] Don't let anyone disqualify you — anyone who gets off on false humility and angel worship, parading visions, puffed up by his own fleshly mind for no reason, [19] not holding on to the Head. The whole body — supplied and held together through joints and ligaments — grows with the growth that comes from God.

[20] If you died with Christ to the elemental forces of this world, why are you still acting like you belong to it? Why submit to its rules — [21] "Don't touch! Don't taste! Don't handle!" — [22] regulations about things that are destined to perish the moment you use them? These are human commands, human teachings. [23] They've got the look of wisdom — self-imposed religion, fake humility, brutal treatment of the body — but they're worthless. They do nothing to curb the appetites of the flesh.

[1] So — if you've been raised with the Messiah, chase what's above, where he sits enthroned at God's right hand. [2] Fix your mind on what's up there, not on what's down here. [3] You died.
