Your real life is tucked away with the Messiah in God. [4] And when he steps onto the stage — the Messiah, who is your life — you'll step out with him, drenched in glory.

[5] So kill it. Every part of you that still belongs to the dirt: sexual chaos, filth, runaway desire, twisted appetite, and greed — which is just idolatry wearing a respectable suit. [6] God's fury is coming down on exactly these things. [7] You used to walk that road. You used to live there. [8] But now — strip it all off. The rage. The temper. The malice. The slander. The trash that falls out of your mouth. [9] Stop lying to each other. You've shed the old self like an old skin, with everything it used to do, [10] and you've pulled on the new one — being remade, image by image, into the likeness of the One who made you, getting truer knowledge every day. [11] In this new humanity there's no Greek, no Jew, no circumcised, no uncircumcised, no barbarian, no Scythian, no slave, no free. The Messiah is everything, and he's in everything.

[12] So — as God's chosen ones, set apart and dearly loved — clothe yourselves: tender compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness, patience. [13] Bear with each other. Forgive each other. If anyone has a grievance against anyone, let it go — the Lord let yours go, so you do the same. [14] And over all of it, throw on love. Love is what binds everything into one seamless whole. [15] Let the Messiah's peace call the shots in your hearts — this is what you were called into, one body together. And be thankful. [16] Let the word of the Messiah move in among you, generous and rich. Teach each other. Warn each other. Wise each other up. Sing — psalms, hymns, songs that come from the Spirit — gratitude pouring out of your hearts to God. [17] Whatever you do — in word or in action — do it all in the name of the Lord Jesus, with thanks rising through him to God the Father.

[18] Wives, lean into your husbands, as fits the Lord. [19] Husbands, love your wives — and don't go bitter on them. [20] Children, obey your parents in everything; this is what delights the Lord. [21] Fathers, don't grind your children down, or they'll lose heart. [22] Slaves, obey your earthly masters in everything — not just when they're watching, trying to score points — but with an undivided heart, in reverence for the Lord. [23] Whatever you're doing, throw your soul into it. You're working for the Lord, not for people. [24] You know the payoff is coming — the Lord's inheritance. The Master you're really serving is the Messiah. [25] And the one who does wrong will get back exactly what he did. No favorites.

[1] Masters, give your slaves what is right and fair. Remember — you have a Master too, and he's in heaven.

[2] Hold the line in prayer. Stay awake at your post. Pray with thanks on your lips.

[3] And while you're at it, pray for us — that God would crack open a door for the message, so we can speak the mystery of Christ. The very thing that landed me in chains.

[4] Pray that I lay it out clearly — the way it needs to be said.

[5] Move wisely among outsiders. Every moment matters — spend them well.

[6] Let your speech carry grace, seasoned with salt — so you know how to answer anyone who asks.

[7] Tychicus will fill you in on everything happening with me. He's a brother I love, a faithful servant, a fellow slave in the Lord's work.

[8] That's exactly why I'm sending him — so you'd know how we're doing, and so he could put heart back into yours.

[9] He's bringing Onesimus with him — also faithful, also a brother I love, and one of your own. Between them, you'll hear everything that's going on here.

[10] Aristarchus, locked up with me, sends his greetings. So does Mark, Barnabas's cousin. (You've already gotten instructions about him — if he comes your way, welcome him.)

[11] Jesus, the one called Justus, also greets you. These three are the only Jewish believers working alongside me for the kingdom of God — and they have been a balm to me.

[12] Epaphras, one of your own and a slave of Christ Jesus, sends greetings. He never stops wrestling for you in prayer — that you'd stand mature and fully convinced in everything God wants.

[13] I'll vouch for him — he pours himself out for you, and for the people in Laodicea and Hierapolis.

[14] Luke, our beloved doctor, greets you. So does Demas.

[15] Pass my greetings to the brothers and sisters in Laodicea, and to Nympha and the church that meets in her house.

[16] Once this letter has been read among you, pass it on — have the Laodiceans read it too. And make sure you read the one coming from Laodicea.

[17] And tell Archippus: "Watch the ministry the Lord handed you. See it through."

[18] This greeting is in my own hand — Paul. Remember my chains. Grace be with you.

[1] In the third year of Jehoiakim's reign over Judah, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon marched on Jerusalem and locked it down. [2] The Lord handed Jehoiakim over to him, along with part of the treasure from God's house. Nebuchadnezzar hauled it all back to the land of Shinar, into the temple of his god, and stored the sacred vessels in his god's treasury.

[3] Then the king gave the order to Ashpenaz, chief of his court officials: bring in some of the Israelites — royal blood, nobility — [4] young men without a flaw on them, striking in appearance, sharp in every kind of learning, quick to grasp knowledge, well-informed, men with the poise to stand in the king's palace. Teach them the literature and the language of the Babylonians. [5] The king assigned them a daily ration from his own table and his own wine, three years of training, and after that they would enter royal service.

[6] Among them, from the tribe of Judah, were Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah. [7] The chief official renamed them: Daniel became Belteshazzar, Hananiah became Shadrach, Mishael became Meshach, and Azariah became Abednego.

[8] But Daniel made up his mind — he would not let the king's food and wine make him unclean. So he asked the chief official for permission to refuse it. [9] God had already given Daniel favor and sympathy in the eyes of the official, [10] but the man was nervous. "I'm afraid of my lord the king," he told Daniel. "He's the one who decided what you eat and drink. If he sees you looking worse than the other young men your age, my head is on the line."

[11] So Daniel went to the steward the chief official had assigned over him and his three friends. [12] "Test us," he said. "Ten days. Give us nothing but vegetables to eat and water to drink. [13] Then compare us with the young men eating the king's food, and decide based on what you see."

[14] The steward agreed and tested them for ten days. [15] At the end of it, they looked healthier and better fed than any of the young men eating from the royal table. [16] So the steward kept taking away their gourmet meals and wine, and brought them vegetables instead.

[17] As for these four — God gave them mastery of every kind of literature and learning. And Daniel could read visions and dreams of every sort.

[18] When the training period ended, the chief official brought them all before Nebuchadnezzar. [19] The king interviewed them, and out of the whole group no one measured up to Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah. So they entered the king's service. [20] On every question of wisdom and discernment the king put to them, he found them ten times better than every magician and enchanter in his entire realm.

[21] And Daniel remained there until the first year of King Cyrus.

[1] In year two of his reign, Nebuchadnezzar started having dreams. They rattled him. Sleep was gone.

[2] So he summoned them all — the magicians, the enchanters, the sorcerers, the Chaldean wise men — every last one. Tell me what I dreamed. They came and stood before the king.

[3] He said to them, "A dream came to me, and my spirit is in pieces trying to understand it."

[4] The Chaldeans answered him in Aramaic: "O king, live forever. Tell your servants the dream — we'll give you the meaning."

[5] The king answered the Chaldeans: "My word stands. If you don't tell me both the dream and its meaning, you'll be cut to pieces and your houses turned into rubble heaps.

[6] But if you reveal the dream and what it means — gifts, rewards, honor beyond measure. So speak. The dream. The meaning. Now."

[7] They answered again: "Let the king tell his servants the dream, and we will give the interpretation."

[8] The king said: "I know exactly what you're doing. You're buying time, because you can see my word stands.

[9] If you cannot tell me the dream, there is only one verdict for you. You've agreed among yourselves to feed me lies and stalling until the situation changes. Tell me the dream — then I'll know you can interpret it."

[10] The Chaldeans answered before the king: "There is no one on earth who can do what the king asks. No king, however great, however powerful, has ever demanded such a thing of any magician or enchanter or Chaldean.

[11] What the king asks is impossible. No one can reveal it to the king except the gods — and the gods do not live among mortals."

[12] At this the king went volcanic. He gave the order: kill every wise man in Babylon.

[13] The decree went out. The wise men were being hunted down to die. They came looking for Daniel and his friends, to kill them too.

[14] Daniel responded to Arioch, captain of the king's guard, who had gone out to execute the wise men of Babylon — and he spoke with discretion and tact.

[15] He asked Arioch, the king's officer, "Why is the decree from the king so severe?" Arioch laid out the whole situation for Daniel.

[16] Daniel went in and asked the king for time — time enough to give the king the interpretation.

[17] Then Daniel went home and told Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah what was happening.

[18] He told them to beg the God of heaven for mercy concerning this mystery — so that Daniel and his friends would not be wiped out with the rest of Babylon's wise men.

[19] That night the mystery was unveiled to Daniel in a vision. And Daniel blessed the God of heaven.

[20] Daniel said:
"Blessed be the name of God — forever, and forever again.
Wisdom is his. Power is his.

[21] He shifts the times and the seasons.
He topples kings and crowns them.
He gives wisdom to the wise
and knowledge to those with eyes to see.

[22] He uncovers the deep things, the hidden things.
He knows what waits inside the dark —
and light lives with him.

[23] You, God of my fathers — I give you thanks, I give you praise.
You gave me wisdom and strength.
What we asked, you have shown us.
You have shown us what the king demanded."

[24] So Daniel went to Arioch — the man the king had appointed to destroy the wise men of Babylon — and said: "Don't execute the wise men. Bring me in to the king. I'll give him the interpretation."

[25] Arioch rushed Daniel into the king's presence and announced: "I have found a man among the exiles from Judah who can tell the king the meaning."

[26] The king said to Daniel — also called Belteshazzar — "Are you really able to tell me the dream I saw, and what it means?"

[27] Daniel answered the king: "The mystery the king is asking about — no wise man, no enchanter, no magician, no astrologer can show the king.

[28] But there is a God in heaven who unveils mysteries. And he has shown King Nebuchadnezzar what will happen in the days to come. Here is your dream, here are the visions that passed through your mind as you lay in bed:

[29] As you lay there, O king, your thoughts turned to what comes next — and the One who unveils mysteries showed you what is coming.

[30] As for me — this mystery has not been revealed to me because I'm wiser than anyone else alive. It was revealed so that the king would know the meaning, and so that you would understand the thoughts of your own heart.

[31] This is what you saw, O king: A statue. Massive. Towering. Blazing with brilliance. Standing in front of you. Terrifying to look at.

[32] The statue's head was pure gold. Its chest and arms were silver. Its belly and thighs were bronze.

[33] Its legs were iron. Its feet were part iron, part clay.

[34] You kept watching — and a stone broke loose. Not by human hands. It struck the statue on its feet of iron and clay and shattered them.

[35] Then the whole thing came down at once — iron, clay, bronze, silver, gold — all of it crushed together, like chaff on a summer threshing floor. The wind carried it off and not a trace remained. But the stone that struck the statue became a mountain. It filled the whole earth.

[36] That was the dream. Now we will tell the king what it means.

[37] You, O king — king of kings — to whom the God of heaven has given the kingdom, the power, the strength, and the glory;

[38] into whose hand he has given all of it — humans, wild animals, the birds of the sky — wherever they live, he has put under your authority — you are that head of gold.

[39] After you, another kingdom will rise — lesser than yours. Then a third, of bronze, that will rule the whole earth.

[40] Then a fourth — strong as iron. Iron shatters everything; it breaks all things. Like iron that crushes, this kingdom will crush and break them all.

[41] The feet and toes you saw — part potter's clay, part iron — that's a divided kingdom. It will still have iron's hardness in it, just as you saw iron mixed with wet clay.

[42] The toes, part iron and part clay — the kingdom will be partly strong and partly brittle.

[43] You saw iron mixed with clay — they will try to bind themselves together through intermarriage, but they will not hold. Iron does not fuse with clay.

[44] In the days of those kings, the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that will never be destroyed. It will not be handed off to another people. It will grind every one of those kingdoms to dust and put an end to them — and it will stand forever.

[45] This is the stone you saw — cut from the mountain, but not by human hands — shattering iron, bronze, clay, silver, gold. The great God has shown the king what comes next. The dream is sure. The interpretation is solid."

[46] Then King Nebuchadnezzar fell on his face before Daniel. He bowed down. He ordered that offerings and incense be presented to him.

[47] The king said to Daniel: "Truly your God is the God of gods, the Lord of kings, the One who unveils mysteries — for you were able to unveil this mystery."

[48] Then the king elevated Daniel — heaped gifts on him, made him ruler over the whole province of Babylon, and chief over all its wise men.

[49] At Daniel's request, the king appointed Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego over the administration of the province of Babylon. Daniel himself remained at the king's court.

[1] King Nebuchadnezzar built a statue of gold — ninety feet tall, nine feet wide — and set it up on the plain of Dura in the province of Babylon.

[2] Then he summoned them all: satraps, prefects, governors, advisers, treasurers, judges, magistrates, every official of every province — ordered them to assemble for the dedication of the statue the king had erected.

[3] So they gathered — every official from every corner of the empire — and stood before the thing Nebuchadnezzar had raised.

[4] A herald cried out with full force: "This is the command, you peoples, nations, every tongue —

[5] the moment you hear the horn, the pipe, the lyre, the harp, the trumpet, the whole orchestra — fall down. Worship the golden statue King Nebuchadnezzar has set up.

[6] Anyone who refuses to fall and worship will be thrown — that same hour — into a furnace blazing white-hot."

[7] So when the music struck — every horn, every pipe, every string — the peoples, the nations, every tongue dropped to the ground and worshiped the golden statue Nebuchadnezzar had set up.

[8] Then certain Chaldeans stepped forward and laid charges against the Jews.

[9] They said to the king: "O King — live forever.

[10] You gave the order, Your Majesty: every man who hears the music must fall and worship the golden statue.

[11] And whoever refuses gets the furnace — blazing.

[12] Well — there are certain Jews you appointed over the affairs of Babylon: Shadrach, Meshach, Abednego. These men, O King, pay you no mind. They do not serve your gods. They will not worship the golden statue you set up."

[13] Nebuchadnezzar exploded — burning with rage — and commanded that Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego be brought in. So they brought the men before the king.

[14] Nebuchadnezzar said to them: "Shadrach. Meshach. Abednego. Is it true — you will not serve my gods? You will not worship the golden statue I have set up?

[15] Here is your chance. The instant you hear the horn, the pipe, the lyre, the harp, the trumpet — the whole orchestra — fall down. Worship the statue I have made. Do that, and all is well. Refuse, and that same hour you will be hurled into the furnace blazing white-hot. And then — what god is there who can rescue you from my hand?"

[16] Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego answered the king: "Nebuchadnezzar — we have no need to defend ourselves to you on this.

[17] If it happens — our God whom we serve is able to rescue us from the blazing furnace. He will rescue us from your hand, O King.

[18] But even if he does not — know this, O King: we will not serve your gods. We will not worship the golden statue you have set up."

[19] Then Nebuchadnezzar was filled with fury — his whole face changed against Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. He ordered the furnace stoked seven times hotter than it had ever been.

[20] He commanded the strongest soldiers in his army to tie up Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego and hurl them into the furnace blazing white-hot.

[21] So they were bound — still in their robes, their trousers, their turbans, all their clothing — and thrown into the heart of the blazing furnace.

[22] Because the king's order was so urgent and the furnace so impossibly hot, the flames killed the men who carried Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego up to it.

[23] And the three of them — Shadrach, Meshach, Abednego — fell, bound, into the blazing furnace.

[24] Then King Nebuchadnezzar jumped to his feet, stunned. He said to his counselors: "Did we not throw three men into the fire — bound?" They answered: "Certainly, O King."

[25] He said: "Look — I see four men. Untied. Walking around in the middle of the fire. Unharmed. And the fourth — he looks like a son of the gods."

[26] Nebuchadnezzar approached the mouth of the blazing furnace and called out: "Shadrach! Meshach! Abednego! Servants of the Most High God — come out. Come here." So they walked out of the fire.

[27] The satraps, prefects, governors, the king's counselors crowded around and stared at these men — the fire had not touched their bodies. Not a hair on their heads was singed. Their robes were unscorched. They did not even smell like smoke.

[28] Nebuchadnezzar said: "Blessed be the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — who sent his angel and rescued his servants. They trusted him. They defied the king's command. They handed over their own bodies rather than serve or worship any god but their own.

[29] So I make this decree: any people, any nation, any tongue that speaks one word against the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — they will be torn limb from limb and their houses turned to rubble. Because no other god can rescue like this."

[30] Then the king promoted Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — raised them high in the province of Babylon.

[1] King Nebuchadnezzar, to every people, nation, and language living on the earth: May your peace overflow.

[2] It seems good to me to publish the signs and wonders the Most High God has worked in my life.

[3] His signs—how vast. His wonders—how mighty. His kingdom is an everlasting kingdom; his rule runs from generation to generation.

[4] I, Nebuchadnezzar, was at ease in my house, flourishing in my palace.

[5] Then a dream came and shook me. The images on my bed, the visions in my head—they terrified me.

[6] So I issued a decree: bring me every wise man in Babylon to interpret the dream.

[7] In came the magicians, the enchanters, the astrologers, the diviners. I told them the dream. Not one could tell me what it meant.

[8] Finally Daniel came in—the one I named Belteshazzar after the name of my god, the one in whom the spirit of the holy gods lives. I told him the dream:

[9] "Belteshazzar, chief of the magicians—I know the spirit of the holy gods is in you, and no mystery defeats you. Here is what I saw. Tell me what it means.

[10] As I lay in bed, this is the vision that crossed my mind: a tree stood at the center of the earth. Enormous.

[11] The tree grew and grew strong. Its top scraped heaven. It could be seen from the ends of the earth.

[12] Its leaves were beautiful, its fruit abundant—food for everything alive. Wild animals took shade beneath it, birds nested in its branches, every living thing fed from it.

[13] Then, still watching from my bed, I saw a holy watcher come down from heaven.

[14] He called out with force: 'Cut down the tree. Hack off its branches. Strip its leaves. Scatter its fruit. Let the animals flee from under it, the birds from its branches.

[15] But leave the stump and the roots in the ground, bound with iron and bronze, in the grass of the open field. Let him be drenched by the dew of heaven. Let him graze with the animals on the earth.

[16] Let his mind be changed from a man's. Let an animal's mind be given to him. Let seven seasons pass over him.

[17] This sentence comes from the watchers. This verdict comes from the holy ones. The point is for every living person to know: the Most High rules over every human kingdom. He gives it to whomever he chooses. He sets the lowest of men over it.'

[18] That is the dream I, King Nebuchadnezzar, saw. Now you, Belteshazzar, give me the interpretation. Every wise man in my kingdom has failed. But you can do it—because the spirit of the holy gods is in you."

[19] Then Daniel—called Belteshazzar—stood there, stunned for a long moment. His thoughts terrified him. The king said, "Belteshazzar, don't let the dream or its meaning frighten you." Belteshazzar answered, "My lord—may the dream be for those who hate you, its meaning for your enemies.

[20] The tree you saw, growing strong, its top reaching heaven, visible across the whole earth,

[21] with beautiful leaves and abundant fruit, food for all, shelter for the wild animals, perch for the birds—

[22] that tree, O king, is you. You have grown vast and strong. Your greatness has climbed to heaven; your dominion stretches to the ends of the earth.

[23] And the watcher you saw coming down from heaven, saying, 'Cut the tree down, destroy it, but leave the stump and roots in the ground, bound with iron and bronze in the grass of the field, drenched with the dew of heaven, sharing life with the wild animals until seven seasons pass over him'—

[24] this is what it means, O king. This is the decree of the Most High that has fallen on my lord the king:

[25] You will be driven out from humanity. You will live with the wild animals. You will graze on grass like an ox, soaked by the dew of heaven. Seven seasons will pass over you—until you learn that the Most High rules over every human kingdom and hands it to whomever he chooses.

[26] As for the order to leave the stump and roots—your kingdom will be returned to you when you finally know that Heaven rules.

[27] So, O king, take my counsel. Break with your sins—do what is right. Break with your wrongs—show mercy to the oppressed. Perhaps then your prosperity may continue."

[28] All of it came down on King Nebuchadnezzar.

[29] Twelve months later, he was walking on the roof of his royal palace in Babylon.

[30] And the king said, "Look at this—Babylon the great. I built it. With my own power. For my royal residence. For the glory of my majesty."

[31] The words were still in his mouth when a voice fell from heaven: "King Nebuchadnezzar, hear this: the kingdom has departed from you.

[32] You will be driven from humanity. You will live with the wild animals. You will graze on grass like an ox. Seven seasons will pass over you—until you know that the Most High rules over every human kingdom and gives it to whomever he chooses."

[33] In that instant the word landed. Nebuchadnezzar was driven from humanity. He ate grass like an ox. His body was drenched with the dew of heaven, until his hair grew long like eagles' feathers and his nails like the claws of a bird.

[34] At the end of the days, I, Nebuchadnezzar, lifted my eyes toward heaven, and my mind came back. I blessed the Most High. I praised and honored the One who lives forever: his rule is an everlasting rule, his kingdom runs from generation to generation.

[35] All the inhabitants of the earth count for nothing. He does as he pleases with the army of heaven and with the inhabitants of the earth. Nothing can stop his hand. No one can ask him, "What have you done?"

[36] At that moment my mind came back to me. The glory of my kingdom, my majesty, my splendor returned. My counselors and nobles came looking for me. My throne was restored. Greater authority than before was added to me.

[37] So now I, Nebuchadnezzar—I praise, I exalt, I glorify the King of heaven. Everything he does is right; every road he walks is just. And those who walk in pride—he is able to bring low.

[1] King Belshazzar threw a massive banquet for a thousand of his nobles, and he was drinking wine in front of all of them.

[2] Tasting the wine, Belshazzar gave the order — bring out the gold and silver vessels his father Nebuchadnezzar had hauled out of the temple in Jerusalem. The king would drink from them. His nobles, his wives, his concubines — all of them.

[3] So they brought in the gold vessels stripped from God's house in Jerusalem, and the king and his court drank from them —

[4] drank wine and toasted the gods of gold and silver, bronze and iron, wood and stone.

[5] That instant, the fingers of a human hand appeared and began writing on the plaster of the palace wall, right beside the lampstand. The king watched the hand as it wrote.

[6] The color drained from his face. His thoughts went wild with terror. His hips went loose beneath him and his knees knocked together.

[7] He bellowed for the enchanters, the Chaldeans, the astrologers to be brought in. He announced to the wise men of Babylon: whoever can read this writing and tell me what it means — purple robes, a gold chain around his neck, and the third place in the kingdom.

[8] So in came all the king's wise men. Not one of them could read the writing or tell the king what it meant.

[9] Belshazzar grew more terrified. His face went whiter still. His nobles were paralyzed.

[10] The queen mother, hearing the uproar from the king and his lords, swept into the banquet hall. "Long live the king!" she said. "Don't let your thoughts terrify you. Don't let the color drain from your face.

[11] There is a man in your kingdom — the spirit of the holy gods is in him. In your father's day, illumination and insight and wisdom like the wisdom of the gods were found in him. King Nebuchadnezzar — your father, O king — appointed him chief of the magicians, enchanters, Chaldeans, and astrologers.

[12] Because in this man Daniel — whom the king renamed Belteshazzar — there was an extraordinary spirit, knowledge, insight, the ability to interpret dreams, explain riddles, and untie tangled knots. Summon Daniel. He will tell you what it means."

[13] So Daniel was brought before the king. The king said to him, "So you are Daniel — one of the exiles from Judah, the ones my father the king brought back from Judah.

[14] I've heard that the spirit of the gods is in you, that illumination and insight and exceptional wisdom were found in you.

[15] The wise men, the enchanters — they were brought in to read this writing and tell me its meaning, but they couldn't get to the bottom of it.

[16] I've heard you can give interpretations and untie knots. Read this writing for me, tell me what it means, and you'll be clothed in purple, wear gold around your neck, and rule as third in the kingdom."

[17] Daniel answered the king: "Keep your gifts. Give your rewards to someone else. But the writing — I will read it for the king, and I will tell him what it means.

[18] O king, the Most High God gave your father Nebuchadnezzar a kingdom, and greatness, and glory, and majesty.

[19] Because of the greatness God gave him, every people, every nation, every language trembled in fear before him. He killed whomever he wanted to kill. He let live whomever he wanted to let live. He raised up whomever he pleased, and brought down whomever he pleased.

[20] But when his heart swelled and his spirit grew hard with pride, he was deposed from his royal throne and stripped of his glory.

[21] He was driven out from human company, his mind reduced to that of an animal. He lived with the wild donkeys, ate grass like an ox, his body soaked with the dew of heaven — until he learned that the Most High God rules over the kingdoms of men and sets over them whomever he chooses.

[22] And you, his son Belshazzar — you knew all of this. And still you have not humbled your heart.

[23] You raised yourself up against the Lord of heaven. The vessels of his house were brought before you, and you and your nobles, your wives and your concubines, drank wine from them. You praised gods of silver and gold, bronze, iron, wood, and stone — gods that cannot see, cannot hear, cannot know a thing. But the God who holds your breath in his hand and owns every road you walk — him you did not honor.

[24] So from his presence the hand was sent, and this writing was inscribed.

[25] And this is what was written: MENE, MENE, TEKEL, PARSIN.

[26] This is what it means: MENE — God has numbered the days of your kingdom and brought it to an end.

[27] TEKEL — you have been weighed on the scales and found wanting.

[28] PERES — your kingdom is divided and handed over to the Medes and Persians."

[29] Then Belshazzar gave the order. They clothed Daniel in purple, hung a gold chain around his neck, and proclaimed him third ruler in the kingdom.

[30] That very night, Belshazzar king of the Chaldeans was killed.

[31] And Darius the Mede received the kingdom, at the age of sixty-two.

[1] Darius decided to organize the kingdom under a hundred and twenty satraps, scattered across every province, [2] with three commissioners over them — Daniel being one — so the satraps would answer to them and the king would not be cheated. [3] Daniel quickly outclassed the other commissioners and satraps. Something extraordinary moved in him, and the king began planning to set him over the whole kingdom.

[4] So the other commissioners and satraps went hunting — looking for something, anything, to pin on Daniel in matters of state. But they found nothing. No corruption, no negligence. The man was clean. [5] Finally they said to each other, "We will never find anything against this Daniel unless it has to do with the law of his God."

[6] So the commissioners and satraps swarmed the king as a delegation. "King Darius — live forever! [7] All of us — commissioners, prefects, satraps, advisors, governors — have agreed: the king should issue a decree, an unbreakable order, that for thirty days anyone who prays to any god or man other than you, O king, will be thrown into the lions' den. [8] Now, O king, sign the decree and put it in writing, so it cannot be altered — according to the law of the Medes and Persians, which cannot be revoked." [9] So King Darius signed the decree into law.

[10] When Daniel learned the document had been signed, he went home. The upper windows of his room faced Jerusalem, and there he knelt — three times a day, as he always had — praying and giving thanks to his God. [11] The men came swarming in and found Daniel — praying. Pleading before his God. [12] So they went straight to the king and reminded him about the decree: "Did you not sign an order that for thirty days anyone who prays to any god or man other than you, O king, would be thrown to the lions?" The king answered, "Yes — the matter stands. By the law of the Medes and Persians, which cannot be revoked." [13] Then they sprang it: "Daniel — one of the exiles from Judah — pays no attention to you, O king, or to the decree you signed. Three times a day he prays."

[14] When the king heard this, it gutted him. He set his mind on saving Daniel, working until sunset to find a way out. [15] But the men crowded back in. "Remember, O king — by the law of the Medes and Persians, no decree the king issues can be changed."

[16] So the king gave the order, and Daniel was brought out and thrown into the lions' den. The king said to Daniel, "May your God — the one you serve without ceasing — rescue you." [17] A stone was rolled over the mouth of the den, and the king sealed it with his own signet ring and the rings of his nobles, so nothing about Daniel's situation could be tampered with.

[18] The king went back to the palace and spent the night fasting. No entertainment was brought before him. Sleep would not come.

[19] At the first gray light of dawn, the king was up and running to the lions' den. [20] As he got close, he called out to Daniel — his voice cracking — "Daniel! Servant of the living God! Has your God, the one you serve without ceasing, been able to save you from the lions?"

[21] And Daniel called back: "O king — live forever! [22] My God sent his angel and shut the lions' mouths. They have not hurt me — because I was found innocent before him. And before you too, O king, I have done no wrong."

[23] The king was overjoyed. He ordered Daniel lifted out of the den. And when they pulled him up — not a scratch on him. Because he had trusted his God.

[24] Then the king gave the order, and the men who had accused Daniel were brought in — along with their wives and their children — and thrown into the den. Before they hit the floor, the lions were on them, crushing every bone.

[25] Then King Darius wrote to every people, nation, and language across the earth:

"Peace and abundance to you. [26] I issue this decree: throughout every part of my kingdom, let people tremble in awe before the God of Daniel.

For he is the living God,
the one who endures forever.
His kingdom will never fall,
his rule has no end.
[27] He rescues. He delivers.
He works signs and wonders
in the heavens and on the earth.
He is the one who saved Daniel
from the power of the lions."

[28] So Daniel flourished — through the reign of Darius and on into the reign of Cyrus the Persian.

[1] In the first year of Belshazzar's reign over Babylon, Daniel had a dream—visions firing across his mind as he lay on his bed. He wrote it down. Here is the substance of it.

[2] Daniel said: I was watching in the night, and the four winds of heaven were churning the great sea into chaos.

[3] Then four massive beasts came surging up out of the water, each one different from the others.

[4] The first looked like a lion, but it had the wings of an eagle. I kept watching, and its wings were ripped off. It was hauled up off the ground and made to stand on two feet like a man—and a human heart was given to it.

[5] Then came a second beast, this one like a bear, hunched up on one side. Three ribs were locked in its teeth, and a voice said to it: Get up. Devour your fill of flesh.

[6] After that, I watched and saw another—like a leopard, but with four wings sprouting from its back like a bird's, and four heads. Authority was handed to it.

[7] Then in the night visions I saw a fourth beast—terrifying, overwhelming, ferocious beyond words. It had massive iron teeth. It crushed and devoured and trampled whatever was left under its feet. It was unlike any of the beasts before it, and it had ten horns.

[8] I was studying the horns when another horn—small, just a sprout at first—pushed up among them, and three of the original horns were torn out by the roots to make room for it. This horn had eyes like a man's eyes, and a mouth speaking outrageous things.

[9] I kept watching until thrones were set in place, and the Ancient of Days took his seat. His robe was white as fallen snow, the hair of his head like pure wool. His throne was flame, its wheels burning fire.

[10] A river of fire flowed out from before him. Thousands upon thousands waited on him; ten thousand times ten thousand stood ready before him. The court convened. The books were opened.

[11] I kept watching because of the arrogant words that horn was spouting. I watched until the beast was killed, its carcass destroyed, handed over to the burning fire.

[12] The other beasts had their dominion stripped away, though they were granted a stay of life for an appointed season.

[13] Then in the night visions I saw—coming with the clouds of heaven—one like a son of man. He approached the Ancient of Days and was presented before him.

[14] To him was given dominion, glory, and a kingdom—every people, every nation, every language would serve him. His dominion is forever, never ending. His kingdom will never collapse.

[15] As for me—Daniel—my spirit was wrecked inside me, and the visions in my head terrified me.

[16] So I approached one of those standing there and asked him what all this really meant. He spoke with me and made the interpretation clear.

[17] These four enormous beasts are four kingdoms that will rise out of the earth.

[18] But the holy ones of the Most High will receive the kingdom and will possess it forever—forever and ever.

[19] Then I wanted to know the truth about the fourth beast—the one different from all the others, the terrifying one with iron teeth and bronze claws, that crushed and devoured and trampled what was left under its feet—

[20] and about the ten horns on its head, and the other horn that pushed up and toppled three of them—that horn with eyes and a mouth talking big, the one that looked larger than its companions.

[21] I had been watching as that horn made war against the holy ones and was winning—

[22] until the Ancient of Days came and judgment was handed down in favor of the holy ones of the Most High, and the time arrived when the holy ones took possession of the kingdom.

[23] He said: The fourth beast is a fourth kingdom that will appear on earth—different from all the other kingdoms. It will consume the whole earth, trampling it down and crushing it to pieces.

[24] The ten horns are ten kings who will rise from that kingdom. After them another will arise, different from the ones before, and he will bring down three kings.

[25] He will speak against the Most High and grind down his holy ones. He will plot to alter sacred seasons and sacred law. The holy ones will be handed into his grip for a time, times, and half a time.

[26] But then the court will convene. His dominion will be stripped away—annihilated, destroyed to the end.

[27] And the kingdom—the dominion, the greatness of every kingdom under all of heaven—will be handed to the people of the holy ones of the Most High. Their kingdom is everlasting. Every dominion will serve them and obey them.

[28] Here the matter ends. As for me, Daniel—my thoughts shook me hard, the color drained from my face. But I kept the matter locked in my heart.

[1] In the third year of King Belshazzar's reign, a vision came to me, Daniel — another one, after the first.

[2] In the vision I saw myself in Susa, the fortress city, in the province of Elam. I was standing by the Ulai canal.

[3] I looked up and saw a ram standing beside the canal. Two horns. Both tall, but one was taller than the other — and the taller one had grown up last.

[4] The ram was charging — west, north, south. No beast could hold its ground against him, and nothing could break free from his power. He did as he pleased. He grew.

[5] I was still trying to take it in when a male goat came hurtling out of the west, skimming the surface of the whole earth so fast his feet never touched ground. Between his eyes — a single, conspicuous horn.

[6] He drove straight at the two-horned ram I had seen by the canal, charging him with murderous fury.

[7] I watched him close on the ram, possessed with rage, and slam into him — shattering both horns. The ram had no strength to stand. The goat threw him to the ground and trampled him, and there was no one to pull the ram from his grip.

[8] The goat swelled with power. But at the height of his strength, the great horn snapped off — and in its place four prominent horns pushed up, pointing toward the four winds of heaven.

[9] Out of one of them a small horn emerged, and it grew — monstrously — toward the south, toward the east, toward the Beautiful Land.

[10] It grew until it reached the host of heaven itself. It threw some of that host, some of the stars, down to the earth and ground them under its heel.

[11] It exalted itself even against the Prince of the host. It stripped him of the daily sacrifice and tore down the place of his sanctuary.

[12] In its rebellion, an army was handed over to it along with the daily offering. It hurled truth to the ground — and everything it did, it succeeded at.

[13] Then I heard a holy one speaking, and another holy one asked the first, "How long does the vision run — the daily sacrifice, the appalling rebellion, the sanctuary and the host trampled underfoot?"

[14] And he said to me, "For two thousand three hundred evenings and mornings. Then the sanctuary will be set right."

[15] As I, Daniel, was watching the vision and straining to understand it, suddenly there stood before me what looked like a man.

[16] And I heard a human voice from over the Ulai calling out, "Gabriel — make this man understand what he has seen."

[17] He came toward where I was standing, and as he approached I was overcome with terror and fell facedown. He said to me, "Understand this, son of man: the vision concerns the time of the end."

[18] While he was speaking, I sank into a dead sleep, my face against the ground. He touched me, lifted me, set me on my feet.

[19] And he said, "I am going to show you what comes in the final stretch of the wrath — because this concerns the appointed end.

[20] The ram you saw, the one with two horns — that is the kings of Media and Persia.

[21] The shaggy goat is the king of Greece, and the great horn between his eyes is the first king.

[22] The horn that snapped and the four that rose in its place — four kingdoms will rise from his nation, but none with his power.

[23] In the last stage of their reign, when the rebels have run their course, a king will arise — hard-faced, a master of intrigue.

[24] His strength will be terrifying, though not his own. He will devastate beyond belief and do whatever he sets out to do. He will destroy the mighty and the holy people.

[25] Through his cunning, deceit will thrive in his hand. In his own mind he will tower over everyone. He will catch men off guard and destroy them. He will rise up even against the Prince of princes — and be shattered, but not by any human hand.

[26] The vision of evenings and mornings that has been spoken — it is true. Seal it up. It is for days far off."

[27] And I, Daniel, was wrecked. I was sick for days. Then I got up and went back to the king's business — but the vision haunted me, and no one could make sense of it.

[1] In the first year of Darius son of Ahasuerus, a Mede by descent, who was installed as king over the realm of the Chaldeans —

[2] in the first year of his reign, I, Daniel, was poring over the books, weighing the number of years the word of the LORD had given to Jeremiah the prophet: seventy years before the desolation of Jerusalem would be complete.

[3] So I turned my face toward the Lord God, seeking him in prayer and pleading, with fasting, sackcloth, and ashes.

[4] I prayed to the LORD my God and confessed:

"Lord — great God, God who terrifies — you keep covenant and steadfast love with those who love you and guard your commandments.

[5] We have sinned. We have twisted what was straight. We have done evil and rebelled. We have turned away from your commandments and your judgments.

[6] We refused to listen to your servants the prophets, who spoke in your name to our kings, our princes, our ancestors, and to everyone in the land.

[7] To you, Lord, belongs righteousness — and to us, the shame written on our faces this very day: the men of Judah, the citizens of Jerusalem, all Israel, near and far, in every country where you scattered them because they betrayed you.

[8] To us, LORD — shame on our faces: on our kings, our princes, our ancestors. We sinned against you.

[9] To the Lord our God belong mercy and pardon, even though we rebelled against him —

[10] even though we did not obey the voice of the LORD our God, did not walk in the laws he set before us through his servants the prophets.

[11] All Israel broke your instruction. They turned aside, refusing to hear your voice. So the curse poured out on us — the sworn oath written in the law of Moses, servant of God — because we sinned against him.

[12] He made good on his words, the warnings he leveled at us and at the rulers who governed us: he brought down on us a catastrophe so vast that nothing under heaven has ever matched what happened to Jerusalem.

[13] Exactly as written in the law of Moses — every disaster came on us. And still we did not soften the face of the LORD our God: we did not turn from our sins, did not wake up to your truth.

[14] So the LORD kept watch over the disaster and brought it down on us. The LORD our God is just in everything he has done — but we would not listen to his voice.

[15] And now, Lord our God — you who pulled your people out of Egypt with a strong hand and made a name for yourself that still stands today — we have sinned. We have done wrong.

[16] Lord, by all your righteous acts, let your anger and your wrath turn away from Jerusalem, your city, your holy mountain. Because of our sins and the sins of our fathers, Jerusalem and your people have become a byword to everyone around us.

[17] So now, our God, listen to the prayer of your servant, to his pleading. For your own sake, Lord, let your face shine on your sanctuary — the one lying in ruins.

[18] My God, lean in and hear. Open your eyes and see the wreckage — and the city that bears your name. We are not laying out our pleas before you because we are righteous. We are laying them out because your mercy runs deep.

[19] Lord, hear. Lord, forgive. Lord, pay attention and act. Don't delay — for your own sake, my God, because your name is on this city and on this people."

[20] I was still speaking — still praying, still confessing my sin and the sin of my people Israel, still laying my plea before the LORD my God for his holy mountain —

[21] still mid-prayer, when Gabriel, the figure I had seen in the earlier vision, came to me in swift flight, arriving at the hour of the evening offering.

[22] He spoke to me, instructing me: "Daniel — I have come now to give you understanding, to make you see.

[23] The moment you began to pray, a word went out, and I came to deliver it, because you are deeply loved. So consider the word. Understand the vision.

[24] Seventy weeks have been decreed for your people and your holy city — to shut down the rebellion, to seal off sin, to cover guilt, to bring in everlasting righteousness, to set the seal on vision and prophet, and to anoint the Most Holy Place.

[25] Know this and grasp it: From the moment the word goes out to restore and rebuild Jerusalem until an anointed one, a ruler, arrives — seven weeks. Then sixty-two weeks more, during which the city will be rebuilt — streets and trench — but in a time of pressure.

[26] After the sixty-two weeks, the anointed one will be cut down — left with nothing. And the people of a coming ruler will destroy the city and the sanctuary. The end will come like a flood. Until the very end: war, and devastations that have been decreed.

[27] He will forge a covenant with the many for one week. Halfway through the week, he will shut down sacrifice and offering. On the wing of the temple — an abomination that lays waste — until the full measure decreed is poured out on the desolator."

[1] In the third year of Cyrus, king of Persia, a word came to Daniel — the one renamed Belteshazzar. The word was true, and it concerned a great war. He grasped the word and understood the vision.

[2] In those days I, Daniel, was mourning. Three full weeks.

[3] No rich food crossed my lips. No meat, no wine. I did not anoint myself with oil — not once — until the three weeks were done.

[4] On the twenty-fourth day of the first month, I was standing on the bank of the great river, the Tigris.

[5] I looked up — and there he was. A man dressed in linen, his waist belted with gold from Uphaz.

[6] His body was like topaz. His face flashed like lightning. His eyes were torches of fire. His arms and feet gleamed like burnished bronze. And his voice — when he spoke, it was the roar of a crowd.

[7] I alone saw it. The men with me saw nothing — but terror fell on them anyway, and they ran and hid.

[8] So I was left alone, staring at this enormous vision. My strength drained out of me. My face went corpse-pale. I had nothing left.

[9] Then I heard him speak — and at the sound of his voice I collapsed, face to the ground, unconscious.

[10] A hand touched me. It lifted me — trembling — onto my hands and knees.

[11] He said, "Daniel, treasured one, listen carefully to what I am about to tell you. Stand up. I have been sent to you." When he said it, I stood — still shaking.

[12] Then he said: "Don't be afraid, Daniel. From the first day you set your heart to understand and to humble yourself before your God, your words were heard. I have come because of your words.

[13] But the prince of the kingdom of Persia opposed me for twenty-one days. Then Michael, one of the chief princes, came to help me — I had been left there alone with the kings of Persia.

[14] Now I have come to make you understand what will happen to your people in the latter days. The vision is for days still to come."

[15] While he was speaking these things to me, I bowed my face to the ground, mute.

[16] Then one who looked like a son of man touched my lips. I opened my mouth and spoke. I said to the one standing in front of me, "My lord — at this vision, anguish has twisted me. I have nothing left.

[17] How can a servant like me speak with a lord like you? My strength is gone. I can barely breathe."

[18] Again the one who looked human touched me. Strength flowed back.

[19] He said, "Don't be afraid, treasured one. Peace. Be strong — be strong." As he spoke, strength rose in me. I said, "Speak, my lord. You have given me strength."

[20] He said: "Do you know why I came to you? Now I must return to fight the prince of Persia. When I leave, the prince of Greece will come.

[21] But first I will tell you what is written in the Book of Truth. No one stands with me against these powers except Michael, your prince."

[1] As for me, in the first year of Darius the Mede, I stood up to back him, to be his strength.

[2] Now I will tell you the truth. Three more kings will rise in Persia, and then a fourth, far richer than all of them. When his wealth has made him strong, he will rouse everyone against the kingdom of Greece.

[3] Then a warrior-king will appear. He will rule a vast dominion and do whatever he wants.

[4] But the moment he stands at his peak, his kingdom will shatter — split to the four winds of heaven. It will not pass to his descendants, and it will not have the power he once wielded. His empire will be uprooted and handed to others.

[5] The king of the south will grow strong. But one of his commanders will grow stronger still and rule a dominion greater than his.

[6] After some years they will make an alliance. The daughter of the southern king will come north to seal the deal — but she will not hold her power, and neither will he hold his. She will be handed over, along with her escorts, her father, and the one who supported her. All of them, in those days.

[7] But a shoot from her roots will rise in his place. He will march against the army of the north, breach their fortress, fight, and win.

[8] He will carry off their gods, their cast images, their precious vessels of silver and gold, back to Egypt as plunder. Then for years he will leave the king of the north alone.

[9] The northern king will invade the south but will retreat to his own land.

[10] His sons will gear up for war and assemble a massive force. One of them will sweep through like a flood, pressing all the way to the enemy's fortress.

[11] Enraged, the southern king will march out and engage the northern king. The north will field an enormous army — and that army will be handed into the south's grip.

[12] When the horde is swept away, his heart will swell with pride. He will cut down tens of thousands — but he will not prevail.

[13] The northern king will return, raising an army larger than the first. After years pass, he will come on hard with a massive force and full supplies.

[14] In those days many will rise against the southern king. Even violent men among your own people will join in, thinking they are fulfilling prophecy — but they will fall.

[15] The king of the north will come, throw up siege ramps, and capture a fortified city. The south's forces will not hold. Even their best troops will have no strength to stand.

[16] The invader will do as he pleases, and no one will stand against him. He will plant himself in the Beautiful Land, with destruction in his hand.

[17] He will set his face to come with the full strength of his kingdom, bringing terms with him. He will give him a daughter in marriage to ruin the kingdom — but it will not succeed; it will not work out for him.

[18] Then he will turn toward the coastlands and seize many of them. But a commander will put an end to his arrogance and turn his contempt back on him.

[19] He will retreat to the fortresses of his own land — and he will stumble and fall, and be no more.

[20] His successor will send out a tax collector to maintain the kingdom's splendor. But within a few days he will be broken — not in battle, not in anger.

[21] In his place will rise a contemptible man, one who was never offered the honor of kingship. He will slip in quietly, in a time of ease, and take the throne by flattery and intrigue.

[22] Armies will be swept away before him like a flood and shattered — including a prince of the covenant.

[23] After making a treaty, he will operate by deceit. With only a small force he will rise to power.

[24] In a time of peace he will invade the richest provinces and do what none of his fathers or forefathers ever did — scattering plunder, spoil, and wealth among his followers. He will scheme against fortresses — but only for a time.

[25] He will summon his strength and courage against the king of the south with a massive army. The southern king will go to war with a huge and mighty force of his own, but he will not stand, because schemes will be plotted against him.

[26] Those who eat from his table will be his undoing. His army will be swept away, and many will fall slain.

[27] Both kings, hearts set on harm, will sit at the same table telling each other lies. But it will get them nowhere, because the end is still waiting for its appointed hour.

[28] He will head home with massive plunder, his heart set against the holy covenant. He will act on it, then return to his own land.

[29] At the appointed time he will invade the south again — but this time it will not go like before.

[30] Ships from the western coasts will come against him, and he will lose his nerve and turn back. He will vent his fury against the holy covenant. As he retreats, he will favor those who abandon the holy covenant.

[31] His forces will rise up and desecrate the temple-fortress. They will abolish the daily sacrifice and set up the abomination that brings desolation.

[32] Those who betray the covenant he will corrupt with smooth words. But the people who know their God will stand firm and act.

[33] The wise among the people will teach many, though for a time they will fall — by sword, by flame, by captivity, by plunder.

[34] When they fall, they will get a little help — and many will attach themselves to them out of mixed motives.

[35] Some of the wise will fall, so they may be refined, purged, and made white until the time of the end — for the appointed time is still coming.

[36] The king will do as he pleases. He will exalt himself, magnify himself above every god, and speak unimaginable things against the God of gods. He will succeed — until the wrath has run its course, because what is decreed must be done.

[37] He will pay no regard to the gods of his fathers, nor to the one women long for, nor to any god at all. He will magnify himself above them all.

[38] Instead, he will honor a god of fortresses — a god his fathers never knew — lavishing on him gold, silver, gems, and treasures.

[39] He will deal with the strongest fortresses with the help of a foreign god. Those who acknowledge him he will load with honor, set as rulers over many, and parcel out land as a reward.

[40] At the time of the end the king of the south will collide with him. The king of the north will storm in with chariots, horsemen, and a fleet of ships. He will invade nations and sweep through like a flood.

[41] He will enter the Beautiful Land, and many will fall. But these will escape his hand: Edom, Moab, and the leading sons of Ammon.

[42] He will reach out his hand against country after country, and Egypt will not escape.

[43] He will seize the gold and silver and all the treasures of Egypt. Libyans and Cushites will follow at his heels.

[44] But reports from the east and the north will alarm him, and he will set out in a great fury to annihilate and destroy many.

[45] He will pitch his royal tents between the seas, against the glorious holy mountain. Then he will come to his end, with no one to help him.

[1] When that moment comes, Michael will rise — the great prince who stands watch over your people. And there will be anguish unlike anything the world has ever seen, from the day nations first existed until that hour. But your people will be rescued — every name written in the book.

[2] Multitudes who sleep in the dust of the earth will wake — some to life that never ends, others to shame and contempt that never lifts.

[3] Then those who saw clearly will blaze like the vault of heaven, and those who turned the many toward righteousness will shine like stars, on and on, forever.

[4] But you, Daniel — seal the words. Lock the book until the end. Many will race back and forth, and knowledge will multiply.

[5] Then I, Daniel, looked — and there were two others standing there, one on this bank of the river, one on the far side.

[6] One of them said to the man clothed in linen who hovered above the water: "How long until these wonders run their course?"

[7] And I heard the man in linen — standing over the river — raise both hands to heaven and swear by the One who lives forever: "A time, two times, and half a time. When the shattering of the holy people's power is complete, all of this will be finished."

[8] I heard, but I did not understand. So I asked: "My lord — what will the end of all this look like?"

[9] He answered: "Go your way, Daniel. The words are sealed shut until the time of the end.

[10] Many will be refined, purified, made white. The wicked will keep doing wickedness — and not one of the wicked will understand. But those with wisdom will see.

[11] From the moment the daily offering is taken away and the desolating horror is set up: 1,290 days.

[12] Blessed is the one who waits — who reaches 1,335 days.

[13] As for you — walk on to the end. You will rest. And when the days have run their course, you will rise to your inheritance."

[1] These are the words Moses spoke to all Israel on the far side of the Jordan—out in the wilderness, in the rift valley across from Suph, between Paran and Tophel, Laban, Hazeroth, and Di-zahab. [2] It is an eleven-day march from Horeb by way of Mount Seir to Kadesh-barnea. [3] But it was in the fortieth year, on the first day of the eleventh month, that Moses delivered to the Israelites everything the LORD had charged him to say. [4] This was after he had crushed Sihon, the Amorite king who ruled from Heshbon, and Og, king of Bashan, who ruled from Ashtaroth and Edrei. [5] There, east of the Jordan in Moab's territory, Moses set himself to explain this instruction. He said:

[6] The LORD our God spoke to us at Horeb: You have camped at this mountain long enough. [7] Break camp. Move out. Head into the hill country of the Amorites and to all their neighbors—into the Arabah, the highlands, the lowlands, the Negev, the seacoast—the whole land of the Canaanites, and on to Lebanon, all the way to the great river, the Euphrates. [8] Look—I have laid the land out before you. Go in. Take it. This is the land the LORD swore to give your ancestors—Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—and to their descendants after them.

[9] At that time I said to you: I cannot carry you alone. The weight is too much for one man. [10] The LORD your God has multiplied you—today you are as countless as the stars. [11] May the LORD, the God of your ancestors, multiply you a thousand times more and bless you, just as he promised. [12] But how can I shoulder, single-handedly, your troubles, your burdens, your endless quarrels? [13] Choose for yourselves—from each tribe—men who are wise, discerning, and respected. I will appoint them as your leaders. [14] And you answered me: What you propose is good. [15] So I took the heads of your tribes—wise men, men of standing—and made them leaders over you: commanders of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens, and officials throughout your tribes.

[16] At the same time I charged your judges: Hear the cases between your people. Judge fairly—whether the dispute is between fellow Israelites or between an Israelite and a foreigner among you. [17] Show no favoritism in court. Listen to the small and the great alike. Fear no one's face—judgment belongs to God. If a case is too hard for you, bring it to me, and I will hear it. [18] And I gave you, at that time, every command you needed for what was coming.

[19] Then we broke camp at Horeb and crossed that vast and terrifying wilderness you saw—the road to the Amorite hill country, exactly as the LORD our God had ordered. We arrived at Kadesh-barnea. [20] I said to you: You have reached the hill country of the Amorites, which the LORD our God is giving us. [21] Look—the LORD your God has placed the land before you. Go up. Take possession, as the LORD, the God of your ancestors, told you. Do not be afraid. Do not flinch.

[22] Then all of you came to me and said: Let us send men ahead of us to scout the land and bring back a report—what route to take, what cities we will face. [23] The idea seemed sound to me, so I picked twelve men, one from each tribe. [24] They set out, climbed into the hill country, and reached the Valley of Eshcol, where they reconnoitered. [25] They took some of the land's fruit in their hands, carried it down to us, and gave their report: It is a good land—the land the LORD our God is giving us.

[26] But you refused to go up. You rebelled against the order of the LORD your God. [27] You sulked in your tents and muttered: The LORD hates us. That is why he dragged us out of Egypt—to hand us over to the Amorites and wipe us out. [28] Where are we even going? Our own brothers have melted our hearts. They told us: The people there are bigger and taller than we are. The cities are huge, walls climbing into the sky. We even saw the Anakim there.

[29] So I told you: Do not be terrified. Do not be afraid of them. [30] The LORD your God, who goes ahead of you—he himself will fight for you, just like everything he did for you in Egypt, right before your eyes, [31] and in the wilderness, where you saw how the LORD your God carried you, the way a father carries his son, the whole road you walked until you arrived at this place.

[32] And still, for all that—you did not trust the LORD your God, [33] the one who went ahead of you on the road to scout out your campsites, who appeared in fire by night and cloud by day to show you the way to go.

[34] When the LORD heard the sound of your words, he was furious. He swore: [35] Not one man of this evil generation will see the good land I swore to give your ancestors— [36] except Caleb son of Jephunneh. He will see it. To him and to his sons I will give the land he walked on, because he followed the LORD with his whole heart.

[37] On account of you, the LORD's anger fell on me too. He said: Not even you will enter it. [38] Joshua son of Nun, who stands before you—he will enter it. Strengthen him. He is the one who will settle Israel into its inheritance.

[39] And your little ones—the ones you said would become plunder, your children who today do not yet know good from bad—they will enter the land. To them I will give it, and they will take possession of it. [40] As for you—turn around. Head back into the wilderness, by the road to the Red Sea.

[41] Then you answered me: We have sinned against the LORD. We will go up. We will fight, just as the LORD our God commanded. And each of you strapped on his weapons, thinking it was a small thing to charge up into the hill country.

[42] But the LORD said to me: Tell them—Do not go up. Do not fight. I am not with you. You will be cut down before your enemies. [43] I told you, but you would not listen. You defied the LORD's command. In your arrogance you charged up into the hill country. [44] And the Amorites who lived there came out against you, swarming like bees, and they routed you from Seir all the way to Hormah.

[45] You came back and wept before the LORD, but the LORD would not listen to your voice. He would not hear you. [46] So you stayed at Kadesh—a long time. As long as you stayed.

[1] Then we turned and headed back into the wilderness, taking the road toward the Red Sea, just as the LORD had told me. For a long stretch of days we circled the hill country of Seir.

[2] Finally the LORD spoke to me:

[3] "You've circled this mountain long enough. Turn north.

[4] And give the people this order: You're about to pass through the territory of your relatives, the descendants of Esau, who live in Seir. They'll be afraid of you — but watch yourselves.

[5] Don't pick a fight with them. I won't give you so much as a footprint of their land. I handed Mount Seir to Esau as his possession.

[6] Pay them for your food in silver. Pay them for your water in silver.

[7] The LORD your God has blessed everything your hands have done. He's watched over your march through this immense wilderness. Forty years now the LORD your God has been with you, and you've lacked nothing."

[8] So we moved past our kinsmen, the sons of Esau in Seir, away from the Arabah road that runs up from Elath and Ezion-geber. We turned and took the wilderness road toward Moab.

[9] Then the LORD said to me: "Don't harass Moab. Don't provoke them to war. I won't hand you any of their land. I gave Ar to the descendants of Lot as their possession."

[10] (The Emim used to live there — a people massive, numerous, towering like the Anakim.

[11] They were counted as Rephaim, like the Anakim, though the Moabites called them Emim.

[12] As for Seir — the Horites lived there first, until Esau's descendants drove them out, wiped them out, and settled in their place, just as Israel would later do with the land the LORD gave them as their inheritance.)

[13] "Now get up. Cross the Wadi Zered." So we crossed the Wadi Zered.

[14] From the day we left Kadesh-barnea to the day we crossed the Wadi Zered, thirty-eight years had passed. An entire generation of fighting men had died off from the camp, exactly as the LORD had sworn to them.

[15] The hand of the LORD was against them, picking them off from inside the camp until every last one was gone.

[16] Once the last of the fighting men had died,

[17] the LORD spoke to me again:

[18] "Today you cross into Moabite territory at Ar.

[19] When you come near the Ammonites, don't provoke them, don't fight them. I won't give you any of the Ammonites' land. I gave it to the descendants of Lot as their possession."

[20] (That land also counted as Rephaim country — Rephaim used to live there, though the Ammonites called them Zamzummim.

[21] They were a great people, numerous, towering like the Anakim. But the LORD destroyed them before the Ammonites, who drove them out and settled in their place.

[22] He did the same for Esau's descendants in Seir, wiping out the Horites before them, so they took over and live there to this day.

[23] As for the Avvim, who lived in villages all the way to Gaza — the Caphtorim, coming out of Caphtor, destroyed them and settled in their place.)

[24] "Get up. Move out. Cross the Arnon gorge. Look — I've handed you Sihon the Amorite, king of Heshbon, and his land. Start the conquest. Take him on in battle.

[25] This day I begin to plant terror of you and dread of you in every nation under heaven. They'll hear the report of you and they will tremble, they will writhe at the thought of you."

[26] From the wilderness of Kedemoth I sent messengers to Sihon king of Heshbon with words of peace:

[27] "Let me pass through your land. I'll stick to the road. I won't turn off, right or left.

[28] Sell me food for silver so I can eat. Sell me water for silver so I can drink. Just let me cross on foot —

[29] the way Esau's sons in Seir let us, and the Moabites at Ar — until I cross the Jordan into the land the LORD our God is giving us."

[30] But Sihon king of Heshbon refused to let us pass through. The LORD your God had hardened his spirit and stiffened his heart, in order to give him into your hand — which is exactly what happened.

[31] The LORD said to me, "Look — I have begun. I've handed Sihon and his land to you. Start now. Take possession of his country."

[32] So Sihon marched out against us — he and all his people — to fight at Jahaz.

[33] And the LORD our God handed him over. We struck him down, along with his sons and all his people.

[34] We captured every one of his cities at that time. We put every city under the ban — men, women, children. We left no survivor.

[35] Only the livestock we kept as plunder for ourselves, along with the spoil from the cities we took.

[36] From Aroer on the rim of the Arnon gorge, and the city in the valley itself, all the way to Gilead — not a single town stood too strong for us. The LORD our God handed them all over.

[37] But the land of the Ammonites you did not approach — none of the banks of the Jabbok, none of the cities of the hill country — wherever the LORD our God had forbidden.

[1] Then we pivoted north, up the road to Bashan. Og, king of Bashan, marched out to meet us — him and every soldier he had — for battle at Edrei.

[2] The LORD said to me: "Don't be afraid of him. I've already handed him over to you — him, his whole army, his land. Do to him what you did to Sihon, the Amorite king who ruled in Heshbon."

[3] So the LORD our God handed Og of Bashan over to us too — him and all his troops. We struck them down. Not one survivor left.

[4] We captured every city he had — all sixty of them, the whole region of Argob, Og's kingdom in Bashan. Not one city escaped us.

[5] All of them fortified — high walls, gates, bars — plus a great many unwalled towns on top of that.

[6] We put them under the ban, the same as we did to Sihon king of Heshbon — every city wiped out: men, women, children.

[7] But the livestock and the plunder from the cities we took as our own.

[8] So we took the land — at that moment — from the hand of those two Amorite kings on the east side of the Jordan, from the Arnon Gorge all the way to Mount Hermon

[9] (Hermon — the Sidonians call it Sirion; the Amorites call it Senir),

[10] every city on the plateau, all of Gilead, all of Bashan as far as Salecah and Edrei — cities of Og's kingdom in Bashan.

[11] (Og of Bashan was the last of the Rephaim. His bed — made of iron — is still on display in Rabbah of the Ammonites. Thirteen feet long, six feet wide, by the standard cubit.)

[12] So we took possession of that land. From Aroer on the Arnon Gorge, plus half the hill country of Gilead and its cities, I gave to the tribes of Reuben and Gad.

[13] The rest of Gilead and all of Bashan — Og's kingdom — I gave to the half-tribe of Manasseh. The whole Argob region, all of Bashan, was known as the land of the Rephaim.

[14] Jair, a descendant of Manasseh, took the entire Argob region as far as the border of the Geshurites and Maacathites. He named the Bashan settlements after himself — Havvoth-jair — and they go by that name to this day.

[15] To Machir I gave Gilead.

[16] And to the Reubenites and Gadites I gave from Gilead down to the Arnon Gorge — the middle of the gorge being the border — and on up to the Jabbok River, the Ammonite frontier.

[17] Plus the Arabah, with the Jordan as its boundary, from Chinnereth down to the Sea of the Arabah — the Salt Sea — under the slopes of Pisgah on the east.

[18] At that time I gave you this order: "The LORD your God has given you this land to possess. Now — every fighting man among you — cross over armed, ahead of your brothers, the Israelites.

[19] Your wives, your children, your livestock (and I know you have a lot of livestock) — they can stay in the towns I've given you,

[20] until the LORD gives your brothers rest the way he's given it to you, and they too take possession of the land the LORD your God is giving them across the Jordan. Then each of you can return to the property I gave you."

[21] And I commanded Joshua at that time: "Your own eyes have seen everything the LORD your God did to these two kings. The LORD will do the same to every kingdom you're about to cross into.

[22] Don't be afraid of them. The LORD your God himself fights for you."

[23] Then I pleaded with the LORD:

[24] "Lord GOD — you've only begun to show your servant your greatness, your mighty hand. What god in heaven or on earth can do what you do, match the power of your works?

[25] Please — let me cross over. Let me see the good land beyond the Jordan, that beautiful hill country, the Lebanon."

[26] But the LORD was furious with me on your account and would not listen. "Enough," he said. "Don't speak to me about this again.

[27] Climb to the top of Pisgah. Look west, north, south, east. See it with your own eyes — because you will not cross this Jordan.

[28] Commission Joshua. Strengthen him. Embolden him. He's the one who will cross at the head of this people. He's the one who will give them the land you see."

[29] So we stayed there in the valley opposite Beth-peor.

[1] So listen, Israel — listen to the statutes and the judgments I'm teaching you. Do them. Live. Walk in and take possession of the land the LORD, the God of your fathers, is handing to you.

[2] Don't add a word to what I'm commanding. Don't subtract one either. Keep the commands of the LORD your God exactly as I'm giving them to you.

[3] Your own eyes saw what the LORD did at Baal-Peor. Every man who chased after that Baal — the LORD wiped him out from among you.

[4] But you who held tight to the LORD your God — every one of you is alive today.

[5] Look — I've taught you statutes and judgments, just as the LORD my God commanded me, so you can live by them in the land you're entering to possess.

[6] Guard them. Live them. This is your wisdom and your understanding in the eyes of the nations. When they hear about all these statutes, they'll say, "This great nation — what a wise and discerning people."

[7] Because what other nation, however great, has gods this close to them — the way the LORD our God is close to us every time we call on him?

[8] And what other nation, however great, has statutes and judgments as just as this whole law I'm setting before you today?

[9] Just watch yourself. Guard your soul with everything in you. Don't forget what your eyes have seen. Don't let it slip from your heart all your days. Teach it to your children. Teach it to your grandchildren.

[10] The day you stood before the LORD your God at Horeb — when the LORD said to me, "Gather the people to me. I'll let them hear my words, so they learn to fear me every day they live on this earth, and so they teach their children" —

[11] You came close. You stood at the foot of the mountain. The mountain was burning with fire that climbed into the very heart of heaven — darkness, cloud, thick blackness.

[12] Then the LORD spoke to you from the middle of the fire. You heard the sound of words but saw no form — just a voice.

[13] He declared his covenant to you and commanded you to keep it — the Ten Words. He wrote them on two tablets of stone.

[14] And at that same time, the LORD commanded me to teach you statutes and judgments to live by in the land you're crossing over to possess.

[15] So guard your souls carefully. You saw no form on the day the LORD spoke to you at Horeb out of the fire.

[16] Don't corrupt yourselves by carving an image — any shape at all: a figure of a male or a female,

[17] a figure of any animal on the earth, any winged bird that flies through the sky,

[18] anything that crawls on the ground, any fish in the waters under the earth.

[19] And when you lift your eyes to the sky and see the sun and the moon and the stars — the whole array of heaven — don't be seduced into bowing to them and serving them. Those the LORD your God assigned to all the other peoples under heaven.

[20] But you — the LORD took you. He pulled you out of the iron furnace, out of Egypt, to be his people, his inheritance — and that's what you are today.

[21] The LORD was furious with me on your account. He swore I would not cross the Jordan, would not enter the good land the LORD your God is giving you as your inheritance.

[22] I'm going to die in this land. I'm not crossing the Jordan. But you — you're crossing. You'll take possession of that good land.

[23] So watch yourselves. Don't forget the covenant the LORD your God cut with you. Don't carve yourselves an image — a likeness of anything the LORD your God has forbidden.

[24] Because the LORD your God is a consuming fire. A jealous God.

[25] When you've had children and grandchildren, when you've grown old in the land — if you corrupt yourselves and carve an image, any form of anything, doing what is evil in the eyes of the LORD your God and provoking him —

[26] I'm calling heaven and earth as witnesses against you today: you will vanish from the land you're crossing the Jordan to possess. Fast. Not many days. You'll be utterly destroyed.

[27] The LORD will scatter you among the peoples. Only a handful of you will be left among the nations the LORD drives you into.

[28] There you'll serve gods — the work of human hands, wood and stone, things that don't see, don't hear, don't eat, don't smell.

[29] But from there you'll seek the LORD your God. And you'll find him — if you seek him with all your heart and all your soul.

[30] When you're in distress, when all these things have caught up with you in the last days, you'll return to the LORD your God and listen to his voice.

[31] Because the LORD your God is a merciful God. He will not abandon you. He will not destroy you. He will not forget the covenant he swore to your fathers.
