[32] Ask now about the former days, before your time — from the day God created humanity on the earth, from one end of the heavens to the other: has anything like this great thing ever happened? Has anything like it ever been heard of?

[33] Has any people heard the voice of God speaking from the middle of fire — the way you have — and lived?

[34] Or has any god ever tried to come and take for himself a nation from the middle of another nation — by trials, by signs, by wonders, by war, by a strong hand and an outstretched arm, by great terrors — the way the LORD your God did for you in Egypt, right before your eyes?

[35] You were shown all this so you would know: the LORD, he is God. There is no other.

[36] From the heavens he let you hear his voice to discipline you. On the earth he showed you his great fire, and you heard his words from the middle of it.

[37] Because he loved your fathers and chose their descendants after them, he himself brought you out of Egypt — with his presence, with his great power —

[38] driving out nations bigger and stronger than you, bringing you in to give you their land as an inheritance, just as it is today.

[39] Know it today. Settle it in your heart: the LORD, he is God in the heavens above and on the earth below. There is no other.

[40] Keep his statutes and his commandments, which I'm commanding you today — so it goes well for you and for your children after you, so you live long on the land the LORD your God is giving you for all time.

[41] Then Moses set aside three cities east of the Jordan, toward the sunrise,

[42] so that a killer could flee there — someone who'd killed his neighbor without meaning to, without prior hatred between them. He could flee to one of these cities and live.

[43] Bezer in the wilderness, on the plateau, for the Reubenites. Ramoth in Gilead for the Gadites. Golan in Bashan for the Manassites.

[44] This is the law Moses set before the sons of Israel.

[45] These are the testimonies, the statutes, the judgments Moses spoke to the sons of Israel when they came out of Egypt,

[46] across the Jordan, in the valley opposite Beth-Peor, in the land of Sihon king of the Amorites who ruled in Heshbon — the one Moses and the sons of Israel struck down when they came out of Egypt.

[47] They took his land, and the land of Og king of Bashan — the two kings of the Amorites east of the Jordan, toward the sunrise,

[48] from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon valley up to Mount Sion (that is, Hermon),

[49] and all the Arabah east of the Jordan, all the way to the Sea of the Arabah under the slopes of Pisgah.

[1] Moses summoned all Israel and said to them: Listen, Israel, to the statutes and the rulings I'm speaking in your ears today. Learn them. Guard them. Live them.

[2] The LORD our God cut a covenant with us at Horeb.

[3] Not with our ancestors did he cut this covenant — but with us, with us, every one of us standing here alive today.

[4] Face to face the LORD spoke with you on the mountain, out of the heart of the fire.

[5] I stood between the LORD and you at that time to relay his word, because you were terrified of the fire and refused to climb the mountain. He said:

[6] I am the LORD your God. I am the one who pulled you out of Egypt, out of the slave house.

[7] You will have no other gods in my presence.

[8] You will not carve yourself an idol — no image of anything in the sky above, on the earth below, or in the waters under the earth.

[9] You will not bow to them. You will not serve them. Because I, the LORD your God, am a jealous God — I track the guilt of fathers down into their children, into the third generation, into the fourth, when they hate me.

[10] But I pour out faithful love to the thousandth generation for those who love me and guard my commands.

[11] You will not lift up the name of the LORD your God for nothing. The LORD will not hold guiltless the one who lifts his name for nothing.

[12] Guard the Sabbath day. Keep it holy — as the LORD your God commanded you.

[13] Six days you work. Six days you do everything you have to do.

[14] But the seventh day is a Sabbath belonging to the LORD your God. On it you do no work — not you, not your son, not your daughter, not your male servant, not your female servant, not your ox, not your donkey, not any of your animals, not the immigrant inside your gates. Your servants rest the way you rest.

[15] Remember: you were a slave in Egypt, and the LORD your God dragged you out of there with a strong hand and an outstretched arm. That is why the LORD your God commands you to keep the Sabbath day.

[16] Honor your father and your mother — as the LORD your God commanded you — so your days will stretch long and life will go well for you on the land the LORD your God is giving you.

[17] You will not murder.

[18] You will not commit adultery.

[19] You will not steal.

[20] You will not testify falsely against your neighbor.

[21] You will not crave your neighbor's wife. You will not set your appetite on your neighbor's house, his field, his male servant, his female servant, his ox, his donkey — anything that belongs to your neighbor.

[22] These are the words the LORD spoke to your whole assembly on the mountain — out of the fire, the cloud, the dense darkness — with a great voice. And he added nothing. Then he wrote them on two stone tablets and handed them to me.

[23] When you heard the voice rolling out of the darkness and saw the mountain burning, all the heads of your tribes and your elders crowded around me.

[24] They said: Look — the LORD our God has shown us his glory and his greatness. We've heard his voice out of the fire. Today we've seen that God can speak to a human being and that human being can still live.

[25] But why should we die now? This massive fire will consume us. If we keep listening to the voice of the LORD our God any longer, we are dead.

[26] What flesh has ever heard the voice of the living God speaking from the heart of fire — as we have — and lived?

[27] You go close. Listen to everything the LORD our God says. Then you tell us what he tells you. We'll listen. We'll do it.

[28] The LORD heard what you said when you spoke to me, and the LORD said to me: I've heard the words this people spoke to you. Everything they said — good.

[29] If only their hearts would stay this way — fearing me, guarding all my commands, every day — so it would go well for them and their children forever.

[30] Go. Tell them: Return to your tents.

[31] But you — stay here with me. I'll give you the whole commandment, the statutes and the rulings you'll teach them, so they can live them out in the land I'm giving them to possess.

[32] So guard this. Do exactly what the LORD your God commanded you. Don't drift right. Don't drift left.

[33] Walk the entire road the LORD your God laid out for you — so you'll live, so things will go well for you, so your days will be long in the land you're about to take.

[1] These are the commands, the statutes, the rulings the LORD your God charged me to teach you, so you'd live them out in the land you're about to cross over and take.

[2] So that you, your children, and your children's children would hold the LORD your God in awe all your days — keeping every statute and command I'm giving you — and your life would stretch long.

[3] Listen, Israel. Guard these. Live these. Then it will go well with you, and you will multiply like wildfire in a land flowing with milk and honey, exactly as the LORD, the God of your fathers, promised.

[4] Listen, Israel: the LORD is our God. The LORD is one.

[5] And you — love the LORD your God with everything you are: every chamber of your heart, every breath in your lungs, every ounce of your strength.

[6] These words I'm hammering into you today — keep them lodged in your heart.

[7] Sharpen them into your children. Speak them when you're sitting at home and when you're walking the road, when you lie down at night and when you rise at dawn.

[8] Tie them to your hand. Fix them between your eyes.

[9] Carve them into the doorposts of your house and onto your gates.

[10] When the LORD your God brings you into the land he swore to your fathers — Abraham, Isaac, Jacob — to give you: cities massive and gleaming that you didn't build,

[11] houses stuffed with good things you didn't fill, wells you didn't dig, vineyards and olive groves you didn't plant — and you eat your fill —

[12] then watch yourself. Don't forget the LORD, the one who hauled you out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.

[13] The LORD your God — him you will fear. Him you will serve. By his name you will swear.

[14] Don't chase after other gods, the gods of the peoples scattered around you.

[15] Because the LORD your God in your midst is a jealous God — and his anger would blaze against you and wipe you off the face of the earth.

[16] Don't put the LORD your God to the test the way you did at Massah.

[17] Keep, keep, keep the commands of the LORD your God — his statutes, his decrees — every one he gave you.

[18] Do what's right and good in the LORD's eyes, so it will go well with you, and you'll go in and take possession of the good land the LORD swore to your fathers,

[19] driving out every enemy before you, just as the LORD said.

[20] One day your son will ask you: "What's the meaning of these decrees, these statutes, these rulings the LORD our God commanded you?"

[21] And you'll tell him: "We were slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt, and the LORD pulled us out of Egypt with a hand of raw power.

[22] Right in front of our eyes, the LORD threw down signs and wonders — staggering, terrible — against Egypt, against Pharaoh, against his whole household.

[23] But us — he led out from there, to bring us in, to hand us the land he swore to our fathers.

[24] So the LORD commanded us to live by all these statutes, to fear the LORD our God, for our own good, all our days — to keep us alive, as we are right now.

[25] And it will count as righteousness for us when we guard and live out every one of these commands before the LORD our God, just as he charged us."

[1] When the LORD your God brings you into the land you're about to enter and possess, and clears out nation after nation before you — the Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites: seven nations larger and stronger than you —

[2] when the LORD your God hands them over and you defeat them, you are to put them under the ban. Total destruction. No treaty with them. No mercy.

[3] Do not intermarry with them. Don't give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons.

[4] Because they will pull your children away from following me, and your children will worship other gods. Then the LORD's anger will blaze against you, and he will wipe you out in a heartbeat.

[5] Here's what you do instead: tear down their altars, smash their sacred pillars, cut down their Asherah poles, burn their idols to ash.

[6] You are a people holy to the LORD your God. Out of every people on the face of the earth, the LORD your God chose you — you — to be his treasured possession.

[7] It wasn't because you were impressive. You weren't. You were the smallest of the lot.

[8] It was because the LORD loved you. Because he was keeping the oath he swore to your fathers. That's why he brought you out with a mighty hand and ransomed you from the slave-house, from the grip of Pharaoh, king of Egypt.

[9] So know this — burn it into your bones: the LORD your God is God. The faithful God. He keeps covenant and steadfast love to a thousand generations of those who love him and keep his commandments.

[10] But to those who hate him — he repays them to their face. He destroys them. He doesn't delay; he settles it with the one who hates him, face to face.

[11] So keep the commandment — the statutes and the rulings I'm giving you today. Do them.

[12] If you listen to these rulings, guard them, do them — then the LORD your God will keep with you the covenant and the steadfast love he swore to your fathers.

[13] He will love you. He will bless you. He will multiply you. He will bless the fruit of your womb and the fruit of your soil — your grain, your new wine, your oil, the calves of your herds, the lambs of your flocks — in the land he swore to your fathers to give you.

[14] You'll be blessed above every people. No man or woman among you will be barren, and the same for your livestock.

[15] The LORD will lift every sickness off you. None of those vicious diseases of Egypt — the ones you remember — will he put on you. He'll lay them on everyone who hates you.

[16] You'll devour every people the LORD your God hands over to you. Don't let your eye go soft on them. Don't serve their gods — that's a snare set for your feet.

[17] You might say in your heart, "These nations are bigger than us. How can we possibly dispossess them?"

[18] Don't be afraid of them. Remember — really remember — what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and all of Egypt:

[19] the massive trials your eyes saw, the signs, the wonders, the mighty hand and the outstretched arm by which the LORD your God brought you out. He will do the same to every people you're afraid of.

[20] And more — the LORD your God will send the hornet against them, until even those who hid from you, who slipped away, are finished off.

[21] Don't shrink before them. The LORD your God is in your midst — a great God, a God to be feared.

[22] The LORD your God will clear out these nations before you little by little. You won't be able to finish them off all at once, or the wild animals will multiply against you.

[23] But the LORD your God will hand them over to you and throw them into utter confusion until they're destroyed.

[24] He'll deliver their kings into your hand, and you'll wipe their names from under heaven. No one will stand against you. You will finish them.

[25] Burn the carved images of their gods. Don't covet the silver or gold on them. Don't take it for yourself, or you'll be ensnared by it — it's detestable to the LORD your God.

[26] And don't bring anything detestable into your house, or you'll become like it — devoted to destruction. Loathe it. Detest it. It's under the ban.

[1] Every command I'm giving you today — live it out. Do it. That's how you stay alive, how you multiply, how you walk into the land the LORD swore to your ancestors and make it yours.

[2] Remember the road. Every mile of those forty years the LORD your God walked you through the wilderness — it was to break you down and find out what was really inside you, whether you'd keep his commands or not.

[3] He humbled you. He let you go hungry. Then he fed you manna — something you'd never seen, something your ancestors had never seen — to teach you this: a person doesn't live on bread alone. A person lives on every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD.

[4] Your clothes didn't wear out on you. Your feet didn't swell. Forty years.

[5] Get this into your bones: the LORD your God disciplines you the way a father disciplines his son.

[6] So keep the commands of the LORD your God. Walk his roads. Hold him in awe.

[7] Because the LORD your God is bringing you into a good land — a land of streams and springs, water surging up from valleys and hills,

[8] a land of wheat and barley, of vines and figs and pomegranates, a land of olive oil and honey,

[9] a land where bread won't be scarce and you'll lack nothing, a land whose stones are iron and whose hills you can mine for copper.

[10] You'll eat. You'll be full. And then — bless the LORD your God for the good land he gave you.

[11] Watch yourself. Don't forget the LORD your God by failing to keep his commands, his rulings, his statutes that I'm giving you today.

[12] When you've eaten and you're full, when you've built fine houses and settled in,

[13] when your herds and flocks have multiplied, when your silver and gold pile up, when everything you own keeps increasing —

[14] don't let your heart swell up and forget the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the slave house.

[15] He led you through that vast and terrible wilderness — venomous snakes, scorpions, parched ground without a drop of water — and he pulled water out of flint for you.

[16] He fed you manna in the wilderness, something your ancestors had never known, to humble you and test you and do you good in the end.

[17] Don't say in your heart, *My own strength, my own hand built this wealth.*

[18] Remember the LORD your God. He's the one who gives you the power to build wealth — so he can keep the covenant he swore to your ancestors, the one he's still keeping today.

[19] But if you do forget the LORD your God, if you chase other gods and serve them and bow to them — I'm warning you right now, you will be destroyed. Completely.

[20] Like the nations the LORD is destroying ahead of you — that's exactly how you'll go down, because you wouldn't listen to the voice of the LORD your God.

[1] Listen, Israel. Today you cross the Jordan to dispossess nations bigger and stronger than you — cities massive, walls scraping the sky.

[2] A people huge and towering — the Anakim. You know them. You've heard it said: "Who can stand against the sons of Anak?"

[3] Get this through your head today: the LORD your God himself goes ahead of you — a devouring fire. He will crush them. He will bring them down in front of you. You will drive them out and wipe them out fast, exactly as he promised.

[4] But when the LORD has shoved them out of your way, do not whisper to yourself, "My righteousness is what brought me here to take this land." No. It is because these nations are rotten with wickedness that the LORD is driving them out before you.

[5] Not because you are righteous. Not because your heart is upright. It is because of their wickedness — and because the LORD is keeping the oath he swore to your fathers, to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

[6] Understand this: the LORD your God is not giving you this good land because you deserve it. You are a hard-necked people.

[7] Remember. Don't forget. You provoked the LORD your God to fury out in the wilderness. From the day you left Egypt until the day you arrived here, you have been in rebellion against the LORD.

[8] At Horeb you provoked him so badly he was ready to annihilate you.

[9] I had gone up the mountain to receive the stone tablets — the tablets of the covenant the LORD cut with you. Forty days, forty nights on that mountain. No bread. No water.

[10] The LORD handed me two stone tablets, written by the finger of God himself. On them were all the words he had spoken to you from the fire on the mountain, on the day of the assembly.

[11] At the end of those forty days and nights, the LORD gave me the two stone tablets — the tablets of the covenant.

[12] Then he said: "Get up. Get down from here, fast. Your people — the ones you brought out of Egypt — have gone rotten. They've already abandoned the way I commanded them. They've cast themselves an idol."

[13] And the LORD said to me: "I have seen this people. Stiff-necked, every one of them.

[14] Stand aside. Let me destroy them. Let me wipe their name out from under heaven. I will make you into a nation stronger and greater than they are."

[15] So I turned and came down the mountain — the mountain blazing with fire — the two tablets of the covenant in both my hands.

[16] I looked. And there it was: you had sinned against the LORD your God. You had made yourselves a calf of cast metal. You had already swerved off the road the LORD commanded you to walk.

[17] So I seized the two tablets, hurled them down out of my hands, and shattered them in front of your eyes.

[18] Then I threw myself down before the LORD again — forty days, forty nights, no bread, no water — because of all the sin you had committed, doing what was evil in the LORD's sight, provoking him.

[19] I was terrified of the burning rage the LORD held against you — enough to destroy you. But the LORD listened to me that time too.

[20] He was furious enough with Aaron to destroy him as well — so I prayed for Aaron at that same time.

[21] As for the thing you made, that calf — your sin — I took it, burned it with fire, ground it down, crushed it to powder fine as dust, and threw the dust into the stream running down from the mountain.

[22] And at Taberah, and at Massah, and at Kibroth-hattaavah — you kept on provoking the LORD.

[23] When the LORD sent you out from Kadesh-barnea, saying "Go up and take possession of the land I have given you," you defied the mouth of the LORD your God. You did not trust him. You did not listen.

[24] You have been in rebellion against the LORD from the day I first knew you.

[25] So I lay face-down before the LORD for those forty days and forty nights — because the LORD had said he would destroy you.

[26] I prayed to the LORD and said: "My Lord, GOD — do not destroy your people, your own inheritance, the ones you redeemed in your greatness, the ones you brought out of Egypt with a mighty hand.

[27] Remember your servants — Abraham, Isaac, Jacob. Do not stare at the stubbornness of this people, at their wickedness, at their sin.

[28] Otherwise the land you brought us out of will say, 'The LORD couldn't get them into the land he promised them. He hated them. That's why he led them out — to kill them in the wilderness.'

[29] But they are your people. Your own inheritance. The ones you brought out by your tremendous strength and your outstretched arm."

[1] At that point the LORD said to me: Carve two stone tablets like the first ones, and come up the mountain to me. And make a wooden ark to hold them.

[2] I will write on the tablets the same words that were on the first ones — the ones you shattered — and you will place them in the ark.

[3] So I built an ark of acacia wood, carved two stone tablets like the first, and climbed the mountain with them in my hands.

[4] And the LORD wrote on the tablets exactly what he had written before — the ten words he spoke to you from the heart of the fire, on the day of the assembly. Then he gave them to me.

[5] I came back down the mountain and placed the tablets in the ark I had made. And there they stayed, just as the LORD commanded.

[6] (The Israelites traveled from the wells of Bene-jaakan to Moserah. Aaron died there and was buried, and his son Eleazar took up the priesthood after him.

[7] From there they journeyed to Gudgodah, and from Gudgodah to Jotbathah, a land laced with streams.

[8] At that time the LORD set apart the tribe of Levi to carry the ark of the covenant of the LORD, to stand before the LORD and serve him, and to pronounce blessing in his name — a calling that has not ended.

[9] This is why Levi has no inheritance, no portion of land alongside his brothers. The LORD himself is his inheritance, just as the LORD your God promised him.)

[10] As for me, I stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights, as I had the first time. And once more the LORD listened to me. He chose not to destroy you.

[11] Then the LORD said to me: Get up. Go. Lead the people on. Let them enter and take possession of the land I swore to give their ancestors.

[12] So now, Israel — what does the LORD your God actually want from you? Only this: to revere him, to walk in every path he sets before you, to love him, to serve him with everything in you — heart and soul —

[13] to keep his commandments and his statutes, the ones I am giving you today, for your own good.

[14] Look — the heavens belong to the LORD your God. The highest heavens. The earth and everything on it. All of it, his.

[15] And yet — out of all of it — he fixed his heart on your ancestors. He loved them. And he chose their descendants, you, above every other people. And here you stand, today, the proof of it.

[16] So circumcise your hearts. Stop being stiff-necked.

[17] Because the LORD your God is God over gods and Lord over lords — the great God, the mighty, the awe-inspiring, who shows no favoritism and cannot be bought.

[18] He defends the orphan and the widow. He loves the foreigner — gives him food, gives him clothing.

[19] So you, too — love the foreigner. You were foreigners yourselves once, in the land of Egypt. Remember.

[20] The LORD your God — fear him. Serve him. Cling to him. Swear by his name and no other.

[21] He is your song. He is your God. He has done for you these massive, terrifying wonders — you saw them with your own eyes.

[22] Your ancestors went down to Egypt seventy people in all. And now? The LORD your God has multiplied you until you are as many as the stars overhead.

[1] So love the LORD your God. Keep watch over what he has entrusted to you — his rulings, his commands, his instructions — every day of your life.

[2] Know this today — and I am not speaking to your children, who never saw it, never lived it — I am speaking to you, who watched the LORD your God school a generation. You saw his greatness. You saw the hand that does not bend, the arm that does not break.

[3] You saw the signs. You saw what he did in the heart of Egypt — to Pharaoh, to his land, to everything that belonged to him.

[4] You saw what he did to Egypt's army — to the horses, to the chariots — when he hurled the waters of the Red Sea over them as they chased you down. The LORD finished them. They are finished still.

[5] You saw what he did for you in the wilderness, all the way to this place.

[6] You saw what he did to Dathan and Abiram, sons of Eliab son of Reuben — how the ground split its mouth open and swallowed them whole, with their families, their tents, every living thing that belonged to them — right there in front of all Israel.

[7] Your own eyes saw it. Every massive act the LORD performed — you were there.

[8] So keep every command I am giving you today. That is how you will have the strength to cross over and take the land you are about to enter and possess.

[9] That is how you will live long on the soil the LORD swore to give your ancestors and their descendants — land flowing with milk and honey.

[10] The land you are entering is not like Egypt, where you came from — where you planted your seed and watered it by foot, like a vegetable garden.

[11] No — the land you are crossing into is a land of hills and valleys, drinking rain straight from the sky.

[12] It is a land the LORD your God personally tends. His eyes are on it from the first day of the year to the last.

[13] Here is the deal: If you actually listen to the commands I am giving you today — if you love the LORD your God and serve him with everything in you, heart and soul —

[14] then he will send rain on your land in its season, the early rain and the late, and you will gather in your grain, your wine, your oil.

[15] He will put grass in your fields for your livestock, and you will eat and be satisfied.

[16] But watch yourselves. Do not let your heart get seduced. Do not turn aside and serve other gods, do not bow down to them.

[17] Because if you do, the LORD's anger will catch fire against you. He will shut the heavens. No rain. No harvest. And you will vanish fast from the good land he is giving you.

[18] So press these words of mine into your heart and into your soul. Tie them on your hand as a sign. Bind them between your eyes.

[19] Teach them to your children. Talk about them — sitting at home, walking the road, lying down, standing up.

[20] Write them on the doorframes of your house and on your gates.

[21] So that your days, and the days of your children, on the soil the LORD swore to give your ancestors, may be as many as the days the sky stretches over the earth.

[22] Because if you carefully keep every command I am giving you today — to love the LORD your God, to walk in all his ways, to hold tight to him —

[23] then the LORD will drive out every one of these nations ahead of you. You will dispossess nations larger and stronger than you.

[24] Every place the sole of your foot touches will be yours. Your border will run from the wilderness to Lebanon, from the Euphrates to the Mediterranean Sea.

[25] No one will stand against you. The LORD your God will spread dread of you, fear of you, across every land you set foot on — exactly as he promised.

[26] Look — today I am setting before you a blessing and a curse.

[27] The blessing, if you listen to the commands of the LORD your God that I am giving you today.

[28] The curse, if you do not — if you turn off the path I am marking out for you today and chase after other gods you have never known.

[29] When the LORD your God brings you into the land you are about to possess, you will pronounce the blessing on Mount Gerizim and the curse on Mount Ebal.

[30] (You know where they are — across the Jordan, west of the road, in the land of the Canaanites who live in the Arabah opposite Gilgal, near the oaks of Moreh.)

[31] You are about to cross the Jordan and take possession of the land the LORD your God is giving you. When you have taken it and settled there,

[32] make sure you do — every statute, every ruling I am laying before you today.

[1] These are the statutes and rulings you must guard carefully — every day you live on the ground the LORD, the God of your ancestors, is handing over to you.

[2] Demolish — completely — every site where the nations you're dispossessing served their gods: high on the mountains, up on the hills, under every green tree.

[3] Tear down their altars. Smash their sacred pillars. Burn their Asherah poles to ash. Cut down the carved images of their gods and wipe their names off that land.

[4] You will not worship the LORD your God the way they worshipped theirs.

[5] No — you will seek out the place the LORD your God chooses from among all your tribes to plant his name. That is where you go. That is where his presence dwells.

[6] Bring it all there: your burnt offerings, your sacrifices, your tithes, the contributions you lift up, the vows you've made, your freewill gifts, the firstborn of your herds and flocks.

[7] There you'll eat in the presence of the LORD your God. There you'll celebrate — you and your households — over everything your hands have built, every blessing the LORD your God has given you.

[8] You can't keep doing what we've been doing here — everyone improvising, everyone deciding for themselves what's right.

[9] Because you haven't arrived yet. You're not in the resting place, the inheritance the LORD your God is giving you.

[10] But you will cross the Jordan. You will settle in the land he's handing down to you. He'll give you rest from every enemy circling you. You will live secure.

[11] Then — at the place the LORD your God chooses to make his name dwell — that's where you bring everything I'm commanding: burnt offerings, sacrifices, tithes, contributions, every choice vow you've sworn to the LORD.

[12] Celebrate there in the presence of the LORD your God — you, your sons and daughters, your male and female servants, and the Levite living in your towns, because he has no land share, no inheritance among you.

[13] Watch yourself. Do not offer your burnt offerings just anywhere you happen to see.

[14] Only at the place the LORD chooses — in one of your tribes — there you offer your burnt offerings. There you do everything I'm commanding.

[15] That said: you can slaughter and eat meat in any of your towns, as much as you want, according to the blessing the LORD your God gives you. Clean or unclean among you can eat it — the same way you'd eat gazelle or deer.

[16] Only — do not consume the blood. Pour it out on the ground like water.

[17] But you cannot eat in your towns the tithe of your grain, wine, or oil, or the firstborn of your herd and flock, or any vowed offering, freewill gift, or contribution you've lifted up.

[18] No — eat those in the presence of the LORD your God, at the place he chooses, you and your son and daughter and servants and the Levite in your town. Celebrate there before the LORD your God over everything your hands have done.

[19] Watch yourself — never abandon the Levite. Not as long as you live in your land.

[20] When the LORD your God expands your territory — as he promised — and you crave meat and say, "I want to eat meat," go ahead. Eat all the meat you want.

[21] If the place the LORD chooses to put his name is too far away, slaughter from your herd or flock — the animals the LORD has given you, just as I instructed — and eat in your towns, all you want.

[22] Eat it the way you'd eat gazelle or deer. Clean and unclean alike can eat together.

[23] Only — be unshakable on this: do not eat the blood. The blood is the life. You do not eat the life with the meat.

[24] Don't consume it. Pour it out on the ground like water.

[25] Don't eat it — so things go well for you and for your children after you, because you'll be doing what is right in the eyes of the LORD.

[26] But the things you've consecrated and your vow-offerings — pick them up and carry them to the place the LORD chooses.

[27] Offer your burnt offerings there — meat and blood — on the altar of the LORD your God. The blood of your sacrifices is to be poured out against the altar, and you eat the meat.

[28] Keep watch over every word I'm speaking — obey it carefully — so things go well for you and your children after you, forever, because you'll be doing what is good and right in the eyes of the LORD your God.

[29] When the LORD your God cuts down the nations ahead of you — the ones you're about to dispossess — and you take their land and settle into their houses,

[30] watch yourself. Don't get snared into copying them after they've been wiped out in front of you. Don't go investigating their gods, asking, "How did these nations serve their gods? I want to try it too."

[31] Do not worship the LORD your God like that. Every disgusting thing the LORD hates, they did for their gods — they even burned their own sons and daughters in fire to them.

[32] Whatever I command you, do it exactly. Add nothing. Subtract nothing.

[1] If a prophet rises among you, or someone who claims to read the future in dreams, and they offer you a sign or a wonder—

[2] and the sign actually happens, the wonder comes true—and then they say, "Let's follow other gods" (gods you've never known) "and serve them"—

[3] do not listen to a word of it. The LORD your God is testing you. He wants to know whether you love him with everything you are, heart and soul.

[4] Walk after the LORD your God. Fear him. Keep his commands. Listen to his voice. Serve him. Cling to him.

[5] As for that prophet, that dreamer—execute him. He preached rebellion against the LORD your God, the One who pulled you out of Egypt, who ransomed you from the house of slaves. He was trying to drag you off the road the LORD commanded you to walk. Burn the evil out from your midst.

[6] If your own brother—your mother's son—or your son or your daughter or the wife in your arms or your closest friend in the world quietly whispers, "Come, let's go serve other gods" (gods neither you nor your fathers ever knew,

[7] gods of the peoples around you, near or far, from one end of the earth to the other)—

[8] do not give in. Do not even listen. Show no pity. Do not shield them. Do not cover for them.

[9] Kill them. Your hand strikes first, then the hand of all the people.

[10] Stone them to death. They tried to pull you away from the LORD your God, the One who brought you out of Egypt, out of the house of slaves.

[11] All Israel will hear and be afraid. No one will dare do something so wicked among you again.

[12] If you hear a report about one of the cities the LORD your God is giving you to live in—

[13] that scoundrels have risen up from your own ranks and seduced the people of their city, saying, "Let's go serve other gods" (gods you've never known)—

[14] investigate. Search. Interrogate thoroughly. If the report turns out to be true, if this abomination really did happen in your midst,

[15] then put every inhabitant of that city to the sword. Devote it to destruction—everything in it, even the livestock—under the edge of the sword.

[16] Gather all its plunder into the public square and burn the city and everything in it as a whole offering to the LORD your God. It will be a ruin forever, never rebuilt.

[17] Let none of the cursed plunder stick to your hand. Then the LORD will turn from his burning anger and show you mercy. He will have compassion on you and multiply you, as he swore to your fathers—

[18] because you listened to the voice of the LORD your God, kept all his commands that I am giving you today, and did what is right in the eyes of the LORD your God.

[1] You are the children of the LORD your God. Don't gash yourselves or shave the front of your heads for the dead.

[2] You are a people set apart, holy to the LORD your God. Out of every nation on earth, he chose you to be his — his treasured possession.

[3] Don't eat anything detestable.

[4] These are the animals you can eat: ox, sheep, goat,

[5] deer, gazelle, roebuck, wild goat, ibex, antelope, mountain sheep.

[6] Any animal with a split hoof — fully divided — that also chews the cud, you can eat.

[7] But these you cannot eat, even though they chew the cud or have a split hoof: the camel, the hare, the rock badger. They chew the cud but their hooves aren't split — they're unclean to you.

[8] The pig either — its hoof is split but it doesn't chew the cud. Unclean. Don't eat its meat. Don't even touch its carcass.

[9] From everything in the water, this is what you can eat: anything with fins and scales.

[10] Anything without fins and scales — don't eat it. Unclean.

[11] You can eat any clean bird.

[12] But these are off limits: the eagle, the vulture, the black vulture,

[13] the red kite, the falcon, any kind of buzzard,

[14] every kind of raven,

[15] the ostrich, the nighthawk, the seagull, any kind of hawk,

[16] the little owl, the great owl, the white owl,

[17] the desert owl, the carrion vulture, the cormorant,

[18] the stork, every kind of heron, the hoopoe, the bat.

[19] Every swarming, winged insect is unclean to you. Don't eat them.

[20] Anything clean with wings — that, you can eat.

[21] Don't eat anything you find already dead. Give it to the foreigner living in your towns, or sell it to an outsider. But you — you are a holy people, belonging to the LORD your God. Don't boil a young goat in its mother's milk.

[22] Every year, take a tenth of everything your fields produce — set it aside.

[23] Then eat it in the presence of the LORD your God, at the place he chooses to plant his name: the tenth of your grain, your new wine, your fresh oil, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks. Do this so you learn — really learn — to revere the LORD your God, always.

[24] If the journey is too far — if the place the LORD chooses to put his name is so distant you can't carry the tithe there because he's blessed you with so much —

[25] then convert it to silver. Tie the silver up, take it in hand, and head to the place the LORD your God chooses.

[26] Once you're there, spend the silver on whatever you crave: cattle, sheep, wine, strong drink — anything your heart desires. Then feast there, in the presence of the LORD your God, you and your household — celebrate.

[27] And don't forget the Levite in your town. He has no land, no inheritance like you do.

[28] At the end of every third year, bring out all the tithe of that year's harvest and store it inside your gates.

[29] Then the Levite (who has no portion of his own), the foreigner, the orphan, the widow in your town — they'll come and eat and be filled. And the LORD your God will bless you in everything your hands set out to do.

[1] Every seventh year, you cancel debts.

[2] Here is how it works: every creditor releases what he loaned to his neighbor. He does not demand it back from his neighbor or his brother, because the LORD's release has been declared.

[3] You may still collect from a foreigner, but whatever your brother owes you, let it go.

[4] There should be no poor among you at all — because the LORD is going to bless you, lavishly, in the land he is giving you as your inheritance —

[5] but only if you actually listen to the voice of the LORD your God and carefully live out every command I am laying before you today.

[6] When he blesses you the way he promised, you will lend to nations and borrow from none. You will rule over nations and none will rule you.

[7] If a poor brother turns up among you — in any of your towns, in the land the LORD your God is giving you — do not harden your heart. Do not clamp your hand shut against him.

[8] Open your hand wide. Lend him whatever he needs, enough to cover the lack.

[9] Watch yourself — do not let this rotten thought slither into your heart: "The seventh year is close, the year of release," and then look coldly at your needy brother and give him nothing. He will cry out to the LORD against you, and you will be carrying guilt.

[10] Give to him — and give freely, without your heart begrudging it — because for this the LORD your God will bless everything you do, every venture of your hand.

[11] The poor will never disappear from the land. That is exactly why I am commanding you: open your hand to your brother, to the needy, to the poor in your land. Open it wide.

[12] If your fellow Hebrew — man or woman — is sold to you and serves you six years, in the seventh year you set him free.

[13] And when you release him, do not send him away empty-handed.

[14] Load him up. Give generously from your flock, your threshing floor, your winepress. Whatever the LORD has blessed you with — share it.

[15] Remember: you were a slave in Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you. That is why I am commanding this today.

[16] But if he says to you, "I do not want to leave you" — because he loves you and your household and is thriving with you —

[17] then take an awl, press it through his earlobe into the door, and he becomes your servant for life. Do the same for a female servant.

[18] Do not resent setting him free. Six years of service from him was worth twice a hired hand's wages. And the LORD your God will bless you in everything you do.

[19] Every firstborn male from your herd and flock — set it apart for the LORD your God. Do not put the firstborn of your ox to work. Do not shear the firstborn of your sheep.

[20] Year after year, you and your household eat it in the presence of the LORD your God, at the place he chooses.

[21] But if there is anything wrong with it — lame, blind, any serious defect — do not sacrifice it to the LORD your God.

[22] Eat it at home. Clean and unclean alike can eat it, like gazelle or deer.

[23] Only — do not consume the blood. Pour it out on the ground like water.

[1] Mark the month of Abib. In that month, keep Passover for the LORD your God — because it was in Abib, under cover of night, that he brought you out of Egypt.

[2] Slaughter the Passover offering to the LORD your God — sheep or cattle — at the place he chooses to plant his name.

[3] Eat no leavened bread with it. For seven days you eat unleavened bread alongside it — the bread of affliction — because you left Egypt in a rush. Eat it so that every day of your life you remember the day you walked out of Egypt.

[4] For those seven days, no yeast is to be seen anywhere in your territory. And none of the meat you sacrifice on the evening of the first day is to be left over until morning.

[5] You don't get to slaughter the Passover wherever you please — not in any of the towns the LORD your God gives you.

[6] Only at the place he chooses to plant his name. There. In the evening. As the sun goes down. The hour you came out of Egypt.

[7] Roast it and eat it at the place the LORD your God chooses. In the morning, turn around and head home to your tents.

[8] Six days you eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day there's a solemn assembly for the LORD your God. No work that day.

[9] Count off seven weeks. Start counting from the moment the sickle first touches the standing grain.

[10] Then keep the Feast of Weeks for the LORD your God — bringing a freewill offering from your hand, measured by the blessing the LORD your God has poured out on you.

[11] And rejoice before the LORD your God — you, your son and daughter, your male and female servants, the Levite living in your town, the foreigner, the orphan, the widow among you — at the place the LORD your God chooses to plant his name.

[12] Remember: you were a slave in Egypt. Keep these statutes. Live them out.

[13] Keep the Feast of Booths for seven days, once you've gathered in everything from your threshing floor and your winepress.

[14] Rejoice at the feast — you, your son and daughter, your male and female servants, the Levite, the foreigner, the orphan, the widow living in your towns.

[15] Seven days of feast for the LORD your God at the place he chooses. Because the LORD your God will bless every harvest and everything your hands do — and your joy will be complete.

[16] Three times a year every male is to appear before the LORD your God at the place he chooses: Feast of Unleavened Bread, Feast of Weeks, Feast of Booths. And no one shows up empty-handed.

[17] Each person brings what he can — measured by the blessing the LORD your God has given him.

[18] Appoint judges and officials in every town the LORD your God gives you, tribe by tribe. Let them govern the people with real justice.

[19] Don't bend the law. Don't play favorites. Don't take a bribe — a bribe blinds the wise and twists the words of the innocent.

[20] Justice. Justice. Chase it down — so that you live, and so that you possess the land the LORD your God is giving you.

[21] Don't plant any sacred pole — any Asherah of any kind of wood — next to the altar you build for the LORD your God.

[22] And don't set up a sacred pillar. The LORD your God hates them.

[1] Never sacrifice to the Lord your God an ox or a sheep with a defect — any flaw at all. He finds that repulsive.

[2] If someone among you — in any of the towns the Lord your God is giving you — a man or a woman — does what the Lord calls evil, breaking his covenant,

[3] going off and serving other gods, bowing down to them, to the sun or the moon or the whole star-host of heaven — which I never authorized —

[4] and word reaches you, and you hear it, investigate it thoroughly. If it turns out to be true, if the evidence holds, if this disgusting thing really happened in Israel —

[5] then bring that man or woman who did this evil thing out to your city gates and stone them to death.

[6] Two or three witnesses are required for execution. Never on the word of just one.

[7] The witnesses throw the first stones. Then everyone else joins in. Purge the evil from among you.

[8] When a case comes before your local court that is too hard to decide — bloodshed, lawsuit, assault, any disputed matter — take it up to the place the Lord your God will choose.

[9] Go to the levitical priests and to the judge presiding in those days. Lay it out. They will hand down the verdict.

[10] Do what they tell you. From that place the Lord chooses, follow their ruling — to the letter.

[11] Whatever instruction they give you, whatever judgment they pronounce — do it. Do not turn from their word, left or right.

[12] Anyone arrogant enough to defy the priest standing in service of the Lord your God, or the judge — that person dies. Purge the evil from Israel.

[13] Everyone will hear about it. Everyone will be afraid. No one will dare act so brazenly again.

[14] When you enter the land the Lord your God is giving you, and you take possession of it and settle there, and you say, "Let's appoint a king over us, like every nation around us" —

[15] then make sure the king you appoint is the one the Lord your God chooses. He must be from your own people. Never set a foreigner over you — someone who is not your brother.

[16] But this king must not stockpile horses. He must not send the people back to Egypt to build up his cavalry — the Lord has told you: never go back that way again.

[17] He must not collect wives — they will turn his heart away. He must not pile up silver and gold for himself.

[18] When he takes the throne, he is to write himself a copy of this law, taken from the scroll the levitical priests keep.

[19] It stays with him. He reads it every day, all his life — so he learns to fear the Lord his God, to guard every word of this instruction, to live by these statutes,

[20] so his heart will not lift itself above his brothers, so he will not swerve from the command, right or left — so that he and his sons may reign long in the midst of Israel.

[1] The Levitical priests — the whole tribe of Levi — get no share of land, no inheritance alongside their brothers. Their inheritance is the fire-offerings of the LORD. That is what they live on.

[2] No inheritance among their brothers. The LORD himself is their inheritance — exactly as he promised them.

[3] Here is what the priests are owed from the people, from anyone offering a bull or a sheep: the shoulder, the jaws, and the stomach go to the priest.

[4] First of your grain, your wine, your oil. First wool from the shearing. All of it to him.

[5] Because the LORD your God chose him out of every tribe — him and his sons after him — to stand and serve in the name of the LORD, always.

[6] If a Levite leaves any town in Israel where he has been living and comes — driven by desire — to the place the LORD chooses,

[7] he gets to serve in the name of the LORD his God, just like his fellow Levites who stand there before the LORD.

[8] Equal share. Equal portion. Whatever he made from selling his family property is his own.

[9] When you enter the land the LORD your God is giving you, do not learn to imitate the abominations of those nations.

[10] No one among you shall pass his son or daughter through fire. No diviners. No fortune-tellers. No readers of omens. No sorcerers.

[11] No casters of spells. No mediums. No spiritists. No one who consults the dead.

[12] Anyone who does these things is repulsive to the LORD. It is precisely because of these abominations that the LORD your God is driving those nations out before you.

[13] You — you are to be whole before the LORD your God. Undivided.

[14] These nations you are dispossessing listen to fortune-tellers and diviners. But you — the LORD your God has not given you that.

[15] A prophet — the LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me, from among your own brothers. Listen to him.

[16] This is what you asked for at Horeb on the day of the assembly, when you said: "Don't let me hear the voice of the LORD my God again, don't let me see this massive fire again — or I will die."

[17] And the LORD said to me: "What they said is good.

[18] I will raise up for them a prophet like you, from among their brothers. I will put my words in his mouth, and he will tell them everything I command him.

[19] And anyone who refuses to listen to my words — the words that prophet speaks in my name — I myself will call him to account.

[20] But the prophet who dares to speak a word in my name that I did not command him to speak, or who speaks in the name of other gods — that prophet dies."

[21] You may be asking yourself: "How will we know which word the LORD has not spoken?"

[22] Here is how. When a prophet speaks in the name of the LORD and the thing does not happen — does not come true — that is a word the LORD did not speak. The prophet spoke it on his own nerve. Do not fear him.

[1] When the LORD your God cuts down the nations whose land he's handing over to you, when you've driven them out and settled into their cities and their houses,

[2] set aside three cities for yourselves in the heart of the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess.

[3] Build roads to them. Divide the territory God is giving you into three districts, so anyone who kills someone can run there and find refuge.

[4] Here's who qualifies — the killer who flees there and lives: someone who struck down his neighbor without meaning to, with no prior hatred between them.

[5] Picture it: a man heads into the forest with his neighbor to cut wood. He swings the axe to fell a tree, the iron head flies off the handle, hits his neighbor, and the neighbor dies. That man runs to one of these cities and lives.

[6] Otherwise the avenger of blood, white-hot with grief, could chase down the killer — and if the distance is too great, overtake him and strike him dead, even though he didn't deserve death, since there was no hatred between them before.

[7] That's why I'm commanding you: set aside three cities.

[8] And if the LORD your God expands your territory, as he swore to your fathers, and gives you the whole land he promised them —

[9] because you carefully kept everything I'm commanding you today, loving the LORD your God and walking in his ways always — then add three more cities to these three.

[10] Don't let innocent blood be spilled in the land the LORD your God is giving you as your inheritance. Don't let bloodguilt land on you.

[11] But if a man hates his neighbor, lies in wait, attacks him, strikes him dead, and then runs to one of these cities —

[12] the elders of his hometown send for him, drag him back, and hand him over to the avenger of blood. He dies.

[13] Don't look at him with pity. Purge the blood of the innocent from Israel. Then it will go well with you.

[14] Don't move your neighbor's boundary marker, the one set by those who came before, in the inheritance you receive in the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess.

[15] One witness isn't enough to convict anyone of anything — any crime, any sin, any offense. A matter stands only on the testimony of two or three witnesses.

[16] If a malicious witness rises against someone, accusing him of wrongdoing,

[17] both parties involved in the dispute must stand before the LORD — before the priests and judges serving in those days.

[18] The judges must investigate thoroughly. If the witness turns out to be a liar, falsely accusing his brother,

[19] do to him exactly what he intended to do to his brother. Burn the evil out from among you.

[20] The rest will hear about it and be afraid, and they won't dare do such an evil thing in your midst again.

[21] Show no pity. Life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.

[1] When you march out to face your enemies and see horses, chariots, and an army that dwarfs yours — do not flinch. The LORD your God is with you, the same God who brought you up out of Egypt.

[2] When the battle is about to begin, the priest will step forward and address the troops.

[3] He will say: "Listen, Israel. Today you go to war against your enemies. Don't lose your nerve. Don't be afraid. Don't panic. Don't break.

[4] The LORD your God marches with you. He fights for you. He will give you the victory."

[5] Then the officers will speak: "Has anyone here just built a new house and not dedicated it? Go home. You might die in battle and another man dedicate it.

[6] Anyone planted a vineyard and never tasted the fruit? Go home. You might die in battle and another man enjoy it.

[7] Anyone engaged to a woman but not yet married? Go home. You might die in battle and another man marry her."

[8] The officers will continue: "Is anyone afraid? Anyone losing their nerve? Go home. Don't let your fear infect the rest."

[9] Once the officers finish speaking, they will appoint commanders to lead the troops into battle.

[10] When you approach a city to attack it, first offer terms of peace.

[11] If they accept and open their gates, every person inside becomes your labor force, and they will serve you.

[12] But if they refuse peace and choose war, lay siege to the city.

[13] When the LORD your God hands it over to you, put every man inside to the sword.

[14] The women, the children, the livestock, everything of value in the city — take it all as plunder. You may enjoy the spoils the LORD your God gives you from your enemies.

[15] This is how you handle cities far from you — cities that don't belong to the nations near at hand.

[16] But the cities of these peoples — the ones the LORD your God is handing over to you as your inheritance — leave nothing alive that breathes.

[17] Devote them all to destruction: the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites — exactly as the LORD your God commanded.

[18] Otherwise they will teach you their detestable practices — the rituals they perform for their gods — and you will sin against the LORD your God.

[19] When you besiege a city for a long time, fighting to capture it, do not take an axe to its trees. Eat from them, yes — but do not cut them down. Is a tree of the field a man, that you should besiege it?

[20] Only trees you know bear no food may be felled, cut down to build siegeworks against the city that wages war on you — until it falls.

[1] If a body turns up in the open country of the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess — slain, dumped in a field, and nobody knows who did it —

[2] your elders and judges shall go out and measure the distance from the corpse to the surrounding towns.

[3] The town nearest the body — its elders shall take a heifer that has never been worked, never pulled a yoke,

[4] and lead it down to a valley with running water, a place untilled, unplanted, and there in the wadi they shall break its neck.

[5] Then the priests, the sons of Levi, step forward — for the LORD your God chose them to serve him and to bless in his name, and every dispute, every assault, is settled by their word.

[6] All the elders of the town nearest the corpse shall wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken in the valley,

[7] and they shall declare: "Our hands did not spill this blood. Our eyes did not see it.

[8] Absolve your people Israel, LORD, the ones you redeemed. Do not lay innocent blood on them." And the blood-guilt will be lifted from them.

[9] So you purge the innocent blood from your midst — doing what is right in the LORD's eyes.

[10] When you go out to war against your enemies and the LORD your God hands them over to you and you take captives,

[11] and among the captives you see a beautiful woman and want her and would take her as your wife —

[12] bring her into your house. She shall shave her head, trim her nails,

[13] strip off the clothes of her captivity, and live in your house mourning her father and mother for a full month. After that you may go in to her — you become her husband, she becomes your wife.

[14] But if she no longer pleases you, let her go free. You shall not sell her, shall not treat her as merchandise — you have already humbled her.

[15] If a man has two wives, one loved and one unloved, and both bear him sons, and the firstborn belongs to the unloved one —

[16] when the day comes to divide the inheritance among his sons, he cannot favor the son of the loved wife over the actual firstborn, the son of the unloved.

[17] No — he must recognize the firstborn, son of the unloved, and give him the double portion of everything he owns. That son is the first fruit of his strength. The right of the firstborn is his.

[18] If a man has a stubborn, rebellious son who will not listen to his father or mother, who will not obey even when they discipline him,

[19] his father and mother shall seize him and bring him to the elders of his town, to the gate of his place,

[20] and they shall say to the elders: "This son of ours is stubborn and rebellious. He will not listen. He is a glutton and a drunk."

[21] Then all the men of the town shall stone him to death. You shall burn the evil out of your midst — and all Israel will hear and tremble.

[22] If a man commits a crime worthy of death and is executed, and you hang his body on a tree,

[23] his corpse shall not stay on the tree overnight. Bury him that same day. A man hanging there is under God's curse — and you shall not defile the land the LORD your God is giving you as inheritance.

[1] If you see your neighbor's ox or sheep wandering off, don't pretend you didn't notice. Bring it back to them. [2] If the owner doesn't live nearby, or you don't know who they are, take the animal home and keep it until they come looking. Then return it. [3] Same goes for a lost donkey, a lost cloak, anything your neighbor has lost and you've found. You can't just look the other way.

[4] If you see your neighbor's donkey or ox collapsed on the road, don't walk past. Help get it back on its feet.

[5] A woman is not to dress as a man, and a man is not to dress as a woman. The Lord your God finds this kind of blurring repulsive.

[6] If you come across a bird's nest on the path — in a tree or on the ground — with the mother sitting on her chicks or her eggs, don't take the mother along with the young. [7] Let the mother go. Take the young if you want. Do this, and life will go well for you and stretch out long.

[8] When you build a new house, put a railing around the roof. Otherwise, if someone falls, their blood is on your hands.

[9] Don't plant two kinds of seed in your vineyard, or the whole crop — what you sowed and what the vineyard produces — becomes off-limits, set apart.

[10] Don't yoke an ox and a donkey together to plow.

[11] Don't wear clothing woven from wool and linen mixed together.

[12] Make tassels on the four corners of the cloak you wrap yourself in.

[13] Suppose a man marries a woman, sleeps with her, then turns against her — [14] accuses her, smears her name, says, "I married this woman, slept with her, and found no evidence she was a virgin." [15] Then the girl's father and mother are to take the proof of her virginity to the elders at the city gate. [16] The father will speak: "I gave my daughter in marriage to this man, and now he's turned against her. [17] He's accusing her — saying he found no proof she was a virgin. Well, here it is — the proof." And they'll spread the cloth before the elders. [18] The elders will take the man and discipline him. [19] They'll fine him a hundred shekels of silver and hand it to the girl's father, because he dragged the name of an Israelite woman through the mud. She remains his wife. He cannot divorce her, ever.

[20] But if the accusation is true — no proof of virginity is found — [21] then they'll bring the girl to the door of her father's house, and the men of her city will stone her to death. She brought shame on Israel by sleeping around while still under her father's roof. Burn the evil out from among you.

[22] If a man is caught in bed with another man's wife, both of them die — the man and the woman. Burn the evil out of Israel.

[23] If a young woman, engaged to be married, is met by a man in the city and he sleeps with her, [24] take them both to the city gate and stone them to death — her because she didn't cry out, though she was in the city, and him because he violated his neighbor's woman. Burn the evil out from among you.

[25] But if the man finds the engaged girl out in the open country and forces himself on her, only the man dies. [26] Do nothing to the girl. She committed no sin worth dying for. This is like a man attacking and murdering his neighbor — [27] he caught her in the open country, and even if she cried out, no one was there to save her.

[28] If a man encounters a young woman who isn't engaged, grabs her, and sleeps with her, and they're caught — [29] the man pays the girl's father fifty shekels of silver, and she becomes his wife because he violated her. He cannot divorce her, ever.

[30] A man is not to take his father's wife. He is not to uncover what belongs to his father.

[1] No man whose genitals are crushed or severed enters the assembly of the LORD.

[2] No one born of a forbidden union enters the assembly of the LORD — not even to the tenth generation.

[3] No Ammonite or Moabite enters the assembly of the LORD — not even to the tenth generation, not ever.

[4] Because they refused you bread and water on the road out of Egypt, and because they hired Balaam son of Beor from Pethor in Mesopotamia to curse you.

[5] But the LORD your God refused to listen to Balaam. He turned the curse into a blessing — because the LORD your God loves you.

[6] Never seek their peace or their prosperity. Not as long as you live.

[7] Do not despise an Edomite — he is your brother. Do not despise an Egyptian — you were a stranger in his land.

[8] Their third generation may enter the assembly of the LORD.

[9] When you march out against your enemies, keep yourself clear of every foul thing.

[10] If a man among you becomes unclean from a nocturnal emission, he leaves the camp and stays outside.

[11] Toward evening he washes himself with water, and when the sun goes down he may return to the camp.

[12] Designate a place outside the camp where you can go to relieve yourself.

[13] Carry a spade with your gear. When you squat outside, dig a hole and cover what comes out of you.

[14] Because the LORD your God walks through the middle of your camp to deliver you and hand your enemies over. So your camp must be holy. He must not see anything indecent among you and turn away.

[15] If a slave escapes to you from his master, do not hand him back.

[16] Let him settle wherever he chooses, in whatever town suits him. Do not crush him.

[17] No daughter of Israel becomes a temple prostitute. No son of Israel becomes one either.

[18] Never bring the wages of a prostitute or the price of a dog into the house of the LORD your God to pay a vow. Both are disgusting to him.

[19] Do not charge your brother interest — not on money, not on food, not on anything that earns interest.

[20] You may charge a foreigner interest, but not your brother — so the LORD your God may bless everything your hand touches in the land you are entering to possess.

[21] When you make a vow to the LORD your God, do not delay paying it. He will hold you to it, and delay becomes sin.

[22] If you simply do not vow, no sin attaches to you.

[23] But whatever crosses your lips, do it. You made the promise freely — to the LORD your God, with your own mouth. Now keep it.

[24] When you walk through your neighbor's vineyard, eat your fill of grapes — but do not carry any away in a container.

[25] When you walk through your neighbor's standing grain, you may pluck the heads with your hand — but do not put a sickle to it.

[1] When a man marries a woman and she fails to please him because he discovers something indecent about her, he may write her a certificate of divorce, place it in her hand, and send her out of his house.

[2] After leaving, she is free to marry another man.

[3] If that second husband also turns against her and writes her a certificate of divorce, places it in her hand, and sends her out — or if he dies —

[4] her first husband cannot take her back as his wife after she has been defiled. That would be detestable before the Lord. Do not bring guilt on the land the Lord your God is giving you as your inheritance.

[5] A newly married man is exempt from military service and any public duty. For one full year he stays home, free, to bring joy to the wife he has taken.

[6] No one may take a millstone — not even the upper one — as collateral on a loan. That is taking a man's livelihood as a pledge.

[7] If a man is caught kidnapping a fellow Israelite, treating him as property or selling him, the kidnapper must die. Burn the evil out from among you.

[8] When skin disease breaks out, watch carefully and follow exactly what the Levitical priests instruct you. Do what I commanded them.

[9] Remember what the Lord your God did to Miriam on the road out of Egypt.

[10] When you make any kind of loan to your neighbor, do not march into his house to seize what he pledged.

[11] Stand outside. Let the man you are lending to bring the pledge out to you.

[12] If he is poor, do not sleep with his pledge in your possession.

[13] Return it to him at sunset, so he can sleep in his own cloak and bless you. That will count as righteousness before the Lord your God.

[14] Do not exploit a hired worker who is poor and needy, whether he is a fellow Israelite or a foreigner living in one of your towns.

[15] Pay him his wages each day before the sun sets. He is poor and counting on it. Otherwise he will cry out to the Lord against you, and you will be guilty.

[16] Parents must not be put to death for their children, nor children for their parents. Each person dies for his own sin.

[17] Do not twist justice against the foreigner or the orphan. Do not seize a widow's cloak as a pledge.

[18] Remember: you were a slave in Egypt, and the Lord your God redeemed you from there. That is why I am commanding you to do this.

[19] When you are harvesting in your field and you forget a sheaf, do not go back for it. Leave it for the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow — so the Lord your God will bless everything your hands do.

[20] When you beat the olives from your trees, do not go over the branches again. What remains belongs to the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow.

[21] When you harvest your vineyard, do not pick it clean. What remains belongs to the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow.

[22] Remember: you were a slave in Egypt. That is why I am commanding you to do this.

[1] When two people have a dispute and bring it to court, the judges will hear the case and rule — vindicating the one in the right, condemning the one in the wrong.

[2] If the guilty party deserves a beating, the judge will have him lie down and be flogged in his presence — the number of lashes matching the severity of the crime.

[3] But the limit is forty. Not one more. Go beyond that and you've degraded a fellow human being in public — and you've degraded your community along with him.

[4] Don't muzzle an ox while it's threshing the grain.

[5] When brothers live together and one of them dies without leaving a son, the widow must not marry a stranger from outside the family. Her husband's brother is to take her as his wife and fulfill the duty of a brother-in-law to her.

[6] The first son she bears will carry the dead brother's name — so his name isn't wiped out of Israel.

[7] But if the man refuses to take his brother's widow, she will go up to the elders at the city gate and say: "My husband's brother won't keep his brother's name alive in Israel. He refuses the duty."

[8] Then the elders of his town will summon him and talk to him. If he stands his ground and says, "I don't want to marry her,"

[9] then his sister-in-law will approach him in front of the elders, pull the sandal off his foot, spit in his face, and declare: "This is what happens to the man who refuses to build up his brother's house."

[10] From that day on, his family in Israel will be known as: "The House of the Unsandaled."

[11] If two men are fighting and the wife of one of them rushes in to rescue her husband from his attacker, and she grabs the attacker by his private parts —

[12] cut off her hand. No mercy.

[13] You must not carry two different weights in your bag — one heavy, one light.

[14] You must not keep two different measures in your house — one large, one small.

[15] Use accurate, honest weights. Use accurate, honest measures. Then you will live long in the land the Lord your God is giving you.

[16] Anyone who cheats with weights and measures is detestable to the Lord your God. Anyone who deals dishonestly — detestable.

[17] Remember what Amalek did to you on the road out of Egypt.

[18] How he ambushed you when you were exhausted and worn down — picking off the stragglers at the back, the weak and the weary. He had no fear of God.

[19] So when the Lord your God gives you rest from all the enemies around you, in the land he's handing you as your inheritance — wipe out the memory of Amalek from under heaven. Don't forget.

[1] When you finally cross into the land the LORD your God is handing you—when you take it, settle in it, make it home—[2] take the first of every harvest the ground gives you, pack it in a basket, and carry it to the place the LORD your God will choose as the dwelling for his name.

[3] Walk up to the priest on duty in those days and say: "Today I declare before the LORD your God that I have arrived in the land he swore to our ancestors he would give us."

[4] The priest will take the basket from your hands and set it before the altar of the LORD your God.

[5] Then, standing there in his presence, you will speak these words:

"My father was a wandering Aramean. He went down into Egypt with almost nothing—just a handful of people—and lived there as an outsider. But there he grew into a nation: great, strong, vast in number. [6] The Egyptians turned on us. They abused us. They crushed us under brutal labor. [7] So we cried out to the LORD, the God of our ancestors—and the LORD heard. He saw our suffering, our exhaustion, our oppression. [8] And the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a fist of iron and an arm stretched wide, with terror and signs and wonders. [9] He led us here and gave us this land—a land flooding with milk and honey. [10] So now, look—I'm bringing the first of the harvest from the soil you, LORD, have given me."

Set the basket down before the LORD your God and bow low before him. [11] Then celebrate. Every good thing the LORD your God has given you and your household—enjoy it. And bring the Levite and the foreigner among you into the celebration.

[12] Every third year—the year of the tithe—when you've finished setting aside a tenth of everything you produced and given it to the Levite, the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow, so they can eat their fill in your towns—[13] then say before the LORD your God:

"I've cleared out the sacred portion from my house. I've given it to the Levite, the foreigner, the orphan, the widow—just as you commanded. I haven't broken your commands. I haven't forgotten them. [14] I didn't eat any of it while mourning. I didn't touch it while ritually unclean. I didn't offer any of it to the dead. I obeyed the voice of the LORD my God. I did everything you commanded. [15] Look down from your holy home, from heaven, and bless your people Israel and this soil you've given us—just as you swore to our ancestors—a land flooding with milk and honey."

[16] Today the LORD your God is commanding you to live by these decrees and rulings. Guard them. Do them. With everything in you—heart and soul.

[17] Today you have declared the LORD to be your God—that you will walk his ways, keep his decrees, his commands, his rulings, and listen to his voice.

[18] And today the LORD has declared you to be his own, his treasured people, just as he promised—and that you will keep all his commands. [19] He will lift you high above every nation he has made—high in praise, in fame, in honor—and you will be a people consecrated to the LORD your God, just as he said.

[1] Moses and Israel's elders gave the people their orders: Keep every command I'm laying on you today.

[2] The day you cross the Jordan into the land the LORD your God is giving you, set up massive stones and coat them with plaster.

[3] Write on them every word of this law — the moment you cross over — so you can enter the land the LORD your God is giving you, a land flowing with milk and honey, just as the LORD, the God of your ancestors, promised you.

[4] So when you've crossed the Jordan, plant these stones on Mount Ebal — the ones I'm telling you about right now — and coat them with plaster.

[5] Build an altar there to the LORD your God, an altar of stones. No iron tool touches them.

[6] Build the altar of the LORD your God with uncut stones, and offer burnt offerings on it to the LORD your God.

[7] Sacrifice fellowship offerings there. Eat them. Celebrate before the LORD your God.

[8] And on those stones write every word of this law — etched plain, etched clear.

[9] Then Moses, with the Levitical priests, spoke to all Israel: Quiet, Israel. Listen. Today you have become the people of the LORD your God.

[10] So obey the voice of the LORD your God. Live out his commands and his statutes — the ones I'm giving you today.

[11] That same day Moses gave the people this charge:

[12] When you've crossed the Jordan, these tribes stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people — Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.

[13] And these stand on Mount Ebal to pronounce the curse — Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.

[14] Then the Levites will raise their voices and call out to every Israelite:

[15] Cursed is the one who carves an idol or casts a metal image — a thing the LORD detests, the handiwork of a craftsman — and sets it up in secret. And all the people answer: Amen.

[16] Cursed is the one who treats his father or mother with contempt. And all the people answer: Amen.

[17] Cursed is the one who moves his neighbor's boundary stone. And all the people answer: Amen.

[18] Cursed is the one who sends a blind man wandering off the road. And all the people answer: Amen.

[19] Cursed is the one who twists justice against the immigrant, the orphan, the widow. And all the people answer: Amen.

[20] Cursed is the man who sleeps with his father's wife — he has uncovered his father's bed. And all the people answer: Amen.

[21] Cursed is the one who has sex with any animal. And all the people answer: Amen.

[22] Cursed is the one who sleeps with his sister — his father's daughter or his mother's daughter. And all the people answer: Amen.

[23] Cursed is the one who sleeps with his mother-in-law. And all the people answer: Amen.

[24] Cursed is the one who strikes down his neighbor in secret. And all the people answer: Amen.

[25] Cursed is the one who takes a bribe to kill the innocent. And all the people answer: Amen.

[26] Cursed is the one who refuses to uphold the words of this law by living them out. And all the people answer: Amen.

[1] If you actually listen to the voice of the LORD your God — if you guard and live out every command I'm giving you today — then the LORD your God will lift you high above every nation on earth.

[2] All these blessings will hunt you down and overtake you, because you listened to the voice of the LORD your God:

[3] Blessed in the city. Blessed in the field.

[4] Blessed: the children of your body, the harvest of your soil, the young of your herds, the calves of your cattle, the lambs of your flocks.

[5] Blessed: your basket and your kneading bowl.

[6] Blessed when you walk in. Blessed when you walk out.

[7] The enemies who rise against you — the LORD will shatter them in front of your eyes. They'll march at you down one road and scatter from you down seven.

[8] The LORD will command the blessing to land on your barns, on everything your hand touches. He will bless you in the land the LORD your God is giving you.

[9] The LORD will establish you as his holy people, just as he swore he would, if you keep the commands of the LORD your God and walk in his ways.

[10] Every nation on earth will see that the name of the LORD is spoken over you — and they will be afraid of you.

[11] The LORD will overwhelm you with good things: the fruit of your body, the young of your livestock, the harvest of your soil — in the land the LORD swore to your ancestors he would give you.

[12] The LORD will throw open his good storehouse — the skies — to send rain on your land in its season and to bless everything your hands do. You will lend to many nations and borrow from none.

[13] The LORD will make you the head, not the tail. You'll be on top, never on the bottom — if you listen to the commands of the LORD your God that I'm giving you today, and guard them, and do them,
